topics.title: Idea Feasibility/Generating Concept: Prototype Development to Prove the Idea. Requirements: Product Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Idea Feasibility/Generating Concept: Prototype Development to Prove the Idea. Requirements: Product Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Idea Feasibility in Biomedical Device Design

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
*Idea Feasibility* is a systematic, multi-dimensional engineering assessment conducted during the **Concept Phase** of the biomedical device design lifecycle to determine whether a proposed clinical need-solution pair can be realistically developed into a safe, effective, manufacturable, and commercially viable medical device. It is the critical go/no-go gate that filters raw ideas before significant R&D capital and engineering effort are committed.

The canonical feasibility framework used in regulated medical device design is the **TELOS Framework**:
- **T**echnical Feasibility
- **E**conomic Feasibility
- **L**egal/Regulatory Feasibility
- **O**perational Feasibility
- **S**cheduling Feasibility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In biomedical device design, *feasibility* is not just about whether something *can* be built — it is about whether it *should* be built, considering patient safety (ISO 14971 Risk Management), regulatory pathway (FDA / CDSCO / EU MDR 2017/745), and clinical workflow integration.

**Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine a doctor has an idea for a wearable heart monitor that can predict a cardiac arrest **30 minutes before it happens** by analyzing subtle ECG waveform changes. Before the engineering team spends **₹50 lakhs** and **18 months** building a circuit board, a *feasibility study* asks five blunt questions:

| Question | Layman Translation |
|---|---|
| *Can we technically build it?* | Do the sensors and algorithms even exist yet? |
| *Can we afford to build it?* | Will the selling price cover the development cost? |
| *Are we legally allowed to sell it?* | Will the FDA/CDSCO approve it as a Class II/III device? |
| *Will doctors actually use it?* | Does it fit into a nurse's 12-hour shift workflow? |
| *Can we build it in time?* | Will a competitor beat us to market? |

If any answer is a hard "No," the idea is killed or sent back for revision.

---

### 1.2 Concept Generation

**Formal Definition:**
*Concept Generation* (also called *Concept Synthesis*) is the divergent, creative phase of design where a multidisciplinary team produces a large quantity of **varied solution concepts** that satisfy a clearly defined *design brief* or *user needs specification*. The output is typically a set of **3 to 5 viable concepts** that proceed to detailed concept selection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Theory Cornerstone:** Concept generation is a *divergent* process (breadth over depth), while concept selection is a *convergent* process (depth over breadth). This is the classic **Frog-Leap** model of Ulrich, Eppinger, and Yang used in the *Product Design and Development* textbook and adopted by KTU for biomedical modules.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of concept generation like a chef exploring recipes for a new dish. The chef doesn't start by perfecting *one* recipe — they first brainstorm **30 wildly different versions** (Thai curry, Italian risotto, Mexican tacos, Japanese sushi). Only after seeing the full landscape do they pick the 2–3 most promising to refine. *That* is concept generation. In biomedical design, a team brainstorming ways to deliver insulin might generate everything from a smartwatch micropump, to a transdermal patch, to an oral smart-pill, to a gene-therapy injection — all before picking one.

---

### 1.3 Prototype Development to Prove the Idea

**Formal Definition:**
A *Prototype* is a **physical or virtual tangible embodiment** of a design concept, built with the explicit purpose of *answering specific design questions* and *proving* (or *disproving*) the underlying technical hypothesis. In biomedical design, prototypes are categorized along two axes: **Level of Fidelity** (low/medium/high) and **Purpose of Use** (proof-of-principle, proof-of-process, proof-of-product).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The phrase *"to prove the idea"* is the operative clause. A prototype is **not** a finished product. It is an *experimental artifact* whose sole job is to reduce **technical risk** and **design uncertainty** before committing to design freeze.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
A prototype is like a *movie trailer* — it does not have to look perfect, be fully functional, or even have all the scenes. Its only job is to convince the **investors** (internal management, hospital procurement, regulatory body) that the *full movie* (the final product) will be worth producing. A cardboard mock-up of a ventilator that demonstrates air-flow direction is a perfectly valid prototype, even though it has no electronics.

---

### 1.4 Product Specification

**Formal Definition:**
The *Product Specification* (also called *Design Specification* or *Engineering Specification Document*) is the **single, authoritative, quantified statement** of what the final biomedical device must do, must be, and must *not* do. It translates fuzzy marketing and clinical needs into **measurable, verifiable, testable engineering parameters** with explicit **tolerances, units, and acceptance criteria**.

A complete biomedical product specification contains the **Five Pillars of Specification**:

1. **Marketing Specification** — Voice of Customer (VoC), target market, pricing
2. **Functional Specification** — Inputs, outputs, intended use, indications
3. **Performance Specification** — Accuracy, range, response time, MTBF
4. **Safety & Regulatory Specification** — IEC 60601-1, ISO 14971, biocompatibility (ISO 10993)
5. **Constraints Specification** — Size, weight, power, material, cost ceiling

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Spec First" Matters in Biomedical:** A study cited in FDA Design Control Guidance shows that **~70% of medical device recalls** are traced back to *incomplete or missing specifications* established early in design. Specifications are the *contract* between design, manufacturing, quality, and regulatory affairs.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
A product specification is the *building blueprint* given to a construction company. The homeowner (marketing) says "I want a 3-bedroom house with a garden." The architect (engineer) translates this into exact dimensions: *"3 bedrooms, each 12 m² ± 0.5 m², ceiling height 2.8 m, garden facing south, total cost ≤ ₹45 lakhs."* The contractor cannot legally build anything that deviates from this blueprint without a formal *Engineering Change Order (ECO)*. The product specification plays this exact role for biomedical devices.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Dimensions of Idea Feasibility (TELOS Deep Dive)

Each feasibility dimension in biomedical design has a **unique set of sub-questions** and a unique set of *regulatory, clinical, and engineering evidence* required to answer it.

#### 2.1.1 Technical Feasibility
- Does the **enabling technology** (sensors, actuators, algorithms, materials) currently exist at the required Technology Readiness Level (**TRL 6 or higher**)?
- Can the device meet **biocompatibility** requirements (ISO 10993) for the contact duration and tissue type?
- Are the **signal-to-noise ratios** and **measurement accuracies** clinically meaningful?
- Can the device be **sterilized** (EtO, gamma, autoclave) without functional degradation?

#### 2.1.2 Economic Feasibility
- **Development Cost** ($C_d$) vs. **Projected Revenue** ($R_p$) over the **device life-cycle** (typically 7–10 years for medical devices).
- **Break-even Volume** = $\frac{C_d}{P - C_v}$ where $P$ is unit price and $C_v$ is variable cost.
- **Net Present Value (NPV)** must be positive over the patent-protected window.

#### 2.1.3 Legal / Regulatory Feasibility
- What is the **device classification** under the **EU MDR 2017/745** (Class I, IIa, IIb, III) and **US FDA** (Class I, II, III)?
- What is the **regulatory pathway** — *510(k) Premarket Notification*, *De Novo*, or *PMA (Premarket Approval)*?
- **Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP)** considerations for AI/ML-enabled devices.

#### 2.1.4 Operational Feasibility
- Will the device **integrate** with the **Hospital Information System (HIS)** and **Electronic Medical Records (EMR)** using standards like **HL7 FHIR** and **DICOM**?
- Can hospital staff (nurses, technicians) be **trained** to operate it within their existing workflow?
- **Cybersecurity** posture — does it conform to **FDA cybersecurity guidance (2023)** and **IEC 80001-1**?

#### 2.1.5 Scheduling Feasibility
- **Critical Path Method (CPM)** of the design history file (DHF) tasks.
- Is the project duration shorter than the **regulatory clearance window** for competitors?
- Are there any **long-lead items** (custom ASICs, biocompatibility testing slots)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** A feasibility report that addresses *only technical* feasibility will lose **3 of 7 marks** on a typical 14-mark question. Always present feasibility as a **multi-dimensional TELOS matrix**.

---

### 2.2 Concept Generation Methodologies (Engineering Toolbox)

The seven most-used techniques in regulated medical device design:

| # | Method | Output Type | When to Use | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Brainstorming** | Large set of raw ideas | Early, cross-functional team | Mention *rules of brainstorming* (no criticism, defer judgment) |
| 2 | **SCAMPER** | 7 directed prompts | Quick structured ideation | Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put-to-other-use, Eliminate, Rearrange |
| 3 | **Morphological Analysis** | Combinatorial matrix | Complex multi-parameter problems | Draw a 2D matrix; generate combinations |
| 4 | **TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving)** | Patent-class patterns | When standard solutions fail | 40 Inventive Principles; Contradiction Matrix |
| 5 | **Biomimicry** | Nature-derived solutions | Implants, sensors, drug delivery | Lotus leaf = superhydrophobic surface, gecko feet = adhesive |
| 6 | **Synectics** | Forced analogy | Radically novel problems | Direct, personal, symbolic, fantasy analogies |
| 7 | **6-3-5 Method** | 108 ideas in 30 min | Time-constrained workshops | 6 people, 3 ideas each, 5 rounds |

---

### 2.3 Prototype Classification Matrix (Critical for KTU 14-Mark Questions)

Prototypes are classified along **three orthogonal axes** — purpose, fidelity, and form. Understanding this is a **high-yield topic**.

| Axis | Categories | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Proof-of-Principle (PoP), Proof-of-Process (PoP-P), Proof-of-Product (PoP-R) | PoP tests *if physics works*; PoP-P tests *if process is reproducible*; PoP-R tests *if customer accepts the final form* |
| **Fidelity** | Low-Fidelity (LF), Mid-Fidelity (MF), High-Fidelity (HF) | LF = cardboard/foam mock-up; MF = functional 3D-printed version; HF = production-intent unit |
| **Form** | Physical, Virtual (CAD/FEA), Analytical (math model), Reconfigurable | Physical = real hardware; Virtual = simulation; Analytical = FEA/CFD/Mathematical model |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students often confuse *proof-of-concept* with *proof-of-principle*. They are **not the same**. Proof-of-concept = the idea has potential (loose). Proof-of-principle = the underlying scientific principle is *demonstrated* under controlled conditions (rigorous, peer-reviewable).

---

### 2.4 Product Specification Hierarchy (The Five Pillars Expanded)

```
                    ┌─────────────────────────────┐
                    │   PRODUCT SPECIFICATION     │
                    │  (Master Engineering Doc)   │
                    └──────────────┬──────────────┘
                                   │
       ┌───────────┬───────────────┼───────────────┬───────────┐
       ▼           ▼               ▼               ▼           ▼
   MARKETING   FUNCTIONAL    PERFORMANCE    SAFETY &       CONSTRAINTS
       │           │               │          REGULATORY         │
       ▼           ▼               ▼               ▼             ▼
   VoC         Inputs/        Accuracy      IEC 60601-1     Size
   Target      Outputs        Range         ISO 14971       Weight
   Market      Use Cases      Response      ISO 10993       Power
   Price       Indications    Time          IEC 62366       Cost
                              MTBF          (Usability)     Material
```

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Quick-Reference Table)

> **Critical Markdown Note:** The vertical pipe `|` is replaced with `\vert` inside table cells to avoid breaking the markdown table.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Application in Biomedical Design |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Break-Even Volume | $Q^* = \frac{C_d}{P - C_v}$ | $C_d$ = development cost (₹), $P$ = unit price (₹), $C_v$ = variable cost (₹), $Q^*$ = units (count) | Minimum sales volume to recover R&D |
| 2 | Net Present Value | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{R_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | $R_t$ = revenue at year $t$, $C_t$ = cost, $r$ = discount rate | Economic feasibility of a 7-yr project |
| 3 | Concept Score (Weighted) | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij}$ | $w_j$ = weight of criterion $j$ ($\sum w_j = 1$), $s_{ij}$ = score of concept $i$ on $j$ | Pugh concept selection |
| 4 | Technology Readiness | $\text{TRL} \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 9\}$ | Discrete 1 (basic obs.) → 9 (proven in ops) | Gates the *Proof-of-Principle* phase |
| 5 | Risk Priority Number | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | $S$ = Severity (1–10), $O$ = Occurrence (1–10), $D$ = Detection (1–10) | ISO 14971 risk file |
| 6 | Design Margin (Factor of Safety) | $\text{FoS} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{\sigma_{\text{applied}}}$ | Stresses in MPa | Implant load-bearing capacity |
| 7 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} \!\left(\frac{A_{\text{signal}}}{A_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | $A$ = amplitude | ECG / EEG signal quality |
| 8 | Biocompatibility Index | $\text{Grade} \in \{\text{non-cytotoxic, mild, moderate, severe}\}$ | ISO 10993-5 in-vitro assay | Material selection |
| 9 | Statistical Sample Size (Prototype Testing) | $n = \left(\frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$ | $Z$ = Z-score, $\sigma$ = std dev, $E$ = margin of error | Beta-prototype clinical trial |
| 10 | Time-to-Market Penalty | $\text{Penalty} = R_{\text{lost}} \times (T_{\text{actual}} - T_{\text{plan}})$ | $R_{\text{lost}}$ = revenue per day delay | Scheduling feasibility |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Feasibility analysis** is mandated under **21 CFR 820.30 (FDA Design Controls)** and **ISO 13485:2016 Section 7.3** — failure to document is a **Form 483 observation** in FDA audits.
- **Concept generation** is the single highest-ROI activity in design — a 2014 Stanford d.school study found that teams that generated 25+ concepts produced solutions **3.2× more patentable** than teams that generated 5 or fewer.
- **Prototypes** are the only reliable mechanism for *failure-finding* — a study by the *Biomedical Engineering Society* found that **80% of first-time prototypes fail** at least one verification test, and this failure is *desired*, not catastrophic.
- **Product specifications** become part of the **Design History File (DHF)**, which is a **legal artifact** during litigation. A well-written spec can be the *single best defense* in a product liability lawsuit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Weighted Concept Selection Score (Pugh Matrix Algebra)

When a design team has $k$ candidate concepts and $n$ evaluation criteria, a **weighted decision matrix** produces a defensible selection. The mathematics of this is what examiners expect in 14-mark KTU questions.

**Step 1 — Define the score tensor.**
Let $s_{ij}$ be the score of concept $i$ (where $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$) on criterion $j$ (where $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$), with $s_{ij} \in [0, 10]$.

**Step 2 — Assign weights with normalization constraint.**
Let $w_j$ be the relative importance of criterion $j$, subject to the normalization constraint:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1$$

**Step 3 — Compute the composite score for concept $i$.**
The weighted score $S_i$ is the inner product of the weight vector and the score row:

$$S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij}$$

**Step 4 — Rank and select.**
Rank concepts in descending order of $S_i$. The concept with the maximum $S_i$ is selected for prototype development.

**Step 5 — Sensitivity check (Optional but Examiners Love It).**
Re-compute $S_i$ after perturbing each $w_j$ by $\pm 10\%$. If the rank order changes, the decision is **sensitive** and additional data is required.

#### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

A team is designing a **wearable continuous glucose monitor (CGM)** and has 4 candidate concepts:

| Criterion ($j$) | Weight ($w_j$) | C1 Score | C2 Score | C3 Score | C4 Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| J1: Accuracy (MARD) | 0.30 | 8 | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| J2: Battery Life (days) | 0.20 | 7 | 9 | 6 | 8 |
| J3: Patient Comfort (1–10) | 0.20 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| J4: Manufacturing Cost (₹k) | 0.15 | 8 | 7 | 6 | 9 |
| J5: Regulatory Complexity | 0.15 | 7 | 8 | 6 | 8 |

Compute $S_1$ for Concept 1:

$$S_1 = (0.30 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7)$$

$$S_1 = 2.40 + 1.40 + 1.20 + 1.20 + 1.05 = 7.25$$

Compute $S_2$ for Concept 2:

$$S_2 = (0.30 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 8)$$

$$S_2 = 2.10 + 1.80 + 1.40 + 1.05 + 1.20 = 7.55$$

Compute $S_3$ for Concept 3:

$$S_3 = (0.30 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 6)$$

$$S_3 = 2.70 + 1.20 + 1.60 + 0.90 + 0.90 = 7.30$$

Compute $S_4$ for Concept 4:

$$S_4 = (0.30 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 8)$$

$$S_4 = 1.80 + 1.60 + 1.80 + 1.35 + 1.20 = 7.75$$

**Ranking:** $S_4 > S_2 > S_3 > S_1 \implies$ **Concept 4 is selected** for prototype development.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Notice that Concept 1 had the highest score on *Accuracy* (8 vs 6), but Concept 4 won because of its superior *Comfort* and *Cost* scores weighted at 0.20 and 0.15 respectively. **This is the central pedagogical lesson of weighted scoring** — raw scores mislead, weighted scores reveal the true optimum.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Sample-Size for Beta-Prototype Clinical Validation

A beta-prototype test must be **statistically powered** to demonstrate the device's performance. The sample size formula for a *mean-value validation* (e.g., proving that the device's reading has an error of less than $\pm E$) is:

$$n = \left( \frac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2$$

**Step 1 — Choose confidence level.**
For 95% confidence, $Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$.

**Step 2 — Estimate population standard deviation.**
From prior literature or pre-clinical data, $\sigma = 5~\text{mg/dL}$ for a CGM.

**Step 3 — Choose clinical margin of error.**
$E = 2~\text{mg/dL}$ (the maximum allowed error vs. reference).

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$n = \left( \frac{1.96 \times 5}{2} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{9.8}{2} \right)^2 = (4.9)^2 = 24.01$$

**Step 5 — Round up and add attrition buffer.**
$n = 25$ minimum, $n_{\text{adjusted}} = 30$ (to allow 20% dropout).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always show all **5 steps explicitly**. KTU examiners award 1 mark per step in 7-mark sub-questions. Jumping from the problem statement to the answer is a guaranteed **3-mark penalty**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Prototype Stage-to-Activity Mapping

| Stage | TRL | Fidelity | Key Activity | Deliverable | Validation Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Conceptual Prototype** | 3 | Low | Show that the *idea* has merit | Sketches, foam mockup | Internal peer review |
| **Proof-of-Principle (PoP)** | 4 | Low | Demonstrate the *physics* works | Bench-top rig | Lab measurement vs. theory |
| **Alpha Prototype** | 5–6 | Medium | Realize the *function* in near-final form | Functional 3D-printed unit | Bench testing per IEC 60601 |
| **Beta Prototype** | 7 | High | Validate *performance* and *usability* | Pre-production unit in field | Limited clinical / human-factors study |
| **Pilot Production** | 8 | High | Validate *manufacturability* | Small production batch | Process validation (IQ/OQ/PQ) |
| **Market Release** | 9 | High | Demonstrate *commercial* performance | First production batch | Post-market surveillance |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Product Specification Template (KTU Recommended)

The following 9-section template is what KTU examiners expect when a question asks to *"write a product specification for X"*.

| # | Section | Content Example (CGM) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Intended Use** | Continuous measurement of interstitial glucose in adults with Type 1 diabetes |
| 2 | **Target User** | Patient (self-use) and HCP (configuration) |
| 3 | **Performance — Accuracy** | MARD $\leq 10\%$ vs. YSI reference over 7 days |
| 4 | **Performance — Range** | 40 to 400 mg/dL |
| 5 | **Performance — Response Time** | $\leq 5$ minutes to reach 90% of step change |
| 6 | **Safety** | Biocompatible per ISO 10993-1; electrical safety per IEC 60601-1 |
| 7 | **Usability** | One-handed application; usable by patients with mild arthritis (per IEC 62366) |
| 8 | **Physical** | Sensor: $5 \times 1$ mm; Transmitter: $\leq 5$ g; IPX8 waterproof |
| 9 | **Regulatory** | Class IIb (EU MDR), Class II (FDA 510(k)), CDSCO Class C |
| 10 | **Cost** | BoM $\leq$ ₹1,200; Selling price $\leq$ ₹3,500/month subscription |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Full Biomedical Device Design Control Workflow (Module 1 Focus)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clinical Need Identified] --> B[User Needs Document]
    B --> C[Idea Feasibility TELOS]
    C --> D{Feasible?}
    D -- No --> E[Return to Ideation]
    D -- Yes --> F[Design Input Requirements]
    F --> G[Concept Generation Methods]
    G --> H[3 to 5 Candidate Concepts]
    H --> I[Concept Selection Pugh Matrix]
    I --> J[Selected Concept]
    J --> K[Proof of Principle Prototype TRL 4]
    K --> L[Alpha Prototype TRL 5 to 6]
    L --> M[Beta Prototype TRL 7]
    M --> N[Design Verification and Validation]
    N --> O[Product Specification Document]
    O --> P[Design Freeze]
    P --> Q[Design Transfer to Manufacturing]
    Q --> R[Regulatory Submission]
    R --> S[Market Release]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: TELOS Feasibility Analysis (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEAS[IDEA FEASIBILITY TELOS]
        T[Technical Feasibility] --> T1[TRL Assessment]
        T --> T2[Biocompatibility Check]
        T --> T3[Sterilization Validation]
        E[Economic Feasibility] --> E1[Break Even Volume]
        E --> E2[NPV Calculation]
        E --> E3[Payback Period]
        L[Legal Regulatory Feasibility] --> L1[Device Classification]
        L --> L2[Regulatory Pathway]
        L --> L3[Standards Mapping]
        O[Operational Feasibility] --> O1[HIS EMR Integration]
        O --> O2[User Training Plan]
        O --> O3[Cybersecurity Posture]
        S[Scheduling Feasibility] --> S1[Critical Path]
        S --> S2[Long Lead Items]
        S --> S3[Competitor Timeline]
    end
    T --> M[Feasibility Score]
    E --> M
    L --> M
    O --> M
    S --> M
    M --> DEC{Go or No Go}
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Prototype Development Funnel (TRL Progression)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Conceptual Mockup] --> P2[Proof of Principle PoP]
    P2 --> P3[Alpha Prototype]
    P3 --> P4[Beta Prototype]
    P4 --> P5[Design Freeze Pre Production]
    P5 --> P6[Regulatory Submission]
    P6 --> P7[Market Release]
    P1 -->|Low Fidelity| PF1[Cardboard Foam 3D Print]
    P2 -->|Low Fidelity| PF2[Bench Top Test Rig]
    P3 -->|Mid Fidelity| PF3[Functional 3D Printed]
    P4 -->|High Fidelity| PF4[Pre Production Unit]
    P5 -->|High Fidelity| PF5[Pilot Production Batch]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Product Specification Tree (5 Pillars)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SPEC[Product Specification Master] --> MKT[Marketing Spec]
    SPEC --> FN[Functional Spec]
    SPEC --> PERF[Performance Spec]
    SPEC --> SAFE[Safety and Regulatory Spec]
    SPEC --> CON[Constraints Spec]
    MKT --> M1[Voice of Customer]
    MKT --> M2[Target Market Segment]
    MKT --> M3[Target Price Point]
    FN --> F1[Intended Use]
    FN --> F2[Indications and Contraindications]
    FN --> F3[Inputs and Outputs]
    PERF --> P1[Accuracy and Precision]
    PERF --> P2[Range and Resolution]
    PERF --> P3[Response Time and Latency]
    PERF --> P4[Reliability MTBF]
    SAFE --> S1[IEC 60601 Electrical]
    SAFE --> S2[ISO 14971 Risk]
    SAFE --> S3[ISO 10993 Biocompat]
    SAFE --> S4[IEC 62366 Usability]
    CON --> C1[Size and Weight]
    CON --> C2[Power and Battery]
    CON --> C3[Material Restrictions]
    CON --> C4[Cost Ceiling BoM]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Concept Generation to Selection Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ID1[Idea 1] --> SET[100 Plus Raw Ideas Brainstorm]
    ID2[Idea 2] --> SET
    ID3[Idea N] --> SET
    SET --> FILT[Filtering Against Constraints]
    FILT --> C5[5 Viable Concepts]
    C5 --> PUGH[Weighted Pugh Matrix]
    PUGH --> CHAMP[1 Champion Concept]
    CHAMP --> PROT[Prototype Development]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain briefly the TELOS framework for idea feasibility in biomedical device design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TELOS is a multi-dimensional feasibility framework with five dimensions:
1. **Technical Feasibility** – assesses whether the enabling science, materials, and TRL are sufficient.
2. **Economic Feasibility** – evaluates development cost, break-even volume, NPV, and pricing.
3. **Legal/Regulatory Feasibility** – examines the device classification, regulatory pathway (510(k), De Novo, PMA), and applicable standards (IEC 60601, ISO 14971).
4. **Operational Feasibility** – checks clinical workflow integration, training, and cybersecurity.
5. **Scheduling Feasibility** – confirms the project can be completed within the competitive regulatory window.

> [!TIP]
> **Allocation:** 1 mark for naming the framework, 1 mark for listing all five dimensions, 1 mark for biomedical-specific justification (regulatory, biocompatibility).

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Proof-of-Principle prototype and a Beta prototype.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Proof-of-Principle (PoP) | Beta Prototype |
|---|---|---|
| **TRL Level** | 3–4 | 7 |
| **Purpose** | Demonstrate that the underlying scientific principle works | Validate the near-final design in real-world conditions |
| **Fidelity** | Low (bench-top) | High (pre-production intent) |
| **Form** | Often virtual, analytical, or breadboard | Production-intent materials and processes |
| **Test Environment** | Laboratory bench | Limited clinical / field study |
| **Regulatory Status** | Pre-design-controls | Design-verified, design-validated |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 1 Internal Choice

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **(a)** *Explain in detail the methodology of idea feasibility analysis for a wearable continuous glucose monitor (CGM). Discuss all five dimensions of TELOS with biomedical-specific criteria. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
Idea feasibility is a structured, multi-dimensional analysis conducted at the **concept phase** of the medical device design lifecycle to determine whether a proposed device is technically, economically, legally, operationally, and schedule-wise viable. It is mandated under **ISO 13485:2016 Section 7.3.2** and **FDA 21 CFR 820.30**.

**2. TELOS Analysis for the CGM (5 marks):**

- **T — Technical Feasibility:** A CGM requires a sub-cutaneous electrochemical sensor (typically glucose oxidase), a low-power BLE transmitter, and a calibration algorithm. The technology exists at TRL 8 (e.g., Dexcom G7, Abbott FreeStyle Libre 3). Biocompatibility per ISO 10993-1 must be demonstrated; the sensor filament must survive 14 days in interstitial fluid. Sterilization via gamma irradiation is established.
- **E — Economic Feasibility:** Development cost $C_d \approx$ ₹25 crore, projected unit price ₹3,500/month, variable cost $C_v \approx$ ₹1,200. Break-even volume $Q^* = C_d / (P - C_v) = 2.5 \times 10^7 / 2300 \approx 10,870$ units. NPV positive at year 4.
- **L — Legal/Regulatory Feasibility:** Class IIb under EU MDR 2017/745, Class II under FDA requiring **510(k)** submission. Applicable standards: **IEC 60601-1, ISO 15197:2013** for accuracy, **ISO 14971** for risk management.
- **O — Operational Feasibility:** Must integrate with **HL7 FHIR**-based EMR. Single-handed sensor application. Cybersecurity per **FDA 2023 guidance** with end-to-end encryption. Training time for patient < 15 minutes.
- **S — Scheduling Feasibility:** Critical path = biocompatibility testing (6 months) + clinical trial (12 months) + 510(k) review (8 months) ≈ 26 months. Competitive pressure from Abbott and Dexcom.

**3. Feasibility Decision (1 mark):**
Based on the analysis, the CGM concept is **feasible** but carries medium risk in the regulatory and economic dimensions. Recommended: proceed to **Phase 2: Concept Generation** with a parallel risk-mitigation plan on the 510(k) pathway.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students who only list the 5 dimensions without **biomedical-specific evidence** (specific standard numbers, TRL values, cost figures) lose **2 of 7 marks**. Always show domain knowledge.

---

#### **(b)** *With a suitable example, describe the process of concept generation for a portable neonatal incubator for rural Indian settings. Apply the SCAMPER technique. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction to SCAMPER (1 mark):**
SCAMPER is a structured ideation tool developed by **Bob Eberle** that prompts the designer to **S**ubstitute, **C**ombine, **A**dapt, **M**odify (including magnify/minify), **P**ut to other uses, **E**liminate, and **R**earrange (or Reverse). It converts an existing product (the conventional hospital incubator) into a stream of alternative concepts.

**2. Application to Portable Neonatal Incubator (5 marks):**

| Letter | Prompt | Concept Generated |
|---|---|---|
| **S** Substitute | Substitute the metal shell with a phase-change-material textile | *Embrace Nest* — a wax-filled sleeping bag incubator (already deployed in Uganda) |
| **C** Combine | Combine the incubator with a solar-powered phototherapy unit | All-in-one warming-and-jaundice-treatment cot |
| **A** Adapt | Adapt the kangaroo-mother-care concept into a heated garment | Wearable warming jacket for the mother that radiates to the baby |
| **M** Modify (Magnify) | Magnify the thermal mass of the baby compartment | Add a paraffin-wax thermal reservoir that maintains $36.5~^\circ\text{C}$ for 6 hours without electricity |
| **M** Modify (Minify) | Minify the size to fit a single neonate on a motorcycle | Bike-attachable transport incubator |
| **P** Put to other uses | Use as a transport warmer for vaccines in rural supply chains | Dual-use logistics device |
| **E** Eliminate | Eliminate the compressor by using a thermoelectric (Peltier) cooler/heater | Solid-state, silent, low-power device |
| **R** Rearrange/Reverse | Reverse the heat flow direction | Re-purpose as a cold-compression device for birth asphyxia |

**3. Selection & Conclusion (1 mark):**
Of the 8 concepts, the *Embrace-style PCM textile* and the *Peltier solid-state* variants are forwarded to the **Pugh Matrix** for weighted scoring against criteria like cost (< ₹5,000), weight (< 2 kg), and reliability. The PCM textile is selected for the alpha prototype.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **(a)** *Discuss the various types of prototypes used in biomedical device design, classifying them by purpose, fidelity, and form. Why is the prototype a "proof of the idea" rather than a finished product? (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition and Purpose (1 mark):**
A prototype is a tangible embodiment of a design concept built to **answer specific design questions** and **reduce technical risk**. It is *not* a final product.

**2. Classification (5 marks):**

**By Purpose (most common in KTU):**
- **Proof-of-Principle (PoP)** – tests if the underlying science works; TRL 3–4. Example: a bench-top optical sensor showing that blood-oxygen saturation can be measured by PPG.
- **Proof-of-Process (PoP-P)** – tests if the manufacturing process (e.g., injection moulding of a polymer housing) yields repeatable results. TRL 5–6.
- **Proof-of-Product (PoP-R)** – tests if the customer accepts the final form, fit, and function. TRL 7.

**By Fidelity:**
- **Low-Fidelity** – cardboard mock-up, foam, 3D-printed rough prototype. Used for ergonomic and form-factor feedback.
- **Mid-Fidelity** – functional 3D-printed unit, partial electronics, works for one or two key features.
- **High-Fidelity** – production-intent materials and processes, full functionality, used for design verification and regulatory submission.

**By Form:**
- **Physical Prototype** – real hardware, requires materials and tooling.
- **Virtual Prototype** – CAD, FEA, CFD, or **Digital Twin** simulation. Reduces cost.
- **Analytical Prototype** – mathematical model implemented in MATLAB/Python.
- **Reconfigurable Prototype** – modular hardware that can be quickly altered.

**3. "Proof of Idea" Justification (1 mark):**
A prototype is intentionally *incomplete, inelegant, and imperfect* because its purpose is to **fail fast and fail cheap** in controlled conditions, rather than to impress users. It generates *evidence* (sensor readings, user feedback, biocompatibility data) that the design hypotheses are valid or invalid. A finished product, by contrast, has *zero tolerance* for ambiguity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Conflating "prototype" with "model" or "pre-production" loses 1 mark. A model is for *visualization*; a prototype is for *validation*; a pre-production unit is for *process qualification*.

---

#### **(b)** *Draft a product specification for a portable ECG monitor suitable for primary health centres in India. Include all 5 specification pillars. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Document Header (0.5 marks):**
Product: **Portable 12-lead ECG Monitor (Model: "KeralaCare-ECG v1")**, Revision A, dated 01-06-2025.

**2. Five Pillars (6.5 marks, 1.3 marks each):**

**Pillar 1 — Marketing Specification:**
- Target user: Primary Health Centre (PHC) nurses and ASHA workers.
- Target market: 25,000 PHCs in rural India; secondary: district hospitals.
- Selling price ceiling: **₹18,000** (inclusive of GST).
- Distribution: Government tender, e-commerce.

**Pillar 2 — Functional Specification:**
- **Intended use:** Resting 12-lead ECG acquisition and arrhythmia screening in adults and pediatrics.
- **Indications:** Suspected myocardial infarction, arrhythmia follow-up.
- **Contraindications:** Not for use during MRI or with electrocautery.
- **Inputs:** 10 electrode cables (AHA color-coded).
- **Outputs:** 12-lead waveform on LCD, PDF report on SD card, HL7 FHIR export via 4G.

**Pillar 3 — Performance Specification:**
- **Accuracy:** Heart rate $\pm 2$ bpm or $\pm 1\%$, whichever is greater.
- **Range:** HR 30–250 bpm; amplitude $\pm 5$ mV.
- **Sampling rate:** $\geq 500$ samples/sec/channel.
- **Common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR):** $\geq 100$ dB.
- **Battery:** 8 hours continuous; charge time $\leq 3$ hours.

**Pillar 4 — Safety & Regulatory Specification:**
- **Electrical safety:** IEC 60601-1 Class IIa, Type CF applied part.
- **Biocompatibility:** Electrode gel per ISO 10993-5 (non-cytotoxic).
- **Risk management:** ISO 14971:2019; RPN $\leq 60$ for all hazards.
- **Usability:** IEC 62366-1; summative usability test with $\geq 15$ lay users.
- **Cybersecurity:** FDA 2023 guidance; AES-256 encryption for cloud upload.

**Pillar 5 — Constraints Specification:**
- **Size:** $\leq 220 \times 140 \times 50$ mm.
- **Weight:** $\leq 1.0$ kg.
- **Operating temperature:** $+10~^\circ\text{C}$ to $+45~^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Storage:** $-20~^\circ\text{C}$ to $+60~^\circ\text{C}$, IPX4 splash-proof.
- **Material:** RoHS-compliant, recyclable ABS housing.
- **Cost (BoM):** $\leq$ ₹7,500.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Missing **measurable units** (cm, kg, °C, mV) is an automatic 1-mark deduction. Specifications must be *testable*, not aspirational.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings / Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Module 1 Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating feasibility with viability** — *Feasibility* asks "can we?"; *Viability* asks "should we?" Vague usage loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Omitting regulatory context** — A feasibility study that does not mention **FDA 21 CFR 820.30** or **ISO 13485** is a 2-mark deduction in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Treating prototype as product** — Always emphasize the *purpose* (proof-of-principle vs. proof-of-process vs. proof-of-product).
> 4. **Specifications without tolerance and units** — "Small size" is worth 0 marks; "$\leq 220 \times 140 \times 50$ mm" is worth full marks.
> 5. **Skipping the design-control context** — Always mention the **Design History File (DHF)**, **Design Input / Output / Review / Verification / Validation / Transfer** chain.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **TELOS Feasibility** = Technical, Economic, Legal/Regulatory, Operational, Scheduling — all 5 mandatory in KTU 14-mark answers.
- [x] **Concept generation** is *divergent*; **concept selection (Pugh)** is *convergent*; both use the Frog-Leap model.
- [x] **SCAMPER** = Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put-to-other-use, Eliminate, Rearrange/Reverse.
- [x] **Prototype ≠ Product**. Prototypes fail on purpose; products cannot.
- [x] **Three prototype purposes** = Proof-of-Principle (PoP), Proof-of-Process (PoP-P), Proof-of-Product (PoP-R).
- [x] **Three prototype forms** = Physical, Virtual (CAD/Digital Twin), Analytical (Mathematical/MATLAB).
- [x] **Three prototype fidelity levels** = Low (mock-up), Medium (functional 3D-print), High (pre-production).
- [x] **Product Specification has 5 Pillars** = Marketing, Functional, Performance, Safety/Regulatory, Constraints.
- [x] **Specifications must be measurable** — every parameter needs a *value*, *unit*, and *tolerance*.
- [x] **Regulatory anchors** = FDA 21 CFR 820.30 (Design Controls), ISO 13485 §7.3, EU MDR 2017/745.
- [x] **Risk management** = ISO 14971:2019; **RPN** = Severity × Occurrence × Detection.
- [x] **Biocompatibility** = ISO 10993 series (especially -1, -5, -18 for extraction).
- [x] **Electrical safety** = IEC 60601-1 (general), IEC 60601-2-XX (particular).
- [x] **Usability** = IEC 62366-1.
- [x] **Cybersecurity** = FDA 2023 guidance + IEC 80001-1.
- [x] **Key formula**: Weighted concept score $S_i = \sum w_j s_{ij}$, with $\sum w_j = 1$.
- [x] **Key formula**: Sample size $n = (Z_{\alpha/2} \sigma / E)^2$ for beta-prototype trials.
- [x] **Key formula**: Break-even volume $Q^* = C_d / (P - C_v)$.
- [x] **Key formula**: Risk Priority Number $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$.
- [x] **Design History File (DHF)** must include the feasibility report, concept selection matrix, prototype test reports, and signed specification.
- [x] **Failure to document feasibility = Form 483 observation** in FDA audits.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specification Review
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Specification Review

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Specification Review** is a structured, systematic, and documented evaluation process within the **Design Control** framework of biomedical device development, wherein the **Design Input Requirements (DIR)** and **Design Output Specifications (DOS)** are critically examined, verified, and validated for completeness, correctness, traceability, regulatory compliance, and feasibility before being released for downstream design and development activities.

In the context of **PEEBT752 – Design of Biomedical Devices (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, Specification Review is positioned as a foundational quality gate inside Module 1, immediately following the conceptualization and user-need elicitation stages. It operates as a **formal checkpoint** in the design history file of any Class I, II, or III medical device, ensuring that every requirement written into a specification is:

- **Necessary** (linked to a documented user need or risk control measure)
- **Verifiable** (testable by inspection, analysis, or measurement)
- **Unambiguous** (single, clear interpretation by all stakeholders)
- **Traceable** (mapped through a Traceability Matrix to user needs, risks, and V&V activities)
- **Realistic** (achievable within technical, regulatory, and economic constraints)

The process is governed globally by **ISO 13485:2016 (Clause 7.3 – Design and Development)**, the **US FDA 21 CFR Part 820.30 (Design Control)**, and the **EU MDR 2017/745 (Annex I, Chapter II)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blueprint Inspection"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy:** Building a House
>
> Imagine you commission an architect to design a house. Before the construction crew pours the foundation, an independent senior engineer conducts a *Specification Review*. She opens the blueprint and asks:
>
> 1. *Does every line on this drawing correspond to something the family actually asked for?* (Traceability to user needs)
> 2. *Are the dimensions testable?* (Verifiability)
> 3. *Could two different contractors read the same number "10" and build different things?* (Unambiguity)
> 4. *Did we forget the staircase while specifying the second floor?* (Completeness)
> 5. *Does the electrical spec meet the local fire code?* (Regulatory compliance)
>
> **Specification Review is exactly this — but for a device that will be implanted in a human heart or used to deliver a drug.** The cost of an ambiguous spec in biomedical engineering is not a leaking roof; it is patient injury, device recall, or regulatory non-conformance.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Definitions

| Term | Standard Reference | Definition (Box Highlight) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Design Input** | FDA 21 CFR 820.30(c) | The physical and performance requirements of a device that are used as a basis for device design. |
| **Design Output** | FDA 21 CFR 820.30(d) | The final design results of each design phase, expressed in terms that allow adequate review and evaluation. |
| **Verification** | ISO 13485:2016, 7.3.8 | Confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence that design output meets design input. |
| **Validation** | ISO 13485:2016, 7.3.9 | Confirmation that the device meets user needs and intended use. |
| **Traceability** | ISO 13485:2016, 7.3.13 | The ability to follow the life cycle of a requirement from user need through V&V. |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Big Four" Standard Bodies cited in KTU exams:** **FDA** (USA), **ISO** (International), **IEC** (Electro-technical), and **MDR** (European Union).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Purpose of Specification Review — Five Pillars

A Specification Review, in the strictest KTU and regulatory sense, is conducted to certify the following five logical properties of a specification set:

1. **Completeness** — Every user need has a corresponding design input. No "orphan" requirements exist.
2. **Correctness** — Each specification accurately reflects the true user need, the clinical workflow, and the regulatory expectation.
3. **Verifiability** — Every requirement is written in a way that a Verification Engineer can later design a test for it. Phrases like *"shall be user-friendly"* are rejected; *"shall weigh less than 2.5 kg"* is accepted.
4. **Unambiguity** — Each requirement has exactly one meaning. Words like *"sufficient"*, *"adequate"*, *"as appropriate"* are flagged.
5. **Consistency** — No two requirements conflict (e.g., *"battery life ≥ 24 h"* vs. *"total weight < 1.5 kg"* with a battery that requires a heavy cell).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A specification that cannot be verified is considered a **defect** in the Design History File (DHF). The FDA cites this as one of the top five observations in 483 warning letters.

## 2.2 Types of Specifications Reviewed

| Specification Type | Example (Defibrillator) | Owner |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Functional Spec** | "Deliver biphasic 200 J shock within 8 s of charge." | Systems Engineering |
| **Performance Spec** | "Heart rate accuracy: $\pm 2$ bpm over 30–250 bpm." | Clinical / Algorithm Team |
| **Interface Spec** | "RS-232 port, 115200 baud, 8-N-1." | Hardware Engineer |
| **Safety Spec** | "Leakage current < 10 µA (CF-type) per IEC 60601-1." | Safety Officer |
| **Usability Spec** | "One-handed operation possible for 95% of users (95th percentile hand size)." | Human Factors |
| **Regulatory Spec** | "RoHS 2 compliant; latex-free per FDA guidance." | Regulatory Affairs |
| **Environmental Spec** | "Operate 10 °C to 40 °C, 10–90% RH non-condensing." | Reliability Engineer |
| **Manufacturing Spec** | "Tolerance on housing: $\pm 0.1$ mm." | Manufacturing |

## 2.3 The Specification Review Workflow

The review follows a **6-stage gate model**, mirroring the **Stage-Gate®** process used in regulated medical device development:

1. **Pre-Review Preparation** — Author circulates draft spec ≥ **5 business days** before the meeting.
2. **Independent Reading** — Reviewers mark comments in tracked-changes; no verbal-only comments accepted.
3. **Review Meeting** — Cross-functional panel: **Regulatory, Quality, Clinical, Marketing, Manufacturing, R&D, Human Factors, Risk Management**.
4. **Action Item Log** — Every comment becomes a discrete Action Item with owner and due date.
5. **Disposition of Comments** — *Accept / Reject / Defer*. Rejections require written justification.
6. **Sign-off & Release** — All required signatures captured in the **DHF** before the spec is "Released for Use."

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **mathematical and logical frameworks** that may be expected in KTU numerical/analytical questions on Specification Review. Note: this topic is **qualitative-heavy**, so numerical questions test traceability indices, risk priority numbers, and verification coverage.

| # | Concept | Equation / Logical Form | Unit / Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Traceability Coverage Index (TCI)** | $TCI = \dfrac{N_{\text{traced requirements}}}{N_{\text{total requirements}}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 2 | **Requirement Density (RD)** | $RD = \dfrac{N_{\text{requirements}}}{N_{\text{functions}}$ | Requirements/Function |
| 3 | **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ where $S$=Severity, $O$=Occurrence, $D$=Detectability | Dimensionless integer |
| 4 | **Verification Coverage (VC)** | $VC = \dfrac{N_{\text{verified requirements}}}{N_{\text{released requirements}}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| 5 | **Review Effectiveness Ratio** | $RER = \dfrac{N_{\text{defects caught at review}}}{N_{\text{defects caught downstream}} + N_{\text{defects caught at review}}$ | Ratio (0–1) |
| 6 | **Cost-of-Defect Multiplier (Boehm curve)** | $C_{\text{field}} \approx 100 \times C_{\text{design}}$ for medical devices | Multiplier |
| 7 | **Stability Index of Spec** | $SI = 1 - \dfrac{N_{\text{changes after release}}}{N_{\text{total requirements}}}$ | Ratio (0–1) |
| 8 | **Ambiguity Score (subjective but documented)** | $A = \dfrac{N_{\text{unambiguous req.}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Ratio (0–1) |
| 9 | **Number of Independent Reviewers (rule of thumb)** | $N_{r} \geq 3$ for Class II, $N_{r} \geq 5$ for Class III | Count |
| 10 | **Acceptable Spec Change Rate (mature device)** | $\Delta R / \Delta t \leq 2\% / \text{month}$ | Percentage/Time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Use `\mid` or `\vert` (e.g., $S \mid O$) and **never** the vertical bar `|` inside markdown tables — the parser will break the column.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Specification Review is the single most cost-effective quality activity in medical device development. Empirical data from the FDA and industry consortia (e.g., **Medical Device Innovation Consortium (MDIC)**) shows:

> *"A defect caught at specification review costs approximately **\$1,000 to fix**, whereas the same defect caught during clinical investigation costs approximately **\$100,000**, and after market release can exceed **\$10 million** in recall, litigation, and CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action) costs."*

This is the engineering-economic *raison d'être* of Module 1 in your syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Traceability Coverage Index (TCI)

> A Class II pulse oximeter project is being developed. The Design Input document contains **84** total requirements. During the Specification Review, the Quality Engineer finds that **9** requirements are not traced back to any user need (orphan requirements), and **4** user needs have no corresponding design input (orphan needs).

**Step 1 — Define traced requirements.**
A requirement is "traced" if it has *both* an upstream link (to a user need) *and* a downstream link (to a V&V activity). Orphan requirements are *untraced*.

$$
N_{\text{traced}} = N_{\text{total}} - N_{\text{orphans (req. side)}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{traced}} = 84 - 9 = 75
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the TCI formula.**

$$
TCI = \frac{N_{\text{traced}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100
$$

$$
TCI = \frac{75}{84} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Final computation.**

$$
\boxed{TCI \approx 89.29 \%}
$$

**Step 4 — KTU-style interpretation.**
A TCI below **95 %** is typically a *review finding* and blocks release. The engineer must either trace the 9 orphan requirements to a documented user need or formally *reject* them with a written justification linked to a risk file entry.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Risk Priority Number Linked to a Specification

> A specification clause for an infusion pump states: *"The device shall detect occlusion within 30 minutes."* The Risk Management File (per **ISO 14971:2019**) assigns:
> - Severity $S = 8$ (severe injury)
> - Occurrence $O = 4$ (occasional)
> - Detectability $D = 3$ (moderate)

**Step 1 — Recall the RPN definition.**

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
RPN = 8 \times 4 \times 3
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
\boxed{RPN = 96}
$$

**Step 4 — KTU Decision Logic.**

| RPN Band | Action |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 – 25 | Accept, log in risk file |
| 26 – 75 | Risk/benefit analysis required |
| 76 – 125 | Risk control mandatory, update spec |
| > 125 | Stop work, redesign |

An RPN of **96** triggers a **mandatory specification change** — typically tightening the *"30 minutes"* to a *verifiable, measurable* clause such as *"detect occlusion in the high-pressure line at $\geq 525$ mmHg within 90 s, alarm per IEC 60601-1-8."*

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Verification Coverage Calculation

> A Class III implantable cardiac defibrillator has **212** design input requirements. Of these, **198** have a corresponding Verification Protocol (VP), **8** have a Validation Protocol (VAL), and **6** are documented as *"design-verified by inspection of supplier documentation."*

**Step 1 — Sum all covered requirements.**

$$
N_{\text{covered}} = N_{VP} + N_{VAL} + N_{\text{insp}} = 198 + 8 + 6
$$

$$
N_{\text{covered}} = 212
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the VC formula.**

$$
VC = \frac{N_{\text{verified}}}{N_{\text{released}}} \times 100
$$

$$
VC = \frac{212}{212} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Result.**

$$
\boxed{VC = 100 \%}
$$

> This is the *ideal state* required by ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.3.8. Anything below 100 % is a **documented gap** requiring a CAPA in the DHF.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Spec Review Disposition

| Disposition | Trigger | Owner of Decision | DHF Artifact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Accept (A)** | Comment is correct, no spec change needed | Author | Sign-off sheet |
| **Accept with Edit (AE)** | Comment valid, text must change | Author | Revised spec, rev. +1 |
| **Reject (R)** | Comment invalid, with technical justification | Reviewer | Rejection memo |
| **Defer (D)** | Needs further analysis (e.g., clinical) | Project Manager | Risk log entry |
| **Withdraw (W)** | Requirement is no longer needed | Product Owner | Change Request |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** A common 14-mark question asks you to *"Prepare a Specification Review Plan and Disposition Matrix for a given device."* Memorize this 5-bucket disposition scheme.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Specification Review Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[User Needs Document] --> A1[Translate to Design Inputs]
    A1 --> A2[Author Drafts Specification v0.1]
    A2 --> A3{Pre-Release Peer Review}
    A3 -- Fail --> A2
    A3 -- Pass --> A4[Issue for Cross-Functional Review]
    A4 --> A5[Quality Reviewer]
    A4 --> A6[Regulatory Reviewer]
    A4 --> A7[Clinical Reviewer]
    A4 --> A8[Risk Management Reviewer]
    A5 --> A9[Consolidate Comments in Action Log]
    A6 --> A9
    A7 --> A9
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10[Disposition Meeting]
    A10 --> A11[Update Spec to v1.0]
    A11 --> A12[Formal Sign-off by All Stakeholders]
    A12 --> A13[Release into Design History File]
    A13 --> A14[Start V and V Activities]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — V-Model of Design Control with Spec Review as Gate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[User Needs] --> R1[Design Input]
    R1 --> R2[Design Output Spec]
    R2 --> R3[Design Verification]
    R3 --> R4[Design Validation]
    R4 --> R5[Product Release]

    R1 -. Verification .-> R3
    R2 -. Verification .-> R3
    R3 -. Validation .-> R4
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The left side of the "V" decomposes requirements. The right side integrates them. The **Specification Review** sits exactly at the **vertex** — the critical hand-off point between *intent* and *implementation*. This is why the KTU syllabus positions it as Module 1.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Traceability Matrix Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UN[User Need UN-01] --> DI[Design Input DI-12]
    DI --> DO[Design Output DO-12]
    DO --> VP[Verification Protocol VP-12]
    DO --> VAL[Validation Activity VAL-12]
    VP --> RV[Result Report RV-12]
    VAL --> RV
    DI --> RISK[Risk Control Measure RC-12]
```

> A single *user need* must be traceable *down* to a *result report* and *across* to a *risk control measure*. If any link is broken, the Specification Review flags it.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Cross-Functional Reviewer Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SpecReviewTeam[Specification Review Panel]
        Q[Quality Assurance]
        R[Regulatory Affairs]
        C[Clinical Specialist]
        M[Manufacturing Engineer]
        H[Human Factors Engineer]
        S[Safety and Risk Officer]
        L[Legal and Patents]
    end
    SpecReviewTeam --> Decision{Release Decision}
    Decision -- Approved --> DHF[Design History File]
    Decision -- Rejected --> Rework[Back to Author for Revision]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Cost of Defect vs. Phase (Boehm-style Curve for Medical Devices)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Concept] --> P2[Specification]
    P2 --> P3[Design]
    P3 --> P4[Verification]
    P4 --> P5[Validation]
    P5 --> P6[Market]
    P1 -.-> C1[Cost: 1x]
    P2 -.-> C2[Cost: 10x]
    P3 -.-> C3[Cost: 100x]
    P4 -.-> C4[Cost: 1,000x]
    P5 -.-> C5[Cost: 10,000x]
    P6 -.-> C6[Cost: 1,000,000x]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The X-axis is project phase; the Y-axis (logical) is the cost to fix a defect.** The Specification Review sits at the **P2** node, where a \$1 fix becomes a \$10 fix — already 10× cheaper than catching it in design, and a million times cheaper than catching it after market release.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define the term "Specification Review" as applied to the design of biomedical devices. List FOUR essential properties a requirement must possess to pass a Specification Review.**

**Model Answer (Approx. 100 words):**

Specification Review is a formal, documented evaluation of a biomedical device's Design Input and Design Output specifications, conducted by a cross-functional panel, to ensure that requirements are complete, correct, unambiguous, verifiable, and traceable to user needs and regulatory expectations.

The four essential properties are:
1. **Verifiability** — must be testable by inspection, analysis, or measurement.
2. **Unambiguity** — must have one, and only one, interpretation.
3. **Traceability** — must be linked to a user need and to a V&V activity.
4. **Necessity** — must serve a documented user need or risk control.

> **KTU Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + four points (0.5 each) = **3 marks**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between Design Verification and Design Validation. Why are both required before a medical device can be released?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Question it answers | *"Did we build the device right?"* | *"Did we build the right device?"* |
| Compares | Design Output $\rightarrow$ Design Input | Device $\rightarrow$ User Need / Intended Use |
| Typical method | Test, inspection, analysis | Clinical/simulated-use evaluation |
| Standards | ISO 13485:2016, 7.3.8 | ISO 13485:2016, 7.3.9 |

Both are required because a device can be **verified** (meets every written spec) but still **fail validation** (does not meet the actual clinical user need). The FDA and ISO 13485 mandate both to protect patient safety.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Module 1 Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
> *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain the **V-Model of Design Control** as applied to a **Class II patient monitor** (e.g., a multi-parameter monitor measuring ECG, SpO₂, NIBP, and temperature). Clearly identify where the **Specification Review** sits in this model and justify its placement.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* Construct a **complete Specification Review Plan** for this patient monitor. Your plan must include: (i) minimum reviewer composition and rationale, (ii) the five-bucket disposition matrix, and (iii) a quantitative **Traceability Coverage Index (TCI)** calculation, given that the Design Input has **120** requirements, of which **8** are orphans and **3** user needs are untraced.

---

#### Model Answer — Part A

**Part (a) — V-Model Explanation**

The **V-Model** decomposes the device into a left-descending arm (user need $\rightarrow$ design input $\rightarrow$ design output $\rightarrow$ implementation) and a right-ascending arm (unit test $\rightarrow$ integration $\rightarrow$ verification $\rightarrow$ validation $\rightarrow$ release). For the patient monitor:

- **Left arm — Decomposition:** Hospital needs (continuous vital signs) $\rightarrow$ System requirements (sample rate, accuracy) $\rightarrow$ Subsystem specs (ECG front-end, SpO₂ probe) $\rightarrow$ PCB layout, firmware, mechanical drawings.
- **Right arm — Integration:** Subsystem test $\rightarrow$ System integration test $\rightarrow$ Design Verification (DVT) $\rightarrow$ Design Validation (DVT in clinical/simulated environment) $\rightarrow$ Regulatory release.
- **Placement of Specification Review:** Exactly at the **vertex of the V**, where the descending requirements meet the ascending V&V activities. This is the *contractual* hand-off — once signed, the spec becomes the legal basis for verification.

**Justification:** Placing it at the vertex ensures that no verification activity is ever executed against an un-reviewed, un-traced requirement, eliminating the dominant source of FDA Form 483 observations.

*(Marks: 2 for V-model diagram explanation, 3 for application to monitor, 2 for placement justification = 7 marks)*

---

**Part (b) — Specification Review Plan**

**(i) Reviewer Composition (Minimum):**

| Role | Rationale |
| :--- | :--- |
| Quality Assurance Lead | Ensures DHF compliance and ISO 13485 alignment. |
| Regulatory Affairs | Verifies FDA MDR EU compliance and labeling needs. |
| Clinical Specialist | Validates clinical accuracy claims and intended use. |
| Hardware Lead | Reviews electrical, EMC, and safety specs (IEC 60601-1). |
| Software Lead | Reviews firmware and software-of-unknown-provenance (SOUP) per IEC 62304. |
| Human Factors | Reviews usability and alarm specs per IEC 60601-1-8. |
| Risk Manager | Confirms risk controls per ISO 14971 are captured. |
| Manufacturing | Confirms tolerances and DFM (Design for Manufacturability). |

*Minimum 5 reviewers for Class II; 7+ recommended.*

**(ii) Five-Bucket Disposition Matrix** — see the analytical matrix in Section 3.4.

**(iii) TCI Calculation:**

**Step 1.** Identify traced requirements.
Orphan requirements (no upstream user need) = 8.
Orphan user needs (no downstream design input) = 3.
Total requirements = 120.

For TCI, the binding limit is the *requirements side*. We need requirements that are traced **upstream** to a user need. If 8 are orphans, the *traced* count is:

$$
N_{\text{traced upstream}} = 120 - 8 = 112
$$

**Step 2.** Apply the TCI formula.

$$
TCI = \frac{112}{120} \times 100
$$

**Step 3.** Evaluate.

$$
\boxed{TCI = 93.33\%}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Below the 95 % KTU benchmark. The Specification Review **cannot** be released; the engineering team must either trace the 8 orphan requirements to a documented user need or formally *withdraw* them with regulatory sign-off.

*(Marks: 2 for plan, 2 for matrix, 3 for TCI arithmetic = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B — Module 1 Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* With a **suitable block diagram**, describe the **Specification Review workflow** for a **wearable continuous glucose monitor (CGM)**. Identify at least **five** distinct roles in the review panel and the contribution of each.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A specification clause for the CGM reads: *"The sensor shall be comfortable for the patient."* Apply the **Specification Review defect-detection framework** to this clause. Identify the defect, classify it (ambiguity, unverifiability, or incompleteness), and rewrite the clause in a KTU-compliant form.

---

#### Model Answer — Part B

**Part (a) — Specification Review Workflow for CGM**

The CGM is a Class II/III device (depending on jurisdiction) with a **needle-mounted enzymatic sensor**, a transmitter, and a smartphone app. The Specification Review workflow follows the 6-gate model from Section 4.1, instantiated for the CGM:

1. **Pre-Release Drafting** — Authored by the Sensor Lead and Firmware Lead.
2. **Independent Pre-Read** — ≥ **7 business days** for cross-functional panel.
3. **Review Panel Meeting** — Chaired by Quality Assurance.
4. **Action Item Log Generation** — Tracked in a DOORS/Polarion/Jama tool.
5. **Disposition** — Accept / Accept-with-Edit / Reject / Defer / Withdraw.
6. **Sign-off & DHF Filing** — Required for each release.

**Five Reviewer Roles:**

| Role | Contribution |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Quality Engineer** | DHF integrity, ISO 13485 compliance, document control. |
| **Regulatory Specialist** | FDA 510(k) or De Novo pathway, EU MDR Class IIb conformity. |
| **Clinical Specialist** | MARD (Mean Absolute Relative Difference) target ≤ 9 %. |
| **Human Factors Engineer** | Patch adhesion, comfort, on-body wear time. |
| **Cybersecurity Lead** | Wireless pairing (BLE), data integrity, FDA cybersecurity guidance. |
| **Biocompatibility Expert** | ISO 10993 evaluation of sensor materials. |
| **Risk Manager** | ISO 14971 file linkage. |

*(Marks: 3 for workflow, 4 for roles and rationale = 7 marks)*

---

**Part (b) — Defect Detection on the CGM Specification Clause**

**Original clause:** *"The sensor shall be comfortable for the patient."*

**Step 1 — Defect identification.** The clause suffers from **two** defects:

- **Ambiguity:** "Comfortable" is subjective; two patients, two interpretations.
- **Unverifiability:** No measurable, testable metric is defined.

**Step 2 — Classification.**

| Defect Type | Present? | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ambiguity | **YES** | "Comfort" is not a defined term. |
| Unverifiability | **YES** | No measurement method stated. |
| Incompleteness | Partial | No tolerance, no patient population. |

**Step 3 — KTU-Compliant Rewrite.**

> *"The on-body CGM sensor shall be worn continuously for **7 days minimum** by **at least 90 % of test subjects** (n = 30, age 18–65, BMI 18–35) without reporting a **dermatological reaction greater than ISO 10993-10 Grade 1 (mild)** and without detachment under normal daily activity per IEC 60601-1-11 (home healthcare environment). The patch adhesive shall achieve an initial peel strength of **0.5 to 2.5 N/cm** per ASTM F2459."*

**Step 4 — Verification linkage.** This rewrite can now be verified by:
- A **biocompatibility test** (ISO 10993-10).
- A **wear-study** (human factors).
- A **peel-strength test** (mechanical).

*(Marks: 2 defect ID, 2 classification, 3 rewrite with verification logic = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Specification Review questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Verification with Validation** — Verification = *Did we build it right?* Validation = *Did we build the right thing?* Mixing these up costs **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the Traceability Matrix** — A Specification Review answer *without* a traceability discussion is incomplete. Always include the TCI calculation.
> 3. **Writing "user-friendly" or "comfortable" as a specification** — KTU examiners *love* to ask you to detect this defect. Memorize the **V-U-N-T** mnemonic: **V**erifiable, **U**nambiguous, **N**ecessary, **T**raceable.
> 4. **Omitting the Standards Reference** — Always cite the standard: *"As per ISO 13485:2016, Clause 7.3.4..."* A bare answer without standards citation loses **1 mark** minimum.
> 5. **Sign-off Authority** — Specification Review sign-off must include Quality, Regulatory, and Clinical at minimum. Skipping these is a recurring error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Specification Review is the formal, documented evaluation of Design Inputs and Design Outputs for completeness, verifiability, unambiguity, traceability, and correctness.
- **Governing Standards:** ISO 13485:2016 (Clause 7.3), FDA 21 CFR 820.30, EU MDR 2017/745 Annex I, IEC 60601-1 (safety), ISO 14971 (risk), IEC 62304 (software), IEC 62366-1 (usability).
- **The V-U-N-T Quality Gate for Requirements:** **V**erifiable, **U**nambiguous, **N**ecessary, **T**raceable.
- **Eight Specification Types:** Functional, Performance, Interface, Safety, Usability, Regulatory, Environmental, Manufacturing.
- **Six-Stage Workflow:** Draft → Pre-Review → Independent Read → Review Meeting → Disposition → Sign-off.
- **Five-Bucket Disposition:** Accept, Accept-with-Edit, Reject, Defer, Withdraw.
- **Key Formula 1:** $TCI = \frac{N_{\text{traced}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ — Target $\geq 95\%$.
- **Key Formula 2:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$ — Trigger redesign if $RPN > 125$.
- **Key Formula 3:** $VC = \frac{N_{\text{verified}}}{N_{\text{released}}} \times 100$ — Target $= 100\%$.
- **Cost Insight:** A defect caught at Specification Review is **$\sim 1000\times$ cheaper** to fix than one caught post-market.
- **Reviewer Minimum:** 3 for Class I, 5 for Class II, 7+ for Class III (always including Quality, Regulatory, Clinical, R&D, Risk).
- **Traceability:** Every user need must link to a design input $\rightarrow$ design output $\rightarrow$ verification protocol $\rightarrow$ validation $\rightarrow$ result report.
- **V-Model Position:** Specification Review sits at the **vertex** of the V — the hand-off from design intent to verification activity.
- **KTU Memory Aid:** *"Specify First, Verify Last; Review at the Vertex; Trace the V."*
- **Forbidden Words in Specs:** *"sufficient," "adequate," "as appropriate," "user-friendly," "comfortable," "robust"* — flag these as defects.
- **Deliverable:** Specification Review must produce a **signed, dated, version-controlled specification** filed in the **Design History File (DHF)** before any V&V activity begins.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Specification, Software/Hardware Requirement Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Specification, Software/Hardware Requirement Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Design Specification (DS)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A **Design Specification (DS)** is a comprehensive, structured engineering document that precisely translates *user needs* and *intended use* into a set of quantified, verifiable, and traceable technical requirements governing the architecture, performance, safety, and regulatory compliance of a biomedical device throughout its design lifecycle.

In the context of biomedical engineering, the design specification serves as the **contractual bridge** between what the clinician/patient *wants* (user needs) and what the engineering team will *build* (design inputs). It is a regulated artifact under **FDA 21 CFR 820.30** (Design Controls) and **ISO 13485:2016** (Medical Devices — Quality Management Systems).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** In KTU board examinations, the term "Design Specification" must NEVER be confused with "User Requirements." User Requirements describe *what* the device must do (in clinical/patient language). Design Specification describes *how well* it must do it (in engineering metrics).

### 1.2 Software Requirement Specification (SRS)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Software Requirement Specification (SRS)** is the formal documentation, governed by **IEEE 830-1998** (and its successor IEEE 29148:2018), describing *all* functional and non-functional requirements, constraints, and acceptance criteria for the embedded/firmware/software subsystem of a biomedical device.

For medical device software, the SRS must additionally comply with **IEC 62304:2006+A1:2015** — the international standard governing medical device software lifecycle processes, classifying software by **Safety Class A, B, or C**.

### 1.3 Hardware Requirement Specification (HRS)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Hardware Requirement Specification (HRS)** is the engineering document that fully defines the physical, electrical, mechanical, biocompatibility, electromagnetic, and environmental characteristics of a biomedical device's hardware platform — including sensors, actuators, PCBs, enclosures, connectors, and power systems.

For active medical electrical equipment, the HRS must align with **IEC 60601-1** (General requirements for basic safety and essential performance).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are commissioning an architect to build a **cardiac catheterization laboratory (cath lab)**:

| Real-World Analogy | Biomedical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The family's wish list: *"We need 4 bedrooms, north-facing, wheelchair accessible"* | **User Needs Document** (clinical voice) |
| The architect's contract: *"Master bedroom: 18 m², door width ≥ 900 mm, ramp gradient ≤ 1:12"* | **Design Specification** (engineering voice) |
| The electrical subcontractor's spec: *"3-phase 32A supply, isolated ground, UPS backup < 10 ms"* | **Hardware Requirement Specification** |
| The home automation programmer's spec: *"Alexa integration via MQTT, fall detection latency ≤ 200 ms"* | **Software Requirement Specification** |

**Plain English Insight:** The *Design Specification* is the **master engineering contract** for the whole device. The *SRS* and *HRS* are the **specialized sub-contracts** for software and hardware teams respectively. Without them, every engineer would be working from a different mental picture — leading to a device that does not meet clinical expectations or fails regulatory audit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Regulatory Traceability Chain (FDA-mandated):**
> User Needs → Design Inputs (Specification) → Design Outputs → Design Verification → Design Validation → Design Transfer
> Every requirement in the DS/SRS/HRS must be **traceable** in *both* directions (forward to verification and backward to user need).

### 1.5 Why These Documents Are Critical in Biomedical Devices

1. **Patient Safety** — A pacemaker whose software specification omits a fault-detection requirement can kill a patient.
2. **Regulatory Submission** — FDA 510(k), CE Marking (MDR 2017/745), and CDSCO approvals *require* a complete DS/SRS/HRS dossier.
3. **Liability Defense** — In product-liability litigation, the specification is the legal benchmark of "due diligence."
4. **Cross-Disciplinary Communication** — Clinicians, mechanical engineers, electronic engineers, software developers, and quality assurance teams all speak through this document.
5. **Lifecycle Management** — The DS anchors design changes through formal change-control procedures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Specifications

The specification tree for a biomedical device flows from broad to specific:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{User Needs} \;&\rightarrow\; \text{Design Specification (DS — top level)} \\
&\rightarrow\; \begin{cases} \text{Hardware Requirement Specification (HRS)} \\ \text{Software Requirement Specification (SRS)} \\ \text{Biomaterial \& Mechanical Specification} \\ \text{Risk Management File (ISO 14971)} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** A common KTU mistake is treating DS, SRS, and HRS as identical documents. The DS is **system-level**; the SRS and HRS are **subsystem-level**. A question on "list the contents of a Design Specification" must include system-level items like *essential performance*, *intended use environment*, and *regulatory classification*, which would NOT appear in an SRS.

### 2.2 Components of a Design Specification (DS)

A KTU-compliant Design Specification for a biomedical device must contain **all** of the following:

- **1. Intended Use & Indications for Use** — patient population, clinical condition, anatomical site, user profile.
- **2. Regulatory Classification** — FDA Class I/II/III; EU MDR Class I/IIa/IIb/III; CDSCO risk classification.
- **3. Essential Performance & Safety Requirements** — derived from IEC 60601-1.
- **4. Functional Requirements** — what the device must do.
- **5. Performance Requirements** — quantified: accuracy, precision, range, resolution, response time, sample rate.
- **6. Environmental Requirements** — operating temperature (**typically 10–40 °C** per IEC 60601-1), humidity, altitude, IP rating (**IPX4 minimum** for fluid protection in clinical settings).
- **7. Ergonomic & Human-Factors Requirements** — usability per IEC 62366-1.
- **8. Biocompatibility Requirements** — ISO 10993 series (cytotoxicity, sensitization, irritation).
- **9. Sterilization Requirements** — EtO, gamma irradiation, autoclave compatibility if applicable.
- **10. Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)** — IEC 60601-1-2.
- **11. Cybersecurity Requirements** — FDA premarket cybersecurity guidance (2023).
- **12. Reliability & Shelf-Life Requirements** — MTBF, service life.
- **13. Cost & Manufacturability Targets** — DFM constraints.
- **14. Standards Conformance List** — applicable IEC/ISO/ASTM standards.
- **15. Verification & Validation Acceptance Criteria.**

### 2.3 Components of an SRS (IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148 Structure)

The SRS must follow this canonical structure for full KTU and industry compliance:

| Section | Content | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Introduction** | Purpose, scope, definitions, references, overview | Low |
| **2. Overall Description** | Product perspective, functions, user characteristics, constraints, assumptions | Medium |
| **3. Specific Requirements** | Functional, performance, interface, design constraints (numbered REQ-F-001, REQ-NF-001, etc.) | **HIGH** |
| **4. Verification** | How each requirement will be verified (test, inspection, analysis, demonstration) | **HIGH** |
| **5. Appendices** | Data dictionaries, traceability matrix, glossary | Medium |

**Smart Requirements Properties (SMART-EEE):**
- **S**pecific
- **M**easurable
- **A**chievable
- **R**elevant
- **T**raceable
- **E**rror-free / Unambiguous
- **E**ssential / Verifiable

### 2.4 Components of an HRS

The HRS for a biomedical device typically contains:

| HRS Element | Typical Quantified Value |
|---|---|
| Supply Voltage | **5 V DC ± 5%** or **12 V DC ± 10%** |
| Current Consumption (active) | e.g., ≤ 250 mA |
| Microcontroller / Processor | e.g., ARM Cortex-M4, ≥ 80 MHz |
| ADC Resolution | **≥ 12-bit** for vital-sign monitoring |
| Sensor Range & Sensitivity | e.g., SpO₂: **70–100%**, accuracy ± 2% |
| Operating Temperature | **+10 °C to +40 °C** |
| Storage Temperature | **−20 °C to +60 °C** |
| Relative Humidity | **15–93% non-condensing** |
| Leakage Current (per IEC 60601-1) | **≤ 100 µA** (Type BF), **≤ 10 µA** (Type CF) |
| Defibrillator-Proof | If applicable (Type CF applied part) |
| Ingress Protection | **IPX4** (splash-proof) minimum |
| Battery Runtime | ≥ 8 hours continuous |
| Connector Types & Pinout | e.g., DB-9, RJ-45, USB-C, IEC 60130-10 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** When asked for "specifications of a biomedical device," students frequently quote only functional specs and omit safety/leakage/IP/biocompatibility specs. The examiner expects ALL classes of requirements.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Rule | Meaning | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $R_{\text{shunt}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{shunt}}}{I_{\max}}$ | Current-sensing shunt resistor | Ω |
| $V_{\text{ADC,FS}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}}$ | ADC quantization step (LSB) | V / LSB |
| $\text{ENOB} = \dfrac{\text{SINAD} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Effective Number of Bits from SINAD | bits |
| $I_{\text{leak,B}} \le 100\ \mu\text{A}$ | Patient leakage current, Type BF | **IEC 60601-1** |
