| $I_{\text{leak,CF}} \le 10\ \mu\text{A}$ | Patient leakage current, Type CF (cardiac) | **IEC 60601-1** |
| $T_{\text{op}} \in [10,\ 40]\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | Clinical operating temperature window | **IEC 60601-1** |
| $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{t_{\text{test}}}{N_{\text{fail}}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| $\text{Resolution \%} = \dfrac{V_{\text{LSB}}}{V_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | Percentage resolution of ADC | % |
| $\text{IP Code}$ | Solid digit + Liquid digit (e.g., **IP54**) | IEC 60529 |
| $\text{SRS Quality} = f(\text{Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Ranked, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable})$ | IEEE 830 quality attributes | — |
| $\text{Software Safety Class (IEC 62304)}$ | **A** = no injury, **B** = non-serious injury, **C** = serious injury or death | Class |
| $\text{Traceability Coverage} = \dfrac{\text{Traced Requirements}}{\text{Total Requirements}} \times 100\%$ | Verification completeness | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The formulas above are evaluated in KTU numerical/descriptive problems. The leakage current values (100 µA and 10 µA) are tested in nearly every board exam for biomedical instrumentation. Memorize them with the patient-protection type (BF = Body Floating; CF = Cardiac Floating).

### 2.6 Software Safety Classification (IEC 62304)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Safety Class A} &= \text{No injury or damage to health is possible} \\
\text{Safety Class B} &= \text{Non-serious injury is possible} \\
\text{Safety Class C} &= \text{Death or serious injury is possible}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Examples in Biomedical Devices:**
- **Class A** — Display-only firmware in a hospital bed.
- **Class B** — Software controlling non-life-critical therapy (TENS unit).
- **Class C** — Embedded software in an implantable pacemaker, insulin pump closed-loop controller, or radiation therapy beam controller.

The **higher the class**, the **stricter** the documentation, verification, and risk-control requirements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — DS vs SRS vs HRS

This comparative matrix is a **favourite KTU exam item** (often asked as a 7-mark comparison question):

| Attribute | Design Specification (DS) | Software Requirement Specification (SRS) | Hardware Requirement Specification (HRS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Whole device (system level) | Software/firmware subsystem only | Hardware/mechanical/electrical subsystem |
| **Audience** | Cross-functional team + regulator | Software engineers, QA, test | Hardware engineers, PCB designers, QA |
| **Standard / Guide** | FDA 21 CFR 820.30, ISO 13485 | IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148, IEC 62304 | IEC 60601-1, IEC 60601-1-2, IPC standards |
| **Abstraction Level** | Mid (system) | High (logical behaviour) | High (physical, electrical) |
| **Granularity** | One requirement per functional block | One requirement per software function | One requirement per hardware block/component |
| **Quantification Focus** | Performance + safety | Functional behaviour + algorithms | Component specs + tolerances |
| **Verification Method** | System-level V&V | Unit test, integration test, code review | Bench test, EMC test, environmental test |
| **Number of Requirements** | Few hundred (system level) | Few hundred to several thousand | Few hundred |
| **Lifecycle Updates** | Change-controlled, infrequent | Agile-friendly, version-controlled | Change-controlled, infrequent |
| **Example Item** | *"Device shall measure SpO₂ within ±2% in motion conditions."* | *"Function spo2_calc() shall compute SpO₂ from red/IR ratio using the look-up table in Appendix A, output updated every 1 s ± 10%."* | *"Red LED: 660 nm ± 5 nm, IR LED: 940 nm ± 5 nm, photodiode sensitivity 0.5 A/W @ 660 nm."* |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Process of Deriving the Design Specification

The process of generating a robust DS for a biomedical device follows a structured methodology:

**Step 1 — Capture User Needs**
Conduct stakeholder interviews with clinicians, biomedical engineers, patients, and regulatory affairs. Document using the format: *"[Actor] needs to [action] so that [outcome]."*

Example: *"The ICU nurse needs to obtain a patient's heart rate within 10 seconds of lead connection so that urgent arrhythmias can be detected."*

**Step 2 — Translate to Design Inputs**
Convert each user need into one or more **design inputs** (measurable technical requirements). Apply the SMART-EEE criteria. Each design input must:
- Have a unique identifier (e.g., **DI-HR-001**).
- Specify the measurand, range, accuracy, units, and test method.

**Step 3 — Risk Analysis (ISO 14971)**
For each design input, perform hazard identification. Assign severity (S), probability (P), and detectability (D). Compute risk priority number:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times P \times D
$$

A high RPN may add **risk-control requirements** to the DS (e.g., dual-fail-safe alarm, redundant sensor).

**Step 4 — Standards Mapping**
Map each design input to applicable clauses of standards:

| Design Input | Standard Reference |
|---|---|
| Leakage current | IEC 60601-1, Clause 8.7 |
| EMC emissions | IEC 60601-1-2 |
| Usability | IEC 62366-1 |
| Software lifecycle | IEC 62304 |
| Biocompatibility | ISO 10993-1 |
| Risk management | ISO 14971:2019 |

**Step 5 — Decompose to Subsystem Specifications**
Each system-level design input is decomposed into one or more HRS or SRS requirements with **forward traceability**.

**Step 6 — Define Verification & Acceptance Criteria**
For each requirement, state:

$$
\text{Acceptance} = \text{Pass} \iff \text{Measured Value} \in [\text{Lower Limit},\ \text{Upper Limit}]
$$

**Step 7 — Traceability Matrix Construction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{User Need (UN-001)} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{Design Input (DI-001)} \\
&\;\rightarrow\; \text{SRS (REQ-S-001)} \cup \text{HRS (REQ-H-001)} \\
&\;\rightarrow\; \text{Verification (VER-001)} \\
&\;\rightarrow\; \text{Validation (VAL-001)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — ADC Specification Derivation

**Problem (KTU-style):** A pulse oximeter requires SpO₂ accuracy of ±2% over a measurement range of 70–100%. The analog front-end produces a voltage between **0 V and 3.3 V**. Calculate the minimum ADC resolution required.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the full-scale range:**

$$
V_{\text{FS}} = V_{\max} - V_{\min} = 3.3\ \text{V} - 0\ \text{V} = 3.3\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the required measurement resolution in volts:**
A ±2% accuracy over the 70–100% SpO₂ range means each least-significant bit must resolve ≤ 1% of the SpO₂ signal (Nyquist-style resolution criterion). For 30% signal span:

$$
V_{\text{LSB}} \le \dfrac{0.01 \times V_{\text{FS}}}{1} = 0.033\ \text{V} = 33\ \text{mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate minimum number of bits:**

$$
n \ge \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{FS}}}{V_{\text{LSB}}}\right)
$$

$$
n \ge \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{3.3}{0.033}\right) = \log_2(100) \approx 6.64
$$

**Step 4 — Round up to the nearest integer (always round UP for safety-critical design):**

$$
n_{\min} = 7\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply engineering safety margin (typically 2×):**

$$
n_{\text{design}} = n_{\min} + 3 = 10\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 6 — Final specification entry (HRS):**
*REQ-H-ADC-001:* "The ADC shall provide a minimum effective resolution of **10 bits** over the input range **0–3.3 V**, with an LSB voltage not exceeding **3.22 mV**."

**Interpretation:** This requirement is now testable: bench-test the ADC with a precision voltage source at 0.033 V increments; the digital output must increment monotonically. Pass criterion: monotonicity + DNL ≤ 1 LSB + INL ≤ 2 LSB.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Patient Leakage Current Specification

**Step 1 — Determine applied-part type:**
An SpO₂ finger probe is a **Type BF** (Body Floating) applied part.

**Step 2 — Apply the standard limit (IEC 60601-1, normal condition):**

$$
I_{\text{leak, BF, NC}} \le 100\ \mu\text{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply single-fault condition:**

$$
I_{\text{leak, BF, SFC}} \le 500\ \mu\text{A}
$$

**Step 4 — HRS entry:**
*REQ-H-LEAK-001:* "Patient leakage current shall not exceed **100 µA** in normal condition and **500 µA** in single-fault condition as measured per IEC 60601-1, Clause 8.7."

**Step 5 — Verification:** Use a measuring device circuit (MDC) per IEC 60601-1, Annex H; record values under normal and fault conditions; both must be within limits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:** The above derivations illustrate how an abstract clinical requirement (e.g., "accurate SpO₂", "safe for patient") becomes a numerically-testable HRS line item. KTU examinations frequently test this translation skill.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 V-Model of Medical Device Specification & Verification

The V-Model is the industry-standard representation of how specifications (left arm) align with verification activities (right arm). This is a high-frequency KTU diagram question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User Needs"] --> B["Design Specification DS"]
    B --> C["System Architecture Spec"]
    C --> D["Hardware Requirement Specification HRS"]
    C --> E["Software Requirement Specification SRS"]
    D --> F["Hardware Design Output"]
    E --> G["Software Design Output"]
    F --> H["Hardware Unit Test"]
    G --> I["Software Unit Test"]
    H --> J["Integration Test"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["System Verification"]
    K --> L["Design Validation Clinical"]
    L --> M["Design Transfer Production"]

    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style E fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style F fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style G fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style H fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style I fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style J fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style K fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style L fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style M fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left arm** (A → B → C → D/E → F/G) represents progressive *decomposition* of specifications from high-level user needs down to detailed hardware/software outputs.
- The **right arm** (H/I → J → K → L → M) represents progressive *integration and validation* from unit-level testing up to clinical validation and production transfer.
- Each left-side specification has a corresponding verification activity on the right arm at the same height — this is the essence of **bidirectional traceability**.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Specification Document Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U["User Needs UN"] --> R["Regulatory Classification"]
    U --> S["Safety Requirements"]
    U --> P["Performance Requirements"]
    U --> E["Environmental Requirements"]
    U --> X["Ergonomic and Human Factors"]
    R --> DS["Design Specification DS Master"]
    S --> DS
    P --> DS
    E --> DS
    X --> DS
    DS --> HRS["Hardware Req Spec HRS"]
    DS --> SRS["Software Req Spec SRS"]
    DS --> BMS["Biomaterial Spec BMS"]
    DS --> RMS["Risk Management File RMF"]
    HRS --> HV["Hardware Verification"]
    SRS --> SV["Software Verification"]
    BMS --> BV["Biocompatibility Test"]
    RMS --> RV["Risk Control Verification"]
    HV --> VV["Design Validation"]
    SV --> VV
    BV --> VV
    RV --> VV
    VV --> TF["Design Transfer to Production"]

    style U fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style DS fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style HRS fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style SRS fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style BMS fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style RMS fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style VV fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style TF fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Requirement Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Draft
    Draft --> Under_Review : Submitted for review
    Under_Review --> Approved : All reviewers sign off
    Under_Review --> Rejected : Discrepancy found
    Rejected --> Draft : Rework
    Approved --> Implemented : Allocated to design output
    Implemented --> Verified : Verification test passed
    Verified --> Validated : Clinical validation passed
    Validated --> Released : Design transfer complete
    Verified --> ChangeControl : Change request raised
    ChangeControl --> Draft : New revision initiated
    Released --> ChangeControl : Post-market change
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Every requirement in the DS/SRS/HRS travels through these states. The *Change Control* branch reflects the regulated requirement that no requirement may be modified without a formal change-control board approval (per ISO 13485 Clause 4.1.4).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`**

**Differentiate between User Needs and Design Specification for a biomedical device. Why is the translation from user need to design specification considered the most critical step in medical device design?**

**Model Answer:**

| User Need (Clinical Voice) | Design Specification (Engineering Voice) |
|---|---|
| *"The clinician needs an early warning of ventricular fibrillation within 10 seconds."* | *"REQ-DS-ECG-007: The ECG monitor shall detect ventricular fibrillation (VF) and trigger an alarm within 10 ± 1 s of onset, with a sensitivity ≥ 90% and specificity ≥ 95% on the MIT-BIH arrhythmia database."* |

**Why critical:**
1. **Patient safety** — Ambiguity at this stage propagates downstream as a safety hazard.
2. **Regulatory audit** — FDA / Notified Body auditors begin their review at this transition.
3. **Verifiability** — A user need cannot be directly tested; a design specification can.
4. **Traceability anchor** — All later SRS/HRS lines must trace back to a design specification item.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**List any six characteristics of a well-written Software Requirement Specification (SRS) as per IEEE 830 standard.**

**Model Answer (any six):**

1. **Correct** — Each requirement accurately reflects the user need it traces to.
2. **Unambiguous** — Only one possible interpretation (no use of words like "approximately," "etc.").
3. **Complete** — All functional and non-functional requirements, including responses to invalid inputs, are defined.
4. **Consistent** — No requirement conflicts with another.
5. **Ranked for importance/stability** — Critical, essential, conditional requirements clearly marked.
6. **Verifiable** — A finite, cost-effective test exists for each requirement.
7. **Modifiable** — Redundant and contradictory requirements eliminated; cross-references used.
8. **Traceable** — Forward trace to design and tests; backward trace to user need and DS.

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE — Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the structure and components of a **Design Specification (DS)** for a biomedical device. Apply your answer to the design of a **portable pulse oximeter** and provide at least **eight sample design input specifications** with measurable acceptance criteria.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **IEEE 830 structure** of a Software Requirement Specification. With a suitable example from an SpO₂ firmware, demonstrate how a functional requirement and a non-functional requirement are written, and how each is verified.

#### Model Solution (a)

**Structure of a Design Specification:**

1. **Document Identification** — Doc ID, revision, date, authors, approvers.
2. **Intended Use & Indications** — "For spot-check monitoring of arterial oxygen saturation (%SpO₂) and pulse rate in adult patients in clinical and home settings."
3. **Regulatory Classification** — FDA Class II (510(k) pathway), EU MDR Class IIa.
4. **Essential Performance** — SpO₂ accuracy ±2% over 70–100%, pulse rate ±3 bpm.
5. **Functional Requirements** — see sample below.
6. **Performance Requirements** — sample below.
7. **Safety Requirements** — Defibrillator-proof applied part, patient leakage ≤ 100 µA.
8. **Environmental Requirements** — Operating 10–40 °C, IPX4 splash protection.
9. **EMC Requirements** — Per IEC 60601-1-2.
10. **Biocompatibility** — Per ISO 10993-1 (cuff, finger probe).
11. **Usability** — Per IEC 62366-1.
12. **Cybersecurity** — Per FDA 2023 premarket guidance.
13. **Standards List** — IEC 60601-1, ISO 13485, ISO 14971, IEC 62304, IEC 62366-1.
14. **Verification & Validation Plan** — methods, pass/fail criteria.

**Eight Sample Design Inputs for a Portable Pulse Oximeter:**

| ID | Requirement | Acceptance Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| DI-PO-001 | SpO₂ measurement range | **70–100%** |
| DI-PO-002 | SpO₂ accuracy (no motion) | **±2% (rms)** vs. arterial blood gas |
| DI-PO-003 | Pulse rate range | **30–240 bpm** |
| DI-PO-004 | Pulse rate accuracy | **±3 bpm** or ±2%, whichever is greater |
| DI-PO-005 | Battery runtime | **≥ 8 hours** continuous operation |
| DI-PO-006 | Patient leakage current (Type BF) | **≤ 100 µA (NC)**, **≤ 500 µA (SFC)** |
| DI-PO-007 | Operating temperature | **+10 °C to +40 °C** |
| DI-PO-008 | Display update interval | **1 s ± 10%** |

Each requirement is **traceable** to a user need and has a defined **verification method** (bench test, comparison with reference device, environmental chamber test, etc.).

#### Model Solution (b)

**IEEE 830 SRS Structure:**

1. **Section 1 — Introduction**
   - 1.1 Purpose
   - 1.2 Scope
   - 1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
   - 1.4 References
   - 1.5 Overview

2. **Section 2 — Overall Description**
   - 2.1 Product Perspective
   - 2.2 Product Functions
   - 2.3 User Characteristics
   - 2.4 Constraints
   - 2.5 Assumptions and Dependencies

3. **Section 3 — Specific Requirements**
   - 3.1 Functional Requirements
   - 3.2 Performance Requirements
   - 3.3 External Interface Requirements (user, hardware, software, communication)
   - 3.4 Design Constraints
   - 3.5 Software System Attributes (reliability, security, maintainability)
   - 3.6 Logical Database Requirements
   - 3.7 Design Constraints

4. **Section 4 — Verification**
   - For each requirement: method (T = Test, I = Inspection, A = Analysis, D = Demonstration) and pass criterion.

5. **Appendices** — Data dictionary, traceability matrix, glossary.

**Functional Requirement (Sample for SpO₂ firmware):**

> **REQ-S-F-001 (Functional):**
> *"The SpO₂ calculation function shall compute the arterial oxygen saturation from the ratio of red (660 nm) to infrared (940 nm) photoplethysmogram amplitudes using the calibrated lookup table provided in Appendix A. The function shall output a value in the range 70–100% with a resolution of 1%, refreshed every 1 s ± 10%."*
>
> **Verification:** Unit test using a synthetic PPG waveform dataset; verify output range, resolution, and refresh interval. Pass criterion: output ∈ [70, 100] for all valid inputs; refresh period measured with oscilloscope trace between 900 ms and 1100 ms.

**Non-Functional Requirement (Sample):**

> **REQ-S-NF-002 (Performance / Reliability):**
> *"The software shall maintain safe operation in the presence of single-bit memory errors by employing CRC-16 integrity checks on all calibration parameters loaded from non-volatile memory at power-on. Any detected integrity failure shall force the system into a safe state (display 'ERROR' and disable SpO₂ readout) within 200 ms."*
>
> **Verification:** Fault-injection test using a modified firmware build that injects single-bit errors in NV memory; observe safe-state transition time using a logic analyzer. Pass criterion: safe-state entered in ≤ 200 ms in all 100 trial runs.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Hardware Requirement Specification (HRS)** for a **digital ECG monitor (3-lead)**. Provide at least **eight HRS entries** covering electrical, mechanical, EMC, and safety aspects.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **IEC 62304 software safety classification**. With suitable biomedical examples, classify each into Safety Class A, B, or C, justifying the classification. Describe two additional documentation deliverables required for a Class C medical device software project beyond those of a Class A project.

#### Model Solution (a)

**HRS for a 3-Lead Digital ECG Monitor (eight entries):**

| HRS ID | Category | Requirement | Acceptance |
|---|---|---|---|
| REQ-H-ECG-001 | Electrical | Input voltage: **12 V DC ± 10%** from medically-approved AC adapter | Bench test with variable DC source |
| REQ-H-ECG-002 | Signal | Sampling rate: **≥ 500 samples/sec/channel** | Counter measurement at ADC output |
| REQ-H-ECG-003 | Signal | ADC resolution: **≥ 24 bits** (effective ≥ 20 bits) | ENOB test per IEEE 1057 |
| REQ-H-ECG-004 | Safety | Patient leakage current (Type CF, cardiac application) **≤ 10 µA (NC)** | IEC 60601-1 Annex H test |
| REQ-H-ECG-005 | Mechanical | Enclosure material: **ABS+PC, flammability UL94 V-0** | Material certification |
| REQ-H-ECG-006 | Environmental | Operating temperature **+10 °C to +40 °C**, RH **15–93% non-condensing** | Environmental chamber test |
| REQ-H-ECG-007 | EMC | Conducted emissions per **IEC 60601-1-2, Class B** | EMC chamber test |
| REQ-H-ECG-008 | Defib-proof | Applied part shall withstand **5 kV defibrillator discharge** with 5 s recovery | Defibrillator discharge test per IEC 60601-1 |
| REQ-H-ECG-009 | Connector | 3-lead patient cable, **AHA color-coded**, snap or banana plug | Visual + continuity test |
| REQ-H-ECG-010 | Display | **≥ 7-inch TFT**, **800×480 min**, viewable at **3 m** in 300 lux | Photometric + readability test |

#### Model Solution (b)

**IEC 62304 Software Safety Classification:**

| Class | Definition | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Software cannot contribute to a hazardous situation resulting in injury or damage to health | Display software on a hospital bed controller; firmware of an electronic thermometer |
| **Class B** | Software can contribute to a hazardous situation resulting in non-serious injury | Software controlling a TENS (transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulator) unit; infusion pump rate limiter for non-critical drugs |
| **Class C** | Software can contribute to a hazardous situation resulting in **death or serious injury** | Embedded software in implantable pacemaker, ventricular assist device (VAD) controller, closed-loop insulin pump, radiation therapy beam control, anaesthetic vaporizer control |

**Justification logic:** Severity of harm drives the class. If failure of the software → death (e.g., pacemaker stops pacing during bradycardia), it is automatically **Class C**. If failure → non-serious injury (e.g., TENS delivers wrong dose causing mild burn), it is **Class B**. If failure → no injury (e.g., wrong display of room temperature on a bed), it is **Class A**.

**Additional Documentation Required for Class C vs Class A (any two):**

1. **Detailed Software Architecture Documentation** — IEC 62304 Clause 5.3 mandates a detailed architectural design for Class C, including segregation of software items, interfaces, and identification of risk-control measures.
2. **Software Risk Management File** — A documented hazard analysis linking each hazardous situation to specific risk-control measures implemented in software, traceable to specific code modules (Clause 7).
3. **Detailed Software Unit Verification Plan & Report** — Unit-level testing per Clause 5.5 with explicit acceptance criteria for every software unit.
4. **Software Configuration Management Plan** — With baseline identification, change-control history, and reproducible build evidence.
5. **Software Problem-Resolution Process Records** — Documented bug-tracking, root-cause analysis, and verification of fixes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing requirements with subjective adjectives (*"user-friendly," "fast," "easy to use"*). Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** because these are unverifiable. Always replace with quantified metrics: *"≤ 3 clicks to start a measurement," "response time ≤ 1 s," "training time ≤ 30 min for a nurse with no prior experience."*
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **leakage current limits**. Type BF = **100 µA (NC)**; Type CF = **10 µA (NC)**. Mixing these up costs the full safety mark.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Writing the **HRS as a component datasheet** rather than a *requirement*. The HRS must say *"the device shall have..."* not *"the device uses a TI ADS1298."* Components are design outputs; the requirement is the function they fulfil.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting **bidirectional traceability**. A requirement without a user need above it OR a verification below it is incomplete and will lose marks.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Omitting **IEC 62304 software safety class** justification when describing SRS for any active therapeutic or monitoring device — this is a direct 7-mark question almost every KTU cohort.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Design Specification (DS)** is a **system-level** document; **SRS** and **HRS** are **subsystem-level** documents.
- ✅ The DS bridges **User Needs** (clinical language) and **Design Outputs** (engineering language).
- ✅ SRS must follow **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148** structure; HRS must align with **IEC 60601-1**.
- ✅ Every requirement must be **SMART-EEE** (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Traceable, Error-free, Essential).
- ✅ **Bidirectional traceability** is mandatory — every requirement must link to both an upstream user need and a downstream verification.
- ✅ **Patient leakage current limits**: Type BF = **100 µA (NC) / 500 µA (SFC)**; Type CF = **10 µA (NC) / 50 µA (SFC)**.
- ✅ **Operating temperature for clinical devices**: **+10 °C to +40 °C** (IEC 60601-1).
- ✅ **IPX4** minimum splash protection for clinical-use handheld/portable devices.
- ✅ **IEC 62304 Software Safety Classes**: A (no injury), B (non-serious injury), C (death or serious injury).
- ✅ **Risk Priority Number**: RPN = S × P × D — used to derive risk-control requirements in the DS.
- ✅ **ADC resolution design**: always round UP bit count and add **engineering safety margin (≥ 2 bits)**.
- ✅ Regulatory anchors to remember: **FDA 21 CFR 820.30**, **ISO 13485:2016**, **ISO 14971:2019**, **IEC 60601-1**, **IEC 62304**, **IEC 62366-1**, **IEC 60601-1-2**, **ISO 10993-1**, **IEEE 830 / IEEE 29148**.
- ✅ KTU examiner expects: **quantified** specs, **standard references**, **traceability**, **risk classification**, and **verification method** for every requirement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software/Hardware Design Description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software/Hardware Design Description

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Design Description?

In the regulated world of biomedical device engineering, a **Design Description** is the formal, controlled document (or set of documents) that describes *how* a medical device will satisfy its **Design Inputs** (the "what" — the user needs, clinical requirements, and engineering specifications). It is the bridge that converts abstract requirements into a concrete engineering blueprint.

According to **FDA 21 CFR 820.30(g)**, each manufacturer shall establish and maintain procedures to ensure that design plans are reviewed and that the design results meet the design requirements. The Design Description is the primary artefact that proves this translation has happened.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition:** A *Software Design Description (SDD)* and a *Hardware Design Description (HDD)* together form the **Design Output** documentation package that translates *Design Inputs* into an implementable, testable, and verifiable medical device architecture. These are mandatory documents under **FDA 21 CFR 820.30 (Design Controls)** and harmonized with **IEC 62304 (Software Lifecycle)** and **IEC 60601-1 (Medical Electrical Equipment)**.

## 1.2 Software Design Description (SDD) — Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are the chief architect designing a hospital's intensive care unit (ICU). The hospital administration gives you a list of requirements:
- Must handle **30 critical patients**
- Each bed must be visible from a **central nursing station**
- Emergency oxygen supply must reach every bed within **3 seconds**
- Power backup must sustain operations for **8 hours**

You would never just start laying bricks. Instead, you would produce a detailed **architectural plan** showing rooms, corridors, gas lines, electrical wiring, and control systems. The **SDD is exactly that architectural plan — but for software inside a medical device.**

### Formal Definition of SDD

The **Software Design Description (SDD)** is a document specified under **IEC 62304, Clause 5.4**, that describes the **software architecture** (major software items and their interfaces), **detailed design** (subordinate software items), and **unit-level implementation** (the actual algorithmic/structural detail of each software unit). It is the complete roadmap a programmer needs to write the code, and a tester needs to verify the code, without ambiguity.

## 1.3 Hardware Design Description (HDD) — Intuitive Analogy

Continuing the ICU analogy, the HDD is the **detailed engineering drawing set** for the ICU: the structural steel drawings, the electrical single-line diagrams, the plumbing isometrics, and the medical gas pipeline schematics. It tells the electricians exactly which gauge wire to run, where the circuit breakers go, and how the UPS backup is wired.

### Formal Definition of HDD

The **Hardware Design Description (HDD)** is a structured collection of documents that describe the **physical and electrical realization** of a medical device. It includes **block diagrams**, **schematic diagrams**, **Bill of Materials (BOM)**, **PCB layout files**, **mechanical drawings**, and **interface specifications**. For medical electrical equipment, compliance with **IEC 60601-1** and, where applicable, **IEC 60601-1-2 (EMC)** must be demonstrable from this documentation.

## 1.4 The Two Documents Working Together

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Inputs → Design Description (SDD + HDD) → Design Verification → Design Transfer → Production**
>
> The SDD governs the **logical/algorithmic** behavior of the device. The HDD governs the **physical/electrical** behavior. For a modern embedded medical device (e.g., a defibrillator, infusion pump, or patient monitor), the SDD and HDD are deeply intertwined — software runs *on* hardware, so any architectural decision in one ripples into the other.

| Aspect | SDD (Software) | HDD (Hardware) |
|---|---|---|
| Governing Standard | **IEC 62304** | **IEC 60601-1** series |
| Primary Content | Architecture, modules, data flow, algorithms | Schematics, BOM, PCB layout, mechanical |
| Audience | Embedded firmware engineers, V&V team | Electrical engineers, manufacturing |
| Tooling | UML, SysML, Simulink, flowcharts | Altium, OrCAD, AutoCAD |
| Risk Standard | **ISO 14971** (links via software safety class) | **ISO 14971** (links via hazard analysis) |

The **standard software safety classification** under IEC 62304 is:
- **Class A**: No injury or damage to health is possible
- **Class B**: Non-serious injury is possible
- **Class C**: Death or serious injury is possible

The **standard basic safety / essential performance** under IEC 60601-1 applies to all hardware regardless of classification.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of the Design Description

Both SDD and HDD follow a **top-down hierarchical decomposition** pattern that mirrors the **V-Model** of medical device development:

### Software Hierarchy (IEC 62304)
1. **Software System Architecture** — High-level partitioning into Software Items (e.g., *Acquisition*, *Signal Processing*, *Display*, *Communication*, *Safety Layer*).
2. **Software Detailed Design** — Each Software Item is decomposed into Software Units (sub-modules with defined interfaces).
3. **Software Unit Implementation** — Source code level (typically described using pseudocode or detailed algorithms in the SDD).

### Hardware Hierarchy
1. **System Block Diagram** — Major functional blocks (e.g., *Analog Front End*, *ADC*, *Microcontroller*, *Power Supply*, *Isolation Barrier*).
2. **Sub-System Schematics** — Detailed schematics for each block.
3. **Component-Level Design** — Resistor values, capacitor selections, IC part numbers, derating analysis.
4. **PCB Layout & Mechanical** — Physical realization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hierarchical?** A flat design description becomes unmaintainable beyond ~50 pages. Hierarchy enables **modular verification**, **change control**, and **traceability** — each block can be traced back to specific design inputs (requirements) and forward to verification tests.

## 2.2 Required Content Elements per IEC 62304 §5.4 (SDD)

According to **IEC 62304:2006+AMD1:2015, Clause 5.4**, the manufacturer must include, as appropriate to the software safety class:

1. **Purpose and scope of the software** — what the software does and what it does *not* do.
2. **Software items and their interfaces** — major components and how data flows between them.
3. **Description of each software item** — internal structure, algorithms, and behaviour.
4. **Detailed design** — sufficient detail for a competent developer to implement without further design input.
5. **Software requirements traceability** — explicit mapping from each software requirement to the design elements that satisfy it.
6. **Risk control measures implemented in software** — for Class B and Class C, the design must show how identified hazards are mitigated.
7. **Software system testing details** — test cases, expected results, and coverage at the integration level.

## 2.3 Required Content Elements per IEC 60601-1 (HDD)

While IEC 60601-1 itself does not specify a document named "HDD", the **General Requirements for Basic Safety and Essential Performance** demand that the hardware design description include:

1. **Block diagram** with all electrical connections.
2. **Schematic diagrams** for each sub-assembly.
3. **Component specifications and BOM** — including component ratings, derating factors.
4. **Insulation diagram** (for patient protection — MOPP, MOOP classification).
5. **EMC compliance documentation** — per IEC 60601-1-2.
6. **Mechanical drawings** showing enclosure, creepage, and clearance distances.
7. **Risk control measures implemented in hardware** — protective earth, isolation barriers, fuses, etc.

## 2.4 Traceability Matrix — The Heart of Design Control

A **Traceability Matrix** is a tabular document that links:
- **Design Inputs** (User Needs → Design Requirements)
- **Design Outputs** (SDD/HDD elements)
- **Verification Tests** (Test Cases)
- **Validation** (User Needs closure)

A simple form:

$$
\text{Traceability Cell}_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if Requirement } R_i \text{ is addressed by Design Element } D_j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

In practice, the matrix is bidirectional:
- **Forward traceability**: Does every requirement have a design element? (Completeness check)
- **Backward traceability**: Is every design element justified by a requirement? (Scope creep check)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield cheat sheet** for the Software/Hardware Design Description topic. Memorize these standard references.

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Standard / Source |
|---|---|---|
| **SDD** | Software Design Description | **IEC 62304, Clause 5.4** |
| **HDD** | Hardware Design Description | **IEC 60601-1**, 21 CFR 820.30 |
| **Class A** | No injury possible | IEC 62304 §4.3 |
| **Class B** | Non-serious injury possible | IEC 62304 §4.3 |
| **Class C** | Death / serious injury possible | IEC 62304 §4.3 |
| **MOPP** | Means of Patient Protection | IEC 60601-1 §8 |
| **MOOP** | Means of Operator Protection | IEC 60601-1 §8 |
| **SOUP** | Software Of Unknown Provenance | IEC 62304 §8 |
| **DFMEA** | Design Failure Mode and Effects Analysis | **IEC 60812**, AIAG-VDA |
| **PFMEA** | Process Failure Mode and Effects Analysis | AIAG-VDA |
| **Hazard** | Potential source of harm | **ISO 14971 §3.4** |
| **Risk** | Combination of probability of harm and severity | ISO 14971 §3.18 |
| **RPN** | Risk Priority Number $= S \times O \times D$ | IEC 60812 |
| **CRA** | Cybersecurity Risk Assessment | **IEC 81001-5-1** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This documentation framework is not academic bureaucracy — it is the **legal proof of due diligence**. When a device is recalled or faces litigation, the SDD/HDD and the associated traceability matrix are the first documents reviewed by the FDA Notified Body, and by plaintiff's attorneys.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice:
- A **pacemaker** SDD must show how the firmware detects ventricular fibrillation (VF) and delivers a defibrillation pulse — with timing tolerances proven by formal methods.
- An **infusion pump** HDD must show the dual-CPU architecture (one as a watchdog over the other), mechanical anti-free-flow mechanisms, and pressure sensor redundancy.
- A **wearable ECG monitor** SDD must describe how the Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) stack handles dropped packets, encryption key rotation, and firmware over-the-air (FOTA) updates — with risk controls per **IEC 81001-5-1**.

Without rigorous design descriptions, the device is non-compliant, non-marketable, and legally indefensible.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure for Constructing an SDD and HDD

> [!NOTE]
> Biomedical device design descriptions are not abstract academic exercises — they are **procedurally constructed** documents. Below is the canonical step-by-step procedure, with full reasoning at each step.

## 3.1 Procedure: Building the Software Design Description (SDD)

**Step 1 — Define the Software Scope and Safety Class.**

Begin by stating the **software safety classification** per IEC 62304 §4.3. This determines the rigor of every subsequent activity. Justification must be risk-based.

$$
\text{Class}_{\text{software}} = f(\text{Hazard Severity}, \text{Probability of Harm})
$$

For an **insulin pump**: incorrect dosing can kill → **Class C**.
For an **electronic thermometer**: incorrect reading is inconvenient → typically **Class A** or **Class B**.

**Step 2 — Develop the Software Architecture.**

Decompose the software into a manageable number of *Software Items*. Each item should have a **single, well-defined responsibility**. Use the **Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)**.

For example, an **ECG Monitor** may be decomposed into:

1. *Acquisition Module* — Driver for the ADC, oversampling, filtering.
2. *QRS Detection Module* — Pan-Tompkins algorithm.
3. *Heart Rate Calculation Module* — R-R interval averaging.
4. *Display Module* — UI rendering on the LCD.
5. *Alarm Module* — Audible/visual alerts when heart rate exceeds thresholds.
6. *Communication Module* — USB / Wi-Fi data upload.
7. *Safety Module* — Watchdog timer, self-test routines.

**Step 3 — Specify Interfaces Between Software Items.**

For each pair of communicating items, document:
- **Data type** and units (e.g., `int16_t` samples at 500 Hz).
- **Communication mechanism** (shared memory, message queue, function call).
- **Direction** (producer/consumer).
- **Error handling** (timeout, retry, fallback).

**Step 4 — Describe Each Software Item in Detail.**

For each Software Item, document:
- **Inputs** and **Outputs** (with units and ranges).
- **Algorithm** (pseudocode, state machine, or control flow).
- **State transitions** (if stateful).
- **Error handling**.
- **Resource usage** (CPU%, RAM, stack depth).

**Step 5 — Map Software Requirements to Design Elements.**

Construct a traceability matrix linking each **software requirement** (typically tagged `SWR-001`, `SWR-002`, …) to the specific **Software Items and Units** that implement it.

$$
\text{Coverage}_{\text{requirement}} = \frac{\vert \text{Requirements with at least one design element} \vert}{\vert \text{Total Requirements} \vert} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 6 — Document Risk Control Measures in Software.**

For each hazard identified in the **Risk Management File (RMF)** that is mitigated in software, document:
- The **risk control measure** (e.g., "watchdog timer resets the processor within 1 second if the main loop hangs").
- The **verification method** (e.g., "induce a deadlock in test build, observe watchdog reset").
- The **traceability link** to the hazard ID (e.g., `HAZ-007`).

**Step 7 — Document SOUP (Software of Unknown Provenance).**

Any third-party software (RTOS, libraries, open-source components) must be listed, including version, license, known anomalies, and the **risk assessment for its use**.

**Step 8 — Define Integration Test Strategy.**

Specify how the Software Items will be integrated and tested at each level (unit, integration, system). Include test case identifiers, expected results, and pass/fail criteria.

## 3.2 Procedure: Building the Hardware Design Description (HDD)

**Step 1 — Generate the System Block Diagram.**

Show all major functional blocks as labeled rectangles, with signal flow indicated by arrows. Include:
- **Analog Front End (AFE)**
- **ADC** with sampling rate specified
- **Microcontroller / Processor** with model number
- **Memory** (Flash, RAM)
- **Power Supply** (AC-DC converter, battery, isolation)
- **Isolation Barrier** (for patient-connected parts)
- **User Interface** (display, buttons, alarms)
- **Communication Interfaces** (USB, Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth)

**Step 2 — Produce Detailed Schematic Diagrams.**

For each functional block, produce a schematic showing:
- All components with reference designators (R1, C5, U3, etc.).
- Component values and tolerances.
- Power and ground nets.
- Signal flow.

**Step 3 — Compile the Bill of Materials (BOM).**

Include:
- Reference designator, value, package, manufacturer part number.
- Component rating (voltage, current, power, temperature).
- **Derating factor** applied (typically $50\%$ of rated value for critical components).

$$
\text{Derating Factor} = \frac{\text{Applied Stress}}{\text{Rated Stress}} \leq 0.5 \quad \text{(for safety-critical components)}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the Insulation and Protection Scheme.**

Apply the **MOPP** (Means of Patient Protection) framework:

$$
\text{Required Creepage}_{\text{MOPP}} = k \times \text{Basic Insulation} \times f(\text{Working Voltage}, \text{Insulation Material}, \text{CTI})
$$

Where the multiplier $k$ depends on the number of MOPPs:
- **1 × MOPP** = basic insulation only.
- **2 × MOPP** = reinforced insulation (typically doubled creepage/clearance).

**Step 5 — Specify the EMC Strategy.**

Reference **IEC 60601-1-2** and document:
- Shielding strategy (enclosure material, gasket design).
- Filtering on power lines and signal lines.
- PCB layout practices (ground planes, trace routing for high-frequency signals).
- Susceptibility and emission test plan.

**Step 6 — Generate Mechanical Drawings.**

Include enclosure drawings, mounting points, cooling provisions (heat sinks, ventilation), and any patient-contact surfaces with their biocompatibility classification (per **ISO 10993**).

**Step 7 — Risk Control Documentation.**

For each hazard mitigated by hardware (e.g., electrical shock, mechanical pinch, fire), document the control measure and its verification (e.g., dielectric withstand test, drop test, thermal test).

**Step 8 — Traceability Matrix.**

Link each **hardware requirement** (e.g., `HWR-012: System shall operate from 100-240 VAC, 50/60 Hz`) to the specific component(s), schematic section(s), and verification test(s) that satisfy it.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: ECG Patient Monitor

**Scenario:** Design a 3-lead ECG monitor with an alarm when heart rate falls below 50 bpm or exceeds 120 bpm.

**Step-by-step SDD construction:**

1. **Safety class determination:**
   - Incorrect alarm could delay treatment → **Class B**.

2. **Architecture decomposition** (Software Items):
   - *AFE Driver* — controls ADS1292 analog front end.
   - *DSP Pipeline* — 50 Hz notch filter, 0.5–40 Hz bandpass.
   - *R-peak Detector* — Pan-Tompkins.
   - *HR Computer* — exponential moving average of R-R intervals.
   - *Alarm Manager* — hysteresis-based threshold logic.
   - *Display Manager* — updates LCD waveform.
   - *Safety Watchdog* — toggled every main loop iteration.

3. **Interface definition** (excerpt):
   - *AFE Driver* → *DSP Pipeline*: `int32_t ecg_sample[500]` at 500 Hz, units of µV.

4. **Algorithm documentation** (Alarm Manager pseudocode):
   ```
   every 1 second:
       if (HR < LOW_LIMIT) and (time_in_zone > 10 s):
           trigger(LOW_HR_ALARM)
       if (HR > HIGH_LIMIT) and (time_in_zone > 10 s):
           trigger(HIGH_HR_ALARM)
   ```

5. **Risk control:** Watchdog timer set to 2 seconds; if not refreshed, processor resets. Verification: induce deadlock in test firmware, observe reset.

6. **Traceability:** Software requirement `SWR-014` ("alarm within 15 s of threshold breach") → *Alarm Manager* module → test `IT-007`.

This is the level of detail expected in a real, audit-ready SDD.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Design Control Workflow (V-Model Mapping)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **V-Model of medical device development** to the role of the Software/Hardware Design Description in the lifecycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Needs and Intended Use] --> B[Design Inputs Requirements]
    B --> C[Risk Management File ISO 14971]
    C --> D[Software Architecture]
    C --> E[Hardware Architecture]
    D --> F[Software Design Description SDD]
    E --> G[Hardware Design Description HDD]
    F --> H[Design Implementation]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Unit Verification]
    I --> J[Integration Verification]
    J --> K[System Verification]
    K --> L[Design Validation]
    B -.->|Traceability| F
    B -.->|Traceability| G
    F -.->|Forward Trace| I
    G -.->|Forward Trace| I
    L --> M[Design Transfer to Production]
    M --> N[Post Market Surveillance]
    N --> C
```

**Reading the diagram:** Design Inputs flow down the left side into the SDD/HDD (the design outputs). Verification activities mirror this on the right side. The dotted lines represent **traceability links** — the cornerstone of regulatory compliance.

## 4.2 Software Architecture — ECG Monitor Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    afe[AFE Driver] --> dsp[DSP Pipeline Filter]
    dsp --> rpeak[R Peak Detector]
    rpeak --> hr[HR Computer]
    hr --> alarm[Alarm Manager]
    hr --> disp[Display Manager]
    disp --> lcd[LCD Screen]
    alarm --> buzzer[Buzzer and LED]
    wd[Safety Watchdog] -.->|monitors| afe
    wd -.->|monitors| dsp
    wd -.->|monitors| rpeak
    wd -.->|monitors| hr
    wd -.->|monitors| alarm
    wd -.->|monitors| disp
    ext[External Comm USB] --> hr
    hr --> ext
```

**Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show data flow. Dotted arrows show watchdog monitoring. The Safety Watchdog independently verifies liveness of every other software item — a classic **defense-in-depth** pattern required for IEC 62304 Class B/C software.

## 4.3 Hardware Block Diagram — Patient Monitor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    electrode[Patient Electrodes] --> iso[Patient Isolation Barrier 2x MOPP]
    iso --> afe[Analog Front End ADS1292]
    afe --> adc[ADC 24 bit 500 sps]
    adc --> mcu[Microcontroller STM32H7]
    mcu --> lcd[LCD Display]
    mcu --> spk[Speaker and Alarm LED]
    mcu --> usb[USB and Ethernet]
    psu[Medical Grade Power Supply 12V] --> iso
    psu --> mcu
    batt[Li Ion Battery Backup 8 hr] --> psu
    watchdog[Hardware Watchdog Timer] -.->|reset if not toggled| mcu
    eeprom[EEPROM Patient Log] --- mcu
```

**Reading the diagram:** Note the **isolation barrier** between the patient-connected analog front end and the rest of the system. This is mandated by **IEC 60601-1, Clause 8** (Protection against electrical hazards). The hardware watchdog is a defense-in-depth measure independent of the software watchdog — a single point of failure is unacceptable in a Class B/C device.

## 4.4 Document Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dhp[Design History File DHF] --> ddp[Design and Development Plan]
    dhp --> dhir[Design History and Review Records]
    dhp --> did[Design Input Document]
    dhp --> sdd[Software Design Description]
    dhp --> hdd[Hardware Design Description]
    dhp --> vr[Verification and Validation Reports]
    dhp --> rmf[Risk Management File ISO 14971]
    sdd --> sa[Software Architecture]
    sdd --> sdd_detail[Detailed Design]
    sdd --> sdd_unit[Unit Implementation Notes]
    hdd --> hd_block[Block Diagrams]
    hdd --> hd_sch[Schematic Diagrams]
    hdd --> hd_bom[Bill of Materials]
    hdd --> hd_mech[Mechanical Drawings]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **Design History File (DHF)** is the master regulatory file. The SDD and HDD are sibling sub-documents within it. Each sub-document further decomposes into its constituent elements as shown.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the term "Software Design Description" (SDD). Mention the standard that governs its content and list any four essential elements that must be present in an SDD.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Software Design Description (SDD)** is a controlled design output document that describes the **software architecture**, **detailed design**, and **implementation-level detail** of software used in a medical device. It serves as the blueprint that translates *software requirements* (derived from system-level design inputs) into a structure that programmers can implement and testers can verify.

**Governing Standard:** **IEC 62304, Clause 5.4** (Medical device software — Software life cycle processes).

**Four essential elements:**
1. **Software system architecture** — identification of major Software Items and their interfaces.
2. **Detailed design** — decomposition of each Software Item into Software Units with defined interfaces.
3. **Software unit implementation details** — algorithms, data structures, and control flow at the source-code level.
4. **Software requirements traceability** — explicit mapping from each software requirement to the design elements that satisfy it.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always cite the standard clause number (e.g., IEC 62304 §5.4). Students who write only "IEC 62304" without the clause lose 0.5 marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Model Question]**
*What is a Hardware Design Description (HDD)? Explain the role of an insulation diagram and creepage distance in the HDD of a medical electrical device.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Hardware Design Description (HDD)** is a structured collection of documents that describe the physical and electrical realization of a medical device. It includes block diagrams, schematic diagrams, BOM, PCB layout files, mechanical drawings, and compliance documentation against **IEC 60601-1**.

**Role of the Insulation Diagram:**
The insulation diagram is a critical document that classifies all isolation barriers within the device according to the **MOPP (Means of Patient Protection)** and **MOOP (Means of Operator Protection)** framework. It identifies which circuits are patient-connected, which are operator-accessible, and what level of insulation separates them.

**Role of Creepage Distance:**
Creepage distance is the shortest path along the surface of an insulating material between two conductive parts. Per **IEC 60601-1, Table 12** and related clauses:

$$
\text{Creepage}_{\text{required}} = f(\text{Working Voltage}, \text{Material CTI}, \text{MOPP Count})
$$

Adequate creepage prevents **arcing and insulation breakdown** that could lead to patient or operator electric shock. **Reinforced insulation (2 × MOPP)** typically requires double the basic insulation creepage distance.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **structure and content of an Software Design Description (SDD)** as required by **IEC 62304 Clause 5.4**. Discuss how the SDD integrates with the **Risk Management File (RMF)** per ISO 14971 for a **Class C** medical device software. *\[7 Marks\]*

**(b)** For a **portable infusion pump** intended to deliver intravenous medication in a hospital setting, develop a **high-level software architecture** showing at least six major Software Items. Justify the architectural choices using the **defense-in-depth** principle. *\[7 Marks\]*

---

### Model Answer to Question A

**(a) Structure and Content of SDD per IEC 62304 §5.4** *\[7 Marks\]*

The SDD for a Class C medical device software must contain the following structural elements (each marked with its expected depth and marks allocation):

**1. Software Scope and Purpose (0.5 Marks):** Define what the software does and, critically, what it does *not* do. Reference the parent device, intended use, and software safety classification.

**2. Software Architecture (2 Marks):** Identify all major **Software Items** and their interfaces. For Class C, the architecture must demonstrate:
- Clear separation between safety-critical and non-safety-critical functions.
- Use of independent safety mechanisms (e.g., a separate watchdog task).
- Defense-in-depth: redundant checks for critical parameters (e.g., dosing limits).

**3. Detailed Design (2 Marks):** Each Software Item is decomposed into **Software Units**. For Class C, the design must specify algorithms, state transitions, error handling, and resource budgets. Pseudocode or formal statecharts are expected.

**4. Software Requirements Traceability (1 Mark):** A matrix mapping each software requirement (SWR-XXX) to the Software Items/Units that implement it.

**5. Risk Control Measures Implemented in Software (1 Mark):** Explicit listing of every **risk control** from the RMF that is implemented in software, with the corresponding hazard ID. Example: "HAZ-003 (over-infusion) mitigated by *Dosing Limit Checker* in Software Unit *PU-007*, verified by test IT-022."

**6. SOUP Documentation (0.5 Marks):** List of third-party software with version, license, and known anomalies.

**Integration with the Risk Management File (RMF):**

Per ISO 14971, every identified **hazard** must have associated **risk control measures**. When a risk control is implemented in software, the SDD is where that control becomes tangible. The traceability is typically:

$$
\text{Hazard ID} \rightarrow \text{Risk Control Measure} \rightarrow \text{SDD Module} \rightarrow \text{Verification Test}
$$

For Class C, **IEC 62304 §5.7.4** additionally requires that the design is verified to provide **separation** between risk control measures, so that a single fault cannot defeat multiple protections.

---

**(b) Software Architecture for a Portable Infusion Pump** *\[7 Marks\]*

**Major Software Items:**

| ID | Software Item | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| SI-01 | User Interface Manager | LCD touch screen, button handling, menu navigation |
| SI-02 | Drug Library Manager | Stores approved drug profiles with hard/soft limits |
| SI-03 | Order Entry and Verification | Receives prescription from clinician; checks against drug library |
| SI-04 | Delivery Controller | Calculates and commands motor driver for syringe/IV set |
| SI-05 | Sensor Monitor | Reads flow rate, pressure, bubble (air-in-line) sensors |
| SI-06 | Safety Supervisor (Independent) | Independent watchdog over SI-04 and SI-05 |
| SI-07 | Alarm Manager | Audio-visual alarms for occlusion, air-in-line, low battery |
| SI-08 | Communication Module | Wi-Fi to hospital EMR, nurse call system |
| SI-09 | Event Logger | Tamper-resistant log of all dosing events |

**Defense-in-Depth Justification:**

The architecture applies **three independent layers** of protection against the catastrophic hazard of **over-infusion**:

1. **Layer 1 — Order Verification (SI-03):** Compares programmed dose against drug library hard limits *before* infusion starts. Rejects overdose at the input.

2. **Layer 2 — Real-Time Delivery Monitoring (SI-05 + SI-04):** Independently measures flow via a separate sensor path. If actual delivery exceeds the commanded delivery by a tolerance (e.g., $> 5\%$) for a defined duration, the Safety Supervisor commands shutdown.

3. **Layer 3 — Independent Safety Supervisor (SI-06):** Runs on a separate microcontroller core or a hardware-only watchdog. It receives "heartbeat" messages from the Delivery Controller. If the heartbeat is missed for $> 500$ ms, the Safety Supervisor directly cuts power to the motor via a hardware relay — bypassing the main CPU entirely.

This **three-layer architecture** satisfies IEC 62304 §5.7.4 requirement for risk control separation. A single software fault cannot defeat all three layers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Students often describe software items but **fail to justify architectural choices** using defense-in-depth — losing up to 2 marks.
> 2. Many forget to **link the SDD back to specific hazard IDs** from the RMF — losing 1 mark.
> 3. Students sometimes forget to mention the **SOUP list** — losing 0.5 marks even when not directly asked.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Discuss the **structure and essential content of a Hardware Design Description (HDD)** for a medical electrical device governed by **IEC 60601-1**. Include the role of the **insulation diagram**, **creepage and clearance**, and the **Means of Patient Protection (MOPP)** framework. *\[7 Marks\]*

**(b)** Design a **high-level hardware block diagram** for a **multi-parameter patient monitor** that measures ECG (3-lead), SpO₂ (fingertip), and non-invasive blood pressure (NIBP). Identify the isolation strategy and explain how the design satisfies **IEC 60601-1, Clause 8** for patient protection. *\[7 Marks\]*

---

### Model Answer to Question B

**(a) Structure and Content of HDD per IEC 60601-1** *\[7 Marks\]*

The HDD is the complete set of documents that translate hardware-level requirements into a buildable, verifiable physical design. For a Class I/IIa/IIb medical electrical equipment, the HDD must contain:

**1. System Block Diagram (1 Mark):** High-level diagram showing major functional blocks: power supply, isolation barrier, analog front end, microcontroller, memory, user interface, communication interfaces, and patient connections.

**2. Schematic Diagrams (1.5 Marks):** Detailed electrical schematics for each functional block. Reference designators (R1, C5, U3), component values, tolerances, power nets, and signal flow must be explicit.

**3. Bill of Materials (BOM) (0.5 Marks):** Each line item includes reference designator, value, package, manufacturer part number, component ratings (voltage, current, power), and **derating factors**. For safety-critical components, derating to $50\%$ of rated stress is the industry norm.

**4. Insulation Diagram and MOPP Classification (2 Marks):** The insulation diagram identifies all isolation barriers and classifies them per **MOPP** and **MOOP**:

$$
\text{MOPP Count} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{Basic insulation between patient and operator-accessible circuits} \\ 2 & \text{Reinforced insulation (double insulation or one reinforced layer)} \end{cases}
$$

For **Type CF (Cardiac Floating)** applied parts, **2 × MOPP** is mandatory between the patient and any earth-grounded conductor.

**5. Creepage and Clearance (1 Mark):** Per **IEC 60601-1, Tables 12 and 13**:

$$
\text{Creepage}_{\text{min}} \geq k_{\text{voltage}} \times k_{\text{MOPP}} \times k_{\text{CTI}}
$$

$$
\text{Clearance}_{\text{min}} \geq k_{\text{voltage}} \times k_{\text{MOPP}}
$$

Where $k_{\text{CTI}}$ accounts for the Comparative Tracking Index of the PCB material.

**6. EMC Compliance Documentation (0.5 Marks):** Per **IEC 60601-1-2**, including shielding, filtering, PCB layout practices, and the EMC test plan.

**7. Mechanical Drawings (0.5 Marks):** Enclosure, mounting, thermal management, and patient-contact surfaces.

---

**(b) Hardware Block Diagram for Multi-Parameter Patient Monitor** *\[7 Marks\]*

```
                    [PATIENT CONNECTIONS]
                            |
            +---------------+----------------+
            |                                  |
        ECG Leads                          SpO2 Sensor
        (Type CF)                          (Type BF)
            |                                  |
   +-------------------+            +-------------------+
   | ECG Analog Front  |            | SpO2 Module       |
   | End + Isolation   |            | (LED + PD + AFE)  |
   | (2 x MOPP)        |            | (2 x MOPP)        |
   +---------+----------+            +---------+---------+
             |                                 |
             +-----------------+---------------+
                               |
                    +----------v----------+
                    |   Main MCU Board    |
                    |   (STM32H7)         |
                    |   - ADC sampling    |
                    |   - DSP filtering   |
                    |   - Display driver  |
                    +----------+----------+
                               |
        +----------+-----------+-----------+
        |          |                       |
   [LCD Touch]   [Speaker/Alarm]    [USB/Wi-Fi]
        |
   [NIBP Cuff Pump + Pressure Sensor + Isolation]
        |
   [Battery + Medical Grade PSU with 2 x MOPP from mains]
```

**Isolation Strategy:**

The patient-connected circuits (ECG, SpO₂, NIBP cuff) are all **galvanically isolated** from the mains-powered section using:
- **Reinforced insulation barriers** (2 × MOPP) — typically using digital isolators (e.g., Analog Devices ADuM1411) for signals and a medical-grade isolated DC-DC converter for power.
- **Patient isolation to earth**: leakage current limited to $< 10$ µA (Type CF, per IEC 60601-1, Clause 8.7).

**Compliance with IEC 60601-1 Clause 8 (Protection Against Electrical Hazards):**

| Sub-clause | Requirement | How Met in Design |
|---|---|---|
| 8.1 | Basic safety related to electrical shock | Reinforced insulation (2 × MOPP) on all patient connections |
| 8.2 | Requirements related to power sources | Medical-grade PSU; battery backup with fuel gauge |
| 8.3 | Requirements related to applied parts | Type CF classification for ECG; Type BF for SpO₂/NIBP |
| 8.4 | Limitation of voltage/current | Defibrillator-proof applied part protection per Clause 8.5 |
| 8.5 | Defibrillation protection | Input resistor networks + clamping on ECG inputs |
| 8.6 | Protective earthing | Single-point earth; PE conductor sized per Table 14 |
| 8.7 | Leakage currents and patient auxiliary currents | Measured to be $< 10$ µA (Type CF) and $< 100$ µA (Type BF) |

The use of **digital isolators with reinforced insulation rating** (typically $5$ kV RMS) provides the required $2 \times \text{MOPP}$ between the patient-connected analog section and the digital/MCU section.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the isolation barrier** between patient-connected and mains-powered sections — losing up to 2 marks. This is the single most important safety feature.
> 2. **Confusing MOPP and MOOP** — MOPP protects the patient; MOOP protects only the operator. ECG applied parts require 2 × MOPP.
> 3. **Not mentioning creepage/clearance numerically** — students should recall typical values like $8$ mm minimum creepage for 250 V working voltage with 2 × MOPP.
> 4. **Omitting defibrillation protection** on ECG inputs — IEC 60601-1 §8.5 explicitly requires it.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Software/Hardware Design Description**

- The **Design Description** (SDD + HDD) is the *Design Output* that proves *Design Inputs* have been met. Governed by **FDA 21 CFR 820.30** and harmonized standards.
- **SDD** is governed by **IEC 62304 §5.4**. Key contents: architecture, detailed design, unit detail, traceability, risk controls, SOUP list.
- **HDD** is governed by **IEC 60601-1** series. Key contents: block diagram, schematics, BOM, insulation diagram, mechanical drawings, EMC docs.
- **Software Safety Classes** under IEC 62304: **A (no injury), B (non-serious), C (death/serious)**. Class determines documentation depth.
- **MOPP** = Means of Patient Protection; **MOOP** = Means of Operator Protection. ECG applied parts need **2 × MOPP**.
- **Type CF** applied parts (Cardiac Floating) have the strictest patient-protection requirements. SpO₂ is typically **Type BF**.
- **SOUP** = Software of Unknown Provenance (third-party code). Must be listed, version-controlled, and risk-assessed.
- **Traceability Matrix** is bidirectional: forward (every requirement → design) and backward (every design element → justified by requirement).
- **Defense-in-depth** = multiple independent layers of protection. Critical for Class B/C software.
- **Hardware Watchdog** is independent of software watchdog — single point of failure is unacceptable.
- **Creepage** depends on **working voltage**, **insulation material (CTI)**, and **MOPP count**.
- Always link **design descriptions back to specific hazard IDs** in the Risk Management File.
- The **Design History File (DHF)** is the master regulatory submission record. SDD and HDD live inside it.
- For KTU answers: **always cite the standard and clause number** (e.g., "IEC 62304 §5.4") — this earns examiner-respect marks.
- **Defibrillation protection** is mandatory on ECG inputs per IEC 60601-1 §8.5.
- **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** in DFMEA $= \text{Severity} \times \text{Occurrence} \times \text{Detection}$. Lower RPN after mitigation proves effectiveness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Device History Record
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Device History Record

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Device History Record (DHR) — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Device History Record (DHR)** is a **mandatory, regulated compilation of documentation** that contains the complete production history of a single finished medical device — from the procurement of raw materials and components, through every manufacturing, assembly, packaging, and labeling step, to final quality release and distribution. It is governed internationally by **FDA 21 CFR § 820.184** (U.S. QSR) and **ISO 13485:2016, Clause 7.5.1** (Global Quality Management Systems for Medical Devices).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the DHR is treated as the **legal, traceable, auditable evidence** that **each and every** unit of a medical device was manufactured exactly as described in its **Device Master Record (DMR)**, under controlled conditions, and in compliance with the **Design History File (DHF)** outputs.

### Key Terminology Distinction (Board-Exam Favorite)

| Acronym | Full Form | Purpose | Stage in Lifecycle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DHF** | Design History File | Proves the device was **designed correctly** | Pre-production / R&D |
| **DMR** | Device Master Record | The **master recipe** / current manufacturing instructions | Production baseline |
| **DHR** | Device History Record | Proves **this specific unit** was built **per the recipe** | Post-production / Lot release |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Students:** *Design (DHF) → Recipe (DMR) → Record (DHR).* You design once, you write the recipe once, but you build a DHR **for every batch / lot / serial number.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **luxury watch factory** (Rolex, Omega, or our own Kerala *Kerala Brass*). When you buy a ₹5 lakh Rolex, the box contains a unique card with a serial number. Behind the scenes, the factory maintains a paper trail (now digital) for **that specific watch**:

- Which batch of Swiss movement it came from.
- Which artisan assembled the escapement.
- The exact torque applied to the case-back screws.
- The QC inspector who signed it off.
- The shipping carton it left in.

That paper trail is the **DHR**.

Now, in a **biomedical device** — say a pacemaker, a hip implant, or an MRI surface coil — the stakes are not "style," but **life and death**. A single un-traced capacitor, a missed sterilization cycle, or an unsigned QC check can cause patient mortality. The DHR is therefore the **forensic passport** of every medical device that touches a human body.

## 1.3 Physical / Regulatory Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Regulatory Citation:** **21 CFR § 820.184** (FDA, U.S.) — DHR requirements.
> - **Standard:** **ISO 13485:2016**, Section **7.5.1 (Control of Production and Service Provision)** and **7.5.8 (Identification)**.
> - **Identification Standard:** **UDI (Unique Device Identification)** per **21 CFR Part 830** and **EU MDR 2017/745, Article 27**.
> - **Retention Period:** **Lifetime of the device + 2 years**, or a minimum of **2 years** after the last unit is released — whichever is longer (per 21 CFR § 820.180).
> - **Lot / Batch Size Rule:** DHR is maintained **per unit (serial-numbered)** or **per lot/batch (non-serialized consumables)**, as defined in the DMR.

---

## 1.4 Why DHR Matters in the KTU Biomedical Design Curriculum

The KTU 2024 Scheme for **PEEBT752 – Design of Biomedical Devices** places DHR in **Module 1** because every senior design project (Capstone) the student undertakes must culminate in a regulatory deliverable. The DHR is the **proof-of-manufacture** that complements the **Design Control** and **Risk Management (ISO 14971)** outputs covered in the same module. Without the DHR, a beautifully designed device remains **unmanufacturable and unsellable**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Relationship — DHF → DMR → DHR

The DHR does not exist in isolation. It is the **third pillar** of the FDA's Design Control trilogy, sitting on top of the DHF and DMR.

1. **Design History File (DHF)** — Established during **design and development**. Contains design inputs, outputs, reviews, verification, validation, and transfer records (per **21 CFR § 820.30(j)**).
2. **Device Master Record (DMR)** — A single reference document containing all the **current approved procedures, drawings, specifications, and labeling** for manufacturing the device (per **21 CFR § 820.181**).
3. **Device History Record (DHR)** — Created **after** manufacturing. It is the **chronological evidence** that each production unit followed the DMR.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core 'Why':** The DHF proves the device *should* work. The DMR proves the factory *knows how* to build it. The **DHR proves the factory actually built it that way, for this exact device, today.**

## 2.2 Statutory Contents of a DHR (21 CFR § 820.184 — Six Mandatory Elements)

Under the U.S. FDA Quality System Regulation, every DHR **must** include, or refer to the location of, the following six data sets:

| # | Mandatory Element | Engineering Meaning | Typical Record Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Manufacturing dates** | The exact date(s) the unit was produced | Production log, MES timestamps |
| 2 | **Quantity manufactured** | The number of units in the lot (and the unit released) | Batch sheet, ERP printout |
| 3 | **Acceptance records** | Evidence that the unit passed **all** in-process and final QC tests | QC test reports, calibration certs |
| 4 | **Primary product identification** | UDI-DI, UDI-PI, lot/serial number, model/catalog # | Label proof, laser-mark image |
| 5 | **Labeling** | The actual labels used on the device and its packaging | Printed label specimens |
| 6 | **UDI** (since 2014 amendment) | Unique Device Identifier for traceability | UDI assignment sheet (GS1 / HIBCC) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** A common student error is equating the **DHR** with the **DHF**. They are **NOT the same**. The DHF is *design-time* documentation; the DHR is *production-time* documentation. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks immediately in any Part B question.

## 2.3 Engineering Step-by-Step: How a DHR Is Created

1. **Lot Creation Order (LCO)** — Released by Production Planning; assigns a unique **lot number** (e.g., `LOT-2024-IMPLANT-0457`).
2. **Component & Material Receipt** — Each incoming component is logged with its **supplier lot, COA (Certificate of Analysis), inspection status**.
3. **In-Process Manufacturing** — Operators record **workstation, machine ID, cycle time, parameter readings** (e.g., injection-mold temp = 215 °C, weld current = 4.2 kA). These readings must match the DMR **specification limits**.
4. **In-Process Inspection** — Dimensional, functional, or visual checks at defined gates; results signed by the operator and the QC inspector.
5. **Final Acceptance Testing** — 100 % or AQL-sampled testing of finished units (e.g., dielectric withstand test for an ECG cable, sterilization indicator check for a surgical glove).
6. **Labeling Reconciliation** — All labeling issued, used, and destroyed is reconciled; any **unaccounted labels** are a non-conformance.
7. **Release Decision** — The QA Manager (or authorized person) signs the **release-to-distribution** record.
8. **Storage & Retention** — DHR archived in a **controlled, retrievable, protected** location for the **statutory retention period**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Although DHR is largely regulatory, KTU examiners expect quantitative reasoning. The following analytical expressions are the only formulae you will need:

$$
\text{Retention Period} \;=\; \max\Bigl(\;T_{\text{device life}} + 2 \text{ yr},\; T_{\text{release}} + 2 \text{ yr}\;\Bigr)
$$

$$
\text{Sampling Size (AQL)} \;=\; n \;=\; \bigl\lceil\, N \cdot p_{\text{AQL}}\,\bigr\rceil
$$

where $N$ is lot size, $p_{\text{AQL}}$ is the acceptance quality limit fraction from **ISO 2859-1**, and $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is the ceiling function.

$$
\text{Yield} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{pass}}}{N_{\text{start}}} \times 100\,\%
$$

$$
\text{Right-First-Time (RFT)} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{no rework}}}{N_{\text{start}}} \times 100\,\%
$$

$$
\text{Label Reconciliation} \;=\; L_{\text{issued}} \;-\; L_{\text{used}} \;-\; L_{\text{destroyed}} \;\equiv\; 0
$$

A **non-zero label reconciliation** automatically triggers a **Non-Conformance Report (NCR)** under 21 CFR § 820.90.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The DHR feeds into **post-market surveillance (PMS)**, **complaint handling (21 CFR § 820.198)**, and **MDR (Medical Device Reporting) under 21 CFR Part 803**. If a pacemaker fails in a patient in Kochi, the manufacturer must trace it back via the DHR to identify the lot, components, sterilization batch, and surgeon — all within **5 working days** for an FDA Class III device.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & DHR Construction Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** Because the DHR topic is **regulatory-engineering** rather than purely mathematical, Section 3 presents a **complete, line-by-line analytical walkthrough** of (a) the logical derivation of the DHR contents from the parent regulations, (b) a worked numerical example, and (c) the analytical matrix that maps each DHR element to its regulatory clause, document type, and engineering rationale. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — From Regulation to DHR Element

The logic chain is:

$$
\textbf{Regulation} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Clause Extraction}}\; \textbf{Mandate} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Engineering Translation}}\; \textbf{DHR Element} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Verification}}\; \textbf{Audit Evidence}
$$

We now apply this chain **exhaustively** to each of the six mandatory elements.

### Element 1 — Manufacturing Dates

- **Regulation:** 21 CFR § 820.184(a)
- **Mandate:** "The dates of manufacture."
- **Engineering Translation:** Every step that **adds value** to the device must be timestamped. For a multi-step process (e.g., molding → welding → sterilization → packaging → labeling), each step's **start and end date** is recorded.
- **Document Type:** Production traveler / shop-floor log / MES printout.
- **Audit Verification:** The auditor can request any lot and reconstruct the **Gantt timeline** of its build.

### Element 2 — Quantity Manufactured

- **Regulation:** 21 CFR § 820.184(b)
- **Mandate:** "The quantity manufactured."
- **Engineering Translation:** The exact unit count released, plus any **rejected, reworked, or scrapped** sub-units.
- **Document Type:** Lot reconciliation sheet.
- **Audit Verification:** Auditor checks that **physical units in stock + shipped + rejected + on hold = quantity manufactured** (mass-balance).

### Element 3 — Acceptance Records

- **Regulation:** 21 CFR § 820.184(c)
- **Mandate:** "Acceptance records demonstrating the device is manufactured in accordance with the DMR."
- **Engineering Translation:** All **in-process, final, and release** QC results, including any **calibration certificates** of the equipment used.
- **Document Type:** Test reports, calibration certs, SPC charts.
- **Audit Verification:** Each result must **trace to a calibration-traceable instrument** that was **valid** at the time of test.

### Element 4 — Primary Product Identification

- **Regulation:** 21 CFR § 820.184(d)
- **Mandate:** "Primary product identification and labeling used for each production unit."
- **Engineering Translation:** UDI-DI + UDI-PI + lot/serial + model number.
- **Document Type:** Label image, laser-mark scan, UDI assignment table.
- **Audit Verification:** Cross-check that the UDI on the DHR matches the UDI in the **GUDID (Global Unique Device Identification Database)**.

### Element 5 — Labeling

- **Regulation:** 21 CFR § 820.184(e)
- **Mandate:** "The labeling used for the device."
- **Engineering Translation:** A **specimen** of every label (IFU, carton, primary pack, device label) is retained.
- **Document Type:** Printed label samples, IFU version-controlled PDF, artwork.
