- **Audit Verification:** Version numbers of all labels **must match** the version numbers referenced in the DMR.

### Element 6 — UDI

- **Regulation:** 21 CFR § 820.184(f) (added 2014)
- **Mandate:** "Unique Device Identifier (UDI) information."
- **Engineering Translation:** The full UDI string (device identifier + production identifier) for the unit.
- **Document Type:** UDI assignment record.
- **Audit Verification:** Compliance with **21 CFR Part 830** and **EU MDR 2017/745**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (AQL Sampling for DHR Acceptance Records)

> **Scenario:** A KTU Capstone team has manufactured a lot of **N = 800 units** of an EEG surface electrode. The acceptance plan is per **ISO 2859-1, General Inspection Level II, AQL = 1.0 %**. How many units must be tested in-process, and what is the acceptance number (Ac) and rejection number (Re)?

**Step 1 — Find the sample-size code letter.**

For General Inspection Level II, N = 800 corresponds to lot-size range **501–1200**, which maps to **code letter H**.

**Step 2 — Look up sample size and Ac/Re for code letter H at AQL 1.0 %.**

Per ISO 2859-1 Table II-A, code letter H at AQL 1.0 gives:

$$
n \;=\; 50 \text{ units},\quad Ac \;=\; 1,\quad Re \;=\; 2
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the decision rule.**

$$
\text{If defects found} \;\le\; Ac \;=\; 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Lot ACCEPTED}
$$

$$
\text{If defects found} \;\ge\; Re \;=\; 2 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Lot REJECTED}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the inspection fraction.**

$$
\frac{n}{N} \;=\; \frac{50}{800} \;=\; 0.0625 \;=\; 6.25\,\%
$$

**Step 5 — Document the result in the DHR.**

The DHR's *Acceptance Record* section must now contain:
- 50 individual test reports,
- The AQL plan reference,
- The inspector's signature,
- The acceptance/rejection decision.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — DHR Content vs. Regulatory Clause vs. Engineering Action

| # | DHR Element | FDA 21 CFR Clause | ISO 13485 Clause | Engineering Action | Risk if Missing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Manufacturing dates | § 820.184(a) | 7.5.1 (f) | Timestamp every process step | Inability to scope recalls |
| 2 | Quantity manufactured | § 820.184(b) | 7.5.1 (b) | Mass-balance all units | Undetected counterfeiting |
| 3 | Acceptance records | § 820.184(c) | 7.5.1 (e), 8.2.6 | Run and sign off all QC tests | Undetected defects reach patients |
| 4 | Primary product ID | § 820.184(d) | 7.5.8 | Assign & scan UDI | Inability to identify fielded units |
| 5 | Labeling specimens | § 820.184(e) | 7.5.1 (c) | Retain label artwork & IFU version | Misbranding / off-label use |
| 6 | UDI string | § 820.184(f) | 7.5.8 | Record DI + PI | Global UDI non-compliance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example:** In **2022**, Philips Respironics recalled **15 million** CPAP / BiPAP devices. The recall scope was bounded **exclusively** by DHR records, which identified the **exact foam-pouring lot, dates, and machines** used. Without the DHR, the recall would have been impossible to scope, and thousands more patients would have been exposed to degraded PE-PUR foam.

## 3.4 Logical Flow of a DHR-Triggered Field Corrective Action

$$
\text{Field Complaint} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Trace UDI}}\; \text{Identify Lot} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Lot → DHR}}\; \text{Identify Component Supplier} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Component Lot → Supplier}}\; \text{Root Cause} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Lot Reconciliation}}\; \text{Recall Scope}
$$

This flow is **examined heavily** in KTU Part B questions on **post-market surveillance** and **risk management**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DHR Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **flow of information and materials** through the DHR lifecycle, from incoming component receipt to release-to-distribution. This is the exact block architecture expected in KTU Module-1 design-control questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Supplier Component Receipt] --> A1[Incoming Inspection Log]
    A1 --> A2[Material Release to Production]
    A2 --> B0[Lot Creation Order - LCO]
    B0 --> B1[Workstation 1 - Molding]
    B1 --> B2[Workstation 2 - Assembly]
    B2 --> B3[Workstation 3 - Welding]
    B3 --> B4[Workstation 4 - Sterilization]
    B4 --> B5[Workstation 5 - Packaging]
    B5 --> B6[Workstation 6 - Labeling]
    B6 --> C0[In-Process QC Gate 1]
    C0 --> C1[In-Process QC Gate 2]
    C1 --> C2[Final Acceptance Test]
    C2 --> C3[Release to Distribution]
    C3 --> C4[DHR Archive Controlled Vault]

    D0[DMR - Master Recipe] -.Reference.-> B1
    D0 -.Reference.-> B2
    D0 -.Reference.-> B3
    D0 -.Reference.-> B4
    D0 -.Reference.-> B5
    D0 -.Reference.-> B6
    D0 -.Reference.-> C0
    D0 -.Reference.-> C1
    D0 -.Reference.-> C2

    E0[DHF - Design Outputs] -.Validated By.-> C2
    F0[ISO 13485 / 21 CFR 820] -.Mandates.-> A1
    F0 -.Mandates.-> C2
    F0 -.Mandates.-> C3
    F0 -.Mandates.-> C4
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Solid arrows (→)** represent the **physical / information flow** during production.
- **Dotted arrows (.-.->)** represent the **regulatory reference relationships** — i.e., DHR pulls its requirements from the DMR, which was itself validated by the DHF, all under FDA / ISO mandates.

## 4.2 DHR vs. DHF vs. DMR — Mermaid Comparison Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    DHF1[Design History File DHF] -- "Verified by V and V" --> DMR1[Device Master Record DMR]
    DMR1 -- "Recipe for production" --> DHR1[Device History Record DHR]
    DHF1 -.-> DHR1
    REG[FDA 21 CFR 820 / ISO 13485] -.Governs.-> DHF1
    REG -.Governs.-> DMR1
    REG -.Governs.-> DHR1
    DHR1 --> PMS1[Post-Market Surveillance]
    DHR1 --> C1[Complaint Handling]
    DHR1 --> R1[Recall Execution]
```

## 4.3 DHR Element Composition Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    DHR_ROOT[Device History Record DHR]

    DHR_ROOT --> EL1[1. Manufacturing Dates]
    DHR_ROOT --> EL2[2. Quantity Manufactured]
    DHR_ROOT --> EL3[3. Acceptance Records]
    DHR_ROOT --> EL4[4. Primary Product ID]
    DHR_ROOT --> EL5[5. Labeling]
    DHR_ROOT --> EL6[6. Unique Device Identifier UDI]

    EL1 --> EL1a[Start Timestamps]
    EL1 --> EL1b[End Timestamps]

    EL2 --> EL2a[Released Units]
    EL2 --> EL2b[Rejected Units]
    EL2 --> EL2c[Reworked Units]
    EL2 --> EL2d[Quarantined Units]

    EL3 --> EL3a[In-Process Test Reports]
    EL3 --> EL3b[Final Test Reports]
    EL3 --> EL3c[Calibration Certificates]

    EL4 --> EL4a[Model Number]
    EL4 --> EL4b[Catalog Number]
    EL4 --> EL4c[Serial or Lot Number]

    EL5 --> EL5a[Primary Device Label]
    EL5 --> EL5b[Carton Label]
    EL5 --> EL5c[Instructions for Use IFU]

    EL6 --> EL6a[Device Identifier UDI-DI]
    EL6 --> EL6b[Production Identifier UDI-PI]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Device History Record (DHR). List its six mandatory elements as per FDA 21 CFR § 820.184.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 Mark):** A Device History Record (DHR) is a compilation of records containing the complete production history of a finished medical device, demonstrating that it was manufactured in accordance with the Device Master Record (DMR) and regulatory requirements (per **21 CFR § 820.184** and **ISO 13485:2016, Clause 7.5.1**).
>
> **Six Mandatory Elements (2 Marks — 0.33 each, rounded):**
> 1. **Manufacturing dates** of each unit / lot
> 2. **Quantity manufactured** and unit reconciliation
> 3. **Acceptance records** (in-process + final QC results)
> 4. **Primary product identification** (model, catalog, serial/lot)
> 5. **Labeling** (specimens of all applied labels and IFU)
> 6. **Unique Device Identifier (UDI)** — Device Identifier (DI) + Production Identifier (PI)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Distinguish between a Device History Record (DHR) and a Device Master Record (DMR) with two engineering-relevant differences.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | Basis | DMR | DHR |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Stage of Lifecycle** | Established *before* production as the current approved recipe (1 Mark) | Created *during / after* production for each unit / lot (1 Mark) |
> | **Number of Instances** | One (per device model / version) | One per manufactured lot / unit (1 Mark) |
> | **Purpose** | *How* the device *should* be built | *Proof* that this unit *was* built that way |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module-Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and provides internal choice (a) and (b) alternatives. Both alternatives are presented below.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the hierarchical relationship between the **Design History File (DHF)**, **Device Master Record (DMR)**, and **Device History Record (DHR)**. Explain how each one supports the lifecycle of a medical device.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A medical-device manufacturer produces a lot of **N = 1,200 units** of an oxygen sensor. The acceptance plan follows **ISO 2859-1, General Inspection Level II, AQL = 0.65 %**. Calculate the **sample size n**, **acceptance number Ac**, and **rejection number Re**, and outline how these results must be documented in the DHR.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Hierarchical Relationship [7 Marks]**

1. **DHF (Design History File) — 2 Marks**
   - Created during the **design and development** phase per **21 CFR § 820.30(j)**.
   - Contains: design inputs, design outputs, design review minutes, verification (V&V) reports, validation reports, design transfer records, and design changes.
   - **Purpose:** Demonstrates the device was *designed correctly*, following user needs and design controls.
2. **DMR (Device Master Record) — 2 Marks**
   - The single, **current, approved reference** document per **21 CFR § 820.181**.
   - Contains: device specifications, manufacturing procedures, QA procedures, packaging and labeling specifications, installation, maintenance, and servicing procedures.
   - **Purpose:** Acts as the *recipe* or *build specification* for production.
3. **DHR (Device History Record) — 2 Marks**
   - Created **per lot / per unit** during production per **21 CFR § 820.184**.
   - Contains the six mandatory elements (dates, quantity, acceptance, ID, labeling, UDI).
   - **Purpose:** Provides the *traceable evidence* that the device was built *as per the DMR*.
4. **Logical Flow [1 Mark]:**
   - DHF → establishes design intent and validated V&V; → DMR encodes that intent into manufacturing instructions; → DHR records the as-built evidence per those instructions. (Reference: see Section 4.2 Mermaid comparison map.)

**(b) AQL Calculation [7 Marks]**

- **Step 1 — Lot-size band (1 Mark):** For N = 1,200, lot-size range in ISO 2859-1 is **1,201 – 3,200**, code letter **J**.
- **Step 2 — Sample size n (2 Marks):** Code letter J at AQL 0.65 % gives **n = 80** units.
- **Step 3 — Acceptance / Rejection numbers (2 Marks):** From Table II-A, **Ac = 1**, **Re = 2**.
- **Step 4 — Decision rule (1 Mark):** If defectives found ≤ 1 → lot accepted; ≥ 2 → lot rejected.
- **Step 5 — DHR documentation (1 Mark):** The 80 inspection reports, the AQL plan reference (ISO 2859-1, Level II, AQL 0.65 %), the inspector signature, the calibration certificate of the test instrument, and the release-or-reject decision must all be filed in the DHR's *Acceptance Records* section.

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain, with examples, the **six mandatory elements** of a Device History Record as mandated by **21 CFR § 820.184**. For each element, give one document type used to capture it in a typical medical-device factory.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role of the DHR in post-market surveillance (PMS)**, **complaint handling**, and **field corrective actions (recalls)**. Illustrate with a **hypothetical scenario** involving a faulty ECG cable.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Six DHR Elements [7 Marks — 1 mark per element + 1 mark for synthesis]**

| # | DHR Element | Document Type | Example (ECG Cable) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Manufacturing dates | Shop-floor traveler | Molding start 12-Mar-2024 09:15, end 10:45 |
| 2 | Quantity manufactured | Lot reconciliation sheet | 500 cables produced, 3 scrapped |
| 3 | Acceptance records | QC test reports | Hi-pot test pass, 4 MΩ insulation |
| 4 | Primary product ID | Label image + scan | Model ECG-LEAD-5, Lot 24-03-L12 |
| 5 | Labeling | Label artwork & IFU | IFU Rev. B, carton label Rev. C |
| 6 | UDI | UDI assignment record | (01)00845670012345(11)240312(10)L12 |

**(b) DHR in PMS / Complaint Handling / Recall [7 Marks]**

1. **Post-Market Surveillance (2 Marks):** The DHR allows the manufacturer to identify the **exact lot** of a device in the field, link it to **component suppliers**, **sterilization batches**, and **production workstations**, enabling continuous monitoring of field performance and CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action).
2. **Complaint Handling (2 Marks):** Per **21 CFR § 820.198**, every complaint is investigated. The DHR lets the QA investigator pull up the **complete manufacturing fingerprint** of the complainant's unit, identify potential failure modes, and decide whether the complaint represents an **isolated** or **systemic** issue.
3. **Field Corrective Action / Recall (2 Marks):** If a recall is required, the DHR scope is the **set of UDI Device Identifiers + Production Identifiers** for the affected lot. Without the DHR, a recall would have to cover the **entire product family**, causing huge unnecessary disruption.
4. **Hypothetical Scenario (1 Mark):** A hospital in Thrissur reports that **ECG cable Lot 24-03-L12** produces noisy traces. The manufacturer opens the DHR, finds that 5 % of that lot used a **suspect batch of copper alloy** from Supplier X, and issues a **Class II recall** of **Lot 24-03-L12 only** (25 units), sparing the other 475 units in production.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Question Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating DHF with DHR (–3 Marks):** Always explicitly state "DHR is *production-time*, DHF is *design-time*."
> 2. **Omitting UDI (–2 Marks):** Since the 2014 amendment, UDI is **mandatory**; forgetting it is an instant trigger for a valuation cut.
> 3. **Skipping the retention period (–1 Mark):** Always mention *"Lifetime + 2 years, or release + 2 years, whichever is longer."*
> 4. **Failing to cite the regulation (–1 Mark):** Every claim must be tied to a clause number, e.g., *"as per 21 CFR § 820.184"*. Vague answers score poorly.
> 5. **No numerical example in Part B (–1 Mark):** KTU 2024 examiners expect at least **one** worked numerical computation (e.g., AQL sample size, label reconciliation, yield).
> 6. **Ignoring ISO 13485 (–1 Mark):** Always pair the U.S. **21 CFR** citation with the international **ISO 13485:2016** clause.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Rapid-Fire Revision Block — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [x] **DHR = Device History Record** — production-time per-unit/per-lot evidence file.
- [x] **Regulated by:** **FDA 21 CFR § 820.184** AND **ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.5.1**.
- [x] **Six mandatory elements:** **D**ates, **Q**uantity, **A**cceptance, **P**roduct ID, **L**abeling, **U**DI (Mnemonic: *D-Q-A-P-L-U*).
- [x] **DHF = design time** | **DMR = recipe (one per device)** | **DHR = proof (one per lot/unit)**.
- [x] **UDI = DI + PI** per 21 CFR Part 830; DI = device identifier, PI = production identifier.
- [x] **Retention:** **Lifetime + 2 years** OR **Release + 2 years** — whichever is **longer**.
- [x] **Label reconciliation equation:** $L_{\text{issued}} - L_{\text{used}} - L_{\text{destroyed}} = 0$. A non-zero result = Non-Conformance.
- [x] **AQL sampling formula:** use **ISO 2859-1** lookup tables; sample-size code letter depends on lot size and inspection level.
- [x] **Always pair the U.S. regulation with ISO 13485** in answers to show international compliance awareness.
- [x] **DHR powers the recall process** — without it, recall scope balloons and patient risk multiplies.
- [x] **For KTU Capstone projects:** maintain a DHR template from Day 1 of prototype builds; your examiner will treat it as a regulatory deliverable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Device Master Record
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Device Master Record

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Device Master Record (DMR)** — As defined under **21 CFR 820.181** of the US FDA Quality System Regulation (QSR), the Device Master Record is a **compilation of all the procedures, drawings, specifications, and other records that describe the complete manufacturing process of a finished medical device**.

In the context of **PEEBT752 – Design of Biomedical Devices**, the DMR is treated as the **single, authoritative reference document** that translates a *designed* medical device into a *manufacturable, inspectable, and servicable* product. It is the bridge between the **Design Input/Output (DI/DO)** records housed in the **Design History File (DHF)** and the **actual production lot** tracked in the **Device History Record (DHR)**.

The DMR is a **living document** — it must be updated whenever a design change, process change, or labelling change is implemented and verified.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍳

> [!NOTE]
> Think of the DMR as the **"Master Recipe Card"** in a professional bakery.
>
> - The **recipe card (DMR)** contains every ingredient, every oven temperature, every plating instruction, the box in which the cake is shipped, and even how to clean the oven after baking.
> - The **DHF** is the *notebook of experimentation* the chef kept while inventing the recipe.
> - The **DHR** is the *logbook* the chef writes while actually baking a *specific batch* on *Tuesday* — recording actual temperatures, lot numbers of flour, and the customer who bought it.
>
> The DMR does not describe *one* cake; it describes *how to make any cake of this type, anywhere, anytime, by any trained baker*. That is exactly what the DMR does for a medical device.

### 1.3 Why the DMR Matters in KTU Context

Under KTU 2024 Scheme's **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, the DMR supports:

- **CO1 (Engineering Knowledge):** Demonstrates structured documentation of design intent.
- **CO5 (Modern Tool Usage):** Shows use of standards (ISO 13485, FDA 21 CFR 820, EU MDR).
- **CO6 (The Engineer & Society):** Ensures patient safety through traceable manufacturing controls.
- **CO12 (Life-long Learning):** Reinforces regulatory literacy demanded by the biomedical industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard References (Statutory):**
> - **21 CFR 820.181** (FDA QSR) — US statutory requirement.
> - **ISO 13485:2016, Section 4.2.3 & 7.5.1** — International general requirements for medical device QMS.
> - **EU MDR 2017/745, Annex IX** — European conformity assessment documentation.

### 1.4 Position of DMR in the Design-Control Hierarchy

$$\text{Design Inputs (DI)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Design Outputs (DO)} \;\longrightarrow\; \boxed{\text{DMR}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{DHR (per unit lot)}$$

The DMR is **downstream** of design activities and **upstream** of manufacturing — it is, in essence, the "manufacturing bible" generated *after* the design has been frozen through verification and validation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Content Matrix

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown — What the DMR Must Contain

The FDA's **21 CFR 820.181** enumerates **eight (8) mandatory elements** that every DMR for a Class II/III medical device must include. Each element is derived from a specific regulatory and engineering rationale.

### 2.2 Element-by-Element Logic

> **Element 1 — Device Specifications**
> - *Why:* Establishes the *quantitative* design baseline (dimensions, materials, electrical ratings, sterility, biocompatibility class).
> - *How:* Pulled directly from **Design Output (DO)** documents in the DHF. Each spec must be **individually traceable** to a Design Input requirement.

> **Element 2 — Production Process Specifications**
> - *Why:* Defines *how* each specification is achieved on the shop floor.
> - *How:* Includes work instructions, in-process control points, equipment settings (e.g., injection-moulding temperature = **220 °C ± 5 °C**), and assembly sequences.

> **Element 3 — Quality Assurance (QA) Procedures & Acceptance Criteria**
> - *Why:* Establishes the *gate* that decides whether a unit may be released.
> - *How:* Sampling plans (e.g., **AQL = 0.65**, ISO 2859-1), test methods, pass/fail thresholds, statistical process control (SPC) rules.

> **Element 4 — Packaging & Labelling Specifications**
> - *Why:* A medical device's *labelling* is itself a regulated device (per FDA). Packaging preserves sterile barrier integrity (SBS — **Sterile Barrier System**).
> - *How:* Artwork masters, bar-coding (e.g., **GS1-128**), UDI (Unique Device Identification) carrier placement, IFU (Instructions For Use) version control.

> **Element 5 — Installation Procedures**
> - *Why:* Applies only when the device is installed by the manufacturer (e.g., MRI, CT scanner, LINAC).
> - *How:* Site-preparation checklist, electrical/mechanical hookup drawings, commissioning test reports.

> **Element 6 — Maintenance & Servicing Procedures**
> - *Why:* Ensures the device remains in safe operating condition throughout its service life.
> - *How:* Preventive-maintenance schedule (e.g., every **500 hours or 6 months**), calibration protocols, recommended spare-parts list.

> **Element 7 — Identification of Components, Materials & Suppliers**
> - *Why:* Critical for **supplier control** and **change-notification** traceability.
> - *How:* Approved Supplier List (ASL), material certificates (e.g., **USP Class VI** for plastics), Certificates of Conformance (CoC).

> **Element 8 — Design Change Documentation**
> - *Why:* Demonstrates that any post-market design change was *reviewed, approved, and propagated* into the DMR.
> - *How:* Engineering Change Orders (ECO), change-control board (CCB) records, re-verification/re-validation evidence.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Content Matrix (Cheat Sheet)

| # | DMR Element | Source / Standard | Engineering Artefact | KTU Exam Weight |
|:-:|:-----------|:------------------|:---------------------|:---------------:|
| 1 | Device Specifications | 21 CFR 820.181(a) | Dimensional drawings, material specs | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | Production Process Specs | 21 CFR 820.181(b) | Work instructions, PFMEA | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | QA Procedures & Acceptance Criteria | 21 CFR 820.181(c) | Test methods, AQL plans | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Packaging & Labelling | 21 CFR 820.181(d) | Artwork, UDI, IFU | ⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Installation Procedures | 21 CFR 820.181(e) | Site-prep manuals | ⭐ |
| 6 | Maintenance & Servicing | 21 CFR 820.181(f) | PM schedules, calibration | ⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Component / Supplier ID | 21 CFR 820.181(g) | ASL, CoC, material certs | ⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Design Change Log | 21 CFR 820.181(h) | ECO, CCB records | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Mnemonic — "D-P-Q-P-I-M-S-D"**
> **D**evice specs → **P**roduction process → **Q**A & acceptance → **P**ackaging/label → **I**nstall → **M**aintain → **S**upplier/Component ID → **D**esign changes.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **biomedical industry**, the DMR is the *contract* between:

- **R&D** (who designed the device) and **Manufacturing** (who must build it).
- **Manufacturer** and **Regulator** (FDA / Notified Body / CDSCO) during audits.
- **Manufacturer** and **Hospital / Clinician** (especially for capital equipment with installation & servicing).

A single missing element — e.g., a **validated packaging seal-strength value** — can trigger an **FDA Form 483 observation** or a **CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action)** in an ISO 13485 audit.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Derivation & Analytical Matrix

> Since the DMR is a **documentary / procedural artefact** rather than a purely mathematical construct, this section derives the **systematic procedure** by which a DMR is *built* from upstream design artefacts, and presents the **logical flow** by which each DMR element is generated and verified.

## 3.1 Procedural Derivation — Building a DMR from Scratch

### Step 1: Freeze the Design Output (DO) Package
Before the DMR can be authored, the design must pass **Design Verification (DV)** and **Design Validation (DVaL)**.

$$\text{Inputs Confirmed} \;\xrightarrow{\text{DV}}\; \text{Design Output Verified} \;\xrightarrow{\text{DVaL}}\; \text{User Needs Met}$$

> **Explanation:** A *verified* design meets its *specifications*; a *validated* design meets its *intended use*. The DMR can only be written once both gates are passed; otherwise, every DMR element would carry the risk of being rewritten.

### Step 2: Extract the Bill of Materials (BOM) and Process Flow
The **Design Output** contains the BOM. Each BOM line is mapped to:

- A **supplier** (Element 7)
- A **receiving inspection criterion** (Element 3)
- A **manufacturing step** (Element 2)

> **Explanation:** This single mapping creates three DMR elements simultaneously, ensuring **bidirectional traceability** (each part can be traced to its supplier and to its role in the device).

### Step 3: Author the Production Process Specifications
For each manufacturing step, document:

- **Equipment** (e.g., injection moulding machine, reflow oven)
- **Process parameters** with measurable limits
- **In-process inspection** checkpoints

> **Example:** For an **Syringe Barrel Moulding Step** —
> - Melt temperature: **220 °C ± 5 °C**
> - Injection pressure: **800 bar ± 20 bar**
> - Cycle time: **12 s ± 1 s**
> - In-process check: Barrel inner diameter via **laser micrometer** every **500 shots**.

### Step 4: Define QA Acceptance Criteria
Using **ISO 2859-1** (sampling by attributes) for visual/defect tests, and **ISO 3951-1** (sampling by variables) for dimensional/functional tests, set the **Acceptance Quality Limit (AQL)**.

$$\text{Inspection Level II, AQL = 0.65} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Defective units ≤ 0.65\% accepted at normal severity}$$

> **Explanation:** The AQL is the *worst* process average that is *tolerable* as an average over time. Setting AQL = 0.65 means the sampling plan accepts lots with ≤ 0.65 % defectives.

### Step 5: Compile Packaging and Labelling Master
Include:

- **Primary package** (e.g., Tyvek/Mylar pouch for sterilization)
- **Sterile Barrier System (SBS) validation** report reference
- **Labelling artwork** with **UDI-DI** and **UDI-PI** (per **21 CFR 830** and **EU MDR Article 27**)
- **IFU** (Instructions For Use) in required languages

### Step 6: Draft Installation, Maintenance & Servicing Sections
Capital equipment (e.g., a defibrillator monitor) requires an **Installation Qualification (IQ)** and **Operational Qualification (OQ)** protocol stored *within* the DMR.

> **Explanation:** The DMR must contain the *procedure* used to qualify each installed unit at the customer site; the **results** of that procedure are stored in the **DHR**, not the DMR.

### Step 7: Establish Change-Control Linkage
Every DMR element is given a **revision number** and linked to the **Engineering Change Order (ECO)** database. A change in the BOM automatically triggers a **DMR re-issue** with re-verification.

$$\text{ECO Approved} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{DMR Section Updated} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{DHF Linked} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Manufacturing Notified}$$

### Step 8: Index, Approve, and Release
The DMR is **indexed** (typically ≥ 1 000 pages for a Class III device), **approved** by Quality, Regulatory, and Manufacturing representatives, and **released** under document-control. The DMR is now the *binding* manufacturing reference.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — DMR vs DHF vs DHR

| Attribute | DMR (Device Master Record) | DHF (Design History File) | DHR (Device History Record) |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Purpose** | How to *make* the device | How the device was *designed* | Proof that *this lot* was made correctly |
| **Stage** | Production / Post-design | Design & Development | Manufacturing (per lot) |
| **Regulation** | 21 CFR 820.181 | 21 CFR 820.30(j) | 21 CFR 820.184 |
| **Granularity** | One per device type | One per device type | One per manufactured lot/batch |
| **Contains** | Specs, BOM, work instructions | Design inputs/outputs, reviews, V&V | Actual test data, signatures, dates |
| **Lifespan** | Living document | Frozen at design transfer | Retained ≥ 2 yrs post-distribution (or per device class) |
| **Audience** | Manufacturer / Auditor | Regulator / Designer | Auditor / Quality Engineer |
| **Created by** | Cross-functional team | R&D | Manufacturing & QA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU One-Liner:** **DMR = "How to build it." DHF = "How we designed it." DHR = "Proof we built this batch right."**

## 3.3 Worked Example — A Disposable Infusion Set

> **Scenario:** A Class IIa disposable infusion set is being transferred from R&D to manufacturing. The DMR must be assembled.

| DMR Section | Content |
|:--|:--|
| 1. Device Specs | 150 cm PVC tubing, ID = 3.0 mm, 15 µm filter, luer-lock per **ISO 80369-7** |
| 2. Production Process | Tubing extrusion → cutting → drip-chamber assembly → filter insertion → luer-lock moulding → EO sterilization |
| 3. QA Criteria | Leak test at 50 kPa for 30 s, AQL 0.65, Visual inspection per ISO 2859-1 |
| 4. Packaging/Label | Tyvek/paper pouch, GS1-128 barcode, UDI-DI, IFU in 4 EU languages |
| 5. Installation | N/A (disposable) |
| 6. Maintenance | N/A (disposable) |
| 7. Component ID | PVC resin (Supplier A, CoC #PB-2024-117), 15 µm filter (Supplier B) |
| 8. Design Changes | ECO-2024-007: Changed luer material from ABS to PP → DMR rev B |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Position of DMR in the Total Product Life Cycle (TPLC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Needs and Intended Use] --> B[Design Inputs DI]
    B --> C[Design Outputs DO]
    C --> D[Design Verification DV]
    D --> E[Design Validation DVaL]
    E --> F[Design Transfer]
    F --> G[Design History File DHF]
    F --> H[Device Master Record DMR]
    H --> I[Manufacturing Process]
    I --> J[Device History Record DHR per lot]
    J --> K[Distribution and Post Market]
    K --> L[Field Feedback and CAPA]
    L --> M[Design Change ECO]
    M --> C
    M --> H
    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1
    style F fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
    style H fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style J fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C
    style M fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The **DMR (green box)** is generated at the **Design Transfer** stage (yellow) and feeds into **Manufacturing**. The **DHR (orange)** is produced per manufacturing lot. Any post-market change (purple) re-triggers the **Design Output** loop and updates the DMR.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a DMR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DMR[Device Master Record DMR]
        S1[Element 1 Device Specifications]
        S2[Element 2 Production Process Specs]
        S3[Element 3 QA Procedures and Acceptance]
        S4[Element 4 Packaging and Labelling]
        S5[Element 5 Installation Procedures]
        S6[Element 6 Maintenance and Servicing]
        S7[Element 7 Component and Supplier ID]
        S8[Element 8 Design Change Documentation]
    end
    S1 -.-> S2
    S2 -.-> S3
    S3 -.-> S4
    S2 -.-> S5
    S2 -.-> S6
    S1 -.-> S7
    S8 -.-> S1
    S8 -.-> S2
    S8 -.-> S3
    S8 -.-> S4
    style S1 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1
    style S2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style S3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style S4 fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F
    style S5 fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92
    style S6 fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40
    style S7 fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E
    style S8 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows (→) show the **logical sequence** of authoring; dashed arrows (-.->) show the **cross-dependencies**. The **Design Change Log (Element 8)** is the only section that has arrows pointing *back* into every other section, reflecting its role in the change-control lifecycle.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — DMR ↔ DHF ↔ DHR Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DHF[Design History File DHF] -- DI DO and V and V --> DMR[Device Master Record DMR]
    DMR -- Specs and Work Instructions --> PROD[Manufacturing Shop Floor]
    PROD -- Actual Records --> DHR[Device History Record DHR]
    DHR -- Quality Data --> AUD[Regulatory Audit FDA NB]
    DHR -- Field Complaints --> CAPA[CAPA System]
    CAPA --> ECO[Engineering Change Order]
    ECO --> DHF
    ECO --> DMR
    style DHF fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C
    style DMR fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style DHR fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C
    style AUD fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17
    style CAPA fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The **DHF (purple)** provides the *design intent*; the **DMR (green)** translates it into *manufacturing instructions*; the **DHR (orange)** records *what actually happened*; the **audit body (yellow)** inspects the DHR; complaints feed **CAPA (red)** which closes the loop via an **ECO** that updates both DHF and DMR.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model]**

**Q1. Define the term "Device Master Record (DMR)" as per 21 CFR 820.181 and list any four of its mandatory elements.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Device Master Record (DMR)** is defined under **21 CFR 820.181** of the US FDA Quality System Regulation as a *compilation of records containing the procedures and specifications for a finished medical device*. (1 mark)

Four mandatory elements of the DMR are: (½ mark each)

1. **Device specifications** (e.g., drawings, material, performance specs).
2. **Production process specifications** (e.g., work instructions, process parameters).
3. **Quality assurance procedures and acceptance criteria** (e.g., AQL, test methods).
4. **Packaging and labelling specifications** (e.g., UDI, artwork, IFU).

---

**Q2. Distinguish between a Device Master Record (DMR) and a Device History Record (DHR).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | DMR | DHR |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Definition** | *How to manufacture* the device (specifications & procedures) | *Proof of manufacture* (records of what was actually done for a specific lot) |
| **Granularity** | One per device **type/model** (1 mark) | One per **manufacturing lot/batch** (1 mark) |
| **Regulatory reference** | 21 CFR 820.181 | 21 CFR 820.184 (1 mark) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **eight mandatory elements** of a Device Master Record (DMR) as specified by 21 CFR 820.181. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **relationship between the DMR, DHF, and DHR**, and explain how design changes are propagated through these three documents. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Eight Mandatory Elements of the DMR (7 marks — ⅞ ≈ 0.875 mark each, round to 1 mark for first 7 and 0 mark for 8th or distribute as 7×1):**

1. **Device Specifications** — Includes dimensional drawings, material specifications, electrical and mechanical ratings, software version (for SaMD), sterility level, biocompatibility classification (e.g., USP Class VI, ISO 10993). Each spec is traceable to a design input.

2. **Production Process Specifications** — Detailed work instructions for every manufacturing step: equipment used, process parameters (e.g., temperature, pressure, cycle time), in-process controls, and assembly sequences.

3. **Quality Assurance Procedures and Acceptance Criteria** — Test methods, sampling plans (AQL per ISO 2859-1), statistical process control (SPC) rules, pass/fail thresholds, and final-release criteria.

4. **Packaging and Labelling Specifications** — Artwork masters, primary/secondary packaging drawings, validated Sterile Barrier System (SBS), UDI carrier placement, and IFU in required languages.

5. **Installation Procedures** — Applicable for capital equipment (e.g., CT, MRI, defibrillators). Includes site preparation, electrical/mechanical hook-up drawings, and commissioning tests (IQ/OQ).

6. **Maintenance and Servicing Procedures** — Preventive-maintenance schedule, recommended spare parts list, calibration protocols, and service manuals.

7. **Identification of Components, Materials, and Suppliers** — Bill of Materials (BOM), Approved Supplier List (ASL), Certificates of Conformance (CoC), and material certificates.

8. **Design Change Documentation** — A register of all Engineering Change Orders (ECOs) that have affected the DMR, with re-verification evidence for each.

**(b) Relationship between DMR, DHF, and DHR, and Design-Change Propagation (7 marks):**

- **DHF (Design History File)** — Governed by **21 CFR 820.30(j)**, contains the *evidence* that the device was developed in accordance with the approved design plan: design inputs, design outputs, design review minutes, verification & validation reports, and risk-management file (ISO 14971). (2 marks)

- **DMR (Device Master Record)** — Governed by **21 CFR 820.181**, is the *recipe* generated *after* design transfer. It is *derived* from the design outputs in the DHF and contains the manufacturing instructions. (2 marks)

- **DHR (Device History Record)** — Governed by **21 CFR 820.184**, is the *batch-wise record* of actual production: dates, quantities, lot numbers of components, equipment used, in-process test results, signatures of operators, and final-release signatures. (1 mark)

- **Design-Change Propagation Flow (2 marks):**

$$\text{Post-Market Feedback / CAPA} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Engineering Change Order (ECO)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Update Design Output in DHF} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Re-verify/Validate} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Revise DMR} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Notify Manufacturing} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{New DHRs Reflect the Change}$$

This *closed-loop* ensures that every released device is always traceable to a *current, approved* DMR.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **position of the DMR in the medical-device design-control lifecycle** as per 21 CFR 820.30. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Class IIa sterile disposable syringe is to be released for manufacturing. Prepare a **sample DMR index** listing the eight mandatory sections, and for **any three sections** give **two representative documents/artifacts** that must be included. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Position of DMR in the Design-Control Lifecycle (7 marks):**

The design-control lifecycle as per **21 CFR 820.30** has the following sequential stages:

1. **Design and Development Planning** — Definition of design activities, responsibilities, and review/verification/validation stages. (1 mark)
2. **Design Input** — Physical, performance, regulatory, and user-need requirements. (1 mark)
3. **Design Output** — Drawings, specifications, BOM, software code, labelling. (1 mark)
4. **Design Review** — Formal, documented review at each phase. (1 mark)
5. **Design Verification (DV)** — *Does the design meet the design inputs?* (1 mark)
6. **Design Validation (DVaL)** — *Does the design meet the user needs and intended use?* (1 mark)
7. **Design Transfer** — **The DMR is generated HERE**, translating the verified & validated design into manufacturing instructions. (1 mark)
8. **Design Changes** — Controlled via the change-control process, with re-verification/re-validation as needed. (1 mark — can be combined with #7)

> The DMR is the *output* of the **Design Transfer** step and the *input* to **Manufacturing**, where each lot's actual data is captured in the **DHR**.

**(b) Sample DMR Index for a Sterile Disposable Syringe (7 marks — 2 marks for index, 5 marks for artefacts):**

**DMR Index (2 marks):**

1. Device Specifications
2. Production Process Specifications
3. Quality Assurance Procedures & Acceptance Criteria
4. Packaging & Labelling Specifications
5. Installation Procedures *(marked N/A for disposable)*
6. Maintenance & Servicing Procedures *(marked N/A for disposable)*
7. Identification of Components, Materials & Suppliers
8. Design Change Documentation

**Three sections — representative artefacts (5 marks ≈ 1.7 marks each):**

- **Section 1 — Device Specs:** (i) Engineering drawing of syringe barrel with GD&T tolerances; (ii) Material specification sheet for medical-grade polypropylene with **USP Class VI** compliance. (1.7 marks)
- **Section 2 — Production Process Specs:** (i) Injection-moulding work-instruction with parameters (melt temp, cycle time); (ii) Assembly sequence sheet including plunger-rod insertion torque. (1.7 marks)
- **Section 3 — QA Procedures:** (i) Leak-test protocol at 50 kPa for 30 s; (ii) Visual-inspection checklist per **ISO 2859-1**, AQL 0.65, General Inspection Level II. (1.6 marks)

> *(Examiner may accept any other valid combination — e.g., Section 4 packaging artefacts: Tyvek pouch drawing + UDI placement diagram.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing DMR with DHF or DHR** — This is the **#1 error**. Remember: *DMR = recipe, DHF = design journal, DHR = batch logbook.* Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the regulatory citation** — Always cite **21 CFR 820.181** for the DMR; do not write "FDA guidelines" vaguely.
> 3. **Listing fewer than 8 elements** — Examiners award partial credit per element; missing 2–3 elements can cost 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Mixing DMR and DHR content** — Do *not* put *actual lot data* into the DMR section of your answer; the DMR contains *procedures and acceptance criteria*, not *measured values*.
> 5. **No diagram in long answers** — A 14-mark answer without a block diagram/flowchart is treated as "incomplete" by KTU valuators and typically loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Skipping design-change propagation** — The *closed-loop* ECO → DHF → DMR → DHR flow is a favourite KTU question; missing it costs full sub-part marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- ✅ **DMR is defined by 21 CFR 820.181** — know the citation *exactly*.
- ✅ DMR has **8 mandatory elements** — recall them in order: *D-P-Q-P-I-M-S-D* (Device-spec, Production, QA, Packaging, Install, Maintain, Supplier, Design-change).
- ✅ DMR is **created at Design Transfer** — *after* design verification and validation; *before* first production lot.
- ✅ DMR is **device-type specific** (one per model), whereas **DHR is lot-specific** (one per batch).
- ✅ DMR does **not** contain *actual measured values* — only *acceptance criteria* and *procedures*.
- ✅ **DMR ≠ DHF ≠ DHR** — they are *three distinct, linked* records; examiners will test this triad.
- ✅ **DMR is a living document** — every design change triggers a controlled revision, with re-verification evidence.
- ✅ **UDI (Unique Device Identification)** is a mandatory element in modern packaging & labelling specs.
- ✅ **Sterile Barrier System (SBS) validation** is a *regulatory expectation* for sterile devices; its validation report must be referenced in the DMR.
- ✅ Capital equipment (e.g., MRI, CT) requires **Installation, Maintenance & Servicing sections**; disposables mark them **N/A**.
- ✅ **CAPA → ECO → DMR update → DHR reflection** is the closed-loop change-control flow expected in long answers.
- ✅ In KTU 2024 OBE framing, DMR touches **CO1, CO5, CO6, and CO12** — frame your answers accordingly.
- ✅ Mnemonic for element order: **"Doctor Please Quick, Pack Injection, Maintain Strict Documentation"**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design History Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design History Files

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Design History File (DHF)** is a formal, regulatory-mandated compilation of records that documents the complete design history of a medical device, tracing the device's development from initial concept through final commercial release and into post-market iterations.

As defined under **21 CFR 820.3(e)** of the U.S. FDA Quality System Regulation (QSR):

> *"Design History File (DHF) means a compilation of records which describes the design history of a finished device."*

The **21 CFR 820.30** clause (Design Controls) further mandates that manufacturers establish and maintain a DHF for each type of device to demonstrate that the design was developed following approved design plans and regulatory requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The DHF is **not** a single document — it is a structured *collection* of records, plans, specifications, reviews, verifications, validations, and change histories that collectively prove the device was designed in a controlled, traceable, and verifiable manner.

### International Equivalent

| Standard | Clause | Equivalent Term |
|----------|--------|-----------------|
| **FDA 21 CFR 820.3(e)** | U.S. QSR | Design History File (DHF) |
| **ISO 13485:2016** | Section 7.3.10 | Design and Development Files |
| **EU MDR 2017/745** | Annex II, Technical Documentation | Design Dossier |
| **IEC 62366-1** | Usability Engineering | Usability Engineering File (subset) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Device's Engineering Biography"

Imagine you are an investigative auditor arriving at a biomedical company ten years after a pacemaker was launched. A patient files a complaint, and you must reconstruct **every** design decision ever made. Without records, the company is paralyzed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The Engineering Passport:** A DHF is like a passport for a device. Every visa stamp (design review, risk analysis, verification test) is recorded so that, at any checkpoint in the device's life, regulators can verify its legitimate origin and journey.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The Legal Case File:** A lawyer builds a case file containing every evidence, witness statement, and legal argument. Similarly, a biomedical engineer builds a DHF containing every design input, output, review, test report, and change order. The "judge" (FDA / Notified Body) examines this file to decide if the device is safe and effective.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — The Recipe Book of a Master Chef:** A Michelin-star chef's recipe book contains not just the final recipe but every failed trial, taste-test feedback, ingredient substitution, and refinement. The DHF similarly captures the iterative journey, not just the final design.

## 1.3 Why the DHF Exists — The Regulatory & Engineering Rationale

The DHF was institutionalized after several medical device disasters (e.g., the **1976 Medical Device Amendments** following Dalkon Shield IUD failures). It serves three foundational purposes:

1. **Traceability:** Every design input must be traceable to a design output, and every output must be traceable to a verification/validation test.
2. **Accountability:** Every design decision has a documented rationale, owner, and date.
3. **Continuous Improvement:** Future design changes can reference historical decisions, preventing repeated mistakes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard:** The FDA mandates that the DHF must be **readily available and auditable** at the manufacturing site during any FDA inspection (Form 483 observations are routinely issued for incomplete DHFs).

---

## 1.4 Relationship Between DHF, DMR, and DHR

Students frequently confuse these three regulatory artifacts. The distinction is critical for the KTU 2024 examination.

| Artifact | Full Name | Purpose | Stage | Audience |
|----------|-----------|---------|-------|----------|
| **DHF** | Design History File | "How the device was designed" | Concept → Commercialization → Lifecycle | Regulators, internal teams |
| **DMR** | Device Master Record | "How to build the device" — finished device specifications, production methods, QA procedures | Post-design, before production | Manufacturing, QA, Suppliers |
| **DHR** | Device History Record | "How a specific unit was built" — batch/lot records | Per production lot | QA, regulators (post-market) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** The DHF describes the *design journey*. The DMR describes the *manufacturing recipe*. The DHR describes the *manufacturing execution* of a specific lot. Conflating them leads to audit findings and exam mark loss.

![Conceptual Relationship Matrix]

The **DHF ⊇ DMR** relationship: The DMR is *derived* from the DHF outputs, but the DHF additionally contains design plans, review records, and historical change orders that are not part of the DMR.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Design Control Framework (21 CFR 820.30)

The DHF is the *output* of executing the **Design Control** process. Understanding the design control workflow is essential because every element of the DHF maps directly to one or more of these design control activities.

### 2.1.1 The 7 Mandatory Design Control Elements (per 21 CFR 820.30)

1. **Design and Development Planning (820.30(a))** — Establishes the project plan, responsibilities, interfaces, and review schedules.
2. **Design Input (820.30(c))** — Physical and performance requirements of the device based on user needs, intended use, and regulatory requirements.
3. **Design Output (820.30(d))** — Final design specifications, drawings, software source code, labeling, and procedures.
4. **Design Review (820.30(e))** — Formal, documented, systematic reviews at defined stages.
5. **Design Verification (820.30(f))** — Confirmation that design outputs meet design inputs (e.g., bench testing).
6. **Design Validation (820.30(g))** — Confirmation that the device meets user needs and intended use (e.g., clinical simulation, usability testing).
7. **Design Transfer (820.30(h))** — Translation of design outputs into production specifications (DMR).
8. **Design Changes (820.30(i))** — Formal control and documentation of all design modifications.
9. **Design History File (820.30(j))** — The compilation itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic:** "**P-I-O-R-V-T-C-H**" → Plan, Input, Output, Review, Verification, Validation, Transfer, Changes, History.

## 2.2 The V-Model for Medical Device Design

The **V-Model** is the dominant engineering framework used to operationalize design controls and structure the DHF.

```
User Needs & Intended Use
         ↓
   Design Inputs
         ↓
   Design Outputs
         ↓
   Design Verification (right side up: ascending)
         ↓
   Design Validation
         ↓
   Production Transfer
```

The "V" shape arises because:
- **Left arm (descending):** Decomposition — User Needs → Design Inputs → Design Outputs
- **Bottom of V:** Implementation
- **Right arm (ascending):** Integration & Testing — Verification → Validation → Transfer

For each Design Input on the left, there must be a corresponding Verification Test on the right (this is the **traceability matrix** concept).

## 2.3 Detailed Contents of the DHF

The DHF must contain, at a minimum, the following elements. This is a **high-yield** section for KTU 2024 examinations.

| # | DHF Element | Source Clause | Content Examples |
|---|-------------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Design & Development Plan** | 21 CFR 820.30(a) | Project schedule, responsibilities, review milestones, risk management plan reference |
| 2 | **User Needs Document** | ISO 13485:2016 §7.3.3 | User research, use environment, patient population |
| 3 | **Design Inputs** | 21 CFR 820.30(c) | Performance specs, biocompatibility (ISO 10993), electrical safety (IEC 60601), sterility, ergonomics |
| 4 | **Design Outputs** | 21 CFR 820.30(d) | Engineering drawings, BOM, software architecture, labeling, packaging specs |
| 5 | **Design Review Records** | 21 CFR 820.30(e) | Minutes, attendees, decisions, action items, sign-offs |
| 6 | **Design Verification Records** | 21 CFR 820.30(f) | Test protocols, raw data, test reports, pass/fail criteria, deviations |
| 7 | **Design Validation Records** | 21 CFR 820.30(g) | Clinical evaluations, simulated-use studies, usability (IEC 62366), animal studies if applicable |
| 8 | **Risk Management File** | ISO 14971:2019 | Risk analysis (FMEA), risk evaluation, risk control measures, residual risk |
| 9 | **Design Transfer Records** | 21 CFR 820.30(h) | DMR, manufacturing procedure acceptance, supplier qualifications |
| 10 | **Design Change Records** | 21 CFR 820.30(i) | Change orders, impact analyses, re-verification/validation triggers |
| 11 | **Traceability Matrix** | Best Practice / FDA Guidance | Input → Output → Verification → Validation linkage |
| 12 | **Software Documentation** | IEC 62304 | SRS, SDS, code reviews, unit test reports (if applicable) |
| 13 | **Usability Engineering File** | IEC 62366-1 | Use specification, UOUP, UEA, summative testing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statute of Limitations (Practical):** The DHF must be retained for a period **at least equivalent to the design life of the device**, but **not less than 2 years after the last device release** (21 CFR 820.180(b)). For Class III devices, the retention period effectively becomes the lifetime of the device.

## 2.4 Design Verification vs. Design Validation — A Critical Distinction

This is the most frequently tested conceptual distinction in KTU biomedical examinations.

| Aspect | Design Verification | Design Validation |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Question Answered** | "Did we build the device **right**?" | "Did we build the **right** device?" |
| **Reference** | Design Inputs | User Needs & Intended Use |
| **Method** | Bench testing, calculations, software simulations | Clinical/simulated-use testing, animal studies, usability tests |
| **Timing** | After design outputs are produced | After design verification is complete, near production transfer |
| **Sample** | Engineering prototypes, test units | Production-equivalent units |
| **Standards** | IEC 60601-1, ISO 10993 (bench) | ISO 14155, IEC 62366-1, clinical evaluation |
| **Number of Tests** | One or more per input | One or more per user need |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** **V**erification = **V**s. **I**nputs (V → I) ; **V**alidation = **V**s. **N**eeds (V → N).

## 2.5 The Traceability Matrix (KTU High-Yield Topic)

A **Traceability Matrix** is a tabular document that links:

$$\text{User Need} \rightarrow \text{Design Input} \rightarrow \text{Design Output} \rightarrow \text{Verification Test} \rightarrow \text{Validation Test}$$

The matrix is a critical DHF element because it allows a regulator to pick *any* design input and trace it forward to validation, or pick *any* user need and trace it backward to design outputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FDA Inspection Reality:** Inspectors routinely select 3–5 design inputs randomly and ask the manufacturer to "trace these to verification and validation evidence." If the matrix is broken, the firm receives a **Form 483 Observation** under 21 CFR 820.30(g) (Design Validation) or 820.30(f) (Design Verification).

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition | Regulatory Reference | Key Constraint |
|---------|------------|----------------------|----------------|
| **DHF** | Compilation of design records | 21 CFR 820.3(e), 820.30(j) | Must be readily available for audit |
| **DMR** | Recipe for production | 21 CFR 820.181 | Must be complete before commercial distribution |
| **DHR** | Per-lot production record | 21 CFR 820.184 | Retained ≥ 2 years post-release |
| **Design Input** | Physical/performance requirement | 21 CFR 820.30(c) | Must be reviewed and approved |
| **Design Output** | Design result documents | 21 CFR 820.30(d) | Must be in terms that can be verified |
| **Verification** | Confirms output meets input | 21 CFR 820.30(f) | Done before validation |
| **Validation** | Confirms device meets user need | 21 CFR 820.30(g) | Done on production-equivalent units |
| **Design Review** | Documented systematic review | 21 CFR 820.30(e) | At defined stages, by independent parties |
| **Traceability** | Forward and backward links | FDA Guidance, ISO 14971 | Every input must be linked to verification |
| **DHF Retention** | Lifecycle of device | 21 CFR 820.180(b) | ≥ 2 years post last release |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DHF is not merely a regulatory burden — it provides **engineering value**:

1. **Knowledge Preservation:** When a design engineer leaves the company, the DHF preserves institutional knowledge.
2. **Change Impact Analysis:** When a supplier changes a raw material, the change control process references the DHF to assess impact.
3. **Defect Investigation:** Post-market complaints trigger CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action), which references the DHF to identify the design root cause.
4. **Platform Leverage:** A successful DHF from Device A becomes the template for Device B in a product family.
5. **Litigation Defense:** In product liability lawsuits, the DHF is the primary evidence of "state of the art" design practice.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & DHF Compilation Methodology

## 3.1 The DHF Compilation Algorithm — Step-by-Step

This section provides the exhaustive, step-by-step methodology for assembling a DHF. This is treated as a "derivation" of the DHF from first principles of regulatory engineering.

### Step 1: Initiate the Design Control Process

The DHF creation begins the moment a design project is officially chartered.

$$\text{Project Charter} \rightarrow \text{Design Control Activated} \rightarrow \text{DHF Index Opened}$$

The **Design and Development Plan** (21 CFR 820.30(a)) is the first document placed into the DHF. It defines:
- Project scope and objectives
- Design phases and milestones
- Responsibilities and interfaces
- Review schedule
- Risk management approach
- Verification & validation strategy

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** Without a plan, the DHF lacks a structural skeleton. The plan becomes Table of Contents (TOC) reference for the entire file.

### Step 2: Capture User Needs & Intended Use

The user needs are the "raw material" of medical device design. These are captured through:
- Voice of Customer (VoC) research
- Clinical literature reviews
- Regulatory pathway analysis
- Stakeholder interviews (clinicians, patients, payers)

**Intended Use Statement** example:

*"The XYZ Infusion Pump is intended for the controlled intravenous delivery of fluids and medications in hospital and clinical settings, by trained healthcare professionals, on adult and pediatric patients."*

This statement becomes the foundation for the **Use Specification** (IEC 62366-1) and the **Design Input** set.

### Step 3: Develop Design Inputs

Design inputs are the *engineering* translation of user needs. They are **measurable, testable, and unambiguous**.

**Example transformation chain:**

$$\text{User Need: "The pump should be easy to use."}$$

This user need is **not** directly testable. It must be decomposed into testable design inputs:

> Design Input DI-014: The pump programming interface shall be operable with fewer than **5 button presses** for standard infusion protocols.
> Design Input DI-015: The pump shall display all critical parameters (rate, volume, time) on a single screen view.
> Design Input DI-016: The pump shall provide auditory alarms at a minimum intensity of **65 dB(A)** at 1 meter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Rule:** Per FDA guidance, "Design inputs shall be appropriate to address the intended use of the device, including the needs of the user and patient." A design input is a *requirement* — it must be **objective and verifiable**.

### Step 4: Generate Design Outputs

Design outputs are the *physical and procedural realizations* of the design inputs. They include:
- Engineering drawings (CAD models)
- Bill of Materials (BOM)
- Software source code and architecture documents
- Labeling and IFU (Instructions for Use)
- Packaging specifications
- Sterilization procedures
- Inspection and test procedures

**Critical principle:** Each design output must be expressed in terms that allow it to be **verified** against the corresponding design input.

### Step 5: Conduct Design Reviews

Design reviews are **formal, documented, cross-functional meetings** held at defined milestones (typically: Concept, Design Freeze, Pre-Validation, Design Transfer).

$$\text{Design Review} = f(\text{Attendees}, \text{Inputs}, \text{Decisions}, \text{Action Items})$$

**Required attendees typically include:** Design engineering, Quality Assurance, Regulatory Affairs, Manufacturing, Clinical/Medical Affairs, Risk Management.

> [!WARNING]
> **Audit Trap:** A "meeting" without formal minutes, attendees, and action item closure is **not** a design review per 21 CFR 820.30(e). Inspectors routinely cite firms for "design reviews conducted without documentation."

### Step 6: Execute Design Verification

For each design input, a corresponding **verification test** must be conducted. The test results (raw data, photographs, signed reports) are filed in the DHF.

**Verification Test Protocol Structure:**
1. Test objective
2. References (input being verified)
3. Acceptance criteria (pass/fail thresholds)
4. Test equipment and calibration
5. Procedure
6. Results
7. Deviations and dispositions
8. Conclusion (Pass/Fail/Conditional)
9. Approvals

### Step 7: Execute Design Validation

Validation is performed on **production-equivalent units** under **simulated or actual use conditions**. The output includes:
- Clinical evaluation reports
- Human factors / usability test reports
- Animal study reports (if applicable)
- Biocompatibility test reports
- Software validation reports

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Point:** Validation must include **software validation** (if applicable, per FDA Guidance for Industry: "General Principles of Software Validation") and **usability validation** per IEC 62366-1.

### Step 8: Risk Management Integration

Throughout Steps 1–7, **ISO 14971:2019** risk management activities are continuously executed. The Risk Management File is a *parallel* DHF element that cross-references all other elements.

**Risk Traceability Loop:**

$$\text{Hazard} \rightarrow \text{Risk Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Risk Control} \rightarrow \text{Verification of Control} \rightarrow \text{Residual Risk Evaluation}$$

### Step 9: Design Transfer to Manufacturing

The DMR is generated by extracting relevant outputs from the DHF. Transfer activities include:
- Production process validation (IQ/OQ/PQ)
- Supplier qualification
- First Article Inspection
- Pilot production runs

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary between DHF and DMR:** The DHF continues to grow throughout the product lifecycle, but the **DMR freezes** at design transfer and only changes via formal change control.

### Step 10: Design Change Control

Every design change post-launch is processed through **Design Change Control** (21 CFR 820.30(i)). The change documentation includes:
- Change request and justification
- Impact analysis (risk, regulatory, labeling, training)
- Re-verification/re-validation requirements
- Approval signatures
- Updated DHF and DMR versions

## 3.2 The DHF vs. Technical File vs. Regulatory Submission

For international regulatory submissions, the DHF contents are repackaged differently:

| Region | Submission Format | Source |
|--------|-------------------|--------|
| **USA (FDA)** | 510(k) Premarket Notification or PMA | DHF |
| **EU (MDR)** | Technical Documentation (Annex II) | DHF + Risk Management File + Clinical Evaluation |
| **Canada (Health Canada)** | Medical Device License Application | DHF |
| **Japan (PMDA)** | Pre-Market Approval (Shin-shin) | DHF |
| **Global Harmonization** | GHTF STED (Summary Technical Documentation) | DHF-derived |

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — DHF for a Hypothetical Pulse Oximeter

Let us trace the DHF compilation for a fingertip pulse oximeter (Class II medical device, 510(k) pathway).

**User Need UN-001:** "The device shall allow home users to non-invasively measure blood oxygen saturation (SpO₂)."

**Design Input DI-001:** "The device shall measure SpO₂ in the range 70%–100% with accuracy of ±2% (ARMS) over the range 70%–100%."

**Design Output DO-001:** Optical sensor module with dual-wavelength LEDs (660 nm red, 940 nm infrared), photodetector, and signal processing firmware.

**Verification Test VT-001:** Bench calibration test on a Fluke Index 2 SpO₂ simulator at 7 saturation points (70%, 75%, 80%, 85%, 90%, 95%, 100%). Sample size n = 10 units.

**Acceptance Criterion:** ARMS ≤ 2% over the full range.

**Validation Activity VA-001:** Clinical validation study on 24 healthy volunteers (per FDA Pulse Oximeter Guidance), with controlled hypoxia protocol. Comparison to arterial blood gas (ABG) co-oximetry.

**Risk Control Link:** Identified risk of inaccurate SpO₂ in low perfusion → control measure DI-019 (signal quality indicator on display) → verification VT-008 (bench test under low perfusion) → validation VA-003 (clinical study subgroup analysis).

This entire chain is **traced bidirectionally** in the Traceability Matrix.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The following Mermaid block renders the complete design control workflow that produces the DHF.
