# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Design History File (DHF). Mention its regulatory basis.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Design History File (DHF)** is a regulatory-mandated compilation of records that describes the complete design history of a finished medical device, as defined in **21 CFR 820.3(e)** of the U.S. FDA Quality System Regulation.

The DHF is established and maintained under **21 CFR 820.30(j)** (Design Controls) and the equivalent **ISO 13485:2016 Section 7.3.10** (Design and Development Files).

The DHF contains, at a minimum, the design and development plan, design inputs, design outputs, design review records, verification and validation records, design transfer records, and design change records. The purpose of the DHF is to demonstrate to regulatory authorities that the device was designed in a controlled, traceable, and verifiable manner consistent with its intended use.

(3 marks)

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between Design Verification and Design Validation.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Design Verification | Design Validation |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Question** | Did we build the device **right**? | Did we build the **right** device? |
| **Reference** | Design Inputs | User Needs and Intended Use |
| **Stage** | After design outputs are produced | After verification, on production-equivalent units |
| **Method** | Bench tests, calculations, simulations | Clinical use, simulated use, usability tests |
| **Regulatory Clause** | 21 CFR 820.30(f) | 21 CFR 820.30(g) |

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): Design History File and its Compliance Role

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the purpose, scope, and key contents of a Design History File (DHF) as per FDA 21 CFR 820.30 and ISO 13485:2016. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the design control process in detail, and explain how the DHF is compiled through each stage. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Purpose, Scope, and Contents of the DHF**

**Purpose:**
- Demonstrate regulatory compliance of the design process.
- Provide traceable evidence of design decisions and their rationale.
- Support post-market investigations, CAPA, and design change control.
- Serve as a knowledge repository for future product generations.

**Scope:**
The DHF applies to all **Class II, III, and certain Class I** medical devices. It begins at the conceptual stage of design and is maintained throughout the product lifecycle, including post-market design changes.

**Key Contents (as per 21 CFR 820.30 and ISO 13485:2016 §7.3.10):**

1. **Design and Development Plan** (820.30(a)) — project scope, responsibilities, review schedules.
2. **User Needs Document** — clinical, patient, and stakeholder requirements.
3. **Design Inputs** (820.30(c)) — physical, performance, regulatory, and usability requirements.
4. **Design Outputs** (820.30(d)) — drawings, BOM, software code, labeling.
5. **Design Review Records** (820.30(e)) — formal minutes and decisions.
6. **Design Verification Records** (820.30(f)) — test protocols and reports confirming output meets input.
7. **Design Validation Records** (820.30(g)) — clinical, usability, and simulated-use evidence confirming device meets user needs.
8. **Design Transfer Records** (820.30(h)) — DMR and production transfer documentation.
9. **Design Change Records** (820.30(i)) — change orders, impact analyses.
10. **Risk Management File** (ISO 14971:2019) — FMEA, risk controls.
11. **Software Documentation** (IEC 62304) — for software-containing devices.
12. **Usability Engineering File** (IEC 62366-1).

(7 marks)

**(b) Design Control Process and DHF Compilation**

**Stage 1: Design Planning**
A formal Design and Development Plan is created. This plan defines the design phases, deliverables, responsibilities, review milestones, and interfaces. This plan is the first document filed in the DHF and serves as its structural index.

**Stage 2: Design Input Development**
User needs are captured through stakeholder engagement, clinical literature, and regulatory analysis. These are translated into **measurable, testable design inputs**. Each input is reviewed and approved. Approved inputs are filed in the DHF.

**Stage 3: Design Output Generation**
Design outputs are generated as engineering drawings, software code, BOM, labeling, and process specifications. Outputs must be expressed in verifiable terms. Outputs are reviewed and approved before verification.

**Stage 4: Design Review**
Formal cross-functional design reviews are conducted at concept, design freeze, and pre-transfer stages. Review records (minutes, attendees, decisions, action items) are filed in the DHF.

**Stage 5: Design Verification**
For each design input, verification tests are conducted. Test protocols, raw data, signed reports, and deviation dispositions are filed in the DHF. Verification confirms the design output meets the design input.

**Stage 6: Design Validation**
On production-equivalent units, validation activities (clinical, usability, animal studies) confirm the device meets user needs. Validation records are filed in the DHF.

**Stage 7: Design Transfer**
The DMR is generated from DHF outputs. Production process validation (IQ/OQ/PQ) and supplier qualification are completed and documented. Transfer records are filed in the DHF.

**Stage 8: Post-Market Design Changes**
Every design change is processed through change control. The change documentation (justification, impact analysis, re-verification/validation) is filed in the DHF, which continues to grow throughout the product lifecycle.

The DHF compilation is therefore not a one-time event but a **continuous documentation discipline** that grows with the device's lifecycle.

(7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Verification, Validation, and Traceability in the DHF

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the distinction between Design Verification and Design Validation, with reference to 21 CFR 820.30(f) and 820.30(g). Provide a biomedical device example illustrating both. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the concept of a Design Traceability Matrix. Construct a representative traceability chain for a hypothetical ECG monitor, showing user need, design input, design output, verification test, and validation activity. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Verification vs. Validation — Detailed Comparison**

**Design Verification (21 CFR 820.30(f))** confirms that **design outputs meet design inputs**. It is a *confirmation of conformance to specifications*. Methods include:
- Bench testing
- Engineering calculations
- Software unit testing
- Material characterization
- Dimensional inspection

**Design Validation (21 CFR 820.30(g))** confirms that the **device meets user needs and intended use**. It is *confirmation of fitness for purpose* under simulated or actual use conditions. Methods include:
- Clinical evaluation studies
- Simulated-use testing
- Usability / human factors testing (IEC 62366-1)
- Animal studies
- Biocompatibility testing (ISO 10993)

**Key Differences:**

| Parameter | Verification | Validation |
|-----------|--------------|------------|
| Reference | Design Inputs | User Needs |
| Sample | Engineering prototypes | Production-equivalent |
| Question | "Right device built?" (vs. inputs) | "Right device built?" (vs. needs) |
| Stage | Pre-validation | Post-verification |

**Biomedical Example — Automated External Defibrillator (AED):**

*Design Input:* "The AED shall deliver a biphasic defibrillation shock of 200 J ± 15% into a 50 Ω load."

*Verification Test:* Bench test on 5 units using a defibrillator analyzer. Measured energy at 200 J ± 10% across 25–175 Ω loads. **PASS**.

*User Need:* "A minimally trained lay rescuer shall be able to deliver a life-saving shock within 60 seconds of opening the AED case."

*Validation Activity:* Simulated-use study with 30 lay rescuers in a simulated cardiac arrest scenario. Mean time to first shock: 47 seconds; 28/30 (93%) delivered shock successfully. **PASS**.

(7 marks)

**(b) Design Traceability Matrix — Concept and Worked Example**

**Concept:**
A **Traceability Matrix** is a tabular document (typically an Excel or DOORS database) that establishes explicit, bidirectional links between user needs, design inputs, design outputs, verification tests, and validation activities. It is a **mandatory best practice** (though not explicitly required by 21 CFR 820.30) and is the principal tool used by FDA inspectors to assess design control completeness.

**Worked Example — Hypothetical Portable ECG Monitor:**

| ID | User Need (UN) | Design Input (DI) | Design Output (DO) | Verification Test (VT) | Validation Activity (VA) |
|----|----------------|--------------------|--------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | UN-01: The device shall enable clinicians to acquire a 12-lead ECG at the bedside | DI-01: Device shall support 10-lead cable with 12-lead output | DO-01: 10-lead patient cable assembly | VT-01: Lead impedance test on 5 units; all leads < 5 kΩ | VA-01: Clinical study with 20 patients; 12-lead ECG successfully acquired in 19/20 (95%) |
| 2 | UN-02: Display shall be readable under operating room lighting | DI-02: Display luminance ≥ 1000 cd/m² | DO-02: 7-inch IPS LCD, 1200 cd/m² | VT-02: Luminance meter measurement; 1180 cd/m² | VA-02: Usability test in OR-simulated environment; 18/20 clinicians rated legibility as "Good" or better |
| 3 | UN-03: Battery shall last ≥ 8 hours of continuous monitoring | DI-03: Battery runtime ≥ 8 hours at full load | DO-03: 7.2 V 6200 mAh Li-ion pack | VT-03: Continuous run test; runtime 8.6 hours | VA-03: Field evaluation at 3 hospitals; mean runtime 8.3 hours |
| 4 | UN-04: Device shall detect lead-off condition and alert user | DI-04: Lead-off alert within ≤ 3 seconds | DO-04: Lead-off detection firmware module | VT-04: Lead-disconnect test; alert at 1.8 ± 0.3 seconds | VA-04: Simulated-use test with 15 nurses; alert correctly identified in all 15 trials |
| 5 | UN-05: Patient data shall be protected against unauthorized access | DI-05: Password protection with role-based access | DO-05: Authentication firmware, AES-256 encryption | VT-05: Penetration testing; 0 vulnerabilities found in 1000 attempts | VA-05: Cybersecurity validation per FDA Guidance; no successful unauthorized access in 50 attempts |

**Bidirectional Trace:**
- **Forward:** UN-01 → DI-01 → DO-01 → VT-01 → VA-01 (every need is justified by validation).
- **Backward:** VA-01 → VT-01 → DO-01 → DI-01 → UN-01 (every validation is justified by an upstream input).

The traceability matrix is therefore the **central nervous system** of the DHF.

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating DHF with DMR:** Many students incorrectly state that the DHF is a "manufacturing document." The DHF describes the **design journey**; the DMR is the **manufacturing recipe**. Mixing them loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Verification with Validation:** A frequent error is using these terms interchangeably. The exam key specifically looks for the *"right device built right"* distinction.
> 3. **Omitting the Traceability Matrix:** When asked about DHF contents, students often list the documents but forget the **traceability matrix** that links them. This is a 1-mark differentiator.
> 4. **Forgetting the Risk Management File:** The ISO 14971 Risk Management File is a parallel mandatory DHF element, often overlooked in basic answers.
> 5. **Citing wrong clause numbers:** Always cite 21 CFR 820.30(j) for the DHF mandate and 820.3(e) for the definition. Wrong citations cost marks.
> 6. **Ignoring international alignment:** A high-scoring answer mentions ISO 13485:2016 §7.3.10 and EU MDR Annex II equivalence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Master These Before the Exam:**

- ✅ The **DHF** is defined in **21 CFR 820.3(e)** and mandated by **21 CFR 820.30(j)**.
- ✅ The DHF is a **compilation of records** — not a single document.
- ✅ The DHF is established and maintained **throughout the product lifecycle**.
- ✅ The **DHF ≠ DMR ≠ DHR**: DHF = design journey; DMR = manufacturing recipe; DHR = per-lot record.
- ✅ Design Controls consist of **9 elements**: Plan, Input, Output, Review, Verification, Validation, Transfer, Changes, History.
- ✅ **Verification** confirms output meets input; **Validation** confirms device meets user need.
- ✅ Verification comes **before** Validation; both must be documented in the DHF.
- ✅ The **Traceability Matrix** links User Need → Design Input → Design Output → Verification → Validation bidirectionally.
- ✅ **ISO 14971 Risk Management File** is a parallel mandatory DHF element.
- ✅ **IEC 62366-1 Usability Engineering File** is required for human factors validation.
- ✅ **IEC 62304 Software Documentation** is required for software-containing medical devices.
- ✅ DHF retention: **≥ 2 years post last release**, or longer for Class III devices.
- ✅ Design Reviews must be **formal, documented, cross-functional, and held at defined milestones**.
- ✅ The FDA routinely audits DHFs during inspections and issues **Form 483** observations for incomplete DHFs.
- ✅ The DHF serves engineering, regulatory, and **litigation defense** purposes — it is not just bureaucratic overhead.
- ✅ International equivalents: **ISO 13485:2016 §7.3.10**, **EU MDR 2017/745 Annex II**, **GHTF STED**.
- ✅ The **DMR is derived from the DHF** at design transfer — it does not exist independently of the DHF.
- ✅ **Class I devices** are largely exempt from formal design controls (with exceptions like automated blood pressure monitors, which require DHF).
- ✅ **Class II and Class III devices** require full DHF per 21 CFR 820.30.
- ✅ The DHF must be **readily available and auditable** at the manufacturing establishment.
- ✅ **Design changes** post-launch must be processed through formal Design Change Control and reflected in the DHF.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Design History Files**
> *These notes are aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. Master the conceptual distinctions (DHF/DMR/DHR, Verification/Validation) and the regulatory citation precision for highest marks.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and System FMEA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design and System FMEA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is FMEA in the Context of Biomedical Devices?

> [!NOTE]
> **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** is a structured, systematic, and proactive engineering methodology used to identify potential failure modes of a product or process, evaluate their effects, and prioritize corrective actions based on their severity, probability of occurrence, and detectability. In the context of **biomedical devices**, FMEA is a regulatory and ethical necessity, forming the backbone of risk management as mandated by **ISO 14971** and **IEC 62304**.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, FMEA is a **Risk Analysis Tool** that maps every conceivable way a device component, subsystem, or system can fail, the consequence of that failure on the patient/operator, and the likelihood that the failure will go undetected before harm occurs.

### 1.2 The Two Faces of FMEA in this Module

| FMEA Type | Focus | Application Stage | Primary Question Answered |
|---|---|---|---|
| **System FMEA (SFMEA)** | Interfaces, interactions, and inter-operability between subsystems of the entire device | Conceptual / Architecture Phase | *"What happens to the patient if the software crashes while the sensor is feeding bad data?"* |
| **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** | Specific component-level design decisions and their physical failure modes | Detailed Design Phase | *"Can the battery of this pacemaker overheat and rupture the titanium casing?"* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEEBT752 Module 2):** Students must be able to *distinguish* between System and Design FMEA, construct a working FMEA worksheet, compute the **RPN (Risk Priority Number)**, and apply it to a real biomedical device such as an **infusion pump, pacemaker, defibrillator, or ventilator**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Engine Service Checklist" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are a senior mechanic inspecting a Formula-1 car before a race. You don't wait for the engine to seize on the track. You methodically list *every* bolt, hose, and sensor:
> - **Failure Mode:** *"What could go wrong?"* → Coolant hose cracks.
> - **Effect:** *"What is the consequence?"* → Engine overheats, car loses power.
> - **Cause:** *"Why would it happen?"* → Heat cycling and material fatigue.
> - **Severity (S):** *"How bad is it?"* → 9/10 (engine destroyed).
> - **Occurrence (O):** *"How often?"* → 3/10 (rare, high-quality parts).
> - **Detectability (D):** *"Can we catch it pre-race?"* → 2/10 (visible hose wear).
> - **Action:** Replace hose, add thermal sensor with alarm.
>
> **FMEA is exactly this checklist — but for a device that sits inside a human body, where the "race track" is a beating heart, and the "engine" is a patient's life.** That is why KTU examiners weight biomedical FMEA questions so heavily: the cost of failure is biological, irreversible, and personal.

### 1.4 Why Biomedical FMEA is Non-Negotiable

> [!WARNING]
> The **USFDA (2016)** reported that **~33% of Class III device recalls** were rooted in design-phase failures that a properly executed FMEA could have predicted. In India, the **CDSCO (Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation)** and the **Medical Device Rules 2017** now require documented risk analysis for all Class B, C, and D devices — making FMEA literacy a **mandatory employability skill** for biomedical engineers graduating under KTU 2024.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of a FMEA Worksheet

Every FMEA document, whether System or Design level, is built on a standardized worksheet defined in the **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019)** and aligned with **ISO 14971:2019**. The columns are:

1. **Item / Function** — What the component or subsystem is supposed to *do*.
2. **Failure Mode** — The way in which the item fails to perform its intended function.
3. **Effect of Failure** — The consequence on the *next higher level* (for DFMEA) or on the *patient/system* (for SFMEA).
4. **Severity (S)** — 1 to 10 rating of how serious the effect is.
5. **Cause of Failure** — The root mechanism initiating the failure.
6. **Occurrence (O)** — 1 to 10 rating of how frequently the cause happens.
7. **Current Controls** — Design verification, validation, or in-process checks already in place.
8. **Detection (D)** — 1 to 10 rating of how likely the controls are to *miss* the failure (note: higher D = *worse* detectability).
9. **RPN** — Computed numeric risk metric.
10. **Recommended Action** — Mitigation step to reduce S, O, or D.

### 2.2 The Core FMEA Equation: Risk Priority Number (RPN)

The classical RPN is the product of three ordinal rating factors:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- $S \in \{1, 2, \dots, 10\}$ — Severity
- $O \in \{1, 2, \dots, 10\}$ — Occurrence
- $D \in \{1, 2, \dots, 10\}$ — Detection (higher = *harder* to detect)
- Therefore $\text{RPN}_{\max} = 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Update (AIAG-VDA 2019):** The classical RPN has been partially replaced by the **Action Priority (AP)** approach, which uses a **risk matrix** of $S \times O$ to assign **Low / Medium / High** AP rating. However, KTU 2024 examiners still expect students to compute and discuss the **classical RPN** alongside the matrix method.

### 2.3 Action Priority (AP) — The Modern Risk Matrix

The AP method evaluates risk as a 2D matrix (Severity × Occurrence) independent of Detection:

$$
\text{Risk Level} = f(S, O)
$$

A typical **biomedical risk matrix** (per **ISO 14971 Annex C** guidance) looks like this:

| | **O = 1 (Rare)** | **O = 3 (Low)** | **O = 5 (Moderate)** | **O = 7 (High)** | **O = 9 (Very High)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S = 10 (Catastrophic)** | M | H | H | H | H |
| **S = 8 (Serious)** | M | M | H | H | H |
| **S = 5 (Marginal)** | L | M | M | H | H |
| **S = 3 (Minor)** | L | L | M | M | H |
| **S = 1 (Negligible)** | L | L | L | M | M |

> L = Low AP, M = Medium AP, H = High AP

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Range | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S$ | Severity rating | 1 to 10 | Dimensionless ordinal |
| $O$ | Occurrence rating | 1 to 10 | Dimensionless ordinal |
| $D$ | Detection rating | 1 to 10 | Dimensionless ordinal (10 = undetectable) |
| $\text{RPN}$ | Risk Priority Number | 1 to 1000 | $S \times O \times D$ |
| $\text{RPN}_{\text{threshold}}$ | Action threshold | Typically **> 100** | KTU benchmark value |
| $\text{AP}$ | Action Priority | Low / Med / High | From $S \times O$ matrix |
| $\text{CF}$ | Criticality (for FMECA) | $S \times O$ | Alternative to RPN |
| $\text{Cm}$ | Mode Criticality | 1 to 10 | Per failure mode |
| $\text{MTBF}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours | Used to estimate $O$ |
| $\lambda(t)$ | Hazard rate | failures/hour | $\lambda = 1/\text{MTBF}$ |

> [!CAUTION]
> In markdown tables, **never** use the pipe symbol `\|` for absolute value or set notation. Use LaTeX commands like $\vert x \vert$ or $\{\,x\,\}$ instead, to avoid breaking the table parser.

### 2.5 Rating Tables Students MUST Memorise (for KTU 2024)

**Severity Scale (Biomedical context — adapted from IEC 62366 and ISO 14971):**

| Rating | Effect on Patient | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | Catastrophic — Death | Ventilator fails to deliver O₂ |
| 9 | Severe — Permanent injury | Pacemaker delivers lethal shock |
| 8 | Serious — Injury requiring intervention | Infusion pump over-dose |
| 5 | Moderate — Requires monitoring | Sensor drift, no alarm |
| 3 | Minor — Inconvenience | Display flicker |
| 1 | Negligible — No impact | Cosmetic blemish |

**Occurrence Scale (per AIAG-VDA):**

| Rating | Probability | Approx. Rate (per device) |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | Very high: failure almost inevitable | $\geq 1 \text{ in } 2$ |
| 7 | High | 1 in 100 |
| 5 | Moderate | 1 in 2,000 |
| 3 | Low | 1 in 100,000 |
| 1 | Remote: failure unlikely | $\leq 1 \text{ in } 1{,}500{,}000$ |

**Detection Scale (higher = worse):**

| Rating | Detectability | Verification Method |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | Absolute uncertainty | No control possible |
| 7 | Very low | Visual only |
| 4 | Moderate | Bench testing at subsystem level |
| 1 | Almost certain | Automatic self-test, watchdog, alarm |

### 2.6 System FMEA vs Design FMEA — The Critical Distinction

| Dimension | System FMEA (SFMEA) | Design FMEA (DFMEA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Whole device, software–hardware interaction, user–machine interface | A single component or sub-assembly |
| **Performed at** | Concept / architecture phase, *before* detailed CAD | Detailed design phase, *after* geometry/material chosen |
| **Failure mode granularity** | Functional (e.g., "alarm system fails to alert user") | Physical (e.g., "piezo buzzer diaphragm cracks") |
| **Customer is** | Patient, clinician, hospital | Sub-system / next assembly |
| **Key output** | Identify *systemic* hazards → inform risk management file | Identify *design* flaws → inform Design Verification plan |
| **Used by** | Systems engineering team | Component / design engineer |

> [!NOTE]
> A complete **Design History File (DHF)** required by USFDA **21 CFR 820.30** must contain *both* an SFMEA and a DFMEA. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction in Part-A 3-mark questions.

### 2.7 Real-World Biomedical Utility

- **Cardiac Pacemaker (Class III):** DFMEA on the lithium-iodine battery prevents catastrophic mid-life depletion.
- **Infusion Pump (Class II):** SFMEA catches software-hardware race conditions (e.g., "free-flow" bolus events).
- **MRI (Class II):** SFMEA identifies RF burn hazards for patients with conductive implants.
- **COVID-era Ventilators:** SFMEA was used to rapidly re-validate designs under accelerated regulatory pathways (USFDA EUA 2020).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of the RPN Logic

The RPN is **not** a probabilistic equation. It is a **heuristic product of ordinal scales**. Here is the formal logical derivation a KTU examiner expects:

**Step 1 — Define the risk of a failure mode $i$** as the joint contribution of three independent ordinal factors.

$$
R_i = f(S_i, O_i, D_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Assume multiplicative independence** (this is the foundational assumption of classical FMEA, published by **Ford Motor Company in 1977** and adopted by the US military in **MIL-STD-1629**).

$$
f(S, O, D) = S \times O \times D
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the ordinal ranges** $S, O, D \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 10\}$:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \cdot O \cdot D, \quad 1 \le \text{RPN} \le 1000
$$

**Step 4 — Define the action threshold** $T$. If $\text{RPN} \ge T$, the failure mode must be mitigated:

$$
\text{Action Required} = \begin{cases} \text{Yes} & \text{if } \text{RPN} \ge T \\ \text{No} & \text{if } \text{RPN} < T \end{cases}
$$

A typical KTU exam assumption is $T = 100$. This is a heuristic, not a regulatory number.

**Step 5 — Define the post-mitigation RPN** as $S' \cdot O' \cdot D'$. The percentage reduction is:

$$
\Delta\text{RPN} \,(\%) = \frac{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}} - \text{RPN}_{\text{after}}}{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}}} \times 100
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Infusion Pump Bolus Failure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A KTU-style 14-mark problem. You are performing a DFMEA on the **motor drive circuit of a syringe infusion pump**. A failure has been identified: *"Motor over-speeds, delivering a 5× normal bolus in <2 seconds."*
> Compute the RPN and recommend an action.

**Step 1 — Assign Severity.** Patient receives overdose; depending on drug, this can be **fatal** (e.g., insulin, potassium chloride).

$$
S = 10 \quad (\text{Catastrophic})
$$

**Step 2 — Assign Occurrence.** Historical pump recall data (USFDA MAUDE database) shows ~1 in 5,000 pumps exhibit this per year.

$$
O = 6 \quad (\text{Moderate-to-High})
$$

**Step 3 — Assign Detection.** The current design has *no* independent flow-rate watchdog; only the software loop monitors motor RPM, and software can have the same bug.

$$
D = 9 \quad (\text{Very low detectability})
$$

**Step 4 — Compute RPN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 10 \times 6 \times 9 \\
&= 540
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to threshold** $T = 100$:

$$
\text{RPN} = 540 \gg 100 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{MANDATORY ACTION}
$$

**Step 6 — Recommended Action:** Add a **hardware-based independent flow-rate sensor** (e.g., ultrasonic flow meter on the IV line) with redundant alarm circuitry.

**Step 7 — Recompute RPN after action** — the new flow sensor dramatically increases detectability:

$$
S' = 10, \quad O' = 6, \quad D' = 2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{after}} &= 10 \times 6 \times 2 = 120 \\
\Delta\text{RPN} \,(\%) &= \frac{540 - 120}{540} \times 100 \approx 77.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** They may also ask you to compute **RPN reduction per dollar of mitigation cost** to justify engineering economics. The metric is:

$$
\eta = \frac{\Delta\text{RPN}}{\text{Cost}_{\text{mitigation}}} \quad \left[\frac{\% \text{ reduction}}{\text{INR}}\right]
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mapping in a Pacemaker

| # | Subsystem | Failure Mode | Effect on Patient | S | Cause | O | Current Control | D | RPN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Li-I₂ Battery | Sudden voltage drop | Loss of pacing → bradycardia | **10** | Seal breach | 3 | Battery telemetry | 4 | 120 |
| 2 | Lead Electrode | Insulation fracture | Microshock to myocardium | **9** | Flexural fatigue | 5 | Impedance check | 6 | 270 |
| 3 | Pulse Generator | Incorrect pulse width | Inadequate capture | **8** | Crystal drift | 4 | Auto-capture algorithm | 3 | 96 |
| 4 | Software | Watchdog timer fails to reset | Device hang → no pacing | **10** | Stack overflow bug | 4 | Hardware WDT (separate IC) | 2 | 80 |
| 5 | Header Connector | Moisture ingress | Signal noise → mis-sensing | **7** | Seal aging | 6 | Visual inspection at implant | 5 | 210 |
| 6 | Telemetry Coil | RF interference | Loss of programming link | **4** | External EMI (MRI) | 7 | EMI filters | 4 | 112 |

> The RPN values immediately tell the engineer to prioritize **#2 (Lead insulation)** and **#5 (Header connector moisture)** for the next design iteration — exactly how FMEA drives design decisions in the medical device industry.

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: SFMEA vs DFMEA — Decision Tree

A common 14-mark KTU question asks: *"Given a problem statement, decide whether to use SFMEA or DFMEA."* Use this decision logic:

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases}
\text{Use SFMEA} & \text{if } (\text{Phase} = \text{Concept}) \;\lor\; (\text{Failure crosses subsystem boundaries}) \;\lor\; (\text{Human factors involved}) \\
\text{Use DFMEA} & \text{if } (\text{Phase} = \text{Detailed Design}) \;\land\; (\text{Single component under study}) \;\land\; (\text{Physical root cause})
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.5 Critical Limits & Validity Boundaries of RPN

The classical RPN has well-known limitations that KTU examiners love to test:

1. **Ordinal Multiplication Problem:** Multiplying ordinal numbers is mathematically dubious (you cannot multiply "high" by "moderate" and get a meaningful "unit").
2. **RPN Duplication:** Different $(S, O, D)$ triplets can give the same RPN (e.g., $5\times 2 \times 8 = 80$ and $4 \times 5 \times 4 = 80$).
3. **Detection Paradox:** Improving detection *raises* the score before mitigation, then *lowers* it after — confusing for novice engineers.
4. **Threshold Arbitrariness:** Why is $T = 100$ and not $T = 200$? The answer is "industry convention," not science.
5. **Mitigation Cost Ignored:** Two failures with the same RPN may have very different mitigation costs.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Part-B questions in Module 2 often ask: *"Critically evaluate the limitations of RPN."* Memorise the 5 points above. Marks are awarded for naming *and explaining* each.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The FMEA Workflow (System + Design)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Scope and Boundary] --> B[Identify Item or Subsystem]
    B --> C{Type of FMEA}
    C -- System Level --> D[SFMEA: List Functions and Interfaces]
    C -- Design Level --> E[DFMEA: List Components and Materials]
    D --> F[Identify Failure Modes]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Assign Severity S]
    G --> H[Identify Root Causes]
    H --> I[Assign Occurrence O]
    I --> J[Identify Current Detection Controls]
    J --> K[Assign Detection D]
    K --> L[Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    L --> M{RPN exceeds Threshold 100}
    M -- Yes --> N[Recommend Mitigation Action]
    M -- No --> O[Document and Monitor]
    N --> P[Implement Action and Re-rate]
    P --> L
    O --> Q[Update Risk Management File ISO 14971]
    Q --> R[End: Risk Acceptable]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Infusion Pump Subsystem Interaction (for SFMEA)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PatientLoop[Patient Interface Loop]
        P1[IV Bag] --> P2[IV Tubing]
        P2 --> P3[Patient Vein]
    end
    subgraph PumpCore[Pump Mechanism]
        A1[Microcontroller MCU] --> A2[Motor Driver]
        A2 --> A3[Stepper Motor]
        A3 --> A4[Lead Screw and Plunger]
        A4 --> A5[Syringe Barrel]
    end
    subgraph SensorsAndAlarm[Sensor and Alarm Subsystem]
        B1[Flow Rate Sensor] --> A1
        B2[Pressure Sensor] --> A1
        B3[Air in Line Detector] --> A1
        A1 --> B4[Audible Buzzer]
        A1 --> B5[Visual LED Display]
    end
    subgraph PowerAndUI[Power and User Interface]
        C1[AC Mains or Battery] --> A1
        C2[Keypad and Touchscreen] --> A1
    end
    A5 --> PatientLoop
    A1 --> PatientLoop
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use this diagram for KTU answers:** In a Part-B SFMEA question, point to *any* arrow crossing the boundaries of these four subsystems and discuss what failure could occur at that interface. For example, the arrow from `A1` to `B4` represents the **alarm signalling path** — a failure here is a classic FMEA case study (alarm silence due to firmware lockup).

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — RPN Risk Matrix Heatmap (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Failure Mode Identified] --> S1[Rate Severity S 1 to 10]
    S1 --> O1[Rate Occurrence O 1 to 10]
    O1 --> D1[Rate Detection D 1 to 10]
    D1 --> C1[Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    C1 --> Q1{RPN Greater Than 100}
    Q1 -- Yes --> X1[HIGH RISK: Mitigate Immediately]
    Q1 -- Between 50 and 100 --> X2[MEDIUM RISK: Schedule Action]
    Q1 -- Less Than 50 --> X3[LOW RISK: Document and Accept]
    X1 --> Y1[Re-evaluate after Action]
    X2 --> Y1
    X3 --> Z1[Archive in Risk File]
    Y1 --> C1
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — ISO 14971 Risk Management Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RM1[Intended Use and Hazard ID] --> RM2[Risk Analysis using SFMEA or DFMEA]
    RM2 --> RM3[Risk Evaluation against Acceptability]
    RM3 --> RM4{Risk Acceptable}
    RM4 -- No --> RM5[Implement Risk Control Measures]
    RM5 --> RM6[Re-evaluate Residual Risk]
    RM6 --> RM4
    RM4 -- Yes --> RM7[Risk Management Report]
    RM7 --> RM8[Production and Post-Market Surveillance]
    RM8 --> RM1
```

> This shows where **FMEA lives** in the regulatory lifecycle — it is the *engine* of the **Risk Analysis** box. KTU examiners reward students who can position FMEA correctly within this ISO 14971 cycle.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Differentiate between System FMEA and Design FMEA. List any three parameters of comparison.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **System FMEA (SFMEA)** is performed at the **conceptual/architecture phase** of a biomedical device and focuses on **functional failures at the interface between subsystems** (e.g., software–hardware, user–machine). Its primary customer is the **patient and clinician**, and the failure mode is described in **functional** terms.
>
> **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** is performed at the **detailed design phase** on a specific **component or sub-assembly**. It focuses on **physical failure mechanisms** (fatigue, corrosion, dielectric breakdown). Its primary customer is the **next-higher assembly**.
>
> **Three comparison parameters:**
>
> 1. **Scope:** SFMEA = whole system; DFMEA = single component.
> 2. **Phase:** SFMEA = concept; DFMEA = detailed design.
> 3. **Failure granularity:** SFMEA = functional; DFMEA = physical/mechanical.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Define Risk Priority Number (RPN). State the standard rating scale used for S, O, and D.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is a numerical scoring metric used in FMEA to rank the relative risk of identified failure modes. It is defined as:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- **$S$ = Severity** (1 to 10): how serious the effect is on the patient.
- **$O$ = Occurrence** (1 to 10): how frequently the failure cause happens.
- **$D$ = Detection** (1 to 10): how likely current controls are to *miss* the failure before harm (10 = absolute uncertainty).

RPN ranges from **1 to 1000**. A typical KTU action threshold is **RPN ≥ 100**.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

---

## **PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEEBT752 Module 2]`

**(a)** *Explain the step-by-step procedure for conducting a Design FMEA (DFMEA) on a biomedical device. Your answer must include the worksheet columns and the rating scales for Severity, Occurrence, and Detection.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A blood glucose monitor (Class II device) has the following failure data: Sensor strip gives a falsely low reading. Severity = 8 (patient delays insulin, may go into hyperglycaemic coma); Occurrence = 4; Detection = 7.*
   *(i) Calculate the RPN. (ii) State whether action is required. (iii) Suggest two engineering mitigations and recompute the new RPN.* **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Define the Scope and Boundary of Analysis (1 mark)**

The DFMEA team identifies the specific sub-assembly, the boundary of analysis (where the function starts and ends), the operating conditions, and the regulatory standard (typically **ISO 13485** + **ISO 14971**). For example, a DFMEA on a pacemaker *battery* has the scope: "from the anode terminal to the can-seal weld."

**Step 2: Construct the Block Diagram (1 mark)**

A hierarchical block diagram (P-diagram or boundary diagram) is drawn showing interfaces to neighbours. This prevents "scope creep" — analysing things that belong to the next assembly.

**Step 3: List Functions, Failure Modes, Effects, and Causes (2 marks)**

For every component, the team fills the worksheet columns:
- **Function:** "Provide 2.8 V open-circuit for 10 years."
- **Failure Mode:** "Voltage drops to 2.2 V within 3 years."
- **Effect:** "Loss of pacing → syncope."
- **Cause:** "Lithium-iodine electrolyte depletion / hermetic seal leak."

**Step 4: Rate Severity, Occurrence, Detection (2 marks)**

- **Severity (1–10)** based on effect on patient (per Table in §2.5).
- **Occurrence (1–10)** based on historical reliability data (e.g., MTBF, $\lambda$).
- **Detection (1–10)** based on existing design verification tests.

**Step 5: Compute RPN, Prioritise, Recommend Action, Re-rate (1 mark)**

Compute $\text{RPN} = S \cdot O \cdot D$. Apply the $T = 100$ threshold. For high-RPN modes, list the **Recommended Action**, owner, target date, and post-action re-rating.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Calculate RPN** — (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN} &= S \times O \times D \\
&= 8 \times 4 \times 7 \\
&= 224
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Action Decision** — (1 mark)

$$
\text{RPN} = 224 \gg 100 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{ACTION REQUIRED (High Priority)}
$$

**(iii) Engineering Mitigations and New RPN** — (4 marks)

**Mitigation 1 — Add a third-electrode "haematocrit compensation" sensor on the strip:** This dramatically increases detectability of a false-low error (the haematocrit is independently measured and cross-checked).

**Mitigation 2 — Add a clinical-decision algorithm in firmware that flags an inconsistent reading pattern** (e.g., sudden large drop in 30 s is flagged as "probable strip contamination" and a re-test is requested).

**New Ratings after Mitigation:**

- $S' = 8$ (severity unchanged — outcome is still serious if it occurs)
- $O' = 4$ (occurrence unchanged — strip chemistry is the same)
- $D' = 2$ (new sensor + algorithm give high detectability)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{after}} &= 8 \times 4 \times 2 = 64 \\
\Delta\text{RPN} \,(\%) &= \frac{224 - 64}{224} \times 100 \approx 71.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The mitigation reduces risk by ~71%, below the action threshold of 100. ✅

---

## **PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEEBT752 Module 2]`

**(a)** *Discuss the role of FMEA in biomedical device design as per ISO 14971. Illustrate with a System FMEA example for a defibrillator, listing at least four failure modes and their RPN values.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Critically evaluate five limitations of the classical RPN method. Mention the AIAG-VDA 2019 alternative approach.* **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Role of FMEA in ISO 14971 (2 marks):**

**ISO 14971:2019** (Medical devices — Application of risk management to medical devices) requires manufacturers to establish a **Risk Management Process** consisting of: (i) Risk Analysis, (ii) Risk Evaluation, (iii) Risk Control, (iv) Residual Risk Evaluation, and (v) Risk Management Review. FMEA is the *primary engineering tool* used in **Step (i) Risk Analysis** to systematically identify hazards, estimate severity, and prioritize controls. Without an FMEA, the **Risk Management File (RMF)** is incomplete and the device cannot receive **CE Marking** (EU MDR 2017/745) or **USFDA 510(k) clearance**.

**Defibrillator SFMEA — Four Failure Modes (5 marks):**

| # | Function | Failure Mode | Effect on Patient | S | Cause | O | Detection | D | RPN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Charge capacitor to 200 J in <7 s | Capacitor fails to charge | No shock delivered → VF persists → death | 10 | Aging electrolytic capacitor | 4 | Self-test at power-on | 3 | **120** |
| 2 | Deliver biphasic waveform | Monophasic delivery (firmware bug) | Reduced defibrillation efficacy | 9 | Software bit-flip in waveform table | 3 | Hardware waveform monitor | 6 | **162** |
| 3 | Sync to patient's R-wave | Failure to sync | Shock delivered on T-wave → VF induction | 10 | ECG lead-off detection failure | 5 | Impedance check | 4 | **200** |
| 4 | Voice prompt system | Silent / wrong language | User confusion, delayed therapy | 6 | Speaker failure | 6 | Audible beep at boot | 5 | **180** |

The highest RPNs (#3 sync failure, #4 prompt) drive the next design iteration.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Five Critical Limitations of Classical RPN** (1.2 marks each):

1. **Ordinal Multiplication Issue:** $S$, $O$, and $D$ are *ordinal* (rank) data, not interval or ratio data. Multiplying them ($8 \times 3 \times 5 = 120$) is mathematically meaningless — a "120 RPN" does not have physical units.

2. **RPN Duplication / Non-uniqueness:** Different triplets yield the same RPN. For example, $10 \times 1 \times 8 = 80$ and $4 \times 5 \times 4 = 80$, but the first is a *catastrophic rare undetected* event, while the second is a *moderate common semi-detected* event. The action plan should differ but RPN says they are equal.

3. **Detection Scale Counter-intuition:** Improving a *control* reduces $D$ (good), but during scoring, a high $D$ means the failure is hard to detect (bad). New engineers get confused; some FMEA tools now invert $D$ into "Detection Effectiveness."

4. **Threshold Arbitrariness:** The threshold of 100 (or any number) is a **convention, not a regulatory standard**. Two companies can have different thresholds for identical devices.

5. **Cost Ignored & Granularity Loss:** RPN does not account for **mitigation cost**, **probability of harm propagation**, or **detectability window** (how late the failure is caught). A high-RPN mode that costs ₹1 lakh to fix is treated equal to one costing ₹1 crore.

**AIAG-VDA 2019 Alternative (2 marks):**

The **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (1st Edition, June 2019)** introduced the **Action Priority (AP)** approach, which **de-emphasises RPN** and instead uses a **two-dimensional Severity × Occurrence matrix** to assign a **Low / Medium / High** priority. Detection is evaluated separately as a *confidence modifier*. This makes the FMEA output a *qualitative risk* rather than a misleading numeric score. KTU 2024 students should mention that both methods coexist in industry today.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in FMEA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $D$ scale direction** — stating "$D$ = 10 means easy detection" is **wrong**. $D = 10$ means *hard to detect*. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Forgetting units of RPN** — RPN has **no units**; it is dimensionless. Writing "120 units" is acceptable, but "120 risk units" is misleading.
> 3. **Skipping re-rating** — a 7-mark question that asks for mitigation *demands* a new RPN calculation. Skipping the recompute loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring ISO 14971 context** — biomedical FMEA is not generic automotive FMEA. Always cite the patient as the end-customer.
> 5. **Wrong threshold** — using $T = 50$ or $T = 200$ without justification loses marks. Justify any threshold you choose.
> 6. **Mixing SFMEA and DFMEA** — many students use component-level language in an SFMEA answer. KTU examiners look for "functional" verbs (e.g., "fails to deliver therapy") vs "physical" verbs (e.g., "solenoid coil burns out").

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Module 2 / FMEA**
>
> - **FMEA** = systematic method to identify, evaluate, and prioritize failure modes in a biomedical device.
> - **Two types:** **SFMEA** (system-level, conceptual phase, functional failures) and **DFMEA** (component-level, detailed design phase, physical failures).
> - **RPN Equation:** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$, range **1 to 1000**.
> - **Rating scales:** $S$, $O$, $D$ each from **1 to 10** (integer, ordinal).
> - **Action threshold** typical KTU assumption: $\text{RPN} \ge 100 \Rightarrow$ mandatory mitigation.
> - **Severity (S)** refers to the *consequence to the patient* — patient safety is the North Star.
> - **Occurrence (O)** refers to the *frequency of the cause* — derived from MTBF, field data, or expert judgement.
> - **Detection (D)** refers to the *probability the failure escapes existing controls* — **higher D = worse**.
> - **AIAG-VDA 2019:** Modern alternative is **Action Priority (AP)** using a **$S \times O$ matrix**, with $D$ as a *modifier*.
> - **5 limitations of RPN:** ordinal multiplication, RPN duplication, detection paradox, threshold arbitrariness, cost ignored.
> - **Regulatory anchor:** FMEA lives inside **ISO 14971 Risk Management** process; mandatory for **USFDA 510(k), CE Marking, and CDSCO Class B/C/D devices** in India.
> - **Biomedical examples to remember:** pacemaker battery, infusion pump over-bolus, glucose strip false-low, defibrillator sync failure, ventilator O₂ sensor drift.
> - **Mitigation goal:** reduce RPN to **< 100**, or shift AP to **Low**, with documented **residual risk acceptance** in the RMF.
> - **Always re-rate** after mitigation — a complete FMEA is a *living document* with **Action Taken** and **New RPN** columns.
> - **Memorise the worksheet columns** in order: Item → Failure Mode → Effect → S → Cause → O → Control → D → RPN → Action.
> - **Use "patient-first" language** in every KTU FMEA answer — the examiner is checking for biomedical context, not automotive.
> - **Real-world linkage:** USFDA 2016 data — **~33% of Class III recalls** were rooted in FMEA-able design failures.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardware Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Hardware Design # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biomedical Hardware Design** is the systematic engineering process of selecting, architecting, integrating, and validating the physical electronic subsystems — sensors, analog front-end (AFE) circuits, signal-conditioning blocks, data converters, microcontrollers/processors, power supplies, isolation barriers, and display units — that together form a **safe, reliable, and standards-compliant** medical device capable of acquiring, processing, and displaying physiological signals from the human body. The discipline operates at the intersection of **analog electronics**, **digital signal processing**, **embedded systems**, and **regulatory compliance (IEC 60601-1 family)**. A biomedical hardware engineer must balance three competing constraints simultaneously: 1. **Signal Fidelity** — preserving the diagnostic content of biosignals (e.g., ECG, EEG, EMG, PPG, SpO₂). 2. **Patient Safety** — preventing hazardous currents, ensuring defibrillation protection, and providing galvanic isolation. 3. **Miniaturization & Power Efficiency** — for wearable and implantable form factors. > [!NOTE] > Per the KTU 2024 scheme outcome mapping, this topic is anchored to **CO2: Design the hardware architecture of biomedical instruments** and aligns with Bloom's cognitive levels **Apply (L3) and Analyze (L4)**. ## 1.2 The Generic Biomedical Instrumentation Chain Every diagnostic or therapeutic biomedical device, regardless of its clinical application, follows a **canonical signal-flow chain**. This chain is the structural skeleton of hardware design: | Stage | Block | Function | Typical Components | |------|------|----------|--------------------| | 1 | **Bio-Sensor / Electrode** | Transduces physiological variable → electrical signal | Ag/AgCl ECG electrodes, thermistor, photodiode, strain gauge, piezoelectric crystal | | 2 | **Pre-amplifier** | Boosts µV–mV biosignals to workable level | Instrumentation amplifier (INA), low-noise op-amp | | 3 | **Isolation Amplifier** | Breaks ground loops; patient protection | ISO122, ADuM3151 (digital isolator) | | 4 | **Analog Filter** | Removes out-of-band noise & aliasing risk | Active RC filters (Butterworth, Bessel) | | 5 | **ADC** | Converts analog → digital | SAR, Sigma-Delta, 12–24 bit | | 6 | **Digital Processor** | DSP, feature extraction, control logic | ARM Cortex-M, DSP, FPGA | | 7 | **Display / Actuator** | User interface or therapeutic output | LCD/OLED, motor, defibrillator pulse | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Stethoscope of the 21st Century":** > Imagine the human body whispering vital information in a language the doctor cannot directly hear (microvolt-level electrical whispers on a noisy street of power-line interference, motion artefacts, and chemical noise). A biomedical hardware system acts like a **highly trained translator + sound engineer + bodyguard** combined: > - The **microphone (sensor)** listens to the whisper. > - The **amplifier** turns up the volume without distorting it. > - The **filter** removes traffic noise (50/60 Hz power-line hum). > - The **isolation barrier** is a one-way glass — the patient is protected from any electrical fault in the recording equipment. > - The **recorder/display** writes down (or shows) the message in a language the doctor can read. ## 1.4 Key Engineering Constants & Standard Metrics The hardware designer routinely interacts with these **standard biosignal amplitudes** (memorize them — they appear in almost every KTU numerical): | Biosignal | Amplitude Range | Bandwidth | Standard Body Location | |-----------|----------------|-----------|------------------------| | ECG (surface) | **0.5 – 5 mV** | **0.05 – 150 Hz** | Chest / limbs (10-lead) | | EEG (scalp) | **10 – 100 µV** | **0.5 – 100 Hz** | Scalp (10–20 system) | | EMG (surface) | **50 µV – 5 mV** | **20 – 500 Hz** | Over muscle belly | | EOG | **50 µV – 3.5 mV** | **DC – 50 Hz** | Around eyes | | Body Temp | **32 – 42 °C** | DC | Axillary / tympanic | | Blood Pressure | **10 – 400 mmHg** | DC – 40 Hz | Brachial / radial artery | | SpO₂ (PPG) | **AC: 1–5 % of DC** | **0.5 – 5 Hz** | Fingertip / earlobe | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Patient-Safety Current Limits (per IEC 60601-1):** > - **Leakage current (normal condition):** ≤ **100 µA** (BF type) / **10 µA** (CF type, cardiac) > - **Leakage current (single-fault):** ≤ **500 µA** (BF) / **50 µA** (CF) > - **Auxiliary current (CF, single-fault):** ≤ **50 µA** > - **Dielectric strength:** 1500 V AC isolation barrier mandatory between patient and mains. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Design Philosophy: The "Top-Down" Biomedical Hardware Design Flow A KTU-grade answer on hardware design should explicitly enumerate the **systematic design sequence**. The hardware architect proceeds in this canonical order: 1. **Specification Definition** — clinical purpose, target biosignal, accuracy, bandwidth, form factor. 2. **Architecture Selection** — discrete analog, mixed-signal, or fully digital front-end. 3. **Sensor & Electrode Selection** — based on biopotential type, contact impedance, motion artefact. 4. **Analog Front-End (AFE) Design** — gain, filtering, isolation. 5. **Power Supply & Battery Management** — low-noise LDO/linear regulators; Li-ion fuel gauge. 6. **Microcontroller / Processor Selection** — ADC resolution, MIPS, peripherals, real-time OS support. 7. **PCB Layout & Grounding** — star-ground, guard rings, 4-layer board for medical devices. 8. **EMI/EMC & Safety Compliance** — IEC 60601-1, IEC 60601-1-2. 9. **Verification, Validation & Bench Testing** — bench test with ECG/EEG simulator, defibrillation-proof test. ## 2.2 The "Big Six" Hardware Performance Metrics Every biomedical hardware design is judged on these six metrics. The KTU examiner loves testing these quantitatively. ### 2.2.1 Gain (A) - Ratio of output signal amplitude to input signal amplitude. - For biopotentials, total gain typically required: **100 – 10000** to bring µV–mV signals up to 0.5–5 V ADC range. - $A = \dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{V_{\text{in}}}$ ### 2.2.2 Bandwidth (BW) - Range of frequencies the system passes without attenuation. - For ECG: BW = **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** (diagnostic) or **0.5 Hz to 40 Hz** (monitor). - $-3\text{dB cutoff} = \dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{\sqrt{2}}$ ### 2.2.3 Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) - The ability to reject signals common to both inputs while amplifying differences. - $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{c}}\right)$ - For ECG INA: **CMRR ≥ 90 dB at 50 Hz** (mains rejection). ### 2.2.4 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) - $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ - For diagnostic ECG: **SNR ≥ 40 dB**. ### 2.2.5 Input Impedance ($Z_{\text{in}}$) - Should be **≥ 10× the source (electrode-skin) impedance** to prevent signal loss. - Typical: $Z_{\text{in}} \geq 10\text{ M}\Omega$ at signal frequency. ### 2.2.6 Power Consumption & Battery Life - Critical for portable/wearable: target **< 50 mW** total, battery life **≥ 24 hours**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the formulas below. They cover ~85% of all KTU hardware design numericals.** | # | Formula | Description | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---------|-------------|----------------------| | 1 | $A_{d} = 1 + \dfrac{2R_{1}}{R_{\text{gain}}}$ | Three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier differential gain | 1 to 1000 (set by $R_{\text{gain}}$) | | 2 | $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log \vert A_{d} / A_{c}\vert$ | Common-mode rejection ratio | 80 – 120 dB | | 3 | $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | First-order RC low-pass or high-pass cutoff | Hz | | 4 | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{c})^{2n}}}$ | Nth-order Butterworth low-pass magnitude | — | | 5 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log \vert V_{s}/V_{n}\vert$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB | | 6 | $V_{n,\text{total}} = \sqrt{V_{n,\text{thermal}}^{2} + V_{n,\text{shot}}^{2} + V_{n,\text{flicker}}^{2}}$ | Total noise (RMS) | V (RMS) | | 7 | $V_{n,\text{thermal}} = \sqrt{4 k_{B} T R \Delta f}$ | Johnson-Nyquist noise | V/√Hz | | 8 | $f_{\text{Nyquist}} = \dfrac{f_{s}}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2 T_{s}}$ | Minimum sampling rate (Nyquist) | Hz | | 9 | $\text{ENOB} = \dfrac{\text{SINAD}_{\text{dB}} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Effective number of bits of ADC | bits | | 10 | $I_{\text{leak}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{mains}}}{Z_{\text{isolation}}}$ | Patient leakage current | µA | | 11 | $\Delta t_{\text{acq}} = N_{\text{samples}} \times T_{s}$ | Total acquisition time per frame | seconds | | 12 | $\text{PSRR} = 20 \log \vert \Delta V_{\text{dd}} / \Delta V_{\text{out}}\vert$ | Power supply rejection ratio | dB | | 13 | $G_{\text{total}} = G_{1} \cdot G_{2} \cdot G_{3} \cdots$ | Cascade of stages (dB sum) | linear or dB | | 14 | $R_{\text{skin}} \approx 10\text{ k}\Omega$ (gel) – $100\text{ k}\Omega$ (dry) | Electrode-skin impedance | Ω | > **Physical constants you will use:** > - $k_{B}$ (Boltzmann) = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** > - $T$ (body temp) = **310 K (37 °C)** > - Standard ECG bandwidth = **0.05 – 150 Hz** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The hardware design principles covered here are not academic exercises — they are the **literal engineering rules** behind: - **Patient monitors** in ICUs (Mindray, Philips IntelliVue) - **Holter ECG recorders** for 24-h ambulatory cardiac monitoring - **Wearable fitness bands** (Apple Watch, Fitbit — PPG-based) - **Implantable pacemakers & ICDs** (Medtronic, Boston Scientific) - **EEG headsets** for seizure detection (Emotiv, NeuroSky) - **Defibrillators** (biphasic, with patient isolation up to 5000 V) - **Infusion pumps** (precise motor + sensor + safety interlocks) A KTU project that demonstrates mastery of these design rules — for example, designing a 2-lead ECG front-end with ≥ 90 dB CMRR and 0.05–150 Hz bandwidth — is essentially a **miniature clone of industrial medical hardware**, and is the most efficient way to score full marks in design-oriented viva questions. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is shown in full. No step is skipped. ## 3.1 Derivation: Differential Gain of the 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier (IA) The instrumentation amplifier is the **single most important building block** of biomedical hardware. Almost every ECG, EEG, EMG, and EOG front-end uses a variant of it. We derive its gain from first principles. ### 3.1.1 Circuit Topology (Assumed) The 3-op-amp IA consists of: - **Input stage**: Two op-amps ($A_1$, $A_2$) with a gain-setting resistor $R_{\text{gain}}$ between their inverting inputs, and two feedback resistors $R_1$ on each op-amp. - **Output stage**: A unity-gain difference amplifier built from $A_3$ with four matched resistors $R_2, R_2, R_3, R_3$. Let $V_{1}$ and $V_{2}$ be the differential inputs. The output is $V_{\text{out}}$. ### 3.1.2 Deriving the Input-Stage Transfer Function Let $V_{x}$ be the voltage at the inverting input of $A_1$ and $V_{y}$ the inverting input of $A_2$. By the virtual-short assumption of ideal op-amps: $$V_{x} = V_{1} \quad \text{and} \quad V_{y} = V_{2}$$ Current through the gain-setting resistor $R_{\text{gain}}$ is the same current that flows through both $R_1$ resistors (KCL on the inverting node of $A_1$ and $A_2$): $$I_{\text{gain}} = \dfrac{V_{x} - V_{y}}{R_{\text{gain}}} = \dfrac{V_{1} - V_{2}}{R_{\text{gain}}}$$ The output of the input stage from $A_1$ (call it $V_{a}$) is: $$V_{a} = V_{1} - I_{\text{gain}} \cdot R_{1} = V_{1} - R_{1} \cdot \dfrac{(V_{1} - V_{2})}{R_{\text{gain}}}$$ Similarly, output of $A_2$ (call it $V_{b}$): $$V_{b} = V_{2} + I_{\text{gain}} \cdot R_{1} = V_{2} + R_{1} \cdot \dfrac{(V_{1} - V_{2})}{R_{\text{gain}}}$$ Subtracting: $$V_{a} - V_{b} = (V_{1} - V_{2}) - \dfrac{2 R_{1}}{R_{\text{gain}}} (V_{1} - V_{2})$$ $$V_{a} - V_{b} = (V_{1} - V_{2}) \left( 1 + \dfrac{2 R_{1}}{R_{\text{gain}}} \right)$$ So the **differential voltage gain of the input stage** is: $$A_{d,\text{input}} = 1 + \dfrac{2 R_{1}}{R_{\text{gain}}}$$ ### 3.1.3 Output Stage Difference Amplifier The output stage is a standard difference amplifier. With matched resistors ($R_2$ for both input paths and $R_3$ for both feedback/ground paths), its transfer function is: $$V_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{R_{3}}{R_{2}} (V_{b} - V_{a})$$ Note the sign convention — the input stage inverts polarity in some texts; here we define $V_{\text{out}}$ as proportional to $(V_2 - V_1)$ or vice versa depending on wiring. The **magnitude** of the output stage gain is: $$A_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{R_{3}}{R_{2}}$$ ### 3.1.4 Total Differential Gain Multiplying the two stages: $$A_{d,\text{total}} = \left( 1 + \dfrac{2 R_{1}}{R_{\text{gain}}} \right) \cdot \dfrac{R_{3}}{R_{2}}$$ In the **classic medical IA** (e.g., AD620, INA128), $R_1$ is internal and $R_2 = R_3$, so the output stage has unity gain: $$\boxed{\, A_{d,\text{INSTRUMENTATION}} = 1 + \dfrac{2 R_{1}}{R_{\text{gain}}} \,}$$ **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** If $R_1 = 25\text{ k}\Omega$ (internal, as in AD620) and we need a total gain of 100, find $R_{\text{gain}}$: $$100 = 1 + \dfrac{2 \times 25000}{R_{\text{gain}}}$$ $$R_{\text{gain}} = \dfrac{50000}{99} \approx 505\text{ }\Omega$$ We typically select $R_{\text{gain}} = 499\text{ }\Omega$ (E48 standard 1% value). ## 3.2 Derivation: CMRR of the Instrumentation Amplifier The CMRR quantifies how well the IA rejects common-mode signals such as the 50 Hz mains interference that appears equally on both ECG electrodes. Let $A_d$ = differential gain, $A_c$ = common-mode gain. The output voltage for a general input $V_1$, $V_2$ is: $$V_{\text{out}} = A_d (V_1 - V_2) + A_c \left( \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2} \right)$$ In terms of common-mode and differential components: $$V_{\text{diff}} = V_1 - V_2, \quad V_{\text{cm}} = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$ $$V_{\text{out}} = A_d \cdot V_{\text{diff}} + A_c \cdot V_{\text{cm}}$$ CMRR in dB: $$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert$$ **Application:** For an ECG front-end: - $A_d = 1000$ (typical for ECG) - Required $A_c$ such that mains interference (assumed $V_{\text{cm}} = 1$ V at 50 Hz) is reduced below the ECG signal level (1 mV). We require $A_c \cdot V_{\text{cm}} < 0.01 \cdot A_d \cdot V_{\text{diff,max}}$, so: $$A_c < \dfrac{0.01 \cdot 1000 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3}}{1} = 0.05$$ $$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log \left( \dfrac{1000}{0.05} \right) = 20 \log (20000) \approx 86 \text{ dB}$$ Thus, **CMRR ≥ 86 dB is the minimum acceptable** for clinical-grade ECG. ## 3.3 Derivation: First-Order Active Low-Pass Filter (Anti-Aliasing Filter) The anti-aliasing filter sits immediately before the ADC. Its cutoff is set at the Nyquist frequency. Transfer function of an inverting active LPF with op-amp: $$H(s) = -\dfrac{R_{f}}{R_{\text{in}}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + s R_{f} C}$$ DC gain: $A_0 = -R_f / R_{\text{in}}$ Cutoff frequency: $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{f} C}$ Magnitude response: $$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{R_{f}/R_{\text{in}}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{c})^{2}}}$$ **Numerical Example (Anti-Aliasing for ECG):** Sampling rate $f_s = 500$ Hz → Nyquist $f_N = 250$ Hz. Choose $f_c = 200$ Hz to leave a 50 Hz guard band. Choose $C = 100$ nF: $$R_{f} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f_{c} C} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 200 \times 100 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$R_{f} = \dfrac{1}{1.2566 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 7.958 \text{ k}\Omega$$ Select standard value $R_f = 7.87\text{ k}\Omega$ (E96 1%) or 8.06 kΩ. For unity passband gain: $R_{\text{in}} = R_f \approx 7.87\text{ k}\Omega$. ## 3.4 Derivation: Thermal Noise of the Front-End Resistor A common KTU numerical: find the RMS thermal noise across an electrode's source resistance. Johnson-Nyquist equation: $$V_{n,\text{thermal}} = \sqrt{4 k_{B} T R \Delta f}$$ For a typical ECG electrode $R = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $T = 310$ K, $\Delta f = 150$ Hz: $$V_{n} = \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 310 \times 10^{4} \times 150}$$ $$V_{n} = \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 310 \times 1.5 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$V_{n} = \sqrt{2.567 \times 10^{-14}}$$ $$V_{n} \approx 1.60 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V} = 160 \text{ nV (RMS)}$$ This noise (160 nV RMS) sets the **noise floor** of the ECG front-end. The ECG R-wave (1 mV) thus has an SNR of: $$\text{SNR} = 20 \log \left( \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1.6 \times 10^{-7}} \right) = 20 \log (6250) \approx 76 \text{ dB}$$ ## 3.5 Nyquist Sampling Theorem Applied to ECG Minimum sampling rate for faithful ECG reconstruction (highest frequency 150 Hz): $$f_{s,\text{min}} = 2 \times 150 = 300 \text{ Hz}$$ In practice, oversampling at $f_s = 500$ Hz – 1 kHz is standard (clinical ECG, IEC 60601-2-25 requires 500 Hz minimum for diagnostic ECG). # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define CMRR. State the minimum CMRR requirement for a clinical ECG amplifier. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** The **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** is the ratio of differential-mode gain ($A_d$) to common-mode gain ($A_c$) of a differential amplifier, expressed in decibels: $$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert \dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{c}} \right\vert$$ It quantifies the amplifier's ability to reject signals (such as 50 Hz mains interference) that appear simultaneously and with equal magnitude on both input terminals. For a clinical-grade **ECG amplifier**, the minimum acceptable CMRR is **≥ 90 dB at 50 Hz** (per IEC 60601-2-25). This high CMRR is essential because the differential ECG signal is only ~1 mV, while common-mode mains pickup can be several volts. --- ### Question 2: Why is an instrumentation amplifier preferred over a simple difference amplifier for ECG signal acquisition? [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Model Answer:** An **instrumentation amplifier (IA)** is preferred over a simple difference amplifier for ECG for three reasons: 1. **Very high input impedance (≥ 10 GΩ)** — the IA's two non-inverting input buffers draw negligible current from the high-impedance electrode-skin interface (which can be 10–100 kΩ), preventing signal attenuation. 2. **Single-resistor gain adjustment** — the differential gain can be set from 1 to 1000 using a single external resistor, $A_d = 1 + 2R_1 / R_{\text{gain}}$, simplifying calibration and allowing different gain settings for ECG, EMG, and EEG using the same chip family. 3. **High CMRR (≥ 90 dB) independent of source impedance mismatch** — the symmetric input stage tolerates up to ±5% mismatch in the two electrode impedances without degrading CMRR, which is critical in real clinical settings. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must answer **either** OR-type (full 14 marks for one question) or sub-part choice as per the question paper. ### Question A (Choice 1): Design a 2-Lead ECG Front-End Hardware **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Draw the complete block diagram of a 2-lead ECG hardware system and explain the function of each block. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** The 2-lead ECG hardware consists of the following cascaded blocks: 1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl):** Three electrodes (RA, LA, RL) form Einthoven's triangle. The RL serves as the right-leg drive reference. 2. **Patient Protection Network:** A combination of gas-discharge tube, Zener diodes (5.6 V), and 10 kΩ current-limiting resistors. This protects the patient from defibrillator pulses (5 kV, several amps) and the device from ESD. 3. **Pre-Amplifier (Instrumentation Amplifier, e.g., AD620):** Provides differential gain of 10, amplifies the differential ECG (1 mV) to 10 mV, rejects common-mode 50 Hz noise with CMRR ≥ 100 dB. 4. **Right-Leg Drive (RLD) Circuit:** An auxiliary op-amp inverts the common-mode voltage and drives it back into the right-leg electrode. This **negative feedback loop** forces the patient's body potential to track the amplifier reference, reducing common-mode interference by another 20–40 dB. 5. **High-Pass Filter (0.05 Hz, 1st order):** Removes DC electrode offset (±300 mV) and baseline wander. 6. **Main Amplifier (Gain = 100):** Further amplification to bring ECG up to 1 V peak for ADC. 7. **Low-Pass Filter (150 Hz, 2nd order Butterworth):** Removes high-frequency noise and acts as anti-aliasing filter. 8. **Notch Filter (50 Hz, Q = 4):** Rejects residual mains hum by ≥ 40 dB. 9. **ADC (16-bit, 500 Hz sampling):** Converts analog ECG to digital. Resolution: 5 V / 2¹⁶ ≈ 76 µV/LSB, well below the desired signal resolution. 10. **Isolation Barrier (ADuM3151):** Digital isolator providing 5 kV isolation between patient side and the host PC/display. Compliant with IEC 60601-1. 11. **DSP / Microcontroller (ARM Cortex-M4):** Performs digital filtering, R-peak detection (Pan-Tompkins), heart-rate computation, and LCD/USB output. **Block Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[3 Ag/AgCl Electrodes RA LA RL] --> B[Protection Network Zener + Fuse] B --> C[Instrumentation Amplifier AD620 Gain 10] C --> D[Right Leg Drive Loop] D --> E[High Pass Filter 0.05 Hz] E --> F[Gain Stage Gain 100] F --> G[Low Pass Filter 150 Hz] G --> H[Notch Filter 50 Hz] H --> I[Isolation Barrier 5 kV] I --> J[16 bit ADC 500 Hz] J --> K[DSP ARM Cortex M4] K --> L1[LCD Display] K --> L2[USB Output to PC] ``` --- **(b)** Design a 2nd-order active Butterworth low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c = 150$ Hz for the anti-aliasing stage. Compute component values. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** A 2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter uses a **Sallen-Key topology** with two capacitors and two resistors. The Butterworth response is chosen for maximally flat passband (no ripple). **Step 1 — Design Equations:** For a Sallen-Key LPF with equal-component design ($C_1 = C_2 = C$, $R_1 = R_2 = R$): $$f_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$$ For Butterworth (Q = 0.707), the gain of the non-inverting configuration is $K = 1.586$ (since $K = 3 - 1/Q$). **Step 2 — Choose Capacitor:** Select $C_1 = C_2 = 100$ nF (standard, available in medical-grade film/ceramic). **Step 3 — Compute Resistor:** $$R = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f_c C} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 150 \times 100 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$R = \dfrac{1}{9.4248 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 10.61 \text{ k}\Omega$$ Select standard E96 value **$R_1 = R_2 = 10.5 \text{ k}\Omega$ (1% tolerance)**. **Step 4 — Feedback Network for Q = 0.707:** For a Sallen-Key equal-component Butterworth: $$R_a / R_b = 2 - 1/Q = 2 - 1/0.707 \approx 0.586$$ Choose $R_b = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, then $R_a \approx 5.86 \text{ k}\Omega$. Use E96 value **$R_a = 5.90 \text{ k}\Omega$**. **Step 5 — Verify Magnitude at 150 Hz:** $$\vert H(jf_c) \vert = \dfrac{1.586}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^{4}}} = \dfrac{1.586}{\sqrt{2}} = 1.121 \text{ (i.e., } -3 \text{ dB)}$$ At 300 Hz (2× cutoff): $\vert H \vert = 1.586 / \sqrt{1 + 16} = 1.586 / \sqrt{17} \approx 0.385$ (≈ −12.3 dB), confirming 2nd-order roll-off. **Final component list:** | Component | Value | Tolerance | |-----------|-------|-----------| | $C_1, C_2$ | 100 nF | 5% | | $R_1, R_2$ | 10.5 kΩ | 1% | | $R_a$ | 5.90 kΩ | 1% | | $R_b$ | 10.0 kΩ | 1% | | Op-amp | OPA2227 (low-noise) | — | --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative Question): Design a Defibrillation-Protected Patient Monitor Front-End **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain with a circuit diagram how an ECG front-end is protected against defibrillator pulses. Why is the input current-limiting resistor value critical? **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** A defibrillator delivers a **monophasic or biphasic pulse of 5 kV and up to 50 A peak** for ~5 ms. This pulse, if not blocked, will destroy the ECG input stage and burn the patient. The protection network consists of three coordinated stages: 1. **External Series Resistor ($R_{\text{ext}} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, 1 W):** Limits the peak current entering the front-end during the defibrillation pulse. 2. **Gas-Discharge Tube (GDT, 90 V):** Crowbars any voltage above 90 V to ground in < 1 µs. This shunts the bulk of the defibrillation energy. 3. **Zener Diode Pair (5.6 V, back-to-back, low capacitance):** Clamps the residual voltage at the IA input to ±5.6 V, well within the absolute-maximum rating of the AD620 (±18 V supply, ±Vs − 0.7 V input). 4. **Series Resistor at IA Input ($R_{\text{in}} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$):** Limits the current that flows into the Zener clamp. 5. **High-voltage-rated Capacitor (X2Y type, 1 nF):** Bypasses residual RF energy. **Circuit Schematic (text representation):** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Patient Electrode] --> R1[R_ext 10 kΩ 1W] R1 --> G[GDT 90V] G --> GND1[Patient Ground] R1 --> R2[R_in 1kΩ] R2 --> Z1[Zener 5.6V] R2 --> Z2[Zener 5.6V] Z1 --> IA[INA AD620] Z2 --> IA Z1 --> GND2[Patient Ground] Z2 --> GND2 IA --> OUT[To Rest of AFE] ``` **Why the 10 kΩ value is critical:** The defibrillator pulse has $\sim 50$ A peak. Without $R_{\text{ext}}$, this current would vaporize the Zener diodes in <1 ms. The 10 kΩ resistor limits the current to: $$I_{\text{peak}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{defib}}}{R_{\text{ext}}} = \dfrac{5000}{10000} = 0.5 \text{ A}$$ The GDT must absorb the bulk of this 0.5 A. The 1 W rating of $R_{\text{ext}}$ is sufficient for a 5 ms pulse because: $$E = I^{2} R t = (0.5)^{2} \times 10000 \times 0.005 = 12.5 \text{ J}$$ Power dissipation: $P = E / t = 12.5 / 0.005 = 2500 \text{ W peak}$ (acceptable for 5 ms pulse in a 1 W resistor, since pulse-power is much higher than continuous rating). However, **$R_{\text{ext}}$ must be small enough** to not attenuate the ECG signal. With $R_{\text{ext}} = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_{\text{skin}} \approx 10\text{ k}\Omega$, the voltage divider attenuates the ECG by only **6 dB**, which can be compensated by increasing the IA gain. --- **(b)** A 2-lead ECG front-end uses an INA with differential gain of 1000. If the maximum ECG amplitude is 5 mV and the input-referred noise is 5 µV RMS, calculate: (i) Output ECG signal voltage, (ii) Output RMS noise voltage, (iii) Output SNR in dB. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $A_d = 1000$ - $V_{\text{ECG,max}} = 5 \text{ mV} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$ - $V_{n,\text{input}} = 5 \text{ µV} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}$ **(i) Output ECG signal voltage:** $$V_{\text{out,signal}} = A_d \times V_{\text{ECG}} = 1000 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 5 \text{ V}$$ **(ii) Output RMS noise voltage:** The noise is amplified by the same differential gain: $$V_{n,\text{out}} = A_d \times V_{n,\text{input}} = 1000 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \text{ mV (RMS)}$$ **(iii) Output SNR:** $$\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{V_{\text{out,signal}}}{V_{n,\text{out}}} \right) = 20 \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{5}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \right)$$ $$\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}(1000) = 20 \times 3 = 60 \text{ dB}$$ **Comments / Interpretation:** - A SNR of 60 dB is **clinically acceptable** (≥ 40 dB required for diagnostic ECG, per IEC 60601-2-25). - The signal occupies the full ±5 V swing of a unipolar ADC; in practice, a 16-bit bipolar ADC with ±5 V input range is used. - The 5 µV input-referred noise is the **total integrated noise** over the ECG bandwidth of 0.05–150 Hz. A high-quality INA (e.g., AD620, INA128) has input noise density ~9 nV/√Hz, so integrated over ~150 Hz, $V_n \approx 9 \times \sqrt{150} \approx 110$ nV — but this is the noise density result; the 5 µV figure includes the electrode thermal noise, op-amp current noise, and resistor noise, which dominate at the input. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in Hardware Design Questions (and how to avoid losing marks):** > 1. **Confusing gain and voltage:** Many students write "Gain = 1000 V" — gain is dimensionless. Always say $A_d = 1000$ (V/V) or $A_d = 60$ dB. > 2. **Forgetting the "1+" in IA gain formula:** The differential gain of a 3-op-amp IA is $1 + 2R_1/R_{\text{gain}}$, not $2R_1/R_{\text{gain}}$. The +1 is the most commonly lost mark. > 3. **Wrong filter order:** A 1st-order filter gives −20 dB/decade roll-off; a 2nd-order Butterworth gives −40 dB/decade. State the order explicitly. > 4. **Mixing up CMRR and PSRR:** CMRR rejects common-mode signal; PSRR rejects power-supply ripple. Don't interchange them in answers. > 5. **Skipping the protection network:** A 14-mark ECG design question that omits defibrillation protection loses **3 marks** automatically. Always include it. > 6. **No isolation barrier mentioned:** A design without galvanic isolation is non-compliant with IEC 60601-1 and will be marked down. > 7. **Forgetting units on noise:** Thermal noise is in **V/√Hz** (noise density) or **V RMS** (integrated). State both clearly. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Hardware Design (Module 2)** - [ ] **Biomedical hardware** = sensor + AFE + ADC + DSP + display + isolation + power. - [ ] **Generic AFE chain:** Electrode → Protection → IA → Filter → ADC. - [ ] **Instrumentation Amplifier (3-op-amp):** $A_d = 1 + 2R_1 / R_{\text{gain}}$ — the *one formula* you must memorize. - [ ] **CMRR ≥ 90 dB** for clinical ECG; defined as $20 \log \vert A_d / A_c \vert$. - [ ] **ECG bandwidth** = 0.05–150 Hz (diagnostic) / 0.5–40 Hz (monitor). - [ ] **EEG bandwidth** = 0.5–100 Hz; amplitude 10–100 µV; **higher gain (1000) required**. - [ ] **EMG bandwidth** = 20–500 Hz; amplitude 50 µV – 5 mV. - [ ] **Right-Leg Drive (RLD)** is mandatory for ECG to reduce common-mode noise by 20–40 dB. - [ ] **Notch filter (50 Hz)** is *always* required for Indian/European/EC mains-powered systems. - [ ] **Anti-aliasing filter** cutoff ≤ Nyquist = $f_s / 2$. Use 2nd-order Butterworth. - [ ] **IEC 60601-1** = the mandatory medical safety standard; leakage current ≤ 100 µA (BF) / 10 µA (CF normal). - [ ] **Defibrillation protection** = GDT + Zener + 10 kΩ series resistor, ALWAYS included. - [ ] **Isolation barrier** = galvanic, ≥ 1500 V AC for mains-powered, 5 kV for defibrillation-proof. - [ ] **PCB design** = 4-layer, star-ground, 8 mm creepage, guard rings around high-Z traces. - [ ] **Thermal noise** $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ — common numerical for KTU. - [ ] **Nyquist sampling**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$; ECG: $f_s \geq 300$ Hz, **use 500 Hz clinically**. - [ ] **ENOB formula**: $\text{ENOB} = (\text{SINAD}_{\text{dB}} - 1.76)/6.02$ — high-yield for ADC questions. - [ ] **Top-down design flow**: Specification → Architecture → Sensor → AFE → Power → MCU → PCB → Compliance. - [ ] **SNR ≥ 40 dB** = clinical ECG minimum; ≥ 60 dB for diagnostic-grade. - [ ] **Common-mistakes** to avoid: forgetting "1+" in IA gain, omitting protection network, no isolation.

topics.title: Software Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Software Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Software Design

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Software Design** in the context of biomedical devices is the disciplined engineering process of defining the architecture, modules, interfaces, data structures, and algorithmic procedures of a medical device's software system, transforming validated software requirements into a complete, verifiable software description that satisfies all functional, performance, safety, and regulatory constraints defined in IEC 62304 and ISO 13485.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner Standard):**
> Software design is the phase of the medical device development life cycle (MDDLC) where the software requirements specification (SRS) is translated into a structured, modular, testable, and traceable architectural framework that enables reliable execution of intended medical functions while ensuring patient safety, data integrity, and regulatory compliance.

## 1.2 Types of Software in Biomedical Devices
