N0 on 10 to 30 units"] --> V2["Step 2: Calculate Verification Dose
VDmax using ISO Table 9"] V2 --> V3["Step 3: Dose Mapping
Highest dose, lowest dose zones"] V3 --> V4["Step 4: Dose Auditing
Quarterly dose at 2 kGy interval"] V4 --> V5{"SAL less than
10 power minus 6?"} V5 -->|"Yes"| V6["Validation Successful
Label with 25 kGy Dose"] V5 -->|"No"| V7["Increase Dose
Re-validate"] V7 --> V4 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Sterile Barrier System (SBS) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Primary_SBS ["Primary SBS (ISO 11607-1)"] direction TB P1["Top Web - Tyvek 1073B
Microbial Barrier
Permeable to Sterilant"] P2["Bottom Web - PET/PE Laminate Film
Provides Seal and Clarity"] P1 --- P2 P3["Device inside Sealed Pouch
Seal Width greater than 6 mm"] P2 --- P3 end subgraph Secondary_Pkg ["Secondary Protective Packaging"] direction TB S1["Cardboard Carton
Drop Test ASTM D4169"] S2["Desiccant Silica Gel
Moisture Control"] S3["IFU and Sterilization Indicator"] S1 --- S2 S2 --- S3 end Primary_SBS --> Secondary_Pkg ``` ## 4.4 Process Decision Tree — Choosing Packaging Material vs. Sterilization Method ```mermaid flowchart TD M{"Sterilization
Method?"} M -->|"Steam"| M1["Use Paper plus Film
Paper porous to steam
NOT Tyvek alone"] M -->|"EtO"| M2["Use Tyvek plus Film
Tyvek permeable to gas
Allows aeration"] M -->|"Gamma"| M3["Use Tyvek plus Foil
Foil blocks UV
Radiation stable"] M -->|"H2O2 Plasma"| M4["Use Tyvek plus Tyvek
No cellulose, no liquids
Plasma compatible"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A common exam trap is asking **"Why can't paper be used for gamma sterilization?"** The correct answer: paper **yellows and degrades** under gamma due to cellulose chain scission; also gamma generates **free radicals** that react with paper moisture. --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]** Define **Sterility Assurance Level (SAL)**. Why is SAL $= 10^{-6}$ chosen as the regulatory benchmark and not absolute sterility? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** **Definition:** SAL is the probability of a single viable microorganism being present on a sterilized device. It is expressed as $10^{-n}$, where lower values indicate higher assurance. - **(1 mark)** **Why $10^{-6}$:** Absolute sterility cannot be proven mathematically because a destructive test on a 100% sample would consume all product. Hence regulators (FDA, ISO) accepted a statistical definition. - **(1 mark)** **Numerical interpretation:** SAL $= 10^{-6}$ means no more than **one viable organism per one million sterilized devices**. This benchmark is recognized globally in ISO 11135, ISO 11137, and ISO 17665, and aligns with the maximum risk acceptable for an invasive medical device. ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** Distinguish between **disinfection** and **sterilization**. Give one example of a high-level disinfectant used in hospital practice. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** **Disinfection** eliminates most vegetative pathogenic microorganisms but **not bacterial spores**; reduces microbial load by 99.9% (3-log). - **(1 mark)** **Sterilization** eliminates **all** viable microorganisms including highly resistant **spores**; achieves SAL $\leq 10^{-6}$. - **(1 mark)** **Example:** **2% Glutaraldehyde (Cidex)** is a high-level disinfectant used for endoscopes at 20–45 min contact; cannot achieve sterilization unless extended to 10 hours (becomes a "sterilant"). --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks each) > **[Internal Choice as per KTU ESE Regulations]** — Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern]* **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the working principle of a **steam autoclave**. List the standard operating parameters and explain why **saturated steam is preferred** over dry heat for metallic implants. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hospital CSSD technician claims to achieve sterilization at **115 °C for 45 min**. The biological indicator used has $D_{121} = 1.5$ min and $Z = 10$ °C. Using Stumbo's equation, determine whether this cycle is **acceptable** for an implant requiring SAL $\leq 10^{-6}$, assuming initial bioburden $N_0 = 10^2$. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question A (a) — 7 Marks** **(i) Working Principle (3 marks):** A steam autoclave operates on the principle that **saturated steam at 121 °C carries latent heat (≈ 2201 kJ/kg)** which, upon condensation on a cooler device surface, releases enormous energy that **denatures microbial proteins and coagulates cytoplasm**. Stages: 1. **Loading** — devices in Tyvek pouches placed on perforated trays. 2. **Air evacuation** — either by gravity displacement (downward displacement of air by incoming steam) or pre-vacuum pump cycles (more efficient). 3. **Heating/sterilization plateau** — saturated steam at **121 °C, 15 psi gauge** for 15–30 min. 4. **Exhaust** — steam released. 5. **Drying** — vacuum drying prevents wet packs. 6. **Unloading** — at $< 40$ °C for storage. **(ii) Standard Parameters (2 marks):** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Temperature | **121 °C** (also 132 °C / 134 °C pre-vacuum) | | Pressure | **15 psi (103.4 kPa) gauge** | | Time | **15–30 min** (porous load) | | Steam quality | **Dryness fraction $\geq 0.95$** | **(iii) Why saturated steam (2 marks):** - **High latent heat** released on condensation penetrates porous loads. - **Moisture** hydrolyzes microbial proteins — denaturation is faster than dry oxidation. - **Lower temperature (121 °C) is sufficient** vs. dry heat (160 °C, 2 h), preserving polymer components and instrument edges. - **Faster cycle, lower energy, deeper penetration**. --- ### **Model Solution to Question A (b) — 7 Marks** **Given:** $T = 115$ °C, $t = 45$ min, $D_{121} = 1.5$ min, $Z = 10$ °C, $N_0 = 10^2$, required SAL $\leq 10^{-6}$. **Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the effective $F_0$ using the Bigelow equation. $$ F_0 = t \cdot 10^{(T - 121.1)/Z} = 45 \cdot 10^{(115 - 121.1)/10} $$ $$ F_0 = 45 \cdot 10^{-0.61} = 45 \cdot 0.2455 \approx 11.05 \text{ min} $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute $D$ at 115 °C from the $Z$-value relation. $$ \log\!\left(\frac{D_{115}}{D_{121}}\right) = \frac{121 - 115}{Z} = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6 $$ $$ D_{115} = 1.5 \cdot 10^{0.6} = 1.5 \cdot 3.981 \approx 5.97 \text{ min} $$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute log reduction achieved by 45 min at 115 °C. $$ \text{Log reduction} = \frac{t}{D_{115}} = \frac{45}{5.97} \approx 7.54 \text{ logs} $$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute expected surviving population. $$ \log_{10} N_t = \log_{10}(N_0) - \text{Log reduction} = 2 - 7.54 = -5.54 $$ $$ N_t = 10^{-5.54} = 2.88 \times 10^{-6} $$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Compare with required SAL. Since $N_t = 2.88 \times 10^{-6} > 10^{-6}$, the surviving probability exceeds the limit. **Step 6 (2 marks):** Decision and corrected cycle. > [!WARNING] > **The cycle is NOT acceptable.** The survival probability ($2.88 \times 10^{-6}$) is approximately 2.88× higher than the regulatory threshold. To achieve SAL $\leq 10^{-6}$ we need: $$ t_{\min} = D_{115} \cdot (6 + 2) = 5.97 \cdot 8 = 47.8 \text{ min} $$ or, **practically recommended**, **increase to 121 °C for $t = (6+2) \times 1.5 = 12$ min** OR adopt a standard **121 °C / 30 min** cycle for compliance with ISO 17665. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern]* **(a)** Compare **Ethylene Oxide (EtO)**, **Gamma Radiation**, and **Hydrogen Peroxide Plasma** as low-temperature sterilization methods. Discuss the mechanism, material compatibility, and key limitations of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Define the **Sterile Barrier System (SBS)** per ISO 11607. Explain the **role of Tyvek** as a packaging material. Why is the **peel-pouch sealing test (ASTM F88)** critical for compliance? **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question B (a) — 7 Marks** | Feature | **EtO** | **Gamma Radiation** | **H$_2$O$_2$ Plasma** | |---|---|---|---| | **Mechanism** | Alkylation of –NH$_2$, –SH, –COOH groups in DNA/proteins | Ionizing radiation produces free radicals, breaks DNA strands | Plasma radicals (·OH, ·OOH) destroy microbes | | **Temp** | 37–63 °C | Ambient (no heat) | 45–55 °C | | **Dose / Conc.** | 600–1200 mg/L for 1–6 h | 25–40 kGy | 6 mg/L H$_2$O$_2$, 12 cycles | | **Material Compat.** | Most polymers, electronics | Most polymers; some embrittle | Heat/moisture-sensitive devices | | **Incompatible** | Liquids, foods | Some fluoropolymers, PTFE | Cellulose, powders, liquids | | **Toxicity** | **Carcinogenic**; needs 8–12 h aeration; residuals $\leq 25$ ppm | Generates ozone, free radicals | Water + oxygen byproducts (safe) | | **Cycle Time** | 4–12 h (incl. aeration) | 4–8 h (incl. transport) | 30–75 min | | **Validation** | ISO 11135 | ISO 11137 | ISO 14937 | | **Limitation** | Toxic, slow | Polymer degradation | High unit cost | **(Scoring: 7 marks distributed — 3 marks for the table, 1 mark each for explicit mechanism, and 3 marks for limitation discussion.)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question B (b) — 7 Marks** **(i) Definition of SBS (2 marks):** Per **ISO 11607-1**, the Sterile Barrier System is the **minimum packaging** that prevents microbial ingress, allows aseptic presentation at the point of use, and maintains sterility through the stated shelf life. It consists of: - **Primary pack** — Tyvek + film pouch (or rigid container). - **Protective packaging** — outer carton. **(ii) Role of Tyvek (3 marks):** - **Tyvek 1073B** is **spun-bonded high-density polyethylene (HDPE)**. - Has a pore size of $\approx 0.5$–$10$ µm — small enough to **block microorganisms** but **permeable to steam, EtO, and H$_2$O$_2$ plasma**. - Strong, tear-resistant, low-linting (no fibre shedding into cleanroom). - Compatible with **all major sterilization methods** — making it the industry gold standard. **(iii) Importance of ASTM F88 Peel Strength Test (2 marks):** ASTM F88 measures the **peel strength (N/15 mm) of heat-sealed pouch seams**. Critical because: - Weak seals allow **microbial ingress** post-sterilization — defeating the entire purpose. - Strong seals cause **fibre tear / particle generation** during opening in the OT. - **Typical target: 1.0–4.5 N/15 mm** — validated at production startup and quarterly per ISO 11607-2. --- ## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - ☐ **Sterilization ≠ Disinfection**: Sterilization kills **spores** too; SAL $\leq 10^{-6}$. - ☐ **Autoclave baseline**: **121 °C, 15 psi, 15–30 min** for steam sterilization of metallic, glass, ceramic devices. - ☐ **D-value = time for 1-log (90%) reduction** at temperature $T$. $D_{121}$ is the reference for steam. - ☐ **Z-value = temperature change for 1-log change in $D$**. For steam BI, $Z \approx 10$ °C. - ☐ **F$_0$ (Bigelow's Eq.)** = equivalent sterilization time at 121.1 °C: $F_0 = \sum \Delta t \cdot 10^{(T-121.1)/Z}$. Minimum $F_0 = 8$ min for porous load. - ☐ **Overkill formula**: $F_0 \geq 12 \cdot D_{121}$ (Stumbo). - ☐ **EtO** is used when heat- and radiation-sensitive (electronics, polymers), but requires **8–12 h aeration** and residuals $\leq 25$ ppm. - ☐ **Gamma dose** for medical devices is **25–40 kGy** (ISO 11137); causes polymer embrittlement over time. - ☐ **H$_2$O$_2$ plasma (Sterrad)** is fastest cycle (30–75 min), cannot process cellulose, powders, or liquids. - ☐ **Tyvek** is HDPE spun-bonded fabric; porous to sterilants but blocks microbes. - ☐ **SBS per ISO 11607** must be validated for seal strength (ASTM F88), dye penetration (ASTM F1929), and accelerated aging (ASTM F1980). - ☐ **The 80/20 rule**: 80% metallic implants → steam; 80% polymer disposables → EtO or gamma. - ☐ **Selection rule**: Method $= f$(material, geometry, bioburden, electronic content, end-of-life residues). - ☐ Always mention **the relevant ISO standard** in exam answers (ISO 11135 / 11137 / 17665 / 11607 / 14937).

topics.title: Human Factors Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Human Factors Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human Factors Engineering (HFE)**, also known as **Ergonomics** or **Usability Engineering**, is the multidisciplinary scientific discipline concerned with the understanding of interactions among **humans** and other elements of a system. In the context of biomedical device design, it is the application of knowledge about human characteristics (physical, cognitive, sensory, and emotional) to the design of medical devices, systems, and environments to optimize **safety, efficacy, comfort, and user performance**.

The discipline applies theory, principles, data, and methodologies from psychology, anthropometry, biomechanics, physiology, and engineering to design equipment, processes, and systems that are compatible with human capabilities and limitations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology Distinction:**
> - **Ergonomics** — European/international term, focuses on the *fit between people and their work environment*.
> - **Human Factors** — North American term, broader scope including cognitive and organizational factors.
> - **Usability Engineering** — The systematic application of human factors in iterative design and testing.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Car Dashboard"**
> Imagine you are driving a car. The speedometer is placed **directly in front of you** (not near your feet). The horn is on the steering wheel (not hidden under the seat). The brake pedal is **larger** than the accelerator. Why? Because human factors engineers studied how drivers perceive, react, and fail. They moved the most critical controls to the most accessible locations and made the visual displays readable at a glance.
>
> Now apply the same principle to a **defibrillator** used in an emergency. A paramedic under stress has only seconds to act. If the shock button is recessed too deep, or the voice prompts are too fast, a life is lost. **Human Factors Engineering ensures the device "fits" the human — physically, cognitively, and emotionally — under realistic conditions of use.**

In biomedical device design, the **user** is often a sick, stressed, fatigued, or untrained person (nurse, patient, doctor). HFE accounts for this and designs devices that are **forgiving of human error** rather than assuming perfect performance.

---

## 1.3 Core Physical Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Anthropometric & Physiological Design Metrics** (used in KTU problems):
> - **5th–95th percentile range** is the standard design population (covering 90% of users).
> - **Strength values**: Grip strength adult male ≈ **400 N**; adult female ≈ **225 N**; pinch ≈ **70 N**.
> - **Reaction time**: Simple visual reaction ≈ **0.25 s**; choice reaction ≈ **0.4 s**.
> - **Visual acuity**: Snellen 20/20 corresponds to a **minimum angle of resolution (MAR)** of **1 arc-minute (1′)**.
> - **Fitts' Law Index of Difficulty**: $ID = \log_2(2A/W)$ bits.
> - **Hick's Law**: $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)$ seconds.
> - **Hearing range**: 20 Hz – 20 kHz; **speech range** ≈ 300 Hz – 3.4 kHz.
> - **Standard reach distance** (functional): 5th percentile female popliteal-to-knuckle = **~42 cm**; 95th percentile male fingertip-to-shoulder = **~88 cm**.

---

## 1.4 Why Human Factors Engineering Matters in Biomedical Devices

| Aspect | Without HFE | With HFE |
|---|---|---|
| **User Error Rate** | High (3–10× normal) | Drastically reduced |
| **Training Time** | Long, complex | Shortened via intuitive design |
| **Patient Safety** | Use errors cause injuries | Errors are designed out |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Fails FDA/CE review | Meets IEC 62366 & FDA HFE Guidance |
| **Market Acceptance** | Poor clinician adoption | Higher user satisfaction |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Exam Focus (2024 Scheme):** The 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes *use-related hazards*, *use error analysis*, and *summative usability evaluation* as CO2/CO3 outcomes. Students must master the link between **design flaw → use error → patient harm → mitigation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Human Factors Engineering Framework (5 Pillars)

The design of any biomedical device must address five inter-locking dimensions of human interaction:

### Pillar 1 — Anthropometry (Physical Fit)
- **Definition:** The study of human body dimensions (height, reach, grip, weight, joint range of motion).
- **Application in Devices:** Designing the grip diameter of a surgical tool, the size of a screen, the force required to press a button, the door of a CT scanner.
- **Design Rule:** Use the **5th percentile female** for the *smallest* reach/strength dimension (to ensure the weakest can operate it) and the **95th percentile male** for the *largest* clearance dimension.

### Pillar 2 — Sensory Ergonomics (Information Reception)
The body takes in information through five channels. The biomedical designer must choose the **right channel** for the right information:
- **Visual:** Displays, alarms, status lights. Strengths — high spatial resolution, easily shared. Weakness — fails if user is blind, dark, or looking elsewhere.
- **Auditory:** Alarms, voice prompts, beeps. Strengths — works eyes-free, alerts a non-attentive user. Weakness — masked by ambient noise, alarms are desensitized (alarm fatigue).
- **Tactile/Haptic:** Vibrations, textures, detents. Strengths — works when eyes & ears are busy.
- **Olfactory/Gustatory:** Rarely used in devices, but emerging (e.g., glucose-detection breath sensors).

### Pillar 3 — Cognitive Ergonomics (Information Processing)
- **Mental model:** How the user thinks the device works. If the actual model differs, errors occur.
- **Workload:** Too much information → information overload; too little → loss of situation awareness.
- **Attention:** Selective (focused) vs. divided. Critical alarms must capture selective attention.
- **Memory:** Short-term memory holds only **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller's Law). Designs should minimize memory load.
- **Decision-making:** Under stress, users regress to **recognition-primed decisions** — designs must make the correct option obvious.

### Pillar 4 — Physical Ergonomics (Biomechanics & Effort)
- **Posture:** Neutral wrist (0°–15° extension) for push-buttons; avoid ulnar deviation.
- **Force limits:** Based on **NIOSH Lifting Equation** for repetitive tasks.
- **Repetition & Fatigue:** Musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs) arise from high-force, high-frequency, awkward-posture tasks (e.g., laparoscopic surgery).

### Pillar 5 — Environmental & Organizational Factors
- Lighting, temperature, noise, vibration, EMR/Wi-Fi interference, shift work, team communication, sterile-field constraints, and SOPs.

---

## 2.2 The Human Factors Engineering Process (FDA/IEC 62366 Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEC 62366-1:2015** defines the **Usability Engineering (UE) process** for medical devices. This is a **mandatory KTU topic**.

The process has 7 iterative steps:

1. **Identify user, use environment, and use scenarios** (the "Use Specification").
2. **Identify the user interface (UI) characteristics** that affect usability.
3. **Identify known use errors and foreseeable misuse** (Hazard Identification).
4. **Define the User Interface of the device** (formative input).
5. **Conduct a Use-Related Risk Analysis (URRA)** to score severity × probability.
6. **Conduct Formative Usability Evaluations** during design (iterative refinement).
7. **Conduct Summative Usability Evaluation (Validation)** — final human-factors validation testing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formative vs. Summative (Critical Distinction):**
> - **Formative** = conducted *during* design to *improve* the design. Not a regulatory submission artifact.
> - **Summative** = conducted *after* design is finalized, on the *finished* device, to *validate* that it is safe for market release. **Required by FDA for Class II/III devices.**

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NOTE ON TABLES:** Per the KTU-Premier-Engine protocol, the vertical pipe symbol has been replaced with `\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption.

| # | Law / Metric | Equation | Variables & Units | Application in Device Design |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fitts' Law** (Movement Time) | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(2A/W\right)$ | $MT$ = movement time (s), $A$ = distance to target, $W$ = width of target, $a,b$ = empirical constants | Sizes & positions of buttons on a device; smaller target $\rightarrow$ longer time |
| 2 | **Hick's Law** (Choice Reaction) | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)$ | $T$ = decision time (s), $N$ = number of choices, $a,b$ = constants | Determines optimal number of menu options; $N$ should be minimized |
| 3 | **Index of Difficulty** | $ID = \log_2\!\left(2A/W\right)$ | $ID$ in bits | Quantifies UI complexity |
| 4 | **SNR for Displays** | $SNR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio in dB | Display legibility in OR environment |
| 5 | **Alarm Sound Pressure** | $L_{p} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_{0}}\right)$ | $p_0 = 20\ \mu Pa$ (hearing threshold) | Ensures alarm audibility over background noise |
| 6 | **Risk Score (URRA)** | $Risk = Severity \times Probability$ | 1–5 ordinal scales | Use error prioritization |
| 7 | **Anthropometric Range** | $Range = P_{95} - P_{5}$ | Percentile values in cm | Defining design population |
| 8 | **Luminance Contrast Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{L_{max}}{L_{min}}$ | $L$ in $\text{cd/m}^2$ | Display visibility (min $\geq$ 3:1) |
| 9 | **Stevens' Power Law** | $\psi = k \cdot \phi^{n}$ | $\psi$ = perceived magnitude, $\phi$ = stimulus, $n$ = exponent | $n < 1$ for brightness (compresses dynamic range) |
| 10 | **Weber's Law (JND)** | $\dfrac{\Delta I}{I} = k$ | $k$ = Weber fraction | Display step size design |

---

## 2.4 Use-Related Hazards & Error Classification

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board-Aligned Classification — Memorize this hierarchy!**

A **use error** is a deviation from the user's expectations that leads to an unintended outcome. Three categories:

1. **Slip** — *Action error* (motor). E.g., presses "Start" instead of "Stop" because the buttons are too close.
2. **Lapse** — *Memory error* (cognitive). E.g., forgets to confirm a critical step.
3. **Mistake** — *Intentional action based on wrong mental model*. E.g., misinterprets a waveform.

For each hazard, mitigation follows the **Hierarchy of Controls** (most → least effective):
1. **Inherent safety by design** (best) — e.g., a connector that physically cannot be mis-mated.
2. **Guards & interlocks** — physical barriers.
3. **Information for safety** — alarms, labels.
4. **Training & SOPs** (weakest) — does not eliminate the hazard.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Per FDA HFE Guidance, training is **not** considered a sufficient risk control for design-induced use errors. **The device must be designed to be intuitive.**

---

## 2.5 Standards Governing HFE in Medical Devices (Memorize for KTU)

| Standard | Title | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **IEC 62366-1:2015** | Application of usability engineering to medical devices | Core process |
| **IEC 62366-2:2016** | Guidance on the application of the process | Implementation |
| **ANSI/AAMI HE75:2009** | Human factors engineering — Design of medical devices | Detailed design principles |
| **FDA HFE Guidance (2016)** | Applying Human Factors and Usability Engineering | U.S. regulatory expectation |
| **ISO 14971:2019** | Application of risk management to medical devices | Risk framework |
| **IEC 60601-1-8** | Alarm systems in medical electrical equipment | Alarm design |
| **ISO 9241-210** | Human-centred design for interactive systems | General HCD |
| **MDR (EU 2017/745)** Annex I, §9 | Usability & ergonomics requirement | EU regulation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I — Fitts' Law: Modeling Touch-Point Acquisition

> **Problem Context:** A biomedical device has a touch-screen button on its front panel. The user's finger must travel **$A = 15$ cm** to reach the button, which has a width of **$W = 1.2$ cm**. Empirical constants for the device's UI are $a = 0.05$ s, $b = 0.20$ s/bit. Determine the predicted movement time.

### Step 1 — Identify the Given Variables
$$A = 15\ \text{cm}, \quad W = 1.2\ \text{cm}, \quad a = 0.05\ \text{s}, \quad b = 0.20\ \text{s/bit}$$

### Step 2 — State Fitts' Law
$$MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Index of Difficulty (ID)
$$\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{2 \times 15}{1.2}\right) \\
   &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{30}{1.2}\right) \\
   &= \log_2(25)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Logarithm
Since $\log_{10}(25) \approx 1.3979$ and conversion factor $\log_{2}(x) = \log_{10}(x)/\log_{10}(2) \approx \log_{10}(x)/0.3010$:
$$\begin{aligned}
ID &= \frac{1.3979}{0.3010} \\
   &\approx 4.644\ \text{bits}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Movement Time
$$\begin{aligned}
MT &= 0.05 + 0.20 \times 4.644 \\
   &= 0.05 + 0.9288 \\
   &\approx 0.979\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation
> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** It takes the user almost **1 full second** to accurately hit the target. In an emergency (e.g., delivering a defibrillation shock), this is too slow. **Design Solution:** Increase the button width to $W = 3$ cm (thumb-friendly "fat-finger" target), or place the critical button closer to the user's natural hand-rest position (reducing $A$).

### Step 7 — Verify by Recomputing with $W = 3$ cm
$$\begin{aligned}
ID_{new} &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{2 \times 15}{3}\right) = \log_2(10) \approx 3.322\ \text{bits} \\
MT_{new} &= 0.05 + 0.20 \times 3.322 = 0.714\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Movement time reduced by **27%**, a clinically significant improvement.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Hick's Law: Optimizing a Defibrillator Menu

> **Problem Context:** A defibrillator has a "Mode Selection" menu. The design team is debating between 4 menu options and 8 menu options. Using Hick's Law with $a = 0.15$ s and $b = 0.15$ s/bit, calculate the decision time in each case.

### Step 1 — State Hick's Law
$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)$$

### Step 2 — Compute for $N = 4$
$$\begin{aligned}
T_4 &= 0.15 + 0.15 \cdot \log_2(4) \\
    &= 0.15 + 0.15 \cdot 2 \\
    &= 0.45\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Compute for $N = 8$
$$\begin{aligned}
T_8 &= 0.15 + 0.15 \cdot \log_2(8) \\
    &= 0.15 + 0.15 \cdot 3 \\
    &= 0.60\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Doubling the menu items increases decision time by 0.15 s.** Although small here, under stress the cost of a wrong choice is severe. **Hick's Law drives the "less is more" principle in life-critical devices.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Weber's Law: Display Step Design

> **Problem Context:** The "dose rate" indicator on a dialysis machine must show increments distinguishable by a clinician. The current dose rate is $I = 300$ mL/min. Weber's fraction for visual amplitude discrimination is $k = 0.02$. Find the **Just-Noticeable Difference (JND)** and recommend a step size.

### Step 1 — State Weber's Law
$$\Delta I = k \cdot I$$

### Step 2 — Compute JND
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta I &= 0.02 \times 300 \\
         &= 6\ \text{mL/min}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Design Recommendation
The display should use increments of **at least 6 mL/min** (rounded up to a clean value, e.g., **10 mL/min**) to ensure users can distinguish adjacent values.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Usability Trade-offs

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix to answer KTU Part B questions on "Compare and contrast..."**

| Design Approach | Strength | Weakness | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Visual display** | High spatial resolution, persistent, shareable | Fails if eyes busy or lighting poor | Infusion pump rate display |
| **Auditory alarm** | Catches attention eyes-free | Alarm fatigue, noise masking | Critical ICU alarms |
| **Tactile feedback** | Works in sterile field with gloves | Limited information bandwidth | Surgical tool grip alerts |
| **Redundant multimodal** (visual+auditory) | Maximizes detection | Cost, complexity, user annoyance | Cardiopulmonary bypass machine |
| **Voice prompts** | Hands-free, intuitive | Slow, language issues, may startle | AED for lay users |
| **Color coding** | Rapid recognition | 8% of males are color-blind | IV line labels (use shape too!) |
| **Push-button** | Tactile confirmation, no menu | Mechanical wear, hygiene | Ventilator mode change |
| **Touchscreen** | Flexible UI | Requires cleaning, glare, no tactile | Modern patient monitor |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Use-Related Risk Analysis (URRA) on an Infusion Pump

> **Scenario:** A nurse mis-programs an infusion pump (rate entered as 100 mL/h instead of 10 mL/h). The patient receives a 10× overdose, leading to critical harm.

**Step 1 — Identify Hazard:** Wrong decimal entry.
**Step 2 — Severity (per ISO 14971 scale):** 5 (catastrophic — death possible).
**Step 3 — Probability (with current design):** 3 (occasional, ~1 in 1000).
**Step 4 — Initial Risk Score:** $5 \times 3 = 15$ (HIGH).
**Step 5 — Mitigation Strategies:**

| Mitigation | Risk Reduction Effect | Final Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Add leading decimal (`.10` not `10`)** | Probability drops to 1 | $5 \times 1 = 5$ (Medium) |
| **Add hard limit / soft limit alert** | Probability drops to 2 | $5 \times 2 = 10$ (Medium) |
| **Add barcode scanning of drug vial** | Probability drops to 1 | $5 \times 1 = 5$ (Medium) |
| **All three combined (inherent safety)** | Probability drops to 1 | **5 (Acceptable)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3-step combined solution is what IEC 62366-1 calls "inherent safety by design"** — the design itself prevents the error rather than relying on training.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The Human Factors Engineering Lifecycle (IEC 62366-1 Process Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Use Specification] --> B[Step 2: Identify UI Characteristics]
    B --> C[Step 3: Identify Known Use Errors and Foreseeable Misuse]
    C --> D[Step 4: Define User Interface of the Device]
    D --> E[Step 5: Perform Use Related Risk Analysis URRA]
    E --> F[Step 6: Formative Usability Evaluation]
    F --> G{Design Acceptable?}
    G -- No --> H[Modify UI Design]
    H --> F
    G -- Yes --> I[Step 7: Summative Usability Evaluation Validation]
    I --> J{Validates Safe Use?}
    J -- No --> H
    J -- Yes --> K[Submit to Regulatory Authority FDA CE]
    K --> L[Market Release]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The formative evaluation loop (Steps 6) allows repeated design refinement. The summative evaluation (Step 7) is the final, regulatory-grade test. Both feed back into design modifications if any test reveals use errors or unacceptable risk.

---

## 4.2 Human-Medical Device Interaction Model (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Device[Medical Device]
        S1[Sensors and Inputs] --> CPU[Processor and Control Logic]
        CPU --> A1[Visual Display]
        CPU --> A2[Auditory Alarm]
        CPU --> A3[Tactile Haptic Feedback]
        CPU --> A4[Mechanical Actuator e.g. Pump]
    end

    subgraph Human[User Clinician or Patient]
        EYE[Visual Perception] --> BRAIN[Cognitive Processing and Decision]
        EAR[Auditory Perception] --> BRAIN
        SKIN[Tactile Perception] --> BRAIN
        BRAIN --> MOTOR[Musculoskeletal Response]
    end

    A1 --> EYE
    A2 --> EAR
    A3 --> SKIN
    MOTOR --> S1

    subgraph Env[Environment]
        LIGHT[Lighting]
        NOISE[Background Noise]
        TEMP[Temperature and Sterility]
    end

    Env -. modulates .-> Device
    Env -. modulates .-> Human
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Reading:** The device **outputs** information via three sensory channels (visual, auditory, tactile) plus physical action. The human **perceives**, **decides**, and **acts back** on the device. **Human Factors Engineering optimizes the bidirectional interface**, while the environment introduces constraints on both sides.

---

## 4.3 The 5 Pillars of HFE — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Human Factors Engineering))
    Anthropometry
      Body dimensions
      Strength
      Reach
      Percentile design 5 to 95
    Sensory
      Visual display
      Auditory alarm
      Tactile haptic
      Olfactory emerging
    Cognitive
      Mental model
      Workload
      Memory 7 plus minus 2
      Decision under stress
    Physical
      Posture neutral
      NIOSH lifting
      Repetition MSD risk
    Organizational
      Lighting
      Noise
      Team workflow
      Sterile field
```

---

## 4.4 Hierarchy of Controls for Use-Related Hazards

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H1[Inherent Safety by Design] --> H2[Guards and Interlocks]
    H2 --> H3[Information for Safety Alarms and Labels]
    H3 --> H4[Training and SOPs]
    H4 --> H5[Personal Protective Equipment Last Resort]

    style H1 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style H5 fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Color Code:** Green = strongest control (always preferred). Red = weakest (only acceptable as a last line of defense). KTU examiners love to ask: *"Why is training the weakest link?"* — answer: it does not eliminate the hazard; it merely shifts the burden to the user.

---

## 4.5 The Fitts' Law Geometry (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph U[User Hand Starting Position]
        U1[Origin]
    end
    subgraph T[Target Button]
        T1[Width W and Centerline]
    end
    U1 -- Distance A --> T1
    T1 -- Movement Time MT --> Result((MT = a + b log2 of 2A over W))
    style Result fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
```

> [!TIP]
> **The smaller the target $W$ or the farther $A$ it is, the harder and slower the acquisition.** This is why life-critical buttons (e.g., AED shock) are large, raised, and color-coded.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> **Define Human Factors Engineering. List any four human factors that must be considered in the design of a medical device.**

**Model Answer (Examiner-Grade, ~80 words):**

> **Human Factors Engineering (HFE)** is the application of knowledge about human physical, cognitive, sensory, and emotional characteristics to the design of medical devices, systems, and processes to optimize safety, efficacy, and user performance under realistic conditions of use.
>
> Four human factors considered in medical device design:
> 1. **Anthropometric data** (body dimensions, reach, strength) — to ensure physical fit.
> 2. **Sensory capabilities** (vision, hearing, touch) — to select appropriate information channels.
> 3. **Cognitive limitations** (memory, attention, mental model) — to minimize mental workload and use errors.
> 4. **Environmental constraints** (lighting, noise, sterile field) — to design for the use environment.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> **Distinguish between "formative" and "summative" usability evaluation as per IEC 62366-1.**

**Model Answer (Examiner-Grade):**

| Aspect | Formative Evaluation | Summative Evaluation |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | *Improve* the design iteratively | *Validate* the final design is safe & effective |
| **Stage** | During design refinement | After design is finalized (pre-market) |
| **Device State** | Prototype / early model | Finished, production-equivalent device |
| **Number of Users** | Small (5–10 per cycle) | Larger, statistically valid (≥ 15 per user group) |
| **Regulatory Submission** | Not submitted | **Required** by FDA & notified bodies |
| **Output** | Design recommendations | Validation report (HFE/UE File) |
| **When errors are found** | Fix the design, re-test | Re-design & re-validate (major rework) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the **seven-step Usability Engineering process** defined by IEC 62366-1:2015 for medical device development. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A cardiac monitor has an "ALARM SILENCE" button located at the **bottom-right corner** of the touchscreen, **8 cm from the corner**. The button's display size is **$W = 0.8$ cm**. The empirical constants for the touch interface are $a = 0.10$ s and $b = 0.25$ s/bit. Using **Fitts' Law**, calculate the predicted **Index of Difficulty (ID)** and **Movement Time (MT)**. Recommend two design modifications to reduce the MT to below 0.5 s. *(7 marks)*

---

### Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — The 7-Step Usability Engineering Process

1. **Step 1 — Use Specification:** Identify the intended users (e.g., nurses, patients, lay caregivers), the use environment (hospital ICU, home, ambulance), and the use scenarios (normal use, edge cases, foreseeable misuse).

2. **Step 2 — Identify UI Characteristics:** Document the user interface elements — physical controls, displays, alarms, software screens, packaging, labeling — that affect usability.

3. **Step 3 — Identify Known Use Errors and Foreseeable Misuse:** Use literature, complaint databases (e.g., FDA MAUDE), and incident reports to anticipate likely use errors (e.g., button mis-press, dose mis-entry).

4. **Step 4 — Define the User Interface of the Device:** Translate the requirements into a concrete UI design — physical layout, screen mock-ups, alarm tones, labeling.

5. **Step 5 — Perform Use-Related Risk Analysis (URRA):** For each potential use error, score **Severity** and **Probability** of harm. Compute **Risk Score** = $S \times P$. List all *critical tasks* — those whose failure could lead to serious harm.

6. **Step 6 — Formative Usability Evaluation:** Conduct iterative tests with representative users using simulated or prototype devices. Observe, interview, and refine the UI. Repeat until no unacceptable use errors remain.

7. **Step 7 — Summative Usability Evaluation (Validation):** Conduct the final test on the **finished, production-equivalent** device, with a statistically valid sample of representative users, performing all critical tasks. The test must demonstrate that the device is **safe and effective for the intended users, uses, and use environments**. The result forms the **HFE/UE Validation Report** submitted to the regulatory authority.

---

#### Part (b) — Fitts' Law Numerical Problem

**Given:** $A = 8$ cm, $W = 0.8$ cm, $a = 0.10$ s, $b = 0.25$ s/bit.

**Step 1 — Index of Difficulty:**
$$\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right) \\
   &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{2 \times 8}{0.8}\right) \\
   &= \log_2(20) \\
   &= \frac{\log_{10}(20)}{\log_{10}(2)} \\
   &= \frac{1.3010}{0.3010} \\
   &\approx 4.322\ \text{bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Movement Time:**
$$\begin{aligned}
MT &= a + b \cdot ID \\
   &= 0.10 + 0.25 \times 4.322 \\
   &= 0.10 + 1.0805 \\
   &\approx 1.18\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** $MT = 1.18$ s is **unacceptably slow** for a critical alarm-silence action during a code-blue.

**Step 4 — Design Modifications to achieve $MT < 0.5$ s:**

**Modification 1 — Increase the target width** (e.g., a full-width "Silence" bar at the bottom of the screen):
Let $W_{new} = 4$ cm, keep $A = 8$ cm.
$$\begin{aligned}
ID_{new} &= \log_2\!\left(\frac{16}{4}\right) = \log_2(4) = 2\ \text{bits} \\
MT_{new} &= 0.10 + 0.25 \times 2 = 0.60\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$
Still slightly above target. Increase further to $W = 8$ cm (full bottom bar):
$$ID = \log_2(16/8) = \log_2(2) = 1\ \text{bit} \quad \Rightarrow \quad MT = 0.35\ \text{s}.$$

**Modification 2 — Add a dedicated physical push-button** at the top of the monitor with a larger $W = 2$ cm and reduced $A = 4$ cm:
$$ID = \log_2(8/2) = \log_2(4) = 2\ \text{bits} \quad \Rightarrow \quad MT = 0.60\ \text{s}.$$
Combined with **audible click feedback** and **tactile detent**, the perceived MT drops below 0.5 s.

> **Final Recommendation:** Replace the small touchscreen button with a **dedicated, large, tactile, color-coded physical button** placed in the natural hand-rest zone. Provide **auditory + tactile confirmation** when pressed.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Internal Choice]

**(a)** Define **anthropometry**. Explain the **5th–95th percentile design rule** with a numerical example for the grip diameter of a surgical forceps. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss any **four categories of use errors** identified in IEC 62366-1, giving a biomedical example for each. State the **hierarchy of risk controls** used to mitigate such errors. *(7 marks)*

---

### Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Anthropometry & Percentile Design

**Definition:** *Anthropometry* is the scientific study of the **measurements and proportions of the human body** (height, weight, limb lengths, joint ranges, grip spans, strength). It provides the empirical data used to design physical interfaces.

**The 5th–95th Percentile Rule:**
Since no single design can fit *all* people, designers use the **5th percentile** to define the *lower boundary* of a dimension (e.g., smallest reach) and the **95th percentile** for the *upper boundary* (e.g., largest clearance). This covers **90% of the user population**.

**Numerical Example — Surgical Forceps Grip Diameter:**

Suppose anthropometric data on hand breadth (measured at metacarpals) of surgeons is normally distributed:
- **Mean** $\mu = 9.0$ cm
- **Standard deviation** $\sigma = 0.8$ cm

From standard normal tables:
- 5th percentile $z = -1.645$: Hand breadth $= 9.0 + (-1.645)(0.8) = 9.0 - 1.316 = 7.68$ cm.
- 95th percentile $z = +1.645$: Hand breadth $= 9.0 + (1.645)(0.8) = 9.0 + 1.316 = 10.32$ cm.

**Forceps grip diameter** should be slightly *less* than the minimum hand breadth (5th percentile) so the smallest hand can wrap around it:
$$D_{design} = 7.68 - \text{clearance} \approx 7.5\ \text{cm}.$$

**Thumb-reach length** (used for the index finger reach to the ring of the forceps) at the 5th percentile:
$$\text{Reach}_{5\%} = \mu_{reach} - 1.645 \sigma_{reach}.$$

> **Design Rule Application:** If the 95th percentile hand breadth is $10.32$ cm but the design fixes the diameter at $9.0$ cm, surgeons with the largest hands will experience **hand fatigue and slippage** (use error). If the design fixes the diameter at $7.0$ cm (below the 5th percentile), the smallest-handed surgeons will have **inadequate grip and pinch-grip substitution** (use error → drop → contamination).

---

#### Part (b) — Use Error Categories & Hierarchy of Controls

**Four Categories of Use Errors** (as per IEC 62366-1 taxonomy):

1. **Slip (Action/Motor Error):** An *unintended* motor action is executed.
   - *Biomedical Example:* A surgeon presses the **"Cautery ON"** pedal thinking it is the **"Bovie Off"** pedal. Result: unintended tissue burn.
   - *Mitigation:* Distinct pedal sizes, shapes, and tactile textures; physical separation by ≥ 15 cm.

2. **Lapse (Memory Error):** A step in a procedure is *omitted* because the user forgot.
   - *Biomedical Example:* A nurse forgets to **clamp the IV line** before switching infusion bags, causing air embolism risk.
   - *Mitigation:* The pump auto-clamps on bag removal (inherent safety); audible prompt "Clamp detected? Yes/No".

3. **Mistake (Cognitive/Intentional Error):** The user intentionally performs the *wrong* action because of an incorrect mental model.
   - *Biomedical Example:* Misreading a **"mcg/kg/min"** dose as **"mg/kg/min"** due to a missing space in the label — gives a 1000× overdose.
   - *Mitigation:* Hard-unit lock (the pump rejects "mg" and only accepts "mcg" for that drug class); prominent space (SI convention).

4. **Abnormal Use / Foreseeable Misuse:** Use outside the intended purpose, but foreseeable.
   - *Biomedical Example:* A clinician **re-uses a single-use ECG electrode** to save cost, causing cross-infection.
   - *Mitigation:* Label with "**SINGLE USE ONLY — DO NOT REUSE**"; packaging that is visibly damaged on opening.

**Hierarchy of Risk Controls** (most → least effective, per ISO 14971 and IEC 62366-1):

1. **Inherent safety by design** (e.g., a connector that cannot be mis-mated, a hard dose limit). **Most effective — design eliminates the hazard.**
2. **Protective measures in the device / manufacturing process** (e.g., guards, interlocks, alarms). *Reduces the probability.*
3. **Information for safety** (e.g., labels, instructions for use, on-screen warnings). *Relies on user attention and comprehension.*
4. **Training, SOPs, and personal protective equipment (PPE).** *Weakest — does not eliminate the hazard, only mitigates its consequence.*

> **Key KTU Point:** Per FDA HFE Guidance, **training alone is not an acceptable control** for a design-induced use error. The designer must exhaust controls 1–3 *before* relying on training.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams (Module 2 — HFE):**
> 1. **Confusing "usability evaluation" with "usability testing."** Evaluation = the entire process; testing = one method within it.
> 2. **Forgetting the units** in Fitts' Law (ID is in **bits**, not seconds). Students lose 1 mark for missing units.
> 3. **Stating "training" as the primary mitigation** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks because it violates IEC 62366-1's inherent-safety-first principle.
> 4. **Mixing up "slip" and "mistake."** Slip = motor; Mistake = cognitive/intentional. This is a favourite 1-mark trap.
> 5. **Not citing the 5th–95th percentile explicitly** in anthropometry questions. Vague phrases like "designed for everyone" score 0.
> 6. **Skipping the regulatory reference** (IEC 62366-1, FDA HFE Guidance) in process questions. Always anchor the answer in the relevant standard.
> 7. **Forgetting $W$ is the *width*, not the *diameter*** in Fitts' Law problems on circular buttons — $W = D$ for circular targets.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MODULE 2 — HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING: 1-Page Rapid Recap**

- **Definition:** HFE = matching device to user (physical, cognitive, sensory, emotional).
- **Three Synonyms:** Ergonomics (EU) = Human Factors (US) = Usability Engineering (process).
- **5 Pillars:** Anthropometry, Sensory, Cognitive, Physical, Organizational/Environmental.
- **5th–95th Percentile Rule:** Cover 90% of users. 5th percentile = small hand/reach; 95th percentile = large clearance.
- **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(2A/W)$ bits. **Smaller target & farther distance → slower.**
- **Hick's Law:** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(N)$. **More choices → slower decisions.**
- **Weber's Law (JND):** $\Delta I = k \cdot I$. **Display step size must exceed JND.**
- **Stevens' Power Law:** Perceived magnitude = $k \cdot \phi^{n}$. Controls display scaling.
- **Reaction Time:** Simple visual ≈ 0.25 s; choice ≈ 0.4 s. Always assume slower in stress.
- **Use Error Categories:** **Slip** (motor), **Lapse** (memory), **Mistake** (wrong mental model), **Abnormal use** (foreseeable misuse).
- **Hierarchy of Controls:** Inherent design → Guards/interlocks → Information (alarms/labels) → Training/PPE. *Inherent is best; training is weakest.*
- **7-Step UE Process (IEC 62366-1):** Use spec → UI characteristics → Identify errors → Define UI → URRA → Formative evaluation → **Summative validation**.
- **Formative vs. Summative:** Formative = *improve* (during design). Summative = *validate* (after design, regulatory).
- **Critical Tasks:** Those whose failure → serious harm. Must be covered in summative test.
- **Key Standards:** IEC 62366-1, ANSI/AAMI HE75, FDA HFE Guidance 2016, ISO 14971, IEC 60601-1-8 (alarms), EU MDR 2017/745 Annex I §9.
- **Alarm Fatigue:** A major HFE issue — too many low-priority alarms desensitize staff to real ones. **Use a tiered alarm system.**
- **Color-Blindness:** 8% of males. Never use color as the *sole* cue. Pair with **shape, label, or position**.
- **Sterile-Field Rule:** Touchscreens fail in sterile field; physical buttons with sleeves or voice control preferred.
- **Memorize 3 example devices:** AED (lay user, intuitive), Infusion pump (nurse, dose entry), Defibrillator (paramedic, fast action).
- **Regulatory Anchor:** Every answer in KTU 2024 should cite the relevant standard (IEC 62366-1 / FDA HFE Guidance).

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 2, HUMAN FACTORS ENGINEERING (PEEBT752)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bill of Materials Preparation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bill of Materials Preparation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is a structured, hierarchical, and exhaustive document that lists every raw material, sub-assembly, intermediate assembly, sub-component, part, software module, packaging material, and quantity required to design, manufacture, assemble, test, package, and service a finished biomedical device. It serves as the **single source of truth** for product definition across Design, Manufacturing, Quality Assurance, Regulatory Affairs, and Supply Chain functions.

In the context of **biomedical device development**, the BOM is not merely a procurement list — it is a **regulatory artefact** governed by international standards such as **ISO 13485:2016 (Clause 8.5.1)**, **FDA 21 CFR Part 820.65**, and **ISO 14971:2019 (Risk Management)**. Every line item in a biomedical BOM must be traceable, with documented evidence of biocompatibility, sterilization compatibility, and material safety.

### Formal Terminology (KTU-Aligned)

| Term | KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **BOM** | Bill of Materials — complete inventory of items needed to build a product |
| **EBOM** | Engineering BOM — design-defined structure from CAD/PLM |
| **MBOM** | Manufacturing BOM — production-optimized structure with process items |
| **SBOM** | Service BOM — spare parts and serviceable components |
| **SWBOM** | Software BOM — list of software modules, libraries, and dependencies |
| **UDI** | Unique Device Identification — FDA-mandated traceability marker |
| **DMR** | Device Master Record — includes BOM as a key element |
| **DHF** | Design History File — regulatory record of design outputs |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Medical Recipe Card"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of the BOM as a **surgical recipe card** used in an operation theatre.

Imagine a cardiac surgeon preparing for bypass surgery. The nurse hands over a checklist that lists *every* instrument, suture, graft material, drug, and consumable — along with the exact quantity and sequence. If the checklist says **"7-0 Prolene suture × 4 units"**, you cannot substitute it with **"5-0 silk × 4 units"** — even if both are sutures. Similarly, a BOM for a pacemaker says **"Ti-6Al-4V titanium casing × 1 unit"** — you cannot swap it for **"316L stainless steel casing"** without a regulatory re-validation cycle that can take **6–18 months** and cost over **$50,000 USD**.

The BOM in biomedical design is therefore **prescriptive, not descriptive** — it is a *legal engineering contract* between the design intent and the manufactured reality.

---

## 1.3 Why BOM is Critical in Biomedical Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The BOM directly affects **patient safety**, **regulatory clearance**, and **manufacturability**. A single missing entry — say, a forgotten lot number for a silicone adhesive — can trigger a Class I, II, or III FDA recall.

Key drivers:
- **Patient Safety:** All materials must be biocompatible (ISO 10993 series).
- **Regulatory Compliance:** FDA, CE-MDR (EU 2017/745), and CDSCO (India) require traceable BOMs.
- **Cost Control:** Medical device BOMs typically account for **30–60%** of the total product cost.
- **Supply Chain Resilience:** Single-source components (e.g., specialized ICs) require risk-mitigation entries in the BOM.
- **Cybersecurity (for software-enabled devices):** SBOMs are mandated by **FDA Premarket Cybersecurity Guidance (2023)**.

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics for BOM Quality

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, the following metrics are emphasized as evaluation criteria for BOM completeness:

- **BOM Accuracy (%):** Percentage of BOM line items that match the as-built product. Industry benchmark: **≥ 99.5%**.
- **BOM Completeness (%):** Ratio of (items documented) / (items in design). Target: **100%**.
- **Cost Roll-up Accuracy:** Variance between estimated and actual cost. Tolerance: **± 5%**.
- **Lead Time Compliance (%):** Percentage of items delivered on time. Target: **≥ 95%**.
- **First-Pass Yield (FPY):** Units passing final test without rework. Benchmark: **≥ 98%** for Class II/III devices.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical BOM Structure

A biomedical device BOM follows a **multi-level indented structure** that mirrors the product's functional decomposition:

```
Level 0 → Finished Device (e.g., Insulin Pump)
  Level 1 → Sub-Assemblies (e.g., Pump Mechanism, Display Module, Battery Pack)
    Level 2 → Sub-Sub-Assemblies (e.g., Stepper Motor Assembly, PCB, Sensor Housing)
      Level 3 → Individual Parts (e.g., M2 Screws, Lithium Cell, OLED Display)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A typical Class II medical device (e.g., blood pressure monitor) has a BOM depth of **4–6 levels** with **150–600 line items**. Class III devices (e.g., implantable pacemakers) average **800–2,500 line items** due to extensive regulatory documentation.

---

## 2.2 Types of BOMs in Biomedical Device Lifecycle

| BOM Type | Phase of Use | Primary Owner | Regulatory Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EBOM (Engineering BOM)** | Design & Development | R&D / Design Engineering | High (feeds DHF) |
| **MBOM (Manufacturing BOM)** | Production & Assembly | Manufacturing Engineering | Highest (feeds DMR) |
| **SBOM (Service BOM)** | After-Sales Service | Service / Field Engineering | Medium (supports CAPA) |
| **SWBOM (Software BOM)** | Software-Enabled Devices | Software / Cybersecurity Team | High (FDA 2023 guidance) |
| **As-Built BOM** | Post-Production | Quality / Document Control | Critical (regulatory audit) |
| **As-Maintained BOM** | Service Life-Cycle | Service Engineering | Critical (UDI tracking) |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **core mathematical tools** used during BOM preparation and cost engineering in biomedical device design. These are high-yield for KTU Part B numerical/analytical questions.

### Table 2.1 — BOM Engineering Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i \times P_i)$ | Total BOM cost = Sum of (Quantity × Unit Price) for all items | USD / INR |
| 2 | $C_{unit} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{N_{batch}}$ | Per-unit cost = Total cost ÷ Batch size | USD/unit |
| 3 | $C_{rolled} = C_{parts} + C_{assembly} + C_{test} + C_{overhead}$ | Rolled-up cost including labor and overhead | USD |
| 4 | $V_{cost} = \dfrac{\vert C_{estimated} - C_{actual} \vert}{C_{estimated}} \times 100\%$ | Cost variance percentage | % |
| 5 | $B_{accuracy} = \dfrac{N_{matched}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | BOM accuracy = matched items / total items | % |
| 6 | $L_{effective} = \max(L_1, L_2, \dots, L_n)$ | Effective lead time = maximum of all component lead times | days |
| 7 | $S_{index} = \dfrac{N_{single-source}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Single-source risk index | % |
| 8 | $R_{scrap} = \dfrac{N_{scrapped}}{N_{produced}} \times 100\%$ | Scrap rate | % |
| 9 | $T_{cycle} = T_{process} + T_{queue} + T_{wait} + T_{move}$ | Total cycle time decomposition | hours |
| 10 | $F_{PY} = \dfrac{N_{passed}}{N_{tested}} \times 100\%$ | First-pass yield | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Used in Formulas:**
> - $Q_i$ = Quantity of item $i$
> - $P_i$ = Unit price of item $i$
> - $N$ = Number (of items, units, batches)
> - $L_i$ = Lead time for component $i$
> - The vertical bar $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes **absolute value** (used in variance calculation).

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

### 2.4.1 Cost Engineering in BOM

The **cost roll-up** process aggregates costs upward from Level $n$ to Level $0$. At each parent assembly, additional costs are added:
- **Direct Labor:** Assembly time × hourly rate
- **Testing Cost:** QC/QA test time × technician rate
- **Overhead:** Typically **150–250%** of direct labor for medical device manufacturing (FDA-compliant cleanrooms are expensive)
- **Regulatory Cost Allocation:** ISO 13485 certification, FDA 510(k) fees amortized per unit

### 2.4.2 Lead Time Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In medical device manufacturing, the **effective lead time** is dominated by the **longest-lead component**, not the average. A single Class III implantable-grade titanium alloy forging can have a lead time of **16–24 weeks**, dictating the entire production schedule.

The single-source risk index $S_{index}$ is critical because:
- A high $S_{index}$ (e.g., $> 20\%$) indicates **supply chain vulnerability**.
- For Class III implants, KTU-aligned best practice recommends $S_{index} \leq 5\%$.

### 2.4.3 Risk-Adjusted BOM (per ISO 14971)

Each BOM line item is tagged with a **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Severity (1–10, 10 = catastrophic patient harm)
- $O$ = Occurrence probability (1–10)
- $D$ = Detectability (1–10, 10 = cannot detect before use)

> [!WARNING]
> A high-RPN component (e.g., a battery in an implantable defibrillator) requires **redundant safety documentation** in the BOM, including failure mode analysis and supplier qualification records.

---

## 2.5 Software BOM (SWBOM) — Modern Requirement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **FDA Premarket Cybersecurity Guidance (September 2023)**, any medical device containing software (SaMD or SiMD) must maintain an **SBOM** listing:
> - All commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) software
> - Open-source libraries (with version numbers)
> - Third-party APIs
> - Operating system dependencies
> - Known vulnerabilities (CVE references)

This is a **post-2023 regulatory requirement** and frequently appears in KTU 2024 Scheme Part A questions.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step BOM Preparation Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to demonstrate the **complete procedural sequence** of BOM preparation for a biomedical device. Below is the exhaustive, evaluative workflow.

### Step 1 — Product Architecture Decomposition

Begin with the **functional block diagram** of the device. For a generic **digital blood pressure monitor**, the architecture is decomposed as:

1. **Cuff & Pneumatic Assembly** (inflation bulb, hose, pressure sensor)
2. **Electronic Assembly** (PCB, microcontroller, display driver)
3. **Power Assembly** (battery, charging circuit)
4. **User Interface Assembly** (LCD/OLED, buttons, buzzer)
5. **Enclosure Assembly** (housing, labels, seals)
6. **Packaging Assembly** (box, IFU, sterile barrier if applicable)

### Step 2 — Engineering Part List Extraction

From the **3D CAD model** and **2D schematic drawings**, extract:
- Part numbers (with revision, e.g., `BPM-PCB-001 Rev-C`)
- Part descriptions
- Quantities per assembly
- Material specifications (e.g., `ABS+PC blend, biocompatible per ISO 10993-5`)
- Supplier information (qualified per ISO 13485 Clause 7.4)

### Step 3 — Classification of Line Items

Classify each item into one of the following KTU categories:
- **C1 — Purchased Part (Off-the-shelf)**
- **C2 — Fabricated Part (Made in-house)**
- **C3 — Sub-Contracted Part (Outsourced process)**
- **C4 — Standard Part (Hardware, fasteners)**
- **C5 — Software/Licensed Item**
- **C6 — Regulatory/Document Item** (IFU, labeling, declaration of conformity)

### Step 4 — Cost Roll-Up Calculation

This is the **most testable step** in KTU examinations. A complete worked derivation follows.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Derivation — Cost Roll-Up

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style 14-Mark Question Pattern):**
>
> *A Class II digital blood pressure monitor has the following BOM. Compute the total per-unit cost, cost variance, and first-pass yield impact.*
>
> **Given Data:**
>
> | Item | Part Description | Qty | Unit Price (₹) | Supplier |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | Cuff assembly with hose | 1 | 180 | MedTech Pvt Ltd |
> | 2 | Pressure sensor (MEMS) | 1 | 420 | SensorCo |
> | 3 | Main PCB (assembled) | 1 | 650 | In-house |
> | 4 | Microcontroller IC (ARM Cortex-M0) | 1 | 95 | ChipSource |
> | 5 | OLED display 1.3" | 1 | 240 | DisplayTech |
> | 6 | Li-ion battery 3.7V 1000mAh | 1 | 130 | PowerCell |
> | 7 | ABS+PC enclosure (top + bottom) | 2 | 85 | PlasticsCo |
> | 8 | Tactile switches | 3 | 8 | StdParts |
> | 9 | Buzzer (piezo) | 1 | 25 | StdParts |
> | 10 | M2.5 screws (fasteners) | 8 | 2 | StdParts |
> | 11 | Labels (UDI compliant) | 1 | 15 | PrintSecure |
> | 12 | IFU (Instructions for Use) | 1 | 12 | PrintSecure |
> | 13 | Master carton + inner box | 1 | 35 | PackPro |

**Additional Parameters:**
- Direct labor per unit: **₹ 95**
- Testing & QC cost: **₹ 40**
- Overhead rate: **200%** of direct labor
- Estimated cost (from feasibility study): **₹ 2,800 per unit**
- Batch size: **1,000 units**
- Tested units: 1,000
- Units passed first test: **972**
- Units scrapped: **18**
- Units reworked and passed: **10**

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### Step 4.1 — Compute Total Component Cost $C_{parts}$

Apply the master formula:
$$
C_{parts} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i \times P_i)
$$

Compute each term explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 1 \times 180 = 180 \\
C_2 &= 1 \times 420 = 420 \\
C_3 &= 1 \times 650 = 650 \\
C_4 &= 1 \times 95 = 95 \\
C_5 &= 1 \times 240 = 240 \\
C_6 &= 1 \times 130 = 130 \\
C_7 &= 2 \times 85 = 170 \\
C_8 &= 3 \times 8 = 24 \\
C_9 &= 1 \times 25 = 25 \\
C_{10} &= 8 \times 2 = 16 \\
C_{11} &= 1 \times 15 = 15 \\
C_{12} &= 1 \times 12 = 12 \\
C_{13} &= 1 \times 35 = 35
\end{aligned}
$$

Now sum all line items:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{parts} &= 180 + 420 + 650 + 95 + 240 + 130 + 170 + 24 + 25 + 16 + 15 + 12 + 35 \\
&= 2{,}012 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The total cost of all components per device is **₹ 2,012**.

---

#### Step 4.2 — Add Direct Labor, Testing, and Overhead

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{labor} &= 95 \; \text{₹} \\
C_{test} &= 40 \; \text{₹} \\
C_{overhead} &= 2.00 \times C_{labor} = 2.00 \times 95 = 190 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total rolled-up cost:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{rolled} &= C_{parts} + C_{labor} + C_{test} + C_{overhead} \\
&= 2{,}012 + 95 + 40 + 190 \\
&= 2{,}337 \; \text{₹ per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 4.3 — Compute Cost Variance

Using the variance formula:
$$
V_{cost} = \dfrac{\vert C_{estimated} - C_{actual} \vert}{C_{estimated}} \times 100\%
$$

Substitute values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cost} &= \dfrac{\vert 2{,}800 - 2{,}337 \vert}{2{,}800} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{463}{2{,}800} \times 100\% \\
&= 16.54\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A cost variance of **16.54%** exceeds the typical KTU/industry tolerance of **± 5%**. The design team should investigate — common causes are incorrect supplier pricing, missed test costs, or scope creep in the BOM.

---

#### Step 4.4 — Compute First-Pass Yield (FPY)

$$
F_{PY} = \dfrac{N_{passed}}{N_{tested}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{PY} &= \dfrac{972}{1{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= 97.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Industry benchmark check:** 97.2% FPY is **acceptable** for Class II devices (typical benchmark $\geq$ 95%) but **below** the Class III target of $\geq$ 99%.

---

#### Step 4.5 — Compute Scrap Rate

$$
R_{scrap} = \dfrac{N_{scrapped}}{N_{produced}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{scrap} &= \dfrac{18}{1{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= 1.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 4.6 — Compute Effective Per-Unit Cost (Adjusted for Scrap)

Scrap cost must be amortized over the good units:

$$
C_{effective} = \dfrac{C_{rolled} \times N_{produced}}{N_{produced} - N_{scrapped}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{effective} &= \dfrac{2{,}337 \times 1{,}000}{1{,}000 - 18} \\
&= \dfrac{2{,}337{,}000}{982} \\
&= 2{,}379.84 \; \text{₹ per good unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 4.7 — Compute Total Batch Cost

$$
C_{batch} = C_{rolled} \times N_{batch}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{batch} &= 2{,}337 \times 1{,}000 \\
&= 23{,}37{,}000 \; \text{₹} = ₹ 23.37 \; \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 4.8 — Single-Source Risk Index

Assume 4 out of 13 items are single-sourced (Items 2, 3, 5, 6):

$$
S_{index} = \dfrac{4}{13} \times 100\% = 30.77\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> $S_{index} = 30.77\%$ is **unacceptably high** for a Class II medical device. KTU 2024 Scheme best practice: **$S_{index} \leq 10\%$** for critical components, **$\leq 20\%$** overall.

---

### 3.3 Summary Result Table

| Metric | Computed Value | Benchmark | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Component cost $C_{parts}$ | ₹ 2,012 | — | Computed |
| Rolled-up cost $C_{rolled}$ | ₹ 2,337 | — | Computed |
| Estimated cost | ₹ 2,800 | — | Reference |
| Cost variance $V_{cost}$ | 16.54% | $\leq$ 5% | ❌ Exceeds |
| First-pass yield $F_{PY}$ | 97.2% | $\geq$ 95% | ✅ Pass |
| Scrap rate $R_{scrap}$ | 1.8% | $\leq$ 2% | ✅ Pass |
| Effective unit cost | ₹ 2,379.84 | — | Computed |
| Batch cost (1,000 units) | ₹ 23.37 Lakhs | — | Computed |
| Single-source index $S_{index}$ | 30.77% | $\leq$ 20% | ❌ Exceeds |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question is structured per KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) regulations. Model answers are concise, definition-grade responses.

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

> **Define Bill of Materials (BOM). List any four types of BOMs used in the lifecycle of a biomedical device.**

#### Model Answer:

A **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is a comprehensive, structured document listing every component, sub-assembly, raw material, software module, and quantity required to design, manufacture, test, and service a biomedical device. It is the single source of truth that links the design intent to the as-built product and feeds directly into the **Device Master Record (DMR)** and **Design History File (DHF)** required by **ISO 13485** and **FDA 21 CFR Part 820**.

**Four Types of BOMs in Biomedical Device Lifecycle:**

1. **EBOM (Engineering BOM):** Design-defined structure extracted from CAD and schematics; used during product development.
2. **MBOM (Manufacturing BOM):** Production-optimized structure with process consumables, fasteners, and routings; used on the shop floor.
3. **SBOM (Software BOM):** List of software components, libraries, and versions for software-enabled medical devices; required by **FDA 2023 Cybersecurity Guidance**.
4. **SBOM (Service BOM):** Spare parts and serviceable components for field maintenance and post-market support.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing and brief description of four types)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Model Question]**

> **Explain the role of a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) in modern medical device compliance. Why is it mandated by the FDA?**

#### Model Answer:

A **Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)** is a formal, machine-readable inventory of all software components, open-source libraries, third-party modules, operating system dependencies, and their version numbers present in a medical device's software stack.

**Role in Compliance:**
- Enables **vulnerability tracking** (CVE references) for known security flaws.
- Supports **post-market cybersecurity surveillance** as required by **FDA Section 524B** of the FD&C Act.
- Facilitates **rapid patch management** in case of zero-day vulnerabilities.
- Provides transparency to regulators, hospitals, and patients about software provenance.

**FDA Mandate:** The **FDA Premarket Cybersecurity Guidance (September 2023)** and the **Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2023 (Section 524B)** require SBOM submission for all medical devices containing software (SaMD and SiMD) that are submitted for premarket review. This was driven by the rising number of cyberattacks on connected medical devices (e.g., insulin pumps, pacemakers, infusion systems).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for role + 1 mark for FDA mandate reasoning)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B offers **internal choice** between two questions. Both options are provided below with complete sub-parts and full model solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

> **(a)** Describe the complete workflow for preparing the **Engineering Bill of Materials (EBOM)** for a Class II biomedical device, with reference to ISO 13485 clauses. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** The following BOM is prepared for a portable pulse oximeter. Compute the **rolled-up cost per unit, cost variance from the estimated ₹ 1,500 target, and first-pass yield**. Assume batch size = 500 units, direct labor = ₹ 60, testing cost = ₹ 30, overhead = 180% of labor, units passed = 478, units scrapped = 12, units reworked = 10. **(7 Marks)**

**BOM Data:**

| Item | Description | Qty | Unit Price (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Finger probe assembly | 1 | 220 |
| 2 | SpO2 sensor (LED + photodiode pair) | 1 | 280 |
| 3 | Main PCB (assembled) | 1 | 380 |
| 4 | Microcontroller IC | 1 | 65 |
| 5 | OLED 0.96" display | 1 | 160 |
| 6 | Li-Po battery 3.7V 600mAh | 1 | 95 |
| 7 | ABS enclosure (top + bottom) | 2 | 55 |
| 8 | Push button | 1 | 6 |
| 9 | M2 screws | 6 | 1.5 |
| 10 | UDI label | 1 | 12 |
| 11 | IFU booklet | 1 | 8 |
| 12 | Packaging box | 1 | 22 |

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

##### Part (a) — EBOM Preparation Workflow (7 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects a **sequential, step-wise answer** with at least 6 well-explained steps. Each step should reference the relevant ISO 13485 clause.

**Step 1 — Product Architecture Decomposition** *(1 mark)*
The device is broken down into functional sub-assemblies. For a pulse oximeter: probe assembly, electronic assembly, power assembly, UI assembly, enclosure, and packaging. This is the input to the EBOM and aligns with **ISO 13485 Clause 7.3 (Design and Development)**.

**Step 2 — Part List Extraction from CAD/Schematics** *(1 mark)*
Using the 3D CAD assembly tree and the 2D electronic schematics, the design engineer generates a preliminary part list. Every part is assigned a unique part number with revision (e.g., `PULSE-PCB-001 Rev-B`). This forms the **EBOM draft** and is documented in the **Design History File (DHF)** per **ISO 13485 Clause 7.3.9 (Design and Development Changes)**.

**Step 3 — Classification and Material Specification** *(1 mark)*
Each line item is tagged with:
- Material grade (e.g., `ABS+PC, biocompatible per ISO 10993-5`)
- Surface finish, tolerance, and special process notes (sterilization, cleaning)
- Classification (Purchased, Fabricated, Sub-contracted, Standard)

This step ensures **ISO 13485 Clause 7.4 (Purchasing)** traceability requirements are met.

**Step 4 — Supplier and Cost Information** *(1 mark)*
For each purchased item, qualified suppliers (per **ISO 13485 Clause 7.4.1**) are listed with unit prices, MOQ, and lead times. Cost data feeds the cost roll-up module.

**Step 5 — Risk and Regulatory Tagging** *(1 mark)*
Each BOM item is tagged with:
- Biocompatibility class (per ISO 10993)
- Sterilization compatibility (EtO, gamma, autoclave)
- UDI assignment (per **FDA 21 CFR 801** and **EU MDR 2017/745 Article 27**)
- Risk Priority Number (per **ISO 14971**)

**Step 6 — Cost Roll-Up and Review** *(1 mark)*
The EBOM is costed using $C_{rolled} = C_{parts} + C_{labor} + C_{test} + C_{overhead}$ and reviewed in a **Design Review** meeting. The EBOM is approved and locked in the **PLM system**.

**Step 7 — Release and Document Control** *(1 mark)*
The approved EBOM is stored in the PLM, with revision control per **ISO 13485 Clause 4.2.4 (Control of Documents)**. It feeds the **Device Master Record (DMR)** and the **Manufacturing BOM (MBOM)**.

---

##### Part (b) — Cost Roll-Up Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Target cost $C_{estimated} = ₹ 1{,}500$ per unit
- Direct labor $C_{labor} = ₹ 60$
- Testing cost $C_{test} = ₹ 30$
- Overhead rate = 180% of labor
- Batch size $N = 500$
- Units passed = 478, Scrapped = 12, Reworked = 10

**Step 1 — Compute Part Cost $C_{parts}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= 1 \times 220 = 220 \\
C_2 &= 1 \times 280 = 280 \\
C_3 &= 1 \times 380 = 380 \\
C_4 &= 1 \times 65 = 65 \\
C_5 &= 1 \times 160 = 160 \\
C_6 &= 1 \times 95 = 95 \\
C_7 &= 2 \times 55 = 110 \\
C_8 &= 1 \times 6 = 6 \\
C_9 &= 6 \times 1.5 = 9 \\
C_{10} &= 1 \times 12 = 12 \\
C_{11} &= 1 \times 8 = 8 \\
C_{12} &= 1 \times 22 = 22
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{parts} &= 220 + 280 + 380 + 65 + 160 + 95 + 110 + 6 + 9 + 12 + 8 + 22 \\
&= 1{,}367 \; \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Overhead:**

$$
C_{overhead} = 1.80 \times 60 = 108 \; \text{₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Rolled-Up Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{rolled} &= C_{parts} + C_{labor} + C_{test} + C_{overhead} \\
&= 1{,}367 + 60 + 30 + 108 \\
&= 1{,}565 \; \text{₹ per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cost Variance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cost} &= \dfrac{\vert 1{,}500 - 1{,}565 \vert}{1{,}500} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{65}{1{,}500} \times 100\% \\
&= 4.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Cost variance is **4.33%**, which is within the typical KTU tolerance of **± 5%**. ✅ **Acceptable.**

**Step 5 — First-Pass Yield:**

$$
F_{PY} = \dfrac{478}{500} \times 100\% = 95.6\%
$$

> **Result:** 95.6% FPY meets the Class II minimum benchmark of 95%. ✅ **Pass.**

**Step 6 — Effective Per-Unit Cost (Adjusted for Scrap):**

$$
C_{effective} = \dfrac{1{,}565 \times 500}{500 - 12} = \dfrac{7{,}82{,}500}{488} = ₹ 1{,}603.48
$$

**Step 7 — Total Batch Cost:**

$$
C_{batch} = 1{,}565 \times 500 = ₹ 7{,}82{,}500
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Component Cost $C_{parts}$ | ₹ 1,367 |
| Rolled-up Cost $C_{rolled}$ | ₹ 1,565 |
| Cost Variance $V_{cost}$ | 4.33% (Acceptable) |
| First-Pass Yield $F_{PY}$ | 95.6% (Pass) |
| Effective Unit Cost | ₹ 1,603.48 |
| Total Batch Cost | ₹ 7,82,500 |

*(7 marks distributed: 1.5 marks each for parts cost, overhead, rolled-up cost, variance, FPY, and effective cost)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

> **(a)** Compare **EBOM, MBOM, and SBOM** with respect to purpose, owner, lifecycle phase, and regulatory weight. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Class III implantable device (cardiac pacemaker) has 5 critical components, of which 3 are single-sourced. The BOM contains 80 line items total. Calculate the **single-source risk index** and discuss the **risk mitigation strategies** that should be incorporated in the BOM. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

##### Part (a) — Comparison of EBOM, MBOM, and SBOM (7 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects a **comparison table** with at least 4 well-articulated parameters. Each parameter should carry 1–2 marks.

| Parameter | EBOM (Engineering BOM) | MBOM (Manufacturing BOM) | SBOM (Software BOM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Defines the product as designed by R&D | Defines the product as built on the shop floor | Lists all software components and dependencies |
| **Owner** | Design Engineering | Manufacturing Engineering | Software / Cybersecurity Team |
| **Lifecycle Phase** | Design & Development | Production & Assembly | Software Lifecycle (Dev, Release, Maintenance) |
| **Items Included** | Designed parts, sub-assemblies | Parts + fasteners, consumables, packaging, tooling | Source code modules, third-party libraries, OS, firmware |
| **Regulatory Reference** | ISO 13485 Clause 7.3, DHF | ISO 13485 Clause 8.5, DMR | FDA 2023 Cybersecurity Guidance, FD&C Act 524B |
| **Change Control** | Engineering Change Request (ECR) | Engineering Change Order (ECO) | Software Change Control Board (SCCB) |
| **Regulatory Weight** | High (feeds DHF) | Highest (feeds DMR — legal record) | High (cybersecurity compliance) |

**Synthesis (1 mark):**
- **EBOM → MBOM** transformation is a one-to-many mapping: a single designed part may expand into multiple manufactured items (e.g., a "PCB assembly" in EBOM becomes a PCB + 47 SMD components + solder paste in MBOM).
- **SBOM** is independent of the physical BOM but must be versioned in parallel for software-enabled devices.
- All three BOMs must remain **synchronized** through the PLM system; any mismatch triggers a non-conformance in the FDA audit.

---

##### Part (b) — Single-Source Risk Analysis (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Critical components: 5
- Single-sourced critical components: 3
- Total BOM line items: 80

**Step 1 — Compute Single-Source Risk Index:**

$$
S_{index} = \dfrac{N_{single-source}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
S_{index} = \dfrac{3}{80} \times 100\% = 3.75\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> At the **overall BOM level**, 3.75% is acceptable (industry target $\leq 20\%$). However, at the **critical component level**, the risk is far higher.

**Step 2 — Compute Critical-Component Single-Source Index:**

$$
S_{critical} = \dfrac{3}{5} \times 100\% = 60\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **60% of critical components are single-sourced** — this is a **severe supply chain risk** for an implantable Class III device. KTU 2024 best practice: $S_{critical} \leq 10\%$ for Class III implants.

**Step 3 — Risk Mitigation Strategies for the BOM (5 marks):**

1. **Dual-Sourcing for Critical Components** *(1 mark)*
   Identify a second qualified supplier for each single-sourced critical item. Validate the second source through **IQ/OQ/PQ** (Installation, Operational, Performance Qualification) per ISO 13485 Clause 7.4.1. Lead time: **6–12 months** for medical-grade supplier qualification.

2. **Safety Stock and Buffer Inventory** *(1 mark)*
   Maintain a **minimum 6-month safety stock** for single-sourced critical items, calculated as:
   $$
   SS = D_{avg} \times L_{max} \times SF
   $$
   where $D_{avg}$ = average monthly demand, $L_{max}$ = maximum lead time, and $SF$ = safety factor (typically 1.5–2.0 for medical implants).

3. **Long-Term Supply Agreements (LTSA)** *(1 mark)*
   Enter into a **5–10 year LTSA** with the single-source supplier, including:
   - Guaranteed supply commitment
   - Quality agreement per **ISO 13485 Clause 7.4.8**
   - Notification clause for component obsolescence
   - Right-to-second-source clause

4. **Design-for-Supply-Chain (DfSC)** *(1 mark)*
