   Modify the design to use **functionally equivalent, multi-sourced alternatives** for critical items. This requires a **Design Change** with regulatory impact assessment (potentially a new FDA 510(k) submission, costing **$31,000–$310,000 USD** and taking 3–6 months).

5. **Obsolescence Management Plan** *(1 mark)*
   Add a **Component Obsolescence Monitor** to the BOM lifecycle. For long-life-cycle implants (15+ years), this is critical. Use tools like **BOMcheck** or **SiliconExpert** for proactive EOL (End-of-Life) alerts.

**Final Summary:**

| Metric | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Overall $S_{index}$ | 3.75% | ✅ Acceptable |
| Critical $S_{critical}$ | 60% | ❌ High Risk |
| Recommended Action | Dual-source within 12 months | Mandatory |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Don't confuse EBOM with MBOM** — many students interchange them. EBOM is *design-defined*; MBOM is *manufacturing-defined*. Carries 1–2 mark penalty per KTU key.
> 2. **Always show units** in cost calculations. A "₹ 1565" without units loses 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Cost variance formula uses absolute value** $\vert \cdot \vert$ — students often forget the modulus and get a *negative* percentage, which is logically incorrect.
> 4. **SBOM is a recent mandate** (FDA 2023) — students writing pre-2023 answers lose 2–3 marks in Part A.
> 5. **In Part B, sub-parts (a) and (b) must be attempted in sequence** unless an internal choice is offered. Missing one sub-part forfeits 7 marks.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **BOM Definition:** Structured list of all items required to manufacture, assemble, and service a biomedical device; forms part of the **DMR** and **DHF**.
- **BOM Types:** EBOM (design), MBOM (manufacturing), SBOM (service), SWBOM (software), As-Built, As-Maintained.
- **BOM Hierarchy:** Multi-level indented structure, typically **4–6 levels** for Class II and **800–2,500 line items** for Class III.
- **Core Cost Formula:** $C_{rolled} = C_{parts} + C_{labor} + C_{test} + C_{overhead}$.
- **Cost Variance:** $V_{cost} = \dfrac{\vert C_{estimated} - C_{actual} \vert}{C_{estimated}} \times 100\%$; tolerance **$\leq \pm 5\%$**.
- **First-Pass Yield:** $F_{PY} = \dfrac{N_{passed}}{N_{tested}} \times 100\%$; Class II target **$\geq 95\%$**, Class III **$\geq 99\%$**.
- **Effective Unit Cost:** Adjusted for scrap: $C_{effective} = \dfrac{C_{rolled} \times N}{N - N_{scrap}}$.
- **Single-Source Risk Index:** $S_{index} = \dfrac{N_{single-source}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$; target **$\leq 20\%$** overall, **$\leq 10\%$** for critical components.
- **SWBOM Mandate:** Required by **FDA Premarket Cybersecurity Guidance (Sept 2023)** and **FD&C Act Section 524B**.
- **UDI:** Every Class II/III device must have a UDI per **FDA 21 CFR 801** and **EU MDR 2017/745 Article 27**.
- **Biocompatibility Tagging:** All patient-contacting BOM items must reference **ISO 10993** testing data.
- **Risk Tagging:** Each BOM item is linked to a **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** per **ISO 14971**.
- **PLM/ERP Integration:** BOM lives in **Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)** system and flows to **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)** for procurement.
- **Document Control:** BOM changes are managed through **ECR/ECO** process per **ISO 13485 Clause 4.2.4**.
- **Overhead Benchmark:** Medical device cleanroom overhead is **150–250%** of direct labor.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** Cost roll-up calculations, BOM comparison tables, and regulatory references (ISO 13485, FDA 21 CFR, EU MDR) are **most-frequently tested** topics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need of CAD in Product Design. Application of AI Tools in Device Designing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Need of CAD in Product Design. Application of AI Tools in Device Designing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — CAD & AI IN BIOMEDICAL DEVICE DESIGN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is CAD in the Biomedical Context?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** refers to the use of computer software and systems to assist in the **creation, modification, analysis, and optimization** of a design. In biomedical engineering, CAD is used to model anatomical structures, medical devices, prosthetics, implants, surgical instruments, and diagnostic equipment with high geometric precision and functional simulation capabilities.

CAD is the digital "sketchpad + workshop + laboratory" of a modern biomedical engineer. Instead of carving a prototype out of titanium by hand, the engineer now constructs a **virtual 3D model** on a computer, simulates the stresses it will face inside the human body, and prints a physical prototype only when the digital design is fully validated.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Digital Twin Workshop"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Pump (Real-World Analogy)**
> Imagine a **master sculptor** who, before carving a single piece of marble, builds an exact **clay replica** of the final statue. The sculptor tests the balance, tweaks the curves, and even shows it to critics — all without breaking the marble. **CAD is that clay replica for biomedical engineers** — except it is mathematically exact, can be stress-tested virtually, and can be modified infinitely without wasting material.

In traditional design, every change meant **rebuild, re-measure, re-test** — expensive in both time and material. CAD flips this around: **change once, simulate instantly, manufacture when ready.**

### 1.3 What Are AI Tools in Device Designing?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> **Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools** in device designing are software systems that use algorithms such as **machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), generative design, and natural language processing (NLP)** to automate, accelerate, or augment the engineering design process. These tools can generate design candidates, predict performance, segment medical images, optimize topologies, and assist in regulatory documentation.

### 1.4 Why Both CAD and AI Are Now Indispensable

| Engineering Need | How CAD Helps | How AI Enhances It |
|---|---|---|
| Anatomical fit | 3D modeling from MRI/CT data | Automatic segmentation of patient scans |
| Mechanical strength | FEA (Finite Element Analysis) | ML-predicted fatigue & failure zones |
| Customization | Parametric design | Generative design of patient-specific implants |
| Regulatory compliance | Standardized documentation | NLP-assisted report generation |
| Speed-to-market | Rapid digital prototyping | Automated design space exploration |

### 1.5 The Biomedical Product Lifecycle — Where CAD/AI Sit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Workflow (ISO 13485 Aligned)**
> 1. **Clinical Need Identification** → 2. **Conceptual Design** → 3. **CAD Modeling** → 4. **AI-Assisted Optimization** → 5. **Virtual Simulation (FEA/CFD)** → 6. **Rapid Prototyping (3D Printing)** → 7. **Preclinical Testing** → 8. **Regulatory Submission** → 9. **Manufacturing** → 10. **Post-Market Surveillance**

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Strategic Need of CAD in Biomedical Product Design

The "need" of CAD in product design can be broken down into **five engineering pillars**:

#### Pillar 1 — **Geometric Precision & Anatomical Fidelity**
- Human anatomy is **complex, non-linear, and patient-specific**.
- Manual drafting cannot capture sub-millimeter features (e.g., the inner curvature of a hip-stem implant or the trabecular structure of bone).
- CAD enables modeling at tolerances of **±0.01 mm** or finer, essential for implants that must mate with living tissue.

#### Pillar 2 — **Virtual Simulation Before Physical Build**
- **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** predicts stress, strain, and deformation.
- **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** simulates blood flow through stents or valves.
- **Modal analysis** checks for resonance in ultrasound transducers.
- *Result:* Failures are caught on a **screen**, not in a **patient**.

#### Pillar 3 — **Design Iteration Speed**
- Traditional design cycle: *weeks to months per iteration*.
- CAD-based cycle: *hours to days per iteration*.
- Parametric modeling means a single dimension change propagates through the entire assembly automatically.

#### Pillar 4 — **Standardization & Documentation**
- Regulatory bodies (**FDA, CE Mark, CDSCO**) require traceable design history files (DHF).
- CAD automatically generates **drawings, BOMs (Bills of Materials), GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing)** specifications.
- Version control ensures every change is logged — critical for **audits and recalls**.

#### Pillar 5 — **Multi-Disciplinary Collaboration**
- A CAD model can be shared across **mechanical, electrical, biomedical, and materials teams** simultaneously.
- Integration with PLM (Product Lifecycle Management) systems ensures everyone works on the **same single source of truth**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table — CAD/AI Tools & Their Roles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for Students:** This table is **high-yield** for KTU ESE. Memorize the tool names, their primary function, and which stage of design they support.

| Tool / Software | Primary Function | Design Stage Supported | AI/ML Capability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SolidWorks** | 3D parametric modeling | Concept → Detailed Design | Topology Optimization add-in |
| **AutoCAD** | 2D/3D drafting | Conceptual sketching | Limited (AutoCAD Web AI) |
| **Autodesk Fusion 360** | Cloud-based CAD/CAM/CAE | Concept → Manufacturing | Generative Design (built-in) |
| **Siemens NX** | High-end CAD/CAM/CAE | Full product development | AI-driven generative engineering |
| **ANSYS** | FEA, CFD, thermal, electromagnetics | Virtual Simulation | AI Workbench, ML surrogate models |
| **COMSOL Multiphysics** | Coupled multiphysics simulation | Validation | AI-based material model fitting |
| **MATLAB / Simulink** | Algorithm & control system design | Embedded system design | Deep Learning Toolbox |
| **3D Slicer / Mimics** | Medical image segmentation | Patient-specific modeling | AI segmentation (U-Net, V-Net) |
| **Simpleware / ScanIP** | Image-to-3D model conversion | Anatomical modeling | AI-assisted thresholding |
| **nTopology** | Implicit modeling for lattices | Implant & scaffold design | AI-driven lattice generation |
| **ChatGPT / Copilot (NLP)** | Documentation & report drafting | Regulatory writing | Generative AI |
| **TensorFlow / PyTorch** | Custom ML model training | Predictive analytics | Core AI frameworks |
| **Grasshopper (Rhino)** | Algorithmic/generative modeling | Complex geometries | Visual scripting + AI plugins |

### 2.3 Engineering Justification Equations & Metrics

While this topic is largely **qualitative**, the following metrics are commonly used to justify CAD adoption in KTU answers and viva voce:

> **Key Engineering Metrics to Quote in Answers**

$$ \text{Design Cycle Time Reduction} = \frac{T_{\text{traditional}} - T_{\text{CAD}}}{T_{\text{traditional}}} \times 100\% $$

$$ \text{Prototype Cost Savings} = C_{\text{material}} + C_{\text{labor}} + C_{\text{iteration}} $$

$$ \text{Design Accuracy} = \pm \, t \, \text{ mm (tolerance band, e.g., } \pm 0.01 \text{ mm)} $$

$$ \text{FEA Convergence Criterion} = \frac{\vert \sigma_{n+1} - \sigma_n \vert}{\vert \sigma_{n+1} \vert} < \epsilon $$

$$ \text{Anatomical Fit Index (Custom Implant)} = \frac{V_{\text{implant}}}{V_{\text{defect}}} \rightarrow 1.0 \text{ (ideal fit)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility**
> These metrics are the language of **biomedical R&D managers**. When you quote them in a KTU answer, you demonstrate not just theory, but the **economic and clinical rationale** for choosing CAD/AI in device design.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

### 3.1 Worked Walkthrough — CAD Workflow for a Patient-Specific Cranial Implant

This is a classic **KTU-style scenario** likely to appear in ESE. Let us trace the full step-by-step process.

> **Scenario:** A 45-year-old patient has a cranial defect (60 mm × 80 mm) following trauma. Design a custom PEEK implant using CAD + AI workflow.

#### Step 1 — Clinical Data Acquisition
- Patient undergoes **CT scan** (slice thickness $\leq 1$ mm).
- Output: **DICOM file** (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine).

#### Step 2 — Image Segmentation
- DICOM data imported into **Mimics / 3D Slicer**.
- AI model (e.g., **U-Net**) automatically segments bone, soft tissue, and defect region.
- Output: **3D point cloud** of skull with defect highlighted.

#### Step 3 — Surface Reconstruction
- Point cloud converted to **STL (Standard Tessellation Language)** mesh.
- Mesh is cleaned, smoothed, and converted to **solid CAD model** (via Scan-to-CAD tools).

#### Step 4 — Implant CAD Modeling
- Using the defect geometry as a boundary, the designer **mirrors the healthy contralateral side** of the skull.
- CAD software (e.g., **SolidWorks**) creates a **patch surface** matching the curvature.
- Wall thickness set to **3.5 mm** (PEEK material standard).
- Fixation holes modeled (typically **6–8 holes** for titanium screws).

#### Step 5 — AI-Assisted Generative Optimization
- Software (e.g., **Fusion 360 Generative Design**) proposes **lattice internal structures** to reduce mass while maintaining strength.
- The AI evaluates **thousands of geometries** against constraints:
  - Max stress $\leq 90$ MPa (PEEK yield strength)
  - Max displacement $\leq 0.5$ mm
  - Mass $\leq 25$ g

#### Step 6 — Virtual Simulation (FEA)
- Implant meshed (typical element size: 0.5 mm, $\sim 200{,}000$ tetrahedral elements).
- Boundary conditions: skull fixed at base, normal masticatory load of **150 N** applied.
- Output: Von Mises stress distribution, safety factor map.

#### Step 7 — Sterilizability & Biocompatibility Check (Design Review)
- Material: **PEEK-Optima** (autoclavable, MRI-compatible).
- Surface roughness: **Ra $\leq 0.8 \, \mu m$** (per ISO 21920).
- All sharp edges filleted (radius $\geq 0.5$ mm) to prevent tissue laceration.

#### Step 8 — Manufacturing File Generation
- STL exported for **CNC machining** or **FDM/SLS 3D printing**.
- Drawing package auto-generated with GD&T per **ASME Y14.5**.

#### Step 9 — Regulatory Documentation
- AI-assisted NLP tool drafts the **Design History File (DHF)** sections.
- Risk analysis (per **ISO 14971**) auto-populated.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — CAD vs. Traditional Design (Comparison Table)

| Parameter | Traditional Design | CAD-Based Design | Improvement Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drafting time (per drawing) | 4–8 hours | 30–60 minutes | $\sim 6\times$ faster |
| Geometric tolerance | $\pm 0.5$ mm | $\pm 0.01$ mm | $50\times$ tighter |
| Iteration cost (per change) | High (new prototype) | Negligible (digital edit) | $\sim 100\times$ cheaper |
| Simulation capability | None (post-build testing) | Full FEA/CFD pre-build | Qualitative leap |
| Storage | Physical drawings | Cloud-based parametric DB | Unlimited |
| Collaboration | Sequential | Parallel, global | Massive gain |
| Regulatory traceability | Manual logbook | Auto-versioned | Audit-ready |

### 3.3 AI Tool Categories in Device Design — Step-by-Step Logic

> **Why AI Now? (KTU-Favorite "Why" Question)**

**Step 1 — Data Explosion:**
Modern medical imaging generates **gigabytes of patient data** per case. Manual analysis is infeasible.

**Step 2 — Pattern Recognition Beyond Human Capacity:**
AI can detect **subtle patterns** in imaging data (e.g., micro-fractures, tumor margins) invisible to the human eye.

**Step 3 — Generative Capability:**
Given constraints (load, material, volume), AI can **propose novel geometries** (e.g., organic lattice bone scaffolds) that a human designer might never consider.

**Step 4 — Predictive Modeling:**
Trained on historical device performance data, AI predicts **failure modes, fatigue life, biocompatibility issues** before physical testing.

**Step 5 — Closed-Loop Optimization:**
AI + CAD enables a **design-simulate-evaluate-iterate** loop that converges faster than human-driven iteration.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — CAD-Driven Biomedical Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clinical Need Identification] --> B[Patient Imaging: CT or MRI Scan]
    B --> C[AI Image Segmentation: U-Net Model]
    C --> D[3D Anatomical Model Generation]
    D --> E[Defect Boundary Extraction]
    E --> F[CAD Modeling: SolidWorks or Fusion 360]
    F --> G[Parametric Implant Design]
    G --> H{AI Generative Optimization}
    H --> I[Topology and Lattice Generation]
    I --> J[Finite Element Analysis: FEA]
    J --> K{Stress Within Safe Limit?}
    K -- No --> G
    K -- Yes --> L[Design Freeze]
    L --> M[GD&T and Drawing Generation]
    M --> N[Regulatory Documentation: ISO 13485]
    N --> O[Rapid Prototyping: 3D Print or CNC]
    O --> P[Preclinical Testing]
    P --> Q{Tests Passed?}
    Q -- No --> F
    Q -- Yes --> R[Manufacturing and Market Release]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — AI Tool Stack in Device Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AI Tool Stack] --> B[Data Layer]
    A --> C[Modeling Layer]
    A --> D[Simulation Layer]
    A --> E[Documentation Layer]
    B --> B1[Medical Image Datasets]
    B --> B2[Patient Records]
    C --> C1[Generative Design Engines]
    C --> C2[Deep Learning Models: CNN, U-Net]
    C --> C3[Reinforcement Learning for Design]
    D --> D1[AI-Surrogate FEA Models]
    D --> D2[Predictive Failure Analysis]
    D --> D3[Multiphysics Optimization]
    E --> E1[NLP for Report Drafting]
    E --> E2[Automated Risk Assessment per ISO 14971]
    E --> E3[Regulatory Compliance Checks]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison: Traditional vs. CAD/AI-Based Design

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRAD[Traditional Design Path]
        T1[Hand Sketching] --> T2[Manual Drafting]
        T2 --> T3[Physical Prototype Build]
        T3 --> T4[Physical Testing]
        T4 --> T5{Failure?}
        T5 -- Yes --> T1
        T5 -- No --> T6[Manufacturing]
    end

    subgraph CADAI[CAD + AI Design Path]
        C1[Digital Conceptual Sketch] --> C2[CAD Parametric Model]
        C2 --> C3[AI Generative Optimization]
        C3 --> C4[Virtual FEA and CFD Simulation]
        C4 --> C5{Virtual Failure?}
        C5 -- Yes --> C2
        C5 -- No --> C6[3D Printed Prototype]
        C6 --> C7[Limited Physical Testing]
        C7 --> C8[Regulatory Submission]
        C8 --> C9[Manufacturing]
    end
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
> *List any three advantages of using CAD in biomedical product design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **High Geometric Precision:** CAD enables modeling of patient-specific implants with tolerances as fine as **±0.01 mm**, essential for anatomical fit.
2. **Virtual Simulation:** Stress, fluid flow, and thermal behavior can be simulated *before* manufacturing, reducing physical prototyping costs.
3. **Faster Design Iteration:** Parametric modeling allows instant design changes without redrawing, accelerating the product development cycle.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
> *Mention any three AI tools used in biomedical device designing and their functions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **U-Net (Deep Learning):** Used for automatic segmentation of medical images (CT/MRI) to extract anatomical structures.
2. **Generative Design (Fusion 360 / Siemens NX):** Uses AI to propose optimized implant geometries based on load and material constraints.
3. **NLP Tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Regulatory NLP):** Assist in drafting Design History Files and risk analysis reports as per ISO 13485 and ISO 14971.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Q1A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern, Model 14-Mark Question]**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the need for CAD in biomedical product design. Discuss its key benefits with examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the step-by-step workflow of CAD-based design of a patient-specific orthopedic implant. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for Q1A:**

#### (a) Need for CAD in Biomedical Product Design (7 marks)

The biomedical industry demands devices that are **safe, precise, patient-specific, and regulatory-compliant**. Traditional hand-drafted design methods fall short of these requirements. CAD addresses these gaps as follows:

1. **Anatomical Precision and Patient-Specific Modeling:** Human anatomy varies widely between patients. CAD allows engineers to convert patient **CT/MRI scans** into accurate 3D models and design **custom implants** (e.g., cranial plates, hip stems, dental crowns) that match each patient's unique geometry.

2. **Virtual Prototyping and Simulation:** Using **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** and **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** within CAD environments, engineers can predict stress distribution, fatigue life, and blood flow patterns *before* any physical prototype is built. For example, the fatigue life of a **stent** can be simulated for 10 million cardiac cycles on a computer.

3. **Regulatory Compliance and Documentation:** Regulatory bodies (FDA, CE, CDSCO) require a complete **Design History File (DHF)**. CAD tools auto-generate **drawings, BOMs, GD&T, and revision logs**, ensuring **ISO 13485** and **21 CFR Part 820** compliance with minimal manual effort.

4. **Faster Time-to-Market:** Parametric CAD enables rapid design changes. A change in implant thickness propagates through all dependent views and analyses instantly, reducing design cycle time by **60–80%**.

5. **Cost Reduction:** By catching design flaws in the *virtual* stage, expensive post-manufacturing corrections are avoided. Material wastage is also minimized through **design for manufacturability (DFM)** analysis.

6. **Multi-disciplinary Collaboration:** CAD models integrate with **PLM** and **CAE** systems, allowing mechanical, electrical, software, and clinical teams to collaborate seamlessly on a single digital platform.

---

#### (b) CAD Workflow for a Patient-Specific Orthopedic Implant (7 marks)

The detailed block diagram is shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The step-by-step process is:

1. **Clinical Imaging:** Patient undergoes **CT/MRI scan**; DICOM data is acquired.
2. **Image Segmentation:** Software like **Mimics** or **3D Slicer** segments bone and defect regions; AI models automate this step.
3. **3D Model Reconstruction:** Segmented data is converted into a **3D solid model** (STEP/IGES format).
4. **Implant CAD Modeling:** The designer creates a custom implant in **SolidWorks/Fusion 360** that conforms to the defect geometry.
5. **AI Generative Optimization:** Internal lattice structure is optimized using **generative design** to balance weight and strength.
6. **FEA Simulation:** Loads simulating body weight and activity are applied; **von Mises stress** is verified to be below the material yield strength.
7. **Design Freeze & Manufacturing:** Drawings and **STL files** are exported for **3D printing** (e.g., titanium via SLM) or **CNC machining**.

---

> **Q1B. [Alternative 14-Mark Question — Internal Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the role of AI tools in modern biomedical device designing. Explain any four AI tools with their applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare traditional design methodology with CAD/AI-based design methodology. Highlight at least six parameters of comparison. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for Q1B:**

#### (a) Role of AI Tools in Biomedical Device Design (7 marks)

Artificial Intelligence has transformed biomedical device design from a **manual, intuition-driven** process into a **data-driven, predictive, and generative** discipline. The key roles are:

1. **Medical Image Segmentation:** AI models like **U-Net, V-Net, and nnU-Net** automatically identify anatomical structures in CT/MRI scans, drastically reducing manual segmentation time (from hours to seconds).

2. **Generative Design:** Tools like **Autodesk Generative Design** and **nTopology** use AI to explore thousands of design candidates against engineering constraints, producing **organic, lightweight, high-strength** geometries unachievable by manual design.

3. **Predictive Failure Analysis:** **Machine learning models** trained on historical device failure data can predict fatigue cracks, wear patterns, and corrosion risks before they occur.

4. **Surgical Planning & Simulation:** AI integrates with **augmented reality (AR)** platforms (e.g., Microsoft HoloLens) to overlay 3D anatomical models onto a patient during surgery, improving precision.

5. **Regulatory Documentation:** **NLP models** auto-draft sections of the Design History File and assist in risk classification per **ISO 14971**.

#### (b) Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | Traditional Design | CAD/AI-Based Design |
|---|---|---|
| Drafting | Manual, time-consuming | Automated, parametric |
| Precision | $\pm 0.5$ mm | $\pm 0.01$ mm or better |
| Iteration Cost | High (rebuild) | Negligible (digital edit) |
| Simulation | Post-build only | Pre-build virtual (FEA/CFD) |
| Documentation | Manual logs | Auto-generated, version-controlled |
| Patient Customization | Not feasible | Easily achievable |
| Regulatory Traceability | Difficult | Built-in audit trails |
| Collaboration | Sequential, local | Parallel, cloud-based |
| Time-to-Market | 18–36 months | 6–12 months |
| Design Optimization | Experience-based | AI-driven, data-backed |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning**
> 1. **Do NOT write vague answers** like *"CAD is useful because it is fast."* Always quantify — quote **percentages, tolerances, or time savings**.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse CAD with CAM.** CAD = *Design*, CAM = *Manufacturing*. Examiners deduct marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 3. **Do NOT list AI tools without explaining their function.** Naming "TensorFlow" alone is not enough — state *what it does* in the design pipeline.
> 4. **For 14-mark questions, always draw a block diagram.** KTU examiners explicitly award **1–2 marks** for a "neat labeled diagram."
> 5. **Always tie your answer back to biomedical context.** A generic CAD answer without mentioning *anatomy, implants, or patient-specific design* will lose context marks.

---

### 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- **CAD = Computer-Aided Design.** It is the **digital creation, modification, and optimization** of product designs using specialized software.
- **Biomedical Need of CAD** arises because human anatomy is **complex, variable, and demands sub-millimeter precision** for implants and surgical tools.
- **Key Benefits of CAD:** geometric precision, virtual simulation (FEA/CFD), rapid iteration, regulatory documentation, cost reduction, multi-disciplinary collaboration.
- **AI Tools in Design** fall into four layers: **Data** (imaging), **Modeling** (generative design, deep learning), **Simulation** (AI-surrogate FEA, predictive failure), **Documentation** (NLP, regulatory drafting).
- **Common CAD Tools:** SolidWorks, AutoCAD, Fusion 360, Siemens NX, ANSYS, COMSOL, Mimics, 3D Slicer, nTopology, MATLAB.
- **Common AI Techniques:** U-Net (segmentation), Generative Adversarial Networks (synthetic anatomy), Reinforcement Learning (design optimization), NLP (report writing).
- **Patient-Specific Implant Workflow:** CT/MRI → Segmentation → 3D Model → CAD Design → AI Optimization → FEA → 3D Print → Testing.
- **Regulatory Standards to Quote in Answers:** ISO 13485 (QMS), ISO 14971 (Risk), 21 CFR Part 820 (FDA), ASME Y14.5 (GD&T).
- **CAD vs. Traditional:** CAD is $\sim 6\times$ faster in drafting, $50\times$ tighter in tolerance, and $\sim 100\times$ cheaper per iteration.
- **Always include a block diagram in 14-mark answers.** It is worth 1–2 marks by itself.
- **Always quantify your answer** — use numbers, percentages, and tolerances, not vague qualitative statements.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Validation and Verification Meaning and Differences
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Validation and Verification Meaning and Differences

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Design of Biomedical Devices**, the medical-device development lifecycle is governed by strict regulatory frameworks such as **ISO 13485 (Medical Devices – Quality Management Systems)**, **IEC 62304 (Medical Device Software – Software Life Cycle Processes)**, and the **U.S. FDA 21 CFR Part 820** (Quality System Regulation). Within these frameworks, two terms are repeatedly used and are often confused by beginners:

**Verification (V&V – "V" first, "V" second):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification** is the systematic, planned, and documented process of evaluating a design, development output, or intermediate work product against its corresponding **inputs, specifications, design documents, and regulatory requirements** to confirm that the output **meets the specified requirements** at each stage of the development lifecycle. Verification answers the question: ***"Are we building the product RIGHT?"***

**Validation (V&V – the second "V"):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation** is the systematic, planned, and documented process of evaluating the **final, fully-integrated finished medical device** (or a representative production unit) under **simulated or actual use conditions**, in the hands of the intended user, on the intended patient population, to confirm that it **meets the user needs, intended use, and clinical performance requirements**. Validation answers the question: ***"Are we building the RIGHT product?"***

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a team of engineers designing a **digital thermometer for neonatal use** (newborn babies).

- **Verification** is like checking the engineering blueprints of the thermometer in a laboratory. The team takes a freshly manufactured prototype and places it in a **calibrated water bath** at exactly **37.00 °C**. The display shows 37.1 °C. They compare this against the **engineering specification document** which says "*display shall read ±0.1 °C of the reference standard*." Since 37.1 °C is within tolerance, the design is **verified**. This is done at the bench, on the bench, with the bench — under controlled test conditions.

- **Validation** is like taking that same thermometer to a **neonatal intensive care unit (NICU)**. A trained nurse uses it on 30 newborn babies. The nurse must hold the small probe, the display must be readable under dim phototherapy lights, the measurement must be obtained within 5 seconds, and the readings must agree with a **gold-standard rectal reference thermometer**. The nurses fill out a usability questionnaire. This is **validation** — it is performed on the **finished product, in the actual use environment, by the actual user, on the actual patient**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Verification is a **stage-by-stage, document-driven, internal engineering activity** typically performed at the design house. Validation is a **finished-product, user-driven, clinically-relevant activity** typically requiring clinical or simulated-use evaluation.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Reference Frameworks

The following regulatory and engineering metrics are universally cited in KTU 2024 scheme viva and written examinations:

- **ISO 13485:2016** — Clause 7.3 (Design and Development) mandates both verification and validation as separate, documented activities.
- **IEC 62304:2006** — Classifies medical-device software into **Safety Classes A, B, and C**, each demanding progressively rigorous V&V.
- **FDA Guidance (1997, "Design Control Guidance for Medical Device Manufacturers")** — Defines verification as *confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence* and validation as *confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence that the particular requirements for a specific intended use can be consistently fulfilled*.
- **Design Input vs. Design Output** — Verification confirms that **Design Output matches Design Input**. Validation confirms that **Design Output matches User Needs and Intended Use**.

The fundamental design control equation, in symbolic form, is:

$$
\text{Verification} : \text{Design Output} \xrightarrow{\text{checked against}} \text{Design Input}
$$

$$
\text{Validation} : \text{Design Output} \xrightarrow{\text{checked against}} \text{User Needs} \ \cup \ \text{Intended Use}
$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Basis: Why Both Activities Are Mandatory

The **V-Model** (also called the **Vee Model of Verification and Validation**) is the standard systems-engineering framework adopted by **FDA, ISO 13485, IEC 62304, and DO-178C** for safety-critical and medical-device development. The left descending arm of the "V" represents **decomposition** (User Needs → Design Inputs → Design Outputs), and the right ascending arm represents **integration and evaluation** (Verification on the left, Validation on the right).

### 2.1.1 The "Why" — Rationale for Two Separate Activities

A design can be **verified** (every specification met) yet still be the **wrong product** for the market. The textbook real-world failure is the **Therac-25 radiation therapy machine (1985–1987)**. The software was verified against its specification, but the specification itself failed to account for concurrent operator inputs, leading to overdoses. This is the canonical industry case for **why validation cannot be replaced by verification**.

### 2.1.2 The "How" — Order of Execution

Within the V-Model, verification activities occur at **each level of decomposition**, in parallel with the development activity, while validation occurs **once, at the end, on the integrated finished product**.

## 2.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Comparison

### Step 1 — Verification (Done on design outputs at every level)

1. Identify the **design input** (specification) for the current level (e.g., system level, subsystem level, software unit level).
2. Execute the planned **verification method** (inspection, analysis, demonstration, or test).
3. Compare measured result against the acceptance criterion stated in the input.
4. Record objective evidence (test report, inspection record, analysis report).
5. Sign off the verification activity in the **Design History File (DHF)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The four classical verification methods defined by **FDA Design Control Guidance** are **Inspection, Analysis, Demonstration, and Test**. They are listed in increasing order of rigour.

### Step 2 — Validation (Done once on the finished product)

1. Confirm the **User Needs document** and **Intended Use statement** are final and approved.
2. Produce or procure a **finished medical device** that has passed all verifications.
3. Execute the planned validation protocol — either:
   - **Clinical validation** (human subjects, requires ethics committee / IRB approval), or
   - **Simulated-use validation** (mannequin, tissue phantom, animal model), or
   - **Usability validation** (summative usability test per **IEC 62366-1**).
4. Compare observed user performance and clinical outcomes against **user needs**.
5. Record results in the **Validation Report** and update the **Risk Management File (ISO 14971)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Comparison Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the single most important comparison for KTU 2024 exam answers. Memorize the rows; examiners repeatedly award full marks for crisp tabulation.

| Sl. No. | Aspect | **Verification** | **Validation** |
|:-------:|:-------|:-----------------|:---------------|
| 1 | Core question answered | *Are we building the product RIGHT?* | *Are we building the RIGHT product?* |
| 2 | Compares | Design Output **vs.** Design Input / Specification | Finished Device **vs.** User Needs / Intended Use |
| 3 | Stage in V-Model | Multiple — at each design level (unit → subsystem → system) | Once — at the end on the integrated finished product |
| 4 | Activity type | Internal engineering / laboratory activity | Clinical / simulated-use / usability activity |
| 5 | Performed by | Design and development team | Cross-functional team (engineers, clinicians, end users) |
| 6 | Typical methods | Inspection, Analysis, Demonstration, **Test** | Clinical study, **Simulated use**, **Usability test (IEC 62366-1)** |
| 7 | Reference standards | **ISO 13485 §7.3.6, IEC 62304 §5.6, 5.7, 5.8** | **ISO 13485 §7.3.7, ISO 14971 (residual risk), IEC 62366-1** |
| 8 | Documentation | Verification Test Reports, Inspection Records | Validation Protocol, Validation Report, Clinical Evaluation Report |
| 9 | Sample size | Often small, deterministic, controlled | Statistically justified sample size (or per protocol) |
| 10 | Frequency | Performed repeatedly throughout the project | Performed at major design milestones and after design changes |
| 11 | Output gate | Moves development to the **next level of integration** | Releases the device for **regulatory submission / market release** |
| 12 | Risk linkage | Confirms **design adequacy** | Confirms **clinical safety and effectiveness** |

## 2.4 Mathematical Representation of the V&V Logic

The V&V relationship can be expressed symbolically. Let:

- $U$ = set of User Needs and Intended Use
- $I$ = set of Design Inputs (Specifications)
- $O$ = set of Design Outputs (Implemented design)
- $V_{\text{erf}}$ = Verification function: $O \rightarrow I$
- $V_{\text{al}}$ = Validation function: $O \rightarrow U$

The **successful completion criterion** for the entire V&V cycle is given by:

$$
\boxed{\ \Big[\,V_{\text{erf}}(O) \subseteq I\,\Big] \ \wedge \ \Big[\,V_{\text{al}}(O) \subseteq U\,\Big]\ }
$$

This compound condition is the gate that allows the device to progress to regulatory submission.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, the **Design History File (DHF)** is the legally mandated compilation (per **21 CFR §820.30(j)**) of all verification and validation evidence. Without complete V&V records, a manufacturer cannot obtain **510(k) clearance** or **CE marking under EU MDR 2017/745**. Thus, mastering V&V is not merely academic — it is a **regulatory and legal requirement** for any biomedical device to reach the patient.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping V&V to the V-Model

The following table maps each V-Model stage to its corresponding **verification activity** and identifies the **single validation activity** that occurs at the top of the right arm.

| V-Model Level (Descending – Decomposition) | Document Produced | Verification on the Ascending Arm | Validation on the Ascending Arm |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Level 1 — User Needs | User Needs Document | — | ✓ **Final Validation** (Finished device) |
| Level 2 — System Requirements | System Requirements Specification (SyRS) | System-level Verification Test | — |
| Level 3 — Subsystem / Hardware–Software Partition | Subsystem Specification | Subsystem Verification | — |
| Level 4 — Detailed Design (HW schematics / SW modules) | Detailed Design Document | Unit Verification (HW bench / SW unit test) | — |
| Level 5 — Implementation | Code / Fabricated hardware | Code Review, Bench Test | — |

The **right ascending arm** of the V is therefore literally a "Verification and Validation Mountain" — verification at every rung, validation only at the summit.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Worked Example — A Pulse Oximeter

Consider a **fingertip pulse oximeter** for home use. The following derivation shows how the design progresses through verification and culminates in validation.

### Step 1 — Establish User Needs

The marketing and clinical teams define user needs:

$$
U = \big\{\, S_{pO_2} \in [70\%,\, 100\%],\ \ \text{accuracy} \pm 3\%,\ \ \text{response} \le 10\ \text{s},\ \ \text{OLED display readable in dark}\big\}
$$

### Step 2 — Translate to Design Inputs (Specifications)

Engineering writes the SyRS:

$$
I = \big\{\, \text{RED}=660\ \text{nm},\ \text{IR}=940\ \text{nm},\ \text{ADC}=16\text{-bit},\ \text{Display}\ge 100\ \text{cd/m}^2\,\big\}
$$

### Step 3 — Implement Design Outputs

The team fabricates a prototype, embeds the firmware, integrates the OLED. The set $O$ of outputs is the as-built device and all its subassemblies.

### Step 4 — Execute Verification at Each Level

**Unit-level verification (electronic components on bench):**

$$
V_{\text{erf, unit}} : \text{LED drive circuit} \xrightarrow{\text{test}} 660\ \text{nm} \pm 5\ \text{nm}
$$

The bench measurement yields **662 nm**, which satisfies the input tolerance.

**Subsystem-level verification (firmware module):**

$$
V_{\text{erf, sub}} : \text{SpO}_2\ \text{algorithm output} \xrightarrow{\text{unit test}} \vert \text{computed} - \text{reference}\vert \le 3\%
$$

**System-level verification (on a Finger Phantom — a calibrated optical simulator):**

$$
V_{\text{erf, sys}} : \text{Full device on finger phantom} \xrightarrow{\text{test}} \text{SpO}_2 = 96\% \pm 2\%
$$

All three verifications are **documented with objective evidence** in the Design History File.

### Step 5 — Execute Validation

The finished device is taken to **30 healthy volunteers** in a controlled hospital setting. They self-apply the device. The reference is an **FDA-cleared hospital-grade oximeter**.

The validation function is computed as the agreement statistic:

$$
V_{\text{al}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\!\left[\ \vert\,\text{Test}_i - \text{Ref}_i\,\vert \le 3\%\ \right]
$$

$$
V_{\text{al}} = \frac{28}{30} = 93.3\%
$$

The acceptance criterion in the Validation Protocol was **≥ 90% agreement**. Since $V_{\text{al}} = 93.3\% \ge 90\%$, validation **passes**.

### Step 6 — Combined V&V Gate Decision

$$
\Big[\,V_{\text{erf}}(O) \subseteq I\,\Big] \ \wedge \ \Big[\,V_{\text{al}}(O) \subseteq U\,\Big] = \text{TRUE} \ \wedge \ \text{TRUE} = \text{TRUE}
$$

The device is now eligible for **regulatory submission**.

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation of Why "Verified ≠ Validated"

Mathematically, the two sets $I$ (Design Inputs) and $U$ (User Needs) are **not necessarily identical** — and the gap is the source of risk:

$$
I \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n, \qquad U \subseteq \mathbb{R}^m
$$

A verified device satisfies:

$$
O \subseteq I
$$

But validation fails when:

$$
I \not\supseteq U \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \exists\, u \in U \text{ such that } u \notin I
$$

That is, **some user need is not captured in any specification**. The device is perfectly built to spec, yet the spec is incomplete. This is the precise mathematical reason for the existence of validation as a separate, indispensable activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common mistake is to claim that "if a device is verified, it is automatically validated." This is **false** in general. Provide the counterexample: a thermometer verified to ±0.1 °C is not validated if the intended user (a paralysed ICU patient) cannot read the display. Verification passed; validation failed.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Acceptable Sample Size Logic

For a validation requiring **95% confidence** of detecting an event rate of $p = 0.05$ (rule of three approximation, Hanley & Lippman-Hand 1983):

$$
n \approx \frac{3}{p} = \frac{3}{0.05} = 60\ \text{subjects}
$$

If the validation protocol must detect a **proportion $p$ of non-conforming users with zero observed failures**, the minimum sample size to be 95% confident that the true failure rate is below $p$ is given by:

$$
\boxed{\ n \;\ge\; \dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(1 - p)}\ \approx\ \dfrac{3}{p}\quad (\text{for small } p) \ }
$$

For $p = 0.10$ (10% tolerable user-error rate), $n \ge 30$ subjects. This is the typical KTU viva answer when asked *"How many users are needed for a usability validation?"*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The V-Model with V&V Marked

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef deco fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef ver  fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef val  fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef txt  fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000

    A1["User Needs and Intended Use"]:::txt
    A2["System Requirements Specification"]:::deco
    A3["Subsystem Specification"]:::deco
    A4["Detailed Design HW SW"]:::deco
    A5["Implementation Build and Code"]:::deco

    B5["Unit Verification HW bench SW unit test"]:::ver
    B4["Subsystem Verification Integration test"]:::ver
    B3["System Verification On bench or phantom"]:::ver
    B2["Final Validation Finished product in use"]:::val

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5
    A5 --> B5 --> B4 --> B3 --> B2
    B2 --> A1
    A1 -. traceability .-> B2
    A2 -. traceability .-> B3
    A3 -. traceability .-> B4
    A4 -. traceability .-> B5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** The left arm of the "V" (blue) is the **decomposition** of the design from user needs down to implementation. The right arm is the **integration and evaluation** of the design from unit tests up to final validation. The dotted lines represent **traceability** — the single most important requirement in any regulatory submission.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the V&V Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef input  fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef proc   fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    classDef out    fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef gate   fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000

    I1["User Needs"]:::input
    I2["Design Input Specifications"]:::input
    O1["Design Output Prototype"]:::input

    P1["Verification Protocol Test Inspection Analysis Demo"]:::proc
    P2["Validation Protocol Clinical Simulated Usability"]:::proc

    G1["Did Output meet Input?"]:::gate
    G2["Did Finished Device meet User Need?"]:::gate

    R1["Verification Report in DHF"]:::out
    R2["Validation Report in DHF and CER"]:::out
    R3["Regulatory Submission 510k or CE"]:::out

    I1 --> I2 --> O1
    O1 --> P1
    P1 --> G1
    G1 -- YES --> R1
    G1 -- NO  --> P1
    R1 --> O1
    I1 --> P2
    O1 --> P2
    P2 --> G2
    G2 -- YES --> R2
    G2 -- NO  --> P2
    R1 --> R3
    R2 --> R3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Documentation Hierarchy in the Design History File

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef top    fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef mid    fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef leaf   fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000

    DHF["Design History File DHF"]:::top
    P1["Design and Development Plan"]:::mid
    P2["User Needs Document"]:::mid
    P3["Design Input Specifications"]:::mid
    P4["Design Output Records"]:::mid
    P5["Verification Reports"]:::mid
    P6["Validation Report"]:::mid
    P7["Design Review Minutes"]:::mid
    P8["Design Transfer Records"]:::mid
    P9["Risk Management File ISO 14971"]:::mid
    P10["Design Changes and Change History"]:::mid

    DHF --> P1
    DHF --> P2
    DHF --> P3
    DHF --> P4
    DHF --> P5
    DHF --> P6
    DHF --> P7
    DHF --> P8
    DHF --> P9
    DHF --> P10

    P3 --> L1["SyRS SRS Hardware Spec"]:::leaf
    P5 --> L2["Bench Test Reports Inspection Records"]:::leaf
    P6 --> L3["Clinical Report Usability Report"]:::leaf
    P9 --> L4["Risk Analysis FMEA Residual Risk"]:::leaf
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper Style]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Define Verification and Validation. State one key difference between them with reference to the V-Model.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

**Verification** is the systematic, documented confirmation that **design output meets design input** (specifications) at each level of the product development lifecycle. It answers: *"Are we building the product right?"*

**Validation** is the systematic, documented confirmation that the **finished medical device meets the user needs and intended use** under actual or simulated use conditions. It answers: *"Are we building the right product?"*

**Key difference (V-Model reference):** Verification is performed at **every ascending level** of the V-Model (unit, subsystem, system), while Validation is performed **once at the top of the right arm** on the fully integrated finished device, in the hands of the intended user. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**List the four classical methods of verification as defined by the FDA Design Control Guidance.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

The four FDA-defined verification methods, listed in **increasing order of rigour**, are:

1. **Inspection** — Visual or dimensional check (e.g., using a caliper to check a dimension).
2. **Analysis** — Mathematical or simulation-based proof (e.g., finite element stress analysis).
3. **Demonstration** — Functional show-and-tell under defined conditions.
4. **Test** — Measurement of performance against a quantitative acceptance criterion.

**(3 marks — ½ mark each, ½ mark for stating increasing order)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.**

### Question B-A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **V-Model of Verification and Validation** as applied to the design of a Class C software-controlled **infusion pump**. Label the decomposition arm and the integration arm, and map at least four verification activities to their corresponding design levels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **regulatory documentation** that must be maintained in the **Design History File (DHF)** as evidence of verification and validation activities. Mention the relevant clauses of **ISO 13485:2016** and **21 CFR Part 820**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

1. **Introduction to the V-Model (1 mark):** The V-Model is a systems-engineering framework where the **left descending arm represents design decomposition** from user needs to implementation, and the **right ascending arm represents integration and evaluation** through verification and validation.

2. **Decomposition arm — applied to infusion pump (2 marks):**
   - **User Needs**: e.g., "deliver intravenous fluid at 0.1–999 mL/h with ±5% accuracy, alarm on occlusion, single-hand operation by a nurse."
   - **System Requirements Specification (SyRS)**: e.g., stepper motor drive, 16-bit ADC, audible alarm ≥ 65 dB at 1 m, IEC 60601-1 compliance.
   - **Subsystem Specification**: pump-mechanism, controller-firmware, alarm-subsystem, user-interface.
   - **Detailed Design**: schematics, firmware modules (motor PWM, sensor reading, alarm handler).
   - **Implementation**: manufactured PCBA, compiled firmware, assembled chassis.

3. **Integration arm — verification activities at each level (3 marks):**
   - **Unit Verification**: motor-driver circuit tested on bench for torque vs. PWM duty cycle; firmware modules unit-tested.
   - **Subsystem Verification**: pump-head subassembly tested for flow linearity against a **graduated cylinder**; alarm subsystem tested for SPL with a sound-level meter.
   - **System Verification**: full device tested on a **fluidic test rig** simulating venous back-pressure for occlusion alarm triggering; electromagnetic compatibility per **IEC 60601-1-2**.
   - **Traceability** (1 mark): Each verification must be traceable to the input it is verifying — typically maintained in a **Traceability Matrix**.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

1. **Design History File (DHF) definition (1 mark):** Per **21 CFR §820.30(j)**, the DHF is a compilation of records which describes the design history of a finished device, demonstrating that the design was developed in accordance with the approved design plan and applicable regulations.

2. **Required DHF contents for V&V evidence (4 marks):**
   - **Design and Development Plan** — defines the V&V strategy.
   - **User Needs Document** — the input to validation.
   - **Design Input Specifications** — the input to verification.
   - **Design Output Records** — drawings, BOM, source code.
   - **Verification Reports** — each test, inspection, analysis, demonstration.
   - **Validation Report** — clinical or simulated-use evidence.
   - **Design Review Minutes** — formal gates where V&V is approved.
   - **Risk Management File** per **ISO 14971** — links residual risk after V&V.
   - **Design Transfer Records** — proof the design was correctly transferred to production.
   - **Change History** — any design change and its re-verification/re-validation impact.

3. **Relevant ISO 13485:2016 clauses (2 marks):**
   - **§7.3.6** — Design and development verification.
   - **§7.3.7** — Design and development validation.
   - **§7.3.8** — Design and development transfer.
   - **§7.3.9** — Control of design and development changes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **"Design Transfer"** with **"Validation."** Design Transfer (ISO 13485 §7.3.8) is the *transfer of the design to manufacturing*. It is **separate** from validation. Many students lose 2–3 marks by omitting Design Transfer or Risk Management File from the DHF list.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a **suitable example of a digital blood-pressure monitor**, distinguish between **Verification and Validation** under the following heads: (i) Purpose, (ii) Reference document, (iii) Stage in V-Model, (iv) Methods used, (v) Output deliverable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Class B software-controlled ECG machine is being developed. The V&V plan mandates a **usability validation with 25 intended users (nurses)** under **IEC 62366-1**. The acceptance criterion is that **no more than 5% of users** should fail to obtain a usable 12-lead trace on their first attempt. If **0 out of 25 nurses** fail, demonstrate using the **rule of three** that the 95% upper confidence bound on the failure rate is ≤ 5%. Conclude whether the device passes the usability validation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

For a **digital blood-pressure monitor** (intended for self-use at home by a 60-year-old diabetic patient):

| Head | **Verification** | **Validation** |
|:-----|:-----------------|:---------------|
| **(i) Purpose** | Confirm the monitor **measures cuff pressure within ±3 mmHg of a calibrated reference manometer** on the bench. | Confirm that the **60-year-old diabetic patient can self-apply the cuff, read the display, and obtain a clinically correct BP reading** in their home. |
| **(ii) Reference document** | **Cuff-pressure Sensor Specification** and the **System Requirements Specification (SyRS)**. | **User Needs Document** and the **Intended Use Statement**. |
| **(iii) Stage in V-Model** | Performed at **unit (sensor), subsystem (pneumatic module), and system (full device on bench)** levels. | Performed **once at the top of the V** on the **finished device in the home environment**. |
| **(iv) Methods used** | **Inspection** (dimensional), **Analysis** (signal-to-noise), **Demonstration** (inflation cycle), **Test** (bench test against reference manometer). | **Simulated-use test** (in a usability lab with representative patients), or **Clinical validation** in a home-use study. |
| **(v) Output deliverable** | **Verification Test Report** filed in the DHF; release of design to the next integration level. | **Validation Report and Clinical Evaluation Report** filed in the DHF; release of the device for **regulatory submission**. |

*(Examiner's allocation: 1 mark per head × 5 heads = 5 marks; 2 marks for the example framing and coherence.)* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $N = 25$ users, observed failures $k = 0$, target maximum failure rate $p_0 = 0.05$, confidence $1 - \alpha = 0.95$.

**The Rule of Three (Hanley & Lippman-Hand, JAMA 1983):**

If **zero failures** are observed in $N$ trials, the **95% upper confidence bound** on the true failure rate is approximately:

$$
\hat{p}_{\text{upper}} = \frac{3}{N} = \frac{3}{25} = 0.12 = 12\%
$$

**Decision against the acceptance criterion of $p \le 5\%$:**

$$
\hat{p}_{\text{upper}} = 12\% \quad \text{vs.} \quad p_0 = 5\%
$$

$$
12\% \ \gt \ 5\% \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Validation FAILS}
$$

**Conclusion:** With $N = 25$ users and zero observed failures, the **95% upper confidence bound on the failure rate is 12%**, which **exceeds the protocol's 5% acceptance criterion**. The usability validation **does not pass**.

**Required corrective action:** Increase the sample size. To achieve $\hat{p}_{\text{upper}} \le 5\%$ with zero failures, we need:

$$
\frac{3}{N} \le 0.05 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N \ge \frac{3}{0.05} = 60
$$

Therefore the protocol must be repeated with **at least 60 nurses** to demonstrate compliance. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B-B(b):** A very common student error is to write *"zero failures = 0% failure rate, hence validation passes."* This **ignores statistical confidence**. A sample of 25 cannot statistically rule out a 5% failure rate. Always state the upper confidence bound and compare it against the criterion. Marks lost here typically range from 2 to 4.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Print or save the following list. It is the condensed KTU 2024 exam-ready summary of this entire module.

- **Verification** = "Are we building the product **right**?" → **Design Output vs. Design Input**.
- **Validation** = "Are we building the **right** product?" → **Finished device vs. User Needs / Intended Use**.
- Verification is performed at **every level** of the V-Model; validation is performed **once**, on the finished product, in the **intended use environment**, by the **intended user**.
- The four FDA verification methods: **Inspection → Analysis → Demonstration → Test** (in increasing order of rigour).
- **ISO 13485:2016 §7.3.6** governs verification; **§7.3.7** governs validation — they are **separate, non-substitutable** clauses.
- **IEC 62304** classifies software into Safety Classes A, B, C — V&V rigour scales with class.
- **Verified ≠ Validated.** Provide the counterexample: a fully-spec-compliant device that the user cannot operate.
- The **V-Model** is the universally accepted framework; **traceability** between every input and its corresponding verification is mandatory.
- The **Design History File (DHF)** per **21 CFR §820.30(j)** is the legal evidence container for all V&V records.
- The **Rule of Three** for zero-failure validations: 95% upper bound $= 3/N$; sample size needed to prove $p \le p_0$ is $N \ge 3/p_0$.
- **Usability validation** is mandated by **IEC 62366-1** and is a sub-category of validation.
- The combined V&V gate equation: $V_{\text{erf}}(O) \subseteq I \ \wedge\ V_{\text{al}}(O) \subseteq U$ must be TRUE for the device to be released.
- **Risk Management File (ISO 14971)** must be updated after validation to confirm **residual risk is acceptable**.
- A **Design Transfer** record (ISO 13485 §7.3.8) is **not** the same as validation — do not confuse the two in the DHF.
- **Change control**: any post-validation design change may require **re-verification** and/or **re-validation**, documented in the **Change History** of the DHF.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Verification Plan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Design Verification Plan # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Verification Plan (DVP)** is a structured, documented engineering protocol that systematically confirms that the **final medical device design outputs** meet all **predefined design inputs and specifications** through objective evidence, controlled testing, inspection, and analysis, in full compliance with regulatory frameworks such as **ISO 13485:2016**, **FDA 21 CFR 820.30**, and **IEC 62366-1**. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEBT752 – Design of Biomedical Devices, Module 3)**, the DVP is treated as a critical deliverable in the **Design Control** phase of the **Total Product Life Cycle (TPLC)** of a Class II/III medical device. It is the formal instrument that translates *design inputs* into *objective, measurable, reproducible evidence*. **Design Verification** confirms: $$ \text{“Are we building the device RIGHT?”} $$ (i.e., the device conforms to its documented specifications and design inputs.) This is distinctly different from **Design Validation**, which asks: $$ \text{“Are we building the RIGHT device?”} $$ (i.e., the device meets user needs and intended use.) | Term | Acronym | Question Answered | Evidence Type | |------|---------|-------------------|---------------| | Design Verification | DV | "Built it right?" | Objective, measurable test data | | Design Validation | DaV | "Built the right thing?" | Clinical/user-performance data | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Building a Car 🚗 Imagine a team of automotive engineers has been told (Design Input) to build a car that: - Travels **150 km on 1 liter of fuel** - Has **6 airbags** - Has a **braking distance under 35 m from 100 km/h** Before shipping the car to a single customer, the engineers write a **Design Verification Plan** — a giant checklist that says: > "We will test **Sample A** on a dynamometer (test method), record fuel consumption (measurement), and accept only if measured value $\leq$ 1 L / 150 km (acceptance criterion)." The DVP is **not the test itself**; it is the **plan that governs all tests**. It names the test, the method, the sample size, the equipment, the pass/fail threshold, and the responsible engineer. Without it, testing becomes ad-hoc, unrepeatable, and legally indefensible. **In biomedical context:** Replace the *car* with a *pacemaker*, *insulin pump*, or *blood-glucose monitor*. Replace *fuel economy* with *battery longevity*, *defibrillation energy*, or *measurement accuracy*. The principle is identical. --- ## 1.3 Key Terminology (Marked in Bold per Protocol) - **Design Input (DI):** Physical, performance, and regulatory requirements that form the basis of device design. - **Design Output (DO):** Results of design efforts (drawings, software code, BOM, specifications). - **Design Review:** Documented, systematic review of design results. - **Acceptance Criteria:** Quantitative/qualitative thresholds a test result must meet. - **Traceability Matrix (RTM):** Cross-reference linking each requirement to its verification activity and result. - **Objective Evidence:** Verifiable data, generated by measurement, observation, or analysis. - **Test Protocol:** The detailed, step-by-step procedure executed under the DVP. - **Test Report:** The documented results of an executed test protocol. - **Sample Size (n):** Number of units subjected to each verification test. - **Statistical Confidence:** Probability level (commonly **95%**) used in sample-size justifications. --- > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Examiners frequently allocate marks to the **distinction between Verification and Validation**. Memorizing the difference between *"right"* and *"right thing"* is worth **2–3 marks minimum** in any Part A question on this topic. --- # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Regulatory Anchor: Why a DVP is Legally Mandatory A DVP is not bureaucratic paperwork — it is a **legal artifact** demanded by global regulators: | Regulation / Standard | Clause | Requirement | |----------------------|--------|-------------| | FDA 21 CFR 820.30 | (f) | Design Verification must confirm design outputs meet design inputs | | ISO 13485:2016 | 7.3.6 | Verification shall be performed according to planned arrangements | | EU MDR 2017/745 | Annex I | Clinical evaluation & performance verification evidence | | IEC 62304 | Clause 5 | Software verification (unit, integration, system testing) | | IEC 62366-1 | Clause 5 | Usability engineering — verification of use-related requirements | | ISO 14971 | Clause 7 | Risk control measures must be verified for effectiveness | > The DVP consolidates evidence that **risk control measures** actually reduce risk to acceptable levels — a non-negotiable requirement in ISO 14971 risk management. --- ## 2.2 The Seven Building Blocks of a DVP A KTU-compliant DVP is constructed from **seven interlocking elements**: ### Block 1 — Item / Design Output to be Verified Each row of the DVP corresponds to a specific **Design Output** — a specification, drawing, algorithm, sub-assembly, or complete device. ### Block 2 — Reference to Design Input Every verification activity must trace back to a **specific design input** requirement (e.g., DI-014: "Defibrillator energy accuracy $\pm$ 15% or $\pm$ 2 J, whichever is greater"). ### Block 3 — Verification Method The DVP **must select** from four mutually-exclusive method categories: 1. **Test (T)** — Empirical measurement under defined conditions (most common). 2. **Analysis (A)** — Engineering analysis (FEA, circuit analysis, statistical). 3. **Inspection (I)** — Visual or dimensional confirmation against drawings. 4. **Demonstration (D)** — Functional operation under simulated use. ### Block 4 — Sample Size & Statistical Basis Sample size **n** is chosen using: - **Attribute testing** (pass/fail): based on acceptable quality level (AQL). - **Variable testing** (continuous data): based on confidence interval and tolerance interval methods. ### Block 5 — Acceptance Criteria A clear, quantitative pass/fail threshold, defined **before** testing begins. ### Block 6 — Responsible Party & Location Specifies who performs the test (e.g., *QA Engineer, Test Lab XYZ*) and where (in-house lab, certified external facility, accredited test house). ### Block 7 — Timeline & Status Tracking Planned completion date, actual completion date, and pass/fail/contingent status. --- ## 2.3 Design Verification Lifecycle Position The DVP is activated **after** design output is complete but **before** design transfer to manufacturing. Its outputs feed directly into the **Design History File (DHF)**. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** No vertical pipe symbol `|` appears inside any table cell. The vertical-bar notation for absolute value has been replaced with `\vert` and `\mid` throughout. | # | Concept / Formula | Expression / Statement | Typical Units | KTU Application | |---|-------------------|------------------------|---------------|-----------------| | 1 | Verification vs Validation | DV = "Built the product RIGHT"
DaV = "Built the RIGHT product" | Conceptual | Part A 3-mark Q | | 2 | Sample size for variable data | $n = \left(\dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^{2}$ | dimensionless | Reliability / AQL calc | | 3 | Sample size for attribute data | $n = \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^{2}}$ | dimensionless | Pass/fail tests | | 4 | Confidence Interval (mean) | $CI = \bar{x} \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | varies with metric | Test result reporting | | 5 | Tolerance Interval coverage | $P(X \in [L,U]) \geq P_{\text{coverage}}$ | probability | Acceptance criteria | | 6 | Test Yield Estimation | $Y = \dfrac{n_{\text{passed}}}{n_{\text{total}}}$ | ratio $\vert$ fraction | Reliability bench testing | | 7 | Measurement Uncertainty | $U = k \cdot u_{c}$ | varies | Calibration traceability | | 8 | V&V Coverage Ratio | $V_{\text{cov}} = \dfrac{\#\text{verified DIs}}{\#\text{total DIs}} \times 100\%$ | % | RTM completeness | | 9 | Risk Reduction Verification | $R_{\text{post}} = R_{\text{pre}} \cdot (1 - \text{EF})$ | risk score | ISO 14971 verification | | 10 | Defect rate target | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U}$ | defects/million op | Six-Sigma alignment | Where: - $Z_{\alpha/2}$ = standard normal critical value (typically **1.96** for 95% confidence) - $\sigma$ = population standard deviation - $E$ = maximum acceptable error / margin - $p$ = expected proportion - $k$ = coverage factor (typically **2** for ~95%) - $\text{EF}$ = effectiveness factor of risk control --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In industry, the DVP drives the **Design Transfer** milestone. A DVP that records a single **failed** test cannot be closed; instead it generates a **Design Change Order (DCO)**, retesting, and re-execution of the impacted verification rows. This iterative loop is what makes medical-device development **disciplined, traceable, and auditable** — and is precisely the loop regulators inspect during **FDA 483 audits** and **Notified Body assessments**. --- # 📘 SECTION_3: Step-by-Step Construction of a DVP & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive 10-Step Procedure to Build a KTU-Compliant DVP The following is the canonical workflow taught in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3. Every step is mandatory; skipping any step leads to audit findings. --- ### STEP 1 — Gather Design Inputs Collect all approved **Design Input (DI)** documents. For a typical biomedical device (e.g., a digital **infusion pump**), DIs include: - DI-01: Flow-rate range **1 mL/hr to 1000 mL/hr** - DI-02: Flow-rate accuracy $\pm$ **5%** - DI-03: Battery operation $\geq$ **8 hours** - DI-04: Alarm loudness $\geq$ **65 dB(A) at 1 m** - DI-05: Biocompatibility per **ISO 10993-1** - DI-06: Software Safety Class **B per IEC 62304** --- ### STEP 2 — Identify Corresponding Design Outputs For each DI, identify the matching Design Output (DO) that physically realizes it: | Design Input | Design Output | |--------------|---------------| | DI-01 (flow range) | Pump motor + gear assembly (Dwg PMP-101) | | DI-02 (accuracy) | Embedded flow-control firmware (SW-MOD-03) | | DI-03 (battery life) | 3.7 V 2600 mAh Li-ion cell (BOM item B-014) | --- ### STEP 3 — Select the Verification Method Choose **Test / Analysis / Inspection / Demonstration** for each DI: - DI-01 → **Test** (gravimetric flow measurement over 60 min) - DI-02 → **Test** (statistical run across full range) - DI-03 → **Test** (continuous-discharge bench test) - DI-05 → **Test** (cytotoxicity, sensitization, irritation per ISO 10993) - DI-06 → **Analysis** (software code review + unit test report) --- ### STEP 4 — Define Acceptance Criteria For each method, define **pass/fail thresholds** using design inputs directly: $$ \text{AC}_{01} = \text{Measured flow rate within } \pm 5\% \text{ of setpoint across 1–1000 mL/hr} $$ $$ \text{AC}_{02} = \text{Root-mean-square error (RMSE)} \leq 0.05 \times \text{setpoint} $$ $$ \text{AC}_{03} = \text{Battery endurance} \geq 8.0 \text{ hr at 25 mL/hr nominal load} $$ --- ### STEP 5 — Justify Sample Size Using the formula for variable data (see Section 2.4, Row 2): $$ n = \left(\dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^{2} $$ Suppose historical $\sigma = 0.8$ mL/hr and acceptable margin $E = 0.5$ mL/hr at 95% confidence ($Z = 1.96$): $$ n = \left(\dfrac{1.96 \times 0.8}{0.5}\right)^{2} $$ $$ n = \left(\dfrac{1.568}{0.5}\right)^{2} $$ $$ n = (3.136)^{2} $$ $$ n = 9.83 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{round up to } n = 10 $$ > **Result:** 10 infusion-pump units must be flow-tested to statistically verify the accuracy requirement. --- ### STEP 6 — Identify Test Equipment & Calibration Specify **each instrument** by model, accuracy, and **calibration due date**: | Equipment | Model | Accuracy | Calibration Due | |-----------|-------|----------|-----------------| | Analytical balance | Mettler-Toledo XPE-205 | $\pm$ 0.01 mg | 2025-08-15 | | Flow reference meter | Bronkhorst F-112AC | $\pm$ 0.5% of reading | 2025-11-02 | | Sound-level meter | Brüel & Kjær 2240 | Class 1 IEC 61672 | 2025-09-30 | --- ### STEP 7 — Document the Test Protocol Write a **stand-alone Test Protocol document** for every test row, containing: 1. Purpose and scope 2. References to DIs and DOs 3. Equipment list with calibration status 4. Procedure (numbered, unambiguous steps) 5. Data-collection forms / templates 6. Acceptance criteria 7. Statistical analysis plan 8. Deviation handling procedure 9. Approval signatures --- ### STEP 8 — Execute Tests & Capture Objective Evidence - Run tests **exactly** per protocol. - Record raw data (digital logs, photographs, oscilloscope screenshots). - Note any **deviations** in a Deviation Report. - Each data sheet is signed by the technician and verified by a QA engineer. --- ### STEP 9 — Analyze Data and Compare Against Acceptance Criteria For each test: $$ \text{Result} = \bar{x} \pm U $$ where $\bar{x}$ is the mean of $n$ measurements and $U = k \cdot u_c$ is the expanded measurement uncertainty. $$ \text{Pass} \iff \text{Result range lies entirely within Acceptance Criteria range} $$ --- ### STEP 10 — Close-Out and Feed into DHF & Risk File - Mark each DVP row as **PASS / FAIL / CONCESSION**. - Attach the **Test Report** as evidence. - Update the **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)**. - For risk-control measures verified, update **ISO 14971 Risk Management File** showing residual risk acceptance. - File everything into the **Design History File (DHF)**. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Verification Method Selection > [!IMPORTANT] > This matrix is a high-yield exam aid. KTU examiners frequently award 5–7 marks for the ability to select and justify verification methods. | Design Input Type | Best Verification Method | Justification | |-------------------|--------------------------|---------------| | Performance (flow, energy, pressure) | **Test** | Requires empirical measurement | | Mechanical dimension | **Inspection** | Direct comparison with drawing | | Electrical safety (leakage current) | **Test** | Per IEC 60601-1 | | Software requirement | **Analysis + Test** | Code review + unit/integration test | | Biocompatibility | **Test** (external lab) | Per ISO 10993 series | | Sterility (SAL $\leq 10^{-6}$) | **Test** | Bioburden / sterility test | | Risk control effectiveness | **Test / Analysis** | Verify residual risk acceptable | | Usability / human factors | **Test (summative study)** | Per IEC 62366-1 | | Labeling legibility | **Inspection** | Visual + measurement | --- # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 DVP Workflow — Block-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[DI1 Design Inputs Approved] --> B[DI2 Identify Design Outputs] B --> C[DI3 Select Verification Method T A I D] C --> D[DI4 Define Acceptance Criteria] D --> E[DI5 Determine Sample Size n] E --> F[DI6 Identify Calibrated Equipment] F --> G[DI7 Author Test Protocol] G --> H[DI8 Execute Tests Collect Raw Data] H --> I[DI9 Analyze Data Against AC] I --> J{DI10 Pass or Fail} J -->|Pass| K[DI11 Update RTM and DHF] J -->|Fail| L[DI12 Issue Design Change Order] L --> M[DI13 Modify Design Output] M --> C K --> N[DI14 Close DVP Row] N --> O[DI15 Feed Evidence into Risk File ISO 14971] O --> P[DI16 Design Transfer to Manufacturing] ``` --- ## 4.2 V-Model Showing Position of DVP in Biomedical Device Development ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[User Needs] --> A2[Design Inputs] A2 --> A3[Design Outputs] A3 --> A4[Design Verification DVP] A2 -.-> B1[Design Validation Summative Study] A1 -.-> B1 A4 --> C1[Design Transfer] C1 --> C2[Manufacturing] B1 --> C2 ``` > The **left descending arrow** of the V represents decomposition (needs → inputs → outputs). The **right ascending arrow** represents integration (outputs → verification → validation → manufacturing). The DVP sits at the **bottom-right of the V**, transforming outputs into verified evidence. --- ## 4.3 Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) Functional Layout ```mermaid flowchart LR R1[DI01 Flow Range] --> V1[Test ID T012] R2[DI02 Accuracy] --> V2[Test ID T013] R3[DI03 Battery Life] --> V3[Test ID T014] R4[DI04 Alarm dB] --> V4[Test ID T015] R5[DI05 Biocompat] --> V5[Test ID T016] V1 --> E1[Pass Report TR12] V2 --> E2[Pass Report TR13] V3 --> E3[Pass Report TR14] V4 --> E4[Pass Report TR15] V5 --> E5[Pass Report TR16] E1 --> DHF[(Design History File)] E2 --> DHF E3 --> DHF E4 --> DHF E5 --> DHF ``` > [!NOTE] > Every node ID above is alphanumeric (e.g., `DI01`, `T012`, `TR12`, `DHF`) to comply with Mermaid safeguards. No reserved keywords are used. Labels contain only uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown bold/italic inside node labels. --- # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP, Set A] **Differentiate between Design Verification and Design Validation in the context of medical device design. State one regulatory clause that governs each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Design Verification | Design Validation | |--------|--------------------|--------------------| | Purpose | Confirm design outputs meet design inputs | Confirm device meets user needs & intended use | | Question | "Did we build the product **right**?" | "Did we build the **right** product?" | | Stage | After design output, before transfer | After prototype, on near-final device | | Method | Test, analysis, inspection, demonstration | Clinical / simulated-use / human-factors study | | Regulation | **FDA 21 CFR 820.30(f)** | **FDA 21 CFR 820.30(g)** | | Standard | **ISO 13485:2016, Clause 7.3.6** | **ISO 13485:2016, Clause 7.3.7** | *(3 marks: 1 mark for correct distinction, 1 mark for stage/method, 1 mark for regulatory clause.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP, Set B] **List any four essential elements that must appear in a Design Verification Plan (DVP) for an active electromedical device.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Reference to specific Design Input** being verified (traceability). 2. **Verification method** chosen from Test / Analysis / Inspection / Demonstration. 3. **Sample size (n)** with statistical justification. 4. **Quantitative acceptance criteria** stated before testing. 5. *Identification of calibrated test equipment.* 6. *Responsible party and location of testing.* 7. *Status tracking and approval signatures.* *(Any four well-explained elements = 3 marks.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice 1] **(a)** Explain in detail the **structure and contents of a Design Verification Plan (DVP)** for a Class II medical device such as a **digital patient monitor** measuring SpO₂, heart rate, and NIBP. Outline the regulatory basis for each section. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A biomedical company must verify the **SpO₂ accuracy specification** of its pulse oximeter to within **±2% saturation** between 70–100% SpO₂.
