Historical standard deviation is **σ = 0.8%**. Determine the **minimum sample size** required at 95% confidence with margin **E = 0.5%**. State the verification method, acceptance criterion, and ISO clause governing this verification. **(7 marks)** --- ### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A #### Part (a) — Structure and Contents of a DVP **(7 marks)** A KTU/ISO 13485-compliant DVP for a digital patient monitor must include the following **nine sections**: 1. **Document Header & Approval Block** — DVP number, revision, date, author, QA approver, design-history-file (DHF) reference. *(0.5 mark)* 2. **Scope & Applicable Design Inputs** — Clear enumeration of DIs being verified (e.g., DI-SpO₂-001, DI-HR-002, DI-NIBP-003). *(0.5 mark)* 3. **Regulatory & Standard Reference Table** — Citations of **FDA 21 CFR 820.30(f)**, **ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.3.6**, **IEC 60601-1** (general electrical safety), **IEC 60601-2-30** (patient monitors), **ISO 14971** (risk-control verification). *(1 mark)* 4. **Verification Method Selection Matrix** — Each DI is mapped to T/A/I/D with rationale. *(1 mark)* 5. **Acceptance Criteria Table** — Quantitative thresholds per ISO 80601-2-61 for SpO₂ (±2% ARMS over 70–100%), IEC 60601-2-30 for NIBP (±3 mmHg), etc. *(1 mark)* 6. **Sample Size Justification** — Statistical basis including chosen confidence level, expected σ, and margin. *(1 mark)* 7. **Equipment List with Calibration Status** — Each instrument traceable to a national standard (e.g., NIST). *(1 mark)* 8. **Test Protocols & Data-Collection Forms** — Reference to stand-alone protocol documents. *(0.5 mark)* 9. **Deviation Handling, Closure & DHF Filing Procedure** — Defines how failures trigger Design Change Orders and RTM updates. *(0.5 mark)* --- #### Part (b) — Sample Size Calculation for SpO₂ Accuracy **(7 marks)** **Step 1 — Identify the statistical model:** Continuous (variable) data → use the **variable sample-size formula**: $$ n = \left(\dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute given values:** - $Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$ (95% two-tailed confidence) - $\sigma = 0.8\%$ SpO₂ - $E = 0.5\%$ SpO₂ (acceptable margin) **Step 3 — Compute numerator:** $$ 1.96 \times 0.8 = 1.568 $$ **Step 4 — Divide by margin and square:** $$ n = \left(\dfrac{1.568}{0.5}\right)^{2} $$ $$ n = (3.136)^{2} $$ $$ n = 9.83 $$ **Step 5 — Round up to integer:** $$ n = 10 \text{ units} $$ **Step 6 — State verification method:** **Test** — using a **Fluke Index 2** or equivalent SpO₂ simulator across the 70–100% range, with paired measurements against a co-oximeter reference on human subjects under IRB-approved protocol. **Step 7 — Acceptance criterion:** $$ \text{Arms accuracy} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (SpO_{2,\text{device}} - SpO_{2,\text{ref}})^{2}}{n}} \leq 2\% $$ **Step 8 — Governing clause:** $$ \text{ISO 80601-2-61:2019, Clause 201.12.1.101 — SpO₂ accuracy determination} $$ **Final Answer:** Minimum sample size **n = 10 pulse-oximeter units**, tested under the method and criterion above, with results documented in a Test Report appended to the DHF. *(Full marks awarded.)* --- ### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3 Choice 2 — Alternative Path] **(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **step-by-step methodology for executing a Design Verification Plan** for an **electrocardiograph (ECG)** device. Mention how verification interacts with the **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** and **Design History File (DHF)**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the role of the DVP in the **ISO 14971 risk management process**. How is the effectiveness of each risk control measure verified through the DVP? Provide one numerical example. **(7 marks)** --- ### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B #### Part (a) — Step-by-Step DVP Execution for an ECG Device **(7 marks)** The flowchart is reproduced below (matching SECTION_4 diagram): ``` Design Inputs approved ↓ Identify Design Outputs (schematic, PCB, firmware, casing) ↓ Select Verification Method per output: • Hardware → Test (EMC per IEC 60601-1-2) • Firmware → Analysis + Unit/Integration Test • Casing → Inspection ↓ Define Acceptance Criteria (e.g., CMRR ≥ 100 dB, sample rate ≥ 500 Hz) ↓ Determine sample size using statistical justification ↓ Identify calibrated equipment (IEC 60601-2-25 test fixtures) ↓ Author Test Protocol, get approval ↓ Execute tests, capture raw waveforms, screenshots ↓ Statistical analysis: mean ± uncertainty vs AC ↓ Pass? → Update RTM row → Append Test Report to DHF Fail? → Issue DCO → Modify DO → Re-verify ``` **RTM linkage:** Each row of the RTM carries columns: `DI-ID | DO-ID | Verification Method | Test Report ID | Status`. After the DVP test closes, the RTM cell flips from **Planned → Verified**. **DHF linkage:** The Test Report, raw data, signed approval sheet, and deviation log are filed into DHF under the device's **Master Record**, creating an immutable audit trail. *(7 marks distributed: 3 for flowchart correctness, 2 for RTM linkage, 2 for DHF linkage.)* --- #### Part (b) — DVP in the ISO 14971 Risk Management Process **(7 marks)** **ISO 14971:2019** mandates that every identified hazard must be followed by: 1. Risk estimation 2. Risk control implementation 3. **Verification of risk control effectiveness** 4. Residual risk evaluation The DVP is the **operational vehicle** for step 3. For each risk-control measure (RCM), a dedicated DVP row must objectively demonstrate that the measure reduces risk to an acceptable level. **Numerical Example — ECG Lead-Off Detection:** - **Hazard:** Failure to detect disconnected lead → misdiagnosis. - **Pre-control risk:** Severity = 5 (catastrophic), Probability = 3 (occasional) → Risk score = **15 (High / Unacceptable)**. - **Risk control:** Software algorithm detects lead impedance > 5 kΩ and triggers alarm within 2 s. - **DVP row:** - **Method:** Test - **Acceptance Criterion:** Alarm triggers within $2.0 \pm 0.2$ seconds for 100% of 50 simulated lead-off events. - **Sample size (n):** 50 simulated events (justified by attribute-testing formula for $p = 0.95$, $E = 0.05$). - **Result:** Alarm triggered in mean $1.83$ s; max $1.98$ s across all 50 trials → **PASS**. - **Effectiveness Factor (EF):** $$ \text{EF} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Post-control Probability}}{\text{Pre-control Probability}} $$ Assuming post-control probability drops from 0.1 to 0.001: $$ \text{EF} = 1 - \dfrac{0.001}{0.1} = 0.99 $$ - **New risk score:** Severity = 5, Probability = 1 (rare) → Risk = **5 (Acceptable)**. This verification evidence is filed into both the **Risk Management File (RMF)** and the **DHF**, satisfying ISO 14971 Clause 7 and FDA 21 CFR 820.30(g) for validation. *(7 marks: 2 for ISO 14971 link, 2 for EF computation, 2 for example, 1 for RMF/DHF link.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Conflating Verification with Validation** — most common 2-mark loss. Memorize "right vs right thing." > 2. **Forgetting statistical justification of sample size** — stating only "n = 5" without formula loses full marks on Part (b). > 3. **Not citing regulation/standard clauses** — every DVP answer must reference at least one of: **FDA 21 CFR 820**, **ISO 13485 Clause 7.3.6**, **IEC 60601-1**, or **ISO 14971**. > 4. **Skipping RTM and DHF** — KTU marks the link between DVP, RTM, and DHF as **2 marks** specifically. > 5. **Defining acceptance criteria after the test** — this is a regulatory violation; criteria must be defined *a priori*. > 6. **Using vague sample sizes like "sufficient"** — always give **numerical n** with the formula used. > 7. **Writing paragraphs instead of tables** — KTU examiners award partial marks more readily to **structured tables and numbered lists**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist 📋 - ✅ A **Design Verification Plan (DVP)** is a documented protocol that confirms design outputs meet design inputs. - ✅ Core question: *"Are we building the device RIGHT?"* (Distinct from Validation's *"RIGHT device?"*). - ✅ Regulatory anchors: **FDA 21 CFR 820.30(f)**, **ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.3.6**, **IEC 62304**, **IEC 62366-1**, **ISO 14971**. - ✅ Verification methods: **T**est / **A**nalysis / **I**nspection / **D**emonstration. - ✅ A DVP must specify: **DI reference, method, sample size n, acceptance criterion, equipment, responsible party, timeline, status**. - ✅ Sample-size formula for variable data: $$ n = \left(\dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^{2} $$ - ✅ For attribute (pass/fail) data: $$ n = \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^{2}} $$ - ✅ Verification must be **planned, objective, reproducible, and traceable** — never ad-hoc. - ✅ Acceptance criteria must be defined **before** testing (a priori). - ✅ DVP outputs feed the **Design History File (DHF)** and update the **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)**. - ✅ Risk-control effectiveness is verified through the DVP using the **Effectiveness Factor (EF)**: $$ \text{EF} = 1 - \dfrac{P_{\text{post}}}{P_{\text{pre}}} $$ - ✅ Failed tests generate **Design Change Orders (DCOs)**, not retesting without modification. - ✅ Always cite the regulatory clause when answering KTU exam questions — examiners reward it explicitly. - ✅ Memorize the **V-model** position: DVP sits at the bottom-right of the V, transforming outputs into evidence. --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **End of Module 3 Notes — Design Verification Plan** > *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol. Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes for PEEBT752.*

topics.title: Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Biomedical Device Design**, a **Protocol** is a formally defined, standardized set of rules, conventions, and data structures that govern the **exchange of information** between two or more medical/electronic entities. These entities may include sensors, actuators, implantable devices, bedside monitors, base stations, hospital servers, or external diagnostic equipment.

A protocol specifies:
- **Physical layer parameters**: frequency, modulation, transmit power
- **Data link layer behavior**: addressing, error detection/correction, framing
- **Application semantics**: data format, units, sampling rates, command sets

For biomedical systems, protocols are typically classified into:

1. **Wired Medical Communication Protocols** (e.g., MIB, CAN, USB, RS-232, Ethernet)
2. **Wireless Medical Communication Protocols** (e.g., Bluetooth, BLE, ZigBee, WiFi, ANT, NFC)
3. **Medical Information Exchange Standards** (e.g., IEEE 11073, HL7, DICOM, FHIR)
4. **Regulatory-Defined Wireless Medical Bands** (e.g., MICS 402–405 MHz, WMTS 608–614/1395–1400/1427–1432 MHz)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to memorize verbatim:**
> *"A protocol in biomedical devices is the standardized communication framework that enables interoperable, reliable, and safe exchange of physiological data between medical devices, sensors, and information systems, conforming to regulatory and clinical requirements."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital as a Multilingual Airport"**
>
> Imagine a huge international airport where doctors, nurses, paramedics, and machines (MRI, ventilator, infusion pump) all need to communicate. Some speak English, some Hindi, some Malayalam, some only "machine-code." If there is no common rule — no agreed alphabet, no agreed sentence structure, no agreed greeting — **chaos** follows: a ventilator cannot tell the central monitor its pressure reading.
>
> A **Protocol** is the **"Universal Translator + Postal Rulebook"** of the hospital. It decides:
> - **WHAT** to say (the message format — e.g., "SPO2 = 98%")
> - **WHEN** to say it (timing, synchronization)
> - **HOW** loud to say it (transmit power, frequency band)
> - **WHOM** to address (device ID, MAC address)
> - **WHAT to do** if the message is corrupted (CRC, retransmission)
>
> Just as air-traffic protocols (ICAO rules) prevent mid-air collisions, **biomedical protocols** prevent life-threatening misinterpretations of patient data.

---

## 1.3 Why Protocols Are Non-Negotiable in Biomedical Design

| Engineering Reason | Clinical Reason |
|---|---|
| Multiple vendors' devices must interoperate | Wrong data interpretation can cause **patient death** |
| EMI/EMC resilience in hospital environment | Regulatory compliance (FDA, CE, BIS, CDSCO) |
| Power-constrained implants (µW budgets) | Data integrity for diagnostics |
| Real-time alarm delivery | Cybersecurity against ransomware on hospital networks |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Memory)

- **Speed of light**: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
- **Free-space impedance**: $Z_0 = 377$ **Ω**
- **Thermal noise floor** at $T = 290$ **K**: $N_0 = -174$ **dBm/Hz**
- **MICS band**: **402–405 MHz** (implant → external)
- **WMTS bands**: **608–614 MHz, 1395–1400 MHz, 1427–1432 MHz**
- **ISM 2.4 GHz band**: **2.400–2.4835 GHz** (unlicensed, shared with WiFi/BT/microwaves)
- **Maximum MICS EIRP**: **25 µW** (= **−16 dBm**)
- **Minimum ECG sampling** (AHA recommendation): **500 Hz**
- **BLE 4.0 data rate**: **1 Mbps** (2 Mbps in BLE 5.0)
- **IEEE 11073 nomenclature codes**: >**1,50,000 terms**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Layered Architecture of a Biomedical Protocol

Almost every modern biomedical protocol is built on a **layered model** (simplified from the OSI 7-layer model to keep designs lean and power-efficient for medical use).

### Layered Stack (Top to Bottom)

1. **Application Layer** — Domain semantics
   - Defines *what* physiological variable is being reported (e.g., MDC_PULS_OXIM_SAT_O2 = SpO₂)
   - Examples: IEEE 11073-104xx device specializations, HL7 v2.x message segments

2. **Transport Layer** — Reliable end-to-end delivery
   - TCP in hospital Ethernet; GATT/ATT in BLE
   - Handles segmentation, reassembly, ACK/NACK

3. **Network Layer** — Addressing & routing
   - IPv4/IPv6 over Ethernet for HIS integration
   - 16-bit short device addresses in ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)

4. **Data Link Layer** — Framing, MAC, error detection
   - MAC addressing (48-bit in Bluetooth, 64-bit in IEEE 802.15.4)
   - **CRC-16** or **CRC-CCITT** for medical-grade error detection

5. **Physical Layer (PHY)** — Bits on the wire/air
   - Modulation (FSK, GFSK, π/4-DQPSK, OFDM)
   - Carrier frequency, channel bandwidth, transmit power

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "ATN-DP"** for the 5 layers: **A**pplication → **T**ransport → **N**etwork → **D**ata Link → **P**hysical

---

## 2.2 Why a Biomedical Protocol Exists — The "Five Pillars"

| Pillar | Engineering Translation | Clinical Translation |
|---|---|---|
| **Interoperability** | Standard packet structure | Plug-and-play between ECG, monitor, EMR |
| **Reliability** | CRC, FEC, ARQ | No silent data loss during surgery |
| **Safety** | Defined max latency, alarm priority | No missed critical alarm |
| **Security** | AES-128, pairing, secure boot | HIPAA / DPDP Act compliance |
| **Low Power** | Sleep currents < 1 µA | 10-year implant battery life |

---

## 2.3 The Master Cheat-Sheet — KTU 2024 Formula Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **high-yield for KTU university exam derivations and numerical problems.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Where used in biomedical context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shannon Channel Capacity** | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $C$ = bit/s, $B$ = Hz, $S/N$ = linear SNR | Max data rate over ECG telemetry channel |
| 2 | **Free-Space Path Loss (linear)** | $FSPL = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ | $d$ = m, $\lambda$ = m | Wireless implant → external range |
| 3 | **FSPL in dB** | $FSPL_{\mathrm{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)$ | $f$ = Hz, $c = 3\times10^{8}$ m/s | Link budget for MICS / WMTS |
| 4 | **Friis Transmission Equation (RX power)** | $P_r = P_t\,G_t\,G_r\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | All in linear; $G_t,G_r$ = antenna gains | RX power at bedside monitor |
| 5 | **Wavelength** | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | $c = 3\times10^{8}$ m/s | Antenna design for MICS (≈ 75 cm) |
| 6 | **Received Power in dBm** | $P_r(\mathrm{dBm}) = P_t(\mathrm{dBm}) + G_t + G_r - FSPL_{\mathrm{dB}}$ | dB units | Link budget closure |
| 7 | **Thermal Noise Power** | $N = kTB$ | $k = 1.38\times10^{-23}$ J/K, $T$ = K, $B$ = Hz | Sensitivity calculation |
| 8 | **Noise in dBm** | $N(\mathrm{dBm}) = -174 + 10\log_{10}\!\left(B_{\mathrm{Hz}}\right)$ | dBm | Receiver noise floor |
| 9 | **Minimum Detectable Signal (MDS)** | $MDS = -174 + NF + 10\log_{10}B + SNR_{\min}$ | All dB | BLE receiver sensitivity ≈ −97 dBm |
| 10 | **Symbol Rate to Bit Rate** | $R_b = R_s \cdot \log_2 M$ | $M$ = M-ary levels | GFSK ($M=2$) vs QPSK ($M=4$) |
| 11 | **Bit Energy** | $E_b = \dfrac{P_r}{R_b}$ | Joules per bit | Energy per ECG sample |
| 12 | **Battery Life Estimate** | $t_{\mathrm{life}} = \dfrac{C_{\mathrm{bat}}\,V_{\mathrm{bat}}}{I_{\mathrm{avg}}\,V_{\mathrm{bat}}} = \dfrac{C_{\mathrm{bat}}}{I_{\mathrm{avg}}}$ | Amp-hours / A = hours | Implant longevity |
| 13 | **Duty-Cycled Average Current** | $I_{\mathrm{avg}} = I_{\mathrm{TX}} \cdot \delta + I_{\mathrm{Sleep}} \cdot (1-\delta)$ | Amps, $\delta$ = duty ratio | BLE beacon design |
| 14 | **Bit Error Rate (coherent FSK)** | $P_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}}\right)$ | dimensionless | QOS for telemetry |
| 15 | **Range from FSPL budget** | $d_{\max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi}\cdot 10^{\frac{P_t+G_t+G_r - MDS}{20}}$ | metres | Wireless coverage area |

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU exam, when writing FSPL or path-loss equations, **never drop the factor of $4\pi$** — that is a known 1-mark deduction trap.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Implant designers (pacemaker, ICD, cochlear implant)** use MICS because it propagates through tissue with low attenuation and has global spectrum allocation specifically for implants.
- **Hospital ward telemetry** (SpO₂, multi-parameter monitors) uses WMTS to avoid interference from WiFi/cellphones — a regulatory mandate in many countries.
- **Consumer wearables (smartwatch, fitness band)** use BLE because the smartphone ecosystem is built on it.
- **Hospital PACS** (Picture Archiving and Communication System) uses DICOM so an MRI scanner from vendor A and a viewer from vendor B interoperate seamlessly.
- **EHR/EMR integration** uses HL7 v2.x or FHIR (newer JSON/REST-based) for structured clinical messaging.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Range of a MICS Implant Link

**Problem statement (typical KTU 14-mark sub-part):**
A subcutaneous cardiac monitor transmits at $P_t = -16$ **dBm** (MICS maximum) at $f = 403$ **MHz** with $G_t = G_r = 0$ **dBi** (isotropic dipoles inside body and on chest). The receiver sensitivity is $MDS = -97$ **dBm**. The link is **line-of-sight** in body tissue with permittivity similar to free space. Find the **maximum reliable range** $d_{\max}$.

### Step-by-step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the link-budget equation.**

We require the received power $P_r \ge MDS$. Using the Friis equation in dB:

$$P_r(\mathrm{dBm}) = P_t(\mathrm{dBm}) + G_t(\mathrm{dBi}) + G_r(\mathrm{dBi}) - FSPL_{\mathrm{dB}}$$

**Step 2 — Express FSPL in dB.**

$$FSPL_{\mathrm{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $d$ by setting $P_r = MDS$.**

$$MDS = P_t + G_t + G_r - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d_{\max} f}{c}\right)$$

Rearrange:

$$20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d_{\max} f}{c}\right) = P_t + G_t + G_r - MDS$$

Let $L_{\max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - MDS$ be the **maximum allowable path loss (dB)**.

$$\dfrac{4\pi d_{\max} f}{c} = 10^{L_{\max}/20}$$

$$d_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{4\pi f}\cdot 10^{L_{\max}/20}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in numerical values.**

- $P_t = -16$ dBm
- $G_t = 0$ dBi, $G_r = 0$ dBi
- $MDS = -97$ dBm

$$L_{\max} = (-16) + 0 + 0 - (-97) = 81 \text{ dB}$$

Therefore:

$$d_{\max} = \dfrac{3\times 10^{8}}{4\pi \cdot 403\times 10^{6}} \cdot 10^{81/20}$$

First compute the wavelength factor:

$$\dfrac{c}{4\pi f} = \dfrac{3\times 10^{8}}{4\pi \cdot 403\times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{3\times 10^{8}}{5.063\times 10^{9}} \approx 0.05925 \text{ m}$$

Now compute $10^{81/20} = 10^{4.05}$:

$$10^{4.05} \approx 1.122 \times 10^{4} = 11{,}220$$

Therefore:

$$d_{\max} \approx 0.05925 \times 11{,}220 \approx 664.7 \text{ m}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering reality check.

Free-space gives ~665 m, but the human body introduces additional attenuation (≈ **20–35 dB** through torso tissue). Subtracting a tissue path loss of 30 dB:

$$L_{\max,\text{body}} = 81 - 30 = 51 \text{ dB}$$

$$d_{\max,\text{body}} = 0.05925 \cdot 10^{51/20} = 0.05925 \cdot 10^{2.55} = 0.05925 \times 354.8 \approx 21 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer for KTU:** $d_{\max} \approx 665$ **m** in free space; ≈ **20 m** through body tissue. This is why MICS programmers are designed to be used within ~5 m of the patient.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Current and Battery Life of a BLE Pulse-Oximeter

**Problem statement:**
A BLE pulse-oximeter transmits one packet (3 bytes SpO₂ + 4 bytes header + 3 bytes CRC = 10 bytes = 80 bits) every $T_{\mathrm{period}} = 1$ **s**. Transmit takes $t_{\mathrm{TX}} = 80\,\mu$s at $I_{\mathrm{TX}} = 8$ **mA**. Sleep current $I_{\mathrm{Sleep}} = 1\,\mu$A. Battery is a CR2032 with $C_{\mathrm{bat}} = 220$ **mAh** at $3$ **V**. Find the average current and the **battery life in days**.

### Step-by-step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the duty cycle.**

$$\delta = \dfrac{t_{\mathrm{TX}}}{T_{\mathrm{period}}} = \dfrac{80 \times 10^{-6}}{1} = 80 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the duty-cycled average current formula.**

$$I_{\mathrm{avg}} = I_{\mathrm{TX}} \cdot \delta + I_{\mathrm{Sleep}} \cdot (1 - \delta)$$

Compute each term:

$$I_{\mathrm{TX}} \cdot \delta = 8 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 8 \times 10^{-5} = 6.4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ A} = 0.64 \,\mu\text{A}$$

$$I_{\mathrm{Sleep}} \cdot (1 - \delta) \approx 1 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1 = 1 \,\mu\text{A}$$

Therefore:

$$I_{\mathrm{avg}} \approx 1.64 \,\mu\text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute battery life in hours.**

$$t_{\mathrm{life}} = \dfrac{C_{\mathrm{bat}}}{I_{\mathrm{avg}}} = \dfrac{220 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Ah}}{1.64 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}}$$

$$t_{\mathrm{life}} = \dfrac{0.220}{1.64 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.341 \times 10^{5} \text{ h}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to days.**

$$t_{\mathrm{life}} \approx \dfrac{1.341 \times 10^{5}}{24} \approx 5589 \text{ days} \approx 15.3 \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $I_{\mathrm{avg}} \approx 1.64\,\mu$A, battery life ≈ **15.3 years**. This matches the marketed life of real BLE pulse-oximeters, validating the duty-cycle design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Achievable Data Rate of a Hospital Telemetry Channel

**Problem statement:**
A WMTS channel has bandwidth $B = 6$ **MHz** centred at 1.4 GHz. The measured SNR at the receiver is **12 dB**. Find the **Shannon capacity**.

### Step-by-step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear.**

$$S/N = 10^{12/10} = 10^{1.2} \approx 15.85$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's formula.**

$$C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$$

$$C = 6 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(1 + 15.85) = 6 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(16.85)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the log base 2.**

$$\log_2(16.85) = \dfrac{\ln(16.85)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{2.825}{0.693} \approx 4.077 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by bandwidth.**

$$C \approx 6 \times 10^{6} \times 4.077 \approx 24.46 \text{ Mbps}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $C \approx 24.5$ **Mbps** — more than enough for 12-lead ECG (≈ 144 kbps at 500 sps, 12-bit) and multi-parameter telemetry.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing a Protocol

| Protocol | Frequency | Data Rate | Range | TX Power | Power Use | Typical Medical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MICS** | 402–405 MHz | up to 800 kbps | ≈ 5–10 m body | ≤ 25 µW | Very low | Implant ↔ programmer |
| **WMTS** | 608–614 / 1395–1400 / 1427–1432 MHz | up to 10 Mbps | Hospital ward | ≤ 20 dBm | Medium | Patient telemetry, alarms |
| **Bluetooth (Classic)** | 2.4 GHz | 1–3 Mbps | ≈ 10 m | 0–20 dBm | 30 mA TX | Diagnostic device ↔ PC |
| **BLE 5.0** | 2.4 GHz | 2 Mbps | ≈ 100 m (LE Coded) | 0–20 dBm | 5–15 mA peak | Wearables, SpO₂, glucose |
| **ZigBee (802.15.4)** | 2.4 GHz | 250 kbps | ≈ 30 m indoor | 0–20 dBm | < 30 mA | Hospital sensor mesh |
| **ANT/ANT+** | 2.4 GHz | 1 Mbps | ≈ 30 m | 0 dBm | < 15 mA | Sports / fitness / HRM |
| **WiFi 802.11n** | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 54–600 Mbps | ≈ 50 m | 10–20 dBm | > 200 mA | Hospital EHR, imaging |
| **NFC** | 13.56 MHz | 106–424 kbps | < 10 cm | < 500 mW | Very low | Patient ID, drug authentication |
| **DICOM (over TCP/IP)** | Wired | LAN-speed | Hospital LAN | — | — | MRI/CT/PACS |
| **HL7 v2.x / FHIR** | Wired | LAN-speed | Hospital LAN | — | — | EHR / EMR messaging |
| **IEEE 11073** | Any PHY | Application layer | — | — | — | Plug-and-trust medical data format |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 3]**
**Define a communication protocol as applied to biomedical devices. List any four key requirements of a biomedical protocol.**

**Model Answer (3-mark, ~80–100 words):**

A communication protocol in biomedical devices is a **standardized set of rules that govern the exchange of physiological data** between medical devices, sensors and information systems in a reliable, safe and interoperable manner.

The four key requirements are:
1. **Interoperability** — devices from different vendors must exchange data without custom code.
2. **Reliability / Data integrity** — bit error rate below $10^{-5}$ for critical vital signs.
3. **Patient safety** — deterministic latency, prioritized alarms, fail-safe defaults.
4. **Regulatory compliance** — adherence to standards such as IEEE 11073, HL7, DICOM and to FCC/ETSI spectrum rules (MICS, WMTS).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 3]**
**Differentiate between MICS and WMTS wireless bands. State one typical medical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3-mark, table form acceptable):**

| Parameter | MICS | WMTS |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency band | 402–405 MHz | 608–614 / 1395–1400 / 1427–1432 MHz |
| Max EIRP | 25 µW (≈ −16 dBm) | 20 dBm (100 mW) typical |
| Typical range | A few metres (body) | Whole hospital ward (≈ 30 m) |
| Typical use | Implantable devices (pacemaker, ICD, cochlear implant) communication with external programmer | In-hospital patient telemetry (ECG, SpO₂, multi-parameter) |
| Regulatory body | FCC Part 95, ETSI EN 301 559 | FCC Part 95, ETSI EN 303 203 |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE — Dec 2024 Model, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **layered architecture of a biomedical communication protocol**. Discuss the function of each layer with an example for a Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) based pulse-oximeter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **maximum reliable transmission range** of a MICS link operating at 403 MHz with $P_t = -16$ dBm, isotropic antennas ($G_t = G_r = 0$ dBi), receiver sensitivity $MDS = -95$ dBm, and an additional **30 dB body-tissue path-loss margin**. State whether this range is suitable for an in-clinic programmer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A (a)

**Layered Architecture (Block Diagram):**

A biomedical protocol is conventionally organised in **five functional layers**, namely (from top to bottom): **Application, Transport, Network, Data Link, and Physical**. The topmost layer deals with *what* the message means in clinical terms, the bottommost with *how* bits are placed on the medium.

| Layer | Function | Example in BLE Pulse-Oximeter |
|---|---|---|
| **Application** | Defines clinical semantics and units | IEEE 11073-10404 code MDC_PULS_OXIM_SAT_O2 = SpO₂ in % |
| **Transport** | Segmentation, reliability, encryption | GATT/ATT with LE Secure Connections (AES-128 CCM) |
| **Network** | Addressing of multiple devices | 6-byte BD_ADDR (BLE public address) |
| **Data Link** | Framing, CRC, channel access | 24-bit CRC, 37 data channels, channel selection algorithm #1 |
| **Physical** | Modulation, RF front-end | 2.4 GHz GFSK, 1 Mbps, 2 MHz channel spacing, 0 dBm TX |

**Working Principle:**
The SpO₂ sensor (red + IR photodiodes) acquires PPG signals at 100 samples/s. The MCU packs two 16-bit values (SpO₂, pulse rate) into an IEEE 11073 attribute, prepends a GATT notification header, encrypts with AES-128, appends a 24-bit CRC, performs GFSK modulation at 2.4 GHz and transmits on a BLE advertising/data channel. The smartphone receives, decrypts, decodes the 11073 code, and displays "SpO₂ = 98 %".

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (b)

**Step 1 — Link-budget equation (Friis in dB):**

$$P_r(\mathrm{dBm}) = P_t(\mathrm{dBm}) + G_t + G_r - FSPL_{\mathrm{dB}} - L_{\mathrm{body}}$$

For reliable reception: $P_r \ge MDS$.

**Step 2 — Solve for $d_{\max}$:**

$$FSPL_{\mathrm{dB}} + L_{\mathrm{body}} = P_t + G_t + G_r - MDS$$

$$L_{\max} = (-16) + 0 + 0 - (-95) = 79 \text{ dB}$$

$$20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d_{\max} f}{c}\right) = 79 - 30 = 49 \text{ dB}$$

$$\dfrac{4\pi d_{\max} f}{c} = 10^{49/20} = 10^{2.45} \approx 281.8$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $f = 403$ MHz:**

$$d_{\max} = \dfrac{281.8 \cdot c}{4\pi f} = \dfrac{281.8 \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{4\pi \times 403 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$d_{\max} = \dfrac{8.454 \times 10^{10}}{5.063 \times 10^{9}} \approx 16.7 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
The maximum range is **≈ 16.7 m**, which is **suitable for an in-clinic programmer** (clinicians typically place the wand within 5 m of the patient's chest). The 30 dB body-loss assumption is conservative; a slimmer patient or shallower implant may give 20–25 m. The link is acceptable for a bedside programmer but **not** for whole-hospital mobility — that is why MICS is not used for ward telemetry.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE — June 2024 Model, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **IEEE 11073 family of standards** for personal health devices. Discuss its domain information model, nomenclature and how it enables plug-and-play interoperability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital telemetry system uses the **WMTS band at 1.4 GHz** with 6 MHz channel bandwidth. If the received SNR is **15 dB**, compute (i) the **Shannon channel capacity**, and (ii) the **minimum received power** if the thermal noise floor at 290 K must be met for the same bandwidth. Boltzmann's constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B (a)

**IEEE 11073 — Personal Health Device (PHD) Standards:**

The IEEE 11073 family is a **suite of standards** that defines how personal health devices (glucometer, BP monitor, weighing scale, pulse-oximeter, thermometer) communicate with computing platforms (phones, gateways, EMR).

**Three Core Components:**

1. **IEEE 11073-104xx — Device Specializations:**
   Defines the specific metrics each device type reports. Example: 11073-10404 for pulse-oximeter (SpO₂, pulse rate, perfusion index), 11073-10407 for blood-pressure monitor, 11073-10415 for weighing scale.

2. **IEEE 11073-20601 — Optimized Exchange Protocol:**
   Defines the **Domain Information Model (DIM)**, the **service model** (event reports, GET/SET actions), and the **transport-independent lower-layer binding**. The DIM describes the device as a **Medical Device System (MDS)** containing one or more **Virtual Medical Objects (VMO)**, each described by attributes (Handle, Type, Metric-Specification, Value).

3. **IEEE 11073-10101 — Nomenclature:**
   A **hierarchical coding system** with codes like:
   - `MDC_PULS_OXIM_SAT_O2` → 0x4A48
   - `MDC_DIM_PERCENT` → unit code
   - `MDC_TEMP_AXILLA` → body site

   Currently > 1,50,000 unique terms, and the system is extensible.

**Plug-and-Play Workflow:**

When a device is connected (USB, BLE), the manager sends an **Association Request**. The device returns its **MDS descriptor** including which 104xx specialization it conforms to, supported metrics, units, and sampling rates. The manager can **auto-configure** display templates, alarm limits, and storage. The user does not need to write custom code. This is the foundation of Continua-certified interoperable ecosystems.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (b)

**Given:**
- $B = 6$ MHz, $\;f = 1.4$ GHz
- SNR = 15 dB
- $T = 290$ K, $\;k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K

**Part (i) — Shannon Capacity:**

Convert SNR to linear:
$$\dfrac{S}{N} = 10^{15/10} = 31.62$$

Apply Shannon:
$$C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$$

$$C = 6 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2(32.62)$$

$$\log_2(32.62) = \dfrac{\ln(32.62)}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{3.485}{0.693} \approx 5.028 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 30.17 \text{ Mbps}}$$

**Part (ii) — Minimum Received Power = Thermal Noise Floor at 290 K:**

$$N = kTB = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 \times 6 \times 10^{6}$$

Compute step by step:
$$1.38 \times 290 = 400.2$$
$$400.2 \times 10^{-23} = 4.002 \times 10^{-21}$$
$$N = 4.002 \times 10^{-21} \times 6 \times 10^{6} = 2.401 \times 10^{-14} \text{ W}$$

Convert to dBm:
$$N(\mathrm{dBm}) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2.401 \times 10^{-14}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10\log_{10}(2.401 \times 10^{-11})$$

$$N(\mathrm{dBm}) = 10\!\left[\log_{10}(2.401) + (-11)\right] = 10 \times [0.380 - 11] = -106.2 \text{ dBm}$$

Equivalently using the kTB shortcut:
$$N(\mathrm{dBm}) = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B_{\mathrm{Hz}}) = -174 + 10\log_{10}(6 \times 10^{6})$$
$$= -174 + 67.78 = -106.22 \text{ dBm}\;\checkmark$$

**Minimum received power** (i.e., the receiver must see at least this noise; to maintain SNR = 15 dB the actual signal must be $N + 15$ dB higher):

$$P_{r,\min}(\text{for SNR}=15\text{ dB}) = -106.2 + 15 = \boxed{-91.2 \text{ dBm}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $4\pi$ factor** in the Friis/FSPL equation → 1 mark lost.
> 2. **Mixing up FSPL linear and dB form**; remember FSPL is a *ratio*, so 20·log₁₀ (not 10·log₁₀) for distances expressed linearly.
> 3. **Not converting SNR from dB to linear** before using in Shannon's formula → 2 marks lost.
> 4. **Confusing MICS with WMTS** in band/frequency questions. MICS = 402–405 MHz and implant use; WMTS = 608/1395/1427 MHz and in-hospital telemetry.
> 5. **In battery-life questions**, not multiplying $I_{\mathrm{avg}}$ by battery voltage *in some cases* — KTU accepts either current-ratio form or energy form, but show both for safety.
> 6. **Skipping unit conversions**: kHz ↔ Hz, dBm ↔ W. Always write them in the answer.
> 7. **Missing the "ATN-DP" layers**: forgetting to name the data link or transport layer explicitly in a 7-mark architecture question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Memory Sheet — Re-read this 1 hour before the KTU exam.**

- ⭐ A **protocol** = rules + format + timing + addressing + error handling for medical data exchange.
- ⭐ **Five layers** (ATN-DP): Application, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical.
- ⭐ **MICS** = 402–405 MHz, ≤ 25 µW, **implant communication**.
- ⭐ **WMTS** = 608–614 / 1395–1400 / 1427–1432 MHz, **hospital telemetry**.
- ⭐ **ISM 2.4 GHz** = unlicensed, used by BLE, WiFi, ZigBee, ANT — congested in hospitals.
- ⭐ **IEEE 11073-10101** = nomenclature; **-104xx** = device specializations; **-20601** = exchange protocol.
- ⭐ **HL7 v2.x** = pipe-delimited clinical messaging; **FHIR** = modern JSON/REST.
- ⭐ **DICOM** = imaging standard (MRI/CT/USG).
- ⭐ **Shannon Capacity**: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.
- ⭐ **Friis (dB)**: $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL_{\mathrm{dB}}$.
- ⭐ **FSPL (dB)**: $20\log_{10}(4\pi d f / c)$.
- ⭐ **Noise floor (dBm)**: $-174 + 10\log_{10}(B_{\mathrm{Hz}})$.
- ⭐ **BLE** uses GFSK, 1 Mbps, 40 channels, 24-bit CRC, 2 MHz spacing.
- ⭐ **Duty-cycled average current** = $I_{\mathrm{TX}}\delta + I_{\mathrm{Sleep}}(1-\delta)$.
- ⭐ **Battery life (h)** = Capacity (mAh) / $I_{\mathrm{avg}}$ (mA).
- ⭐ For **interoperability**, a protocol must define *nomenclature*, *units*, *timing*, *transport* and *error handling*.
- ⭐ **Security** in modern protocols: AES-128 (BLE LE Secure, WiFi WPA3), TLS 1.3 (HL7 FHIR).
- ⭐ **Range** for MICS link: ≈ 5–10 m in body, ≈ 665 m free-space (no tissue loss).
- ⭐ **Most-tested KTU sub-questions**: derive range, derive battery life, draw protocol stack, compare MICS/WMTS/BLE, list IEEE 11073 components.
- ⭐ **Regulation**: MICS/WMTS are FCC Part 95 (US) and ETSI (Europe) — not the same as ISM, so medical devices have *protected* spectrum.
- ⭐ **Maximum MICS EIRP = 25 µW** is a hard regulatory cap; question may ask you to convert this to dBm (−16 dBm).
- ⭐ **KFU** the **physical layer parameters** of BLE by heart: 2.4 GHz, GFSK, BT = 0.5, 1 Msymbol/s, 1 Mbps, 40 channels (37 data + 3 adv).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software-Hardware Test Plan for Medical Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Software-Hardware Test Plan for Medical Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software-Hardware Test Plan for Medical Devices** is a formally documented, regulatory-compliant master document that defines the **strategy, objectives, scope, resources, schedule, deliverables, and acceptance criteria** for systematically verifying and validating the integrated behavior of software components executing on the hardware platform of a medical device, in accordance with international standards such as **IEC 62304 (Medical Device Software Life Cycle)**, **IEC 60601-1 (Medical Electrical Equipment Safety)**, **ISO 14971 (Risk Management)**, and **FDA 21 CFR Part 820 (Quality System Regulation)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PEEBT752 – Design of Biomedical Devices (Module 3)**, this topic sits at the intersection of **regulatory engineering**, **software quality assurance**, and **embedded systems validation**. A test plan is not a test script — it is the *controlling contract* that governs *how*, *when*, *by whom*, and *against what evidence* every software module and every hardware-software interface will be exercised, measured, and signed off before the device is released for clinical use.

### Key Terminological Anchors

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Test Plan** | The master document specifying test scope, methodology, resources, and exit criteria. |
| **Verification** | Confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence that specified requirements have been **fulfilled** ("Are we building the product right?"). |
| **Validation** | Confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence that the product meets **user needs and intended use** ("Are we building the right product?"). |
| **Traceability Matrix** | A mapping table linking each design requirement to its corresponding test case to ensure **100% coverage**. |
| **SOUP / COTS** | Software Of Unknown Provenance / Commercial Off-The-Shelf software requiring **special risk control**. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition Layer

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Heart of a Car Before Delivery**
> Imagine Tata Motors has just finished assembling a car. Before handing it to a customer, it does not just *drive* the car once around a parking lot. It:
> 1. **Bench-tests** every sub-assembly (brakes alone on a rig),
> 2. **Integration-tests** the brake pedal with the brake disc via the ABS ECU,
> 3. **System-tests** the car on a test track in rain, snow, and heat,
> 4. **Acceptance-tests** it with a real customer on real roads.
>
> Each of these stages is documented in a **Test Plan** that the regulator (ARAI in India) audits. If a brake fails at Stage 4 because Stage 2 was skipped, the car is recalled and the company is liable. *A medical device test plan is the exact same discipline — except the consequence of failure is not a scratched bumper, it is a human life.*

A **software-hardware test plan** therefore answers five questions for every line of code running on a patient-connected device:

1. **What** is being tested? (Requirement → Test Case)
2. **How** is it being tested? (Method, equipment, environment)
3. **When** is it being tested? (Stage, schedule, dependencies)
4. **Who** tests it? (Roles, independence, qualifications)
5. **What evidence** proves the test passed? (Records, signatures, traceability)

## 1.3 The Regulatory Backbone (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU examiner will award marks if you can name **at least three standards** and briefly state their focus. Memorize the table below.

| Standard | Focus Area | Relevance to Test Plan |
|---|---|---|
| **IEC 62304:2006 + Amd 1:2015** | Medical device software life cycle | Defines software **safety classes (A, B, C)** which dictate test depth. |
| **IEC 60601-1** | Basic safety & essential performance of medical electrical equipment | Hardware-software integration **must not compromise basic safety**. |
| **ISO 14971:2019** | Risk management process | Test plan must explicitly address **risk control measures**. |
| **IEC 62366-1:2015** | Usability engineering | Summative evaluation = a validation test. |
| **FDA General Principles of Software Validation (2002)** | US FDA guidance | Reference for software test documentation. |
| **ISO 13485:2016** | Quality management systems for medical devices | Requires **documented procedures** for testing. |

### Physical / Quantitative Constants to Remember

- **Software Safety Classification (IEC 62304):**
  - **Class A** — No injury or damage to health is possible. (e.g., software running a hospital information display)
  - **Class B** — Non-serious injury is possible. (e.g., software for an infusion pump alarm)
  - **Class C** — Death or serious injury is possible. (e.g., software controlling radiation therapy beam)
- **Test Independence Rule:** For Class C software, verification **must be performed by a different person** than the developer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The V-Model — The Backbone of the Test Plan

> [!NOTE]
> The **V-Model** is the universally adopted framework for medical device development AND testing. The left arm is *development*, the right arm is *testing*. The test plan must contain a test level for every design level.

| Left Arm (Development) | Right Arm (Verification / Test) |
|---|---|
| User Needs | **Validation Testing** (clinical/simulated use) |
| System Requirements | **System Testing** |
| Software/Hardware Architecture | **Integration Testing** (HW-SW interface) |
| Detailed Design (Module) | **Unit Testing** |
| Coding | (Source) |

**Engineering Logic — Why the V-Model is Mandatory for Medical Devices:**
- It forces **traceability** in *both* directions: forward (requirement → test) and backward (test → requirement).
- It guarantees that no requirement is left untested and no test exists without a requirement (the "orphan" and "gold-plating" anti-patterns).
- It satisfies **IEC 62304 Clause 5.1** which mandates a software development model that supports traceability.

## 2.2 Levels of Testing in the Plan

### 2.2.1 Unit Testing (Module Level)
- **Scope:** Individual functions, subroutines, classes, interrupt service routines.
- **Method:** White-box testing using code coverage tools (branch coverage ≥ 100% for Class C, per IEC 62304 5.5.5).
- **Evidence:** Code coverage report, test case execution log.
- **Hardware-in-the-loop?** No — typically executed on a host PC simulator or target board stub.

### 2.2.2 Integration Testing (Hardware-Software Interface)
- **Scope:** Verifying the interaction between software modules AND between software and physical hardware peripherals.
- **Critical Sub-categories:**
  - *Software-Software Integration:* Inter-module data flow.
  - *Software-Hardware Integration:* ADC sampling, sensor signal conditioning, motor control loops, display refresh, communication bus (SPI/I²C/UART/CAN).
  - *External Interface Integration:* Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, USB, HL7 messaging.
- **Tools:** Oscilloscope, logic analyzer, signal injector, in-circuit emulator (ICE).

### 2.2.3 System Testing
- **Scope:** The **complete medical device** operating under its full hardware-software configuration.
- **Verifies:** Functional requirements, performance, EMC (per IEC 60601-1-2), electrical safety (per IEC 60601-1), environmental (temperature, humidity, vibration per IEC 60068).
- **Method:** Black-box testing against the **System Requirements Specification (SyRS)**.

### 2.2.4 Acceptance / Validation Testing
- **Scope:** The device is used for its **intended use** in a **simulated or actual clinical environment**.
- **Participants:** End-users (clinicians, nurses, patients).
- **Standards link:** ISO 62366-1 (Usability) and the clinical evaluation report (per EU MDR Article 61).

## 2.3 The Structure of a Compliant Test Plan (IEEE 829 Template Adapted for Medical Devices)

A KTU-expected test plan for a medical device should contain, at minimum, the following sections:

1. **Test Plan Identifier** (Unique document number, version, approval signatures)
2. **Introduction & Scope** (device name, model, intended use)
3. **Reference Documents** (SyRS, SwRS, Risk Management File, applicable standards)
4. **Test Items** (HW version, SW version, accessories)
5. **Software Safety Classification** (A / B / C per IEC 62304)
6. **Test Levels & Types** (Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance)
7. **Test Strategy & Approach** (manual, automated, tools to be used)
8. **Test Environment** (lab equipment, simulators, test fixtures, calibrated instruments)
9. **Entry & Exit Criteria** for each test level
10. **Test Deliverables** (Test Cases, Test Procedures, Test Reports, Traceability Matrix)
11. **Roles & Responsibilities** (Test Manager, Test Engineers, Independent Verifier)
12. **Schedule** (Gantt chart / milestone linkage)
13. **Risk-Based Test Prioritization** (mapping test cases to ISO 14971 hazards)
14. **Defect Management Process**
15. **SOUP / COTS Testing Strategy**
16. **Regression Testing Strategy**
17. **Approval & Sign-off Block**

## 2.4 Risk-Based Testing — The Critical Bridge to ISO 14971

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Theorem (KTU Favourite):** *Test effort and depth must be **proportional to the severity and probability** of harm the software could cause.*

The test plan must explicitly cross-reference the **Risk Management File (RMF)**. For every identified hazard, there must be a corresponding test case that verifies the **risk control measure** is effective.

### 2.4.1 Risk Priority Number (RPN) — Severity × Probability × Detectability

The RPN is the single most testable quantitative metric the examiner will ask for. It is calculated in the Failure Mode Effects and Criticality Analysis (FMECA) and is then used to **prioritize test cases** in the test plan.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every quantitative formula you may need for a 14-mark KTU question on this topic. Master the **RPN** and **Code Coverage** definitions.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Standard Reference | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $RPN = S \times P \times D$ | ISO 14971 / IEC 60812 | Dimensionless (1–1000 scale) |
| 2 | **Code Coverage (Statement)** | $\text{StmtCov} = \dfrac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100\%$ | IEC 62304 §5.5.5 | Percent (%) |
| 3 | **Code Coverage (Branch)** | $\text{BrCov} = \dfrac{\text{Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100\%$ | IEC 62304 §5.5.5 | Percent (%) |
| 4 | **Modified Condition / Decision Coverage (MC/DC)** | Every condition shown to affect decision outcome independently | DO-178C / IEC 62304 Class C | Boolean (Pass/Fail) |
| 5 | **Requirement Coverage** | $\text{ReqCov} = \dfrac{\text{Requirements with at least one passing test}}{\text{Total Requirements}} \times 100\%$ | IEEE 830 / IEC 62304 | Percent (%) |
| 6 | **Defect Density** | $DD = \dfrac{\text{Number of confirmed defects}}{\text{Size (KLOC or Function Points)}}$ | CMMI / Industry metric | Defects / KLOC |
| 7 | **Test Effectiveness** | $TE = \dfrac{\text{Defects found in test}}{\text{Defects found in test} + \text{Defects escaped to field}} \times 100\%$ | Quality Metric | Percent (%) |
| 8 | **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Detection Time} - \text{Injection Time})}{\text{Number of Defects}}$ | Process metric | Hours |
| 9 | **Test Effort Estimation** | $E = \dfrac{N \times (1 + r)^{d}}{v}$ (Halstead-style) | Effort estimation | Person-hours |
| 10 | **Bolam's Test Compatibility (no)** | Not mathematical — qualitative | Clinical standard | N/A |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Rule:** When writing RPN inline, **never** write `|S|` style. Use $\vert S \vert$ or just $S$ since severity is always a positive rating.

### 2.5.1 RPN Component Ratings (Standard Industry Scale)

| Rating | Severity (S) | Probability (P) | Detectability (D) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Negligible | Very Low | Very High (easy to detect) |
| 2–3 | Minor | Low | High |
| 4–6 | Moderate | Medium | Medium |
| 7–8 | Major | High | Low |
| 9–10 | Catastrophic / Death | Very High | Very Low (hard to detect) |

**Action thresholds (typical):**
- $RPN \le 50$ — Acceptable risk, continue monitoring.
- $50 < RPN \le 100$ — Risk reduction required, test must verify control.
- $RPN > 100$ — **Mandatory redesign or additional control measures.**

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of the Test Plan

A compliant test plan provides:
- **Regulatory evidence** for FDA 510(k), EU MDR Technical File, CDSCO (India) submission.
- **Defence in liability litigation** — if a patient is harmed, the test plan demonstrates *due diligence*.
- **Repeatable engineering practice** for design iterations and derivative products.
- **Auditability** — a notified body auditor must be able to reconstruct what was tested, when, by whom, with what result.
- **Basis for Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)** — failure modes discovered after release feed back into the next test plan revision.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — RPN Calculation & Test Prioritization

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic **KTU 14-mark Part B style** analytical question. We will solve it exhaustively.

**Problem Statement (KTU University Exam Style):**
During a Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) of a **patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) infusion pump**, three potential software-related failure modes are identified. Calculate the Risk Priority Number for each, and recommend the order in which corresponding test cases must be executed in the test plan.

| Failure Mode | Severity (S) | Probability (P) | Detectability (D) |
|---|---|---|---|
| FM-1: Pump fails to stop when patient presses STOP button | 10 | 6 | 4 |
| FM-2: Display shows incorrect drug name (cosmetic) | 3 | 8 | 7 |
| FM-3: Audible alarm silenced when battery is low | 9 | 5 | 6 |

### Step 1 — Recall the Definition

The Risk Priority Number is defined as the product of three ordinal ratings:

$$RPN = S \times P \times D$$

where $S$ is the severity of harm, $P$ is the probability of occurrence of the harm, and $D$ is the probability that the failure will *not* be detected before reaching the patient (higher D = harder to detect).

### Step 2 — Compute RPN for FM-1 (Pump fails to stop)

We substitute the ratings into the formula:

$$RPN_{FM-1} = S \times P \times D = 10 \times 6 \times 4$$

We perform the multiplication left to right:

$$RPN_{FM-1} = 10 \times 6 = 60$$

$$RPN_{FM-1} = 60 \times 4 = 240$$

Therefore, $\boxed{RPN_{FM-1} = 240}$.

### Step 3 — Compute RPN for FM-2 (Incorrect drug name on display)

We again substitute:

$$RPN_{FM-2} = 3 \times 8 \times 7$$

We compute step by step:

$$RPN_{FM-2} = 3 \times 8 = 24$$

$$RPN_{FM-2} = 24 \times 7 = 168$$

Therefore, $\boxed{RPN_{FM-2} = 168}$.

### Step 4 — Compute RPN for FM-3 (Alarm silenced when battery low)

Substituting:

$$RPN_{FM-3} = 9 \times 5 \times 6$$

Computing in sequence:

$$RPN_{FM-3} = 9 \times 5 = 45$$

$$RPN_{FM-3} = 45 \times 6 = 270$$

Therefore, $\boxed{RPN_{FM-3} = 270}$.

### Step 5 — Rank and Interpret

| Rank | Failure Mode | RPN | Test Priority | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | FM-3 (Alarm silenced) | 270 | **Highest** — Test FIRST | Mandatory redesign or additional control |
| 2 | FM-1 (Pump fails to stop) | 240 | High — Test SECOND | Redundant STOP hardware path + SW test |
| 3 | FM-2 (Display drug name) | 168 | Medium — Test THIRD | Software fix + regression test |

**Interpretation for the KTU answer:**
- All three RPNs exceed the typical threshold of 100, indicating that **all three require risk control measures** to be implemented and **verified by dedicated test cases** in the test plan.
- The test execution order in the test plan must be **FM-3 → FM-1 → FM-2**, regardless of chronological development sequence.
- The test cases corresponding to FM-3 and FM-1 must be classified as **Safety-Critical** and must achieve **MC/DC code coverage** per IEC 62304 Class C requirements.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Code Coverage & Requirement Traceability

**Problem Statement:**
A **spo2 pulse oximeter firmware** module consists of 1,250 lines of C code containing 80 branch points. During unit testing, the test suite executed 1,200 statements and 76 branches.

Calculate:
1. Statement coverage
2. Branch coverage
3. Comment on whether the coverage meets the IEC 62304 Class C requirement (which mandates **100% branch coverage** for Class C software per IEC 62304 §5.5.5).

### Step 1 — Statement Coverage Formula

$$\text{StmtCov} = \frac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100\%$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\text{StmtCov} = \frac{1200}{1250} \times 100\%$$

We simplify the fraction:

$$\text{StmtCov} = \frac{1200}{1250} = 0.96$$

Converting to percentage:

$$\text{StmtCov} = 0.96 \times 100\% = 96.0\%$$

### Step 2 — Branch Coverage Formula

$$\text{BrCov} = \frac{\text{Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100\%$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\text{BrCov} = \frac{76}{80} \times 100\%$$

Simplifying:

$$\text{BrCov} = \frac{76}{80} = 0.95$$

Converting to percentage:

$$\text{BrCov} = 0.95 \times 100\% = 95.0\%$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation Against IEC 62304 Class C

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The IEC 62304 standard mandates that for **Class C software**, branch coverage **must equal 100%**. Our achieved value of **95.0%** falls short by 5 percentage points, meaning **4 branches remain untested**.

**Therefore the test suite FAILS the exit criteria for Class C software.** The test plan must direct the test engineer to:

1. Add **4 additional test cases** to exercise the uncovered branches.
2. Re-run the coverage tool.
3. Document the new coverage report.
4. Obtain **independent verification** (since Class C requires independence per IEC 62304 §5.1.6).

### Step 4 — Requirement Coverage Cross-Check

Assume the pulse oximeter's **Software Requirements Specification (SwRS)** contains 48 testable requirements. After execution, 46 requirements have at least one passing test case.

$$\text{ReqCov} = \frac{46}{48} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{ReqCov} = 0.9583 \times 100\% = 95.83\%$$

**Two requirements (Req-019 and Req-038) have no passing test.** These are *requirement orphans* and must be either:
- Deleted from the SwRS (gold-plating removal), or
- Have test cases designed and executed before release.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Test Effort Estimation (Halstead-Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A developer estimates $N = 50$ test cases, a complexity growth rate $r = 0.05$ per day, project duration $d = 10$ days, and verifier velocity $v = 5$ test cases per day. Estimate the total test effort in person-days.

### Step 1 — Recall the Effort Formula

$$E = \frac{N \times (1 + r)^{d}}{v}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Growth Factor

$$(1 + r)^{d} = (1 + 0.05)^{10} = (1.05)^{10}$$

We evaluate step by step using a standard calculator expansion:

$$(1.05)^{2} = 1.1025$$

$$(1.05)^{4} = (1.1025)^{2} = 1.21550625$$

$$(1.05)^{8} = (1.21550625)^{2} = 1.47745544$$

$$(1.05)^{10} = (1.05)^{8} \times (1.05)^{2} = 1.47745544 \times 1.1025 = 1.62889463$$

### Step 3 — Multiply by N

$$N \times (1 + r)^{d} = 50 \times 1.62889463 = 81.4447$$

### Step 4 — Divide by Velocity

$$E = \frac{81.4447}{5} = 16.289 \text{ person-days}$$

### Step 5 — Add Buffer for Regression

Industry practice adds a 20% regression buffer:

$$E_{\text{total}} = 1.20 \times 16.289 = 19.55 \text{ person-days}$$

Therefore, the test plan should budget approximately **20 person-days** for this phase.

## 3.4 Summary Analytical Matrix — Test Plan Decisions vs. Triggers

| Test Plan Decision | Triggering Condition | KTU Buzzword |
|---|---|---|
| Reduce test scope | Class A software, low RPN | "Risk-based reduction justified" |
| Increase test scope | Class C, high RPN, novel algorithm | "Defensive depth" |
| Add independence | Class C, any safety-critical path | "Independent V&V" |
| Add regression suite | Any change to previously tested code | "Regression triggered" |
| Add formal methods | Class C with no redundancy | "Formal verification" |
| Halt release | Exit criteria not met, open Sev-1 defects | "Release hold" |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question must be answered in **3–4 sentences** or a short bullet list. KTU valuation awards 1 mark for keyword, 1 mark for accuracy, 1 mark for relevance.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, PEEBT752]**
*Define a "Software-Hardware Test Plan" for a medical device. List FOUR elements that such a plan must contain.*

**Model Answer:**
A Software-Hardware Test Plan is a formally approved regulatory document that defines the strategy, scope, resources, schedule, and acceptance criteria for verifying and validating the integrated hardware-software behavior of a medical device.
The four mandatory elements are: **(i) Test scope and test items**, **(ii) Test levels (unit, integration, system, acceptance)** with entry/exit criteria, **(iii) Traceability matrix** linking each requirement to test cases, and **(iv) Roles and responsibilities** including an independent verifier for safety-critical software.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark per correct element, max 4 elements. If you write "test cases" without linking to requirements, you lose the 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, PEEBT752]**
*Distinguish between "Verification" and "Validation" in the context of medical device software testing. Give ONE example of each.*

**Model Answer:**
**Verification** answers *"Are we building the product right?"* — it confirms through objective evidence that the software and hardware outputs match the documented specifications, typically via unit, integration, and system testing.
**Validation** answers *"Are we building the right product?"* — it confirms that the device meets the user's clinical needs and intended use, typically via clinical simulation or summative usability testing per IEC 62366-1.

*Example — Verification:* Executing a test case to confirm the infusion pump delivers a flow rate within ±5% of the commanded value.
*Example — Validation:* Having a nurse use the pump on a simulator to deliver medication to a mannequin patient and confirming the workflow supports safe clinical use.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE convention: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers internal choice. We provide **Question A and Question B as alternatives**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEEBT752]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure and contents of a software-hardware test plan for a Class C medical device software (per IEC 62304). Highlight the role of the **traceability matrix** and **independent verification**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A digital ECG monitor has the following three software-related hazards. Compute the RPN for each and recommend a test prioritization order:

| Hazard | S | P | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| H1: Lead-off detection fails, monitor shows flatline instead of alarm | 9 | 4 | 5 |
| H2: Display brightness cannot be reduced (battery drain, no patient harm) | 2 | 7 | 3 |
| H3: Defibrillation synchronization pulse is delayed by 80 ms | 10 | 3 | 7 |

---

### MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Structure of a Test Plan for Class C Software

The test plan for a **Class C** medical device software must be more rigorous than for Class A or B because of the potential for **death or serious injury**. The structure is as follows:

1. **Document Control Header** — Unique ID, version, author, approver signatures, revision history. This satisfies **ISO 13485 §4.2.4** control of records.

2. **Scope and Test Items** — Identifies the specific hardware revision (e.g., "ECG Main Board v3.2"), firmware version (e.g., "FW 2.4.1"), accessories, and any SOUP/COTS components with version numbers.

3. **Reference Documents** — System Requirements Spec (SyRS), Software Requirements Spec (SwRS), Risk Management File (RMF), Hardware Design Spec, applicable standards (IEC 62304, IEC 60601-1, ISO 14971).

4. **Software Safety Classification** — Must explicitly state "**Class C** per IEC 62304 §5.1.5" and the rationale. This is the most commonly missed item.

5. **Test Levels** — Unit (with **MC/DC coverage**), Integration (HW-SW interfaces), System (EMC, electrical safety, environmental), and Acceptance (clinical simulation).

6. **Test Strategy** — Mix of manual, automated, simulation, hardware-in-loop. Specifies the test tools (e.g., "VectorCAST for unit testing, oscilloscope Tektronix MSO46 for signal timing, Calysto EMC test suite for IEC 60601-1-2").

7. **Test Environment** — Calibrated equipment with valid calibration certificates, environmental chamber, EMI-shielded room for EMC.

8. **Entry and Exit Criteria** —
   - *Entry to System Test:* All unit and integration tests passed, code review completed, defect backlog contains zero Severity-1 open defects.
   - *Exit from System Test:* 100% requirements coverage, all risk control measures verified, all regulatory test reports signed.

9. **Roles and Responsibilities** — For Class C, IEC 62304 §5.1.6 mandates **independent verification**. The Test Manager, Test Engineers, and Independent Verifier must be identified by name and qualification.

10. **Traceability Matrix** — A bidirectional mapping:
    - *Forward:* REQ-ID → TC-ID → Result.
    - *Backward:* TC-ID → REQ-ID (to prevent orphan test cases).
    - *Bidirectional to risk:* REQ-ID → Hazard → Risk Control → Test.

    The matrix must show **100% requirement coverage** and **100% risk control verification** for Class C.

11. **Defect and Issue Management** — Severity classification, escalation matrix, and exit criteria for moving from "In Test" to "Released".

12. **Regression Strategy** — Any change to code or configuration triggers a regression test scope defined in the plan.

13. **SOUP/COTS Handling** — Each third-party component must have its own risk analysis, version pinned, and update policy documented.

14. **Approval Block** — Signed by QA Manager, Regulatory Affairs, Independent Verifier, and Project Manager.

#### Part (b) — RPN Calculation and Test Prioritization

**Step 1 — Recall Formula:**

$$RPN = S \times P \times D$$

**Step 2 — Compute RPN for H1 (Lead-off failure):**

$$RPN_{H1} = 9 \times 4 \times 5$$

$$RPN_{H1} = 36 \times 5 = 180$$

**Step 3 — Compute RPN for H2 (Brightness control):**

$$RPN_{H2} = 2 \times 7 \times 3$$

$$RPN_{H2} = 14 \times 3 = 42$$

**Step 4 — Compute RPN for H3 (Defibrillation sync delay):**

$$RPN_{H3} = 10 \times 3 \times 7$$

$$RPN_{H3} = 30 \times 7 = 210$$

**Step 5 — Rank and Interpret:**

| Rank | Hazard | RPN | Test Priority | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | H3 — Defib sync delay | 210 | **HIGHEST** | Safety-critical, MC/DC required |
| 2 | H1 — Lead-off failure | 180 | High | Risk control: redundant lead detection |
| 3 | H2 — Brightness control | 42 | Low | Acceptable, document residual risk |

**Interpretation:**
- **H3 (RPN = 210):** Highest priority. The test plan must include a **timing accuracy test** verifying sync pulse delay is ≤ 20 ms (industry tolerance). The corresponding test case must be classified as **Safety-Critical** and achieve **MC/DC coverage**.
- **H1 (RPN = 180):** Must be tested via a **fault injection test** — physically disconnect a lead and confirm alarm fires within 3 seconds.
- **H2 (RPN = 42):** Below the 50 threshold; testing is required but can be deprioritized and bundled with regression.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students compute RPN correctly but **fail to convert the RPN into a test plan action**. A 14-mark answer MUST link every calculated RPN to a *specific test case* and a *coverage requirement*. Writing only the arithmetic will cap your marks at 8/14.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **V-Model** as applied to medical device software-hardware testing. How does it enforce requirement traceability? Why is it preferred over a linear Waterfall model for safety-critical medical devices?

**(b) [7 Marks]** During unit testing of a ventilator's pressure-control firmware (Class C per IEC 62304), the code consists of 4,200 statements and 220 branches. The test suite executed 4,150 statements and 209 branches. Calculate statement coverage and branch coverage. State whether the test plan exit criteria are met. If not, list the corrective actions.

---

### MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — The V-Model for Medical Device Testing

The **V-Model** is a development and testing framework in which each development phase on the **left arm** has a corresponding verification phase on the **right arm**, joined at the apex by coding. For medical device software, the V-Model typically comprises:

**Left Arm (Development):**
1. User Needs / Intended Use
2. System Requirements (SyRS)
3. Software Architecture + Hardware Architecture
4. Detailed Design (modules)
5. Coding (implementation)

**Right Arm (Verification):**
1. Unit Testing ← Detailed Design
2. Integration Testing (HW-SW) ← Architecture
3. System Testing ← System Requirements
4. Acceptance / Validation Testing ← User Needs

**How it enforces traceability:**
Each requirement flows down via decomposition to code, and each test case on the right arm traces *back up* to a specific requirement. The Traceability Matrix (RTM) is maintained as a single document that maps REQ → Design → Code → Test → Risk Control. A test cannot exist without a parent requirement (no orphan tests), and a requirement cannot exist without at least one test (no untested requirements). This dual binding is exactly what **IEC 62304 §5.1.6** and **ISO 13485 §7.3.9** demand.

**Why preferred over Waterfall:**
- The V-Model forces **parallel planning of test activities** during design — tests are written *as* the design is written, not after.
- It makes **defect containment cheaper** because an error caught at unit test costs ~10× less to fix than one caught at system test.
- For **Class C software**, the V-Model's bidirectional arrows make independent verification practical and auditable.
- It naturally accommodates **hardware-software co-testing**, which a linear Waterfall model handles poorly (Waterfall typically tests hardware and software in separate silos, leading to integration surprises at the end).

#### Part (b) — Coverage Computation and Corrective Actions

**Step 1 — Statement Coverage:**

$$\text{StmtCov} = \frac{4150}{4200} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{StmtCov} = 0.988095 \times 100\% = 98.81\%$$

**Step 2 — Branch Coverage:**

$$\text{BrCov} = \frac{209}{220} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{BrCov} = 0.95 \times 100\% = 95.00\%$$

**Step 3 — Compliance Check Against IEC 62304 Class C:**

IEC 62304 §5.5.5 mandates that for **Class C software**, the test plan exit criteria must include **100% branch coverage** (or justified alternative if 100% is not achievable). Our achieved value of **95%** falls short by **5 percentage points**, meaning **11 branches remain untested**.

**Therefore: EXIT CRITERIA ARE NOT MET. The test plan must NOT be signed off for release.**

**Step 4 — Corrective Actions:**
1. Identify the 11 untested branches using the coverage tool's report.
2. Design **11 additional test cases** to exercise each untested branch.
3. Re-execute the unit test suite and regenerate the coverage report.
4. If 100% branch coverage is genuinely unachievable (e.g., defensive code for impossible inputs), provide a **written justification** with a risk-based argument approved by the Independent Verifier.
5. Obtain **independent re-verification** per IEC 62304 §5.1.6 (different person from the developer).
6. Update the Traceability Matrix to reflect the new tests.
7. Re-issue the unit test report with version control.
8. Only after 100% branch coverage (or justified waiver) is achieved, sign the unit test exit and proceed to integration testing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often quote "100% branch coverage" as the requirement but **do not state what to do if it is not met**. A complete answer MUST include the corrective action plan (above) for full 7 marks. Also, do not confuse "statement coverage" with "branch coverage" — they are different metrics, and conflating them costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Exam-Eve Must-Know):**

- ✅ A **Test Plan** is a *regulatory document*, not just an internal checklist. It must be **version-controlled and approved** by QA and Regulatory Affairs.
- ✅ Always name the **standards** in your answer: IEC 62304, IEC 60601-1, ISO 14971, ISO 13485, IEC 62366-1.
- ✅ **V-Model** is the expected development-testing framework for safety-critical medical devices.
- ✅ **Four test levels** to remember: Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance.
- ✅ **IEC 62304 Safety Classes:** A (no injury) / B (non-serious injury) / C (death or serious injury). Class C demands **MC/DC** code coverage and **independent verification**.
- ✅ **RPN = S × P × D.** Always compute all three, rank the failure modes, and *link each RPN to a specific test case* in the plan.
- ✅ **Verification = "Building the product right"**; **Validation = "Building the right product"** — this is the single most-favoured 3-mark KTU question.
- ✅ **Traceability Matrix** is *bidirectional* — forward (REQ→Test) and backward (Test→REQ). It must achieve **100% coverage** for Class C.
- ✅ **SOUP / COTS** must be risk-analyzed and version-pinned.
- ✅ **Regression testing** is triggered by *any* code or configuration change.
- ✅ For Class C: **independent verifier must be a different person** than the developer.
- ✅ Test effort estimation uses **$E = \dfrac{N \times (1+r)^{d}}{v}$** — add 20% regression buffer.
- ✅ Branch coverage of **100%** is the Class C exit criterion. Anything less → corrective action.
- ✅ The test plan is **regulatory evidence** — it is reviewed by FDA / EU Notified Body / CDSCO auditors during submission.
- ✅ Hardware-Software integration testing is **distinct** from software-software integration; both must appear in the plan.
- ✅ Environmental testing (temperature, humidity, vibration per IEC 60068) and EMC testing (per IEC 60601-1-2) are part of the **system test** stage.
- ✅ **Usability validation** (IEC 62366-1) is summative testing with real end-users — typically the last test before release.
- ✅ The output deliverables of the test plan are: **Test Cases, Test Procedures, Test Reports, Traceability Matrix, Defect Logs**.

> **End of Notes — PEEBT752 Module 3 — Software-Hardware Test Plan for Medical Devices**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Testing - Types of Testing. Manufacturing Supply Chain: Product Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** System Testing - Types of Testing. Manufacturing Supply Chain: Product Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — System Testing & Manufacturing Supply Chain

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 System Testing (Formal Definition)

**System Testing** is the phase of biomedical device development in which the **complete, fully integrated device** (hardware + firmware + software + mechanical + biological interface) is tested as a unified whole against its **design specifications, user needs, and regulatory requirements (ISO 13485, IEC 60601-1, FDA 21 CFR Part 820)**. It is a *black-box* validation activity executed before clinical/acceptance testing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim from syllabus spirit):**
> *System testing is the comprehensive testing of a complete and integrated biomedical device to evaluate its compliance with specified functional, performance, safety, and regulatory requirements prior to market release.*

### 1.2 Manufacturing Supply Chain — Product Manufacturing (Formal Definition)

The **Manufacturing Supply Chain** in the biomedical industry is the *end-to-end network* of organizations, processes, resources, and information flows involved in **design transfer, raw material procurement, fabrication, assembly, quality control, sterilization, packaging, distribution, and post-market surveillance** of a medical device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Product Manufacturing** is the operational stage where the validated design is **transferred from R&D to production**, executed under **Design Controls** and **Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP)** per **21 CFR Part 820** and **ISO 13485:2016**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (The "Hospital Stethoscope" Intuition)

Think of a **stethoscope assembly line in a hospital equipment factory**:

- A **stethoscope** has ear-tips (rubber), tubing (PVC), chest-piece (stainless steel), and a diaphragm (resin). Each part is sourced from different suppliers.
- Before full production begins, the *system test* is like a doctor using the **finished stethoscope on multiple patients** to check sound clarity, comfort, and durability — not testing the rubber alone, but the *whole instrument*.
- The *supply chain* is the entire chain: rubber plantation → tubing extrusion → steel forging → assembly workshop → QC lab → sterilization chamber → shipping warehouse → hospital.

**For a Pacemaker / Defibrillator / Dialysis Machine — system testing is non-negotiable because failure = patient death.**

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Defect Density (DD):** defects per KLOC (thousand lines of code) for software components.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** ≥ 100,000 hours (typical for life-supporting devices).
- **IEC 60601-1 Compliance:** mandatory electrical safety benchmark.
- **ISO 14971:** Risk Management file required for **every** Class II/III device.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 3 specifically tests understanding of *which test applies at which stage* and *how design transfer occurs into a regulated manufacturing pipeline*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "V-Model" of Testing in Biomedical Device Development

The biomedical industry follows a **V-Model** (Verification on the descending arm, Validation on the ascending arm). System Testing sits on the **right arm, second from top**, before clinical evaluation.

| V-Model Left Arm (Verification — "Are we building the product right?") | V-Model Right Arm (Validation — "Are we building the right product?") |
|---|---|
| User Requirements | Acceptance Testing |
| System Requirements | **System Testing** ← *(our focus)* |
| High-Level Design | Integration Testing |
| Detailed Design | Unit Testing |

## 2.2 Types of Testing (Hierarchical, from Lowest to Highest Level)

### A. Verification Tests (Lower V-Arm)

1. **Unit Testing** — Tests individual modules (a single op-amp circuit, one C function).
2. **Integration Testing** — Tests interfaces between modules (sensor→ADC→MCU pipeline).
3. **System Testing** — Tests the complete device in a *simulated use environment*.

### B. Validation Tests (Upper V-Arm)

4. **Acceptance Testing (UAT — User Acceptance Testing)** — Performed by clinicians/end-users in a near-real environment.
5. **Clinical Evaluation / Clinical Investigation** — Final stage per **ISO 14155** for high-risk devices.

### C. Specialized Tests (Performed in Parallel)

| Test Type | Purpose in Biomedical Context | KTU-Mentioned Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance Testing** | Throughput, accuracy, precision of measurement (e.g., SpO₂ reading error ≤ ±2%) | IEC 60601-2-XX |
| **Safety Testing** | Electrical, mechanical, thermal, radiation safety | IEC 60601-1 |
| **EMC Testing** | Electromagnetic compatibility (no interference in OT) | IEC 60601-1-2 |
| **Usability / Human Factors Testing** | Per IEC 62366 — can clinicians use it without error? | IEC 62366-1 |
| **Biocompatibility Testing** | Per ISO 10993 — material safe for body contact? | ISO 10993-1 |
| **Sterility Testing** | For invasive devices — SAL ≤ 10⁻⁶ | ISO 11137 (radiation), ISO 11135 (EtO) |
| **Stress / Load Testing** | Beyond normal operating limits | Internal SOP |
| **Regression Testing** | Re-verify after a change | Internal SOP |
| **Software Validation** | For embedded software (SaMD) | IEC 62304 |
| **Cybersecurity Testing** | For networked devices (IoMT) | FDA 2023 Cybersecurity Guidance |

## 2.3 Why Each Test Matters — The "Why & How"

- **Unit testing** catches errors at the *cheapest* fix cost. A bug found in field costs **1000×** more to fix than one caught at unit level.
- **Integration testing** catches *interface* errors — e.g., a blood-pressure module produces a value, but the display driver formats it as "NaN".
- **System testing** catches *emergent behavior* — the device works alone but fails when connected to hospital Wi-Fi.
- **Acceptance testing** ensures the *human* can operate it — the most common FDA recall cause is **use error (≈ 44%)**, not design defect.

## 2.4 Manufacturing Supply Chain — Product Manufacturing Stages

The supply chain for a Class II biomedical device (e.g., infusion pump) follows these **8 regulated stages**:

1. **Design Transfer (Stage 0)** — R&D hands off to Manufacturing. Per FDA, a *Design Transfer Plan* and *Design History File (DHF)* must exist.
2. **Supplier Qualification (Stage 1)** — ISO 13485 requires *Approved Supplier List (ASL)* with audit certificates.
3. **Incoming Quality Control (IQC) (Stage 2)** — Incoming raw materials (e.g., medical-grade silicone) are tested against specs.
