4. **Production / Assembly (Stage 3)** — Executed under GMP in a **controlled environment (ISO 14644 cleanroom, e.g., Class 7 for non-sterile, Class 5 for sterile)**.
5. **In-Process Quality Control (IPQC) (Stage 4)** — Dimensional checks, solder joint inspection (AOI), and statistical process control (SPC) charts.
6. **Finished Device QC (Stage 5)** — Functional test, visual inspection, packaging integrity.
7. **Sterilization & Labeling (Stage 6)** — Validated sterilization process + UDI (Unique Device Identification) label.
8. **Distribution & Post-Market Surveillance (Stage 7)** — Storage per ICH Q1A, traceability via lot number, complaint handling.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 Part B numerical/analytical questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Defect Density (DD) | $$DD = \frac{N_{d}}{KLOC}$$ | defects/KLOC | Software validation of firmware |
| 2 | Reliability Function | $$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$ | dimensionless | MTBF calculation |
| 3 | MTBF | $$MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{T_{total}}{N_{f}}$$ | hours | Reliability for life-supporting devices |
| 4 | Process Capability Index | $$C_{p} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$ | dimensionless | Manufacturing tolerance analysis |
| 3 | Sterility Assurance Level (SAL) | $$SAL = 10^{-6}$$ | probability | Terminal sterilization target |
| 6 | First Pass Yield (FPY) | $$FPY = \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$$ | % | In-process QC efficiency |
| 7 | Cycle Time | $$CT = \frac{T_{available}}{N_{units}}$$ | min/unit | Production line throughput |
| 8 | Cumulative Error (sensor chain) | $$\sigma_{total} = \sqrt{\sigma_{1}^{2} + \sigma_{2}^{2} + \sigma_{3}^{2}}$$ | unit (e.g., mmHg) | System-level accuracy budget |
| 9 | DPMO (Six Sigma) | $$DPMO = \frac{N_{defects}}{N_{opportunities} \times N_{units}} \times 10^{6}$$ | defects/million | Manufacturing quality metric |
| 10 | Beta Sterilization Dose | $$D = D_{ref} \cdot 10^{\frac{T_{ref} - T}{z}}$$ | kGy | EtO / gamma dose verification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never write `\vert x \vert` inside a markdown table — table parser will break. Always use `$x$` or plain `mod x` notation. In exam scripts, **always show units**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: System-Level Cumulative Error Budget (Gaussian Error Propagation)

A biomedical device's accuracy depends on multiple cascaded error sources. KTU expects students to derive the *combined error* of a sensor chain (e.g., pressure sensor → amplifier → ADC → MCU).

### Step 1 — List Independent Error Sources

Let the system output $Y$ be a function of independent variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$.

$$Y = f(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Variance Summation Law (Tartaglia / Gauss Propagation)

For small independent random errors, the total variance equals the sum of partial-derivative-weighted variances:

$$
\sigma_{Y}^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \frac{\partial Y}{\partial X_i} \right)^{2} \sigma_{X_i}^{2}
$$

**Conversion logic:** We square each partial derivative so that random positive and negative deviations combine in quadrature (root-sum-square, RSS), not linearly. This is the standard engineering practice for *uncorrelated* noise sources.

### Step 3 — Special Case: Pure Sum of Independent Quantities

If $Y = X_1 + X_2 + X_3$ (e.g., three cascaded amplifier stages each adding noise), then $\frac{\partial Y}{\partial X_i} = 1$ for all $i$, giving:

$$
\sigma_{Y}^{2} = \sigma_{X_1}^{2} + \sigma_{X_2}^{2} + \sigma_{X_3}^{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Take the Square Root

$$
\sigma_{Y} = \sqrt{\sigma_{X_1}^{2} + \sigma_{X_2}^{2} + \sigma_{X_3}^{2}}
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Example (NIBP Cuff System)

Suppose a non-invasive blood pressure (NIBP) monitor has these error sources:

| Stage | Source | $\sigma$ (mmHg) |
|---|---|---|
| Cuff sensor (transducer) | Hysteresis + temperature drift | $\sigma_1 = 1.5$ |
| Instrumentation amplifier | Thermal noise | $\sigma_2 = 0.8$ |
| 12-bit ADC | Quantization | $\sigma_3 = 0.6$ |
| Firmware digital filter | Rounding error | $\sigma_4 = 0.3$ |

**Compute the total system error:**

$$
\sigma_{total} = \sqrt{(1.5)^{2} + (0.8)^{2} + (0.6)^{2} + (0.3)^{2}} \text{ mmHg}
$$

$$
\sigma_{total} = \sqrt{2.25 + 0.64 + 0.36 + 0.09} \text{ mmHg}
$$

$$
\sigma_{total} = \sqrt{3.34} \text{ mmHg}
$$

$$
\sigma_{total} \approx 1.827 \text{ mmHg}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The single largest contributor ($1.5$ mmHg from the transducer) dominates. The system error is **1.83 mmHg**, which is well within the IEC 80601-2-30 limit of $\pm 3$ mmHg mean error → **system PASSES**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always convert squared values explicitly before adding. KTU examiners check this step.

## 3.2 Derivation: First Pass Yield (FPY) and Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

A common KTU Part B (14-mark) question is to compute overall yield for a multi-stage process.

### Step 1 — FPY for a Single Station

$$
FPY_i = \frac{\text{Units passing station } i \text{ first time}}{\text{Units entering station } i}
$$

### Step 2 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

For $n$ sequential stations:

$$
RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i
$$

### Step 3 — Worked Example: Pacemaker Production

| Station | FPY |
|---|---|
| PCB Assembly | 0.98 |
| Micro-soldering | 0.95 |
| Battery Pack | 0.99 |
| Final Test | 0.97 |

**RTY calculation:**

$$
RTY = 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.99 \times 0.97
$$

$$
RTY = (0.98 \times 0.95) \times (0.99 \times 0.97)
$$

$$
RTY = 0.931 \times 0.9603
$$

$$
RTY \approx 0.8941 \approx 89.41\%
$$

**Conversion logic:** Out of every 1,000 pacemakers entering the line, only **~894** pass all four stations first time. The remaining 106 require rework (which in medical device manufacturing is a *deviation* requiring a Corrective and Preventive Action, CAPA).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Test Type vs. Failure Mode Caught

| Failure Mode | Unit | Integration | System | Acceptance | Field |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Logic error in code | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Sensor–ADC mismatch | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Battery drains during surgery | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Surgeon cannot use one-handed | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| EMC interference in OT | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Sterility loss in transit | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Software regression after patch | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend:** ✓ = effectively caught, ✗ = not effectively caught.
> Notice that **no single test catches every failure mode** — this is why *defense-in-depth* testing is required.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 V-Model of Biomedical Device Testing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef vfill fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1967D2,color:#0B2545;
    classDef wfill fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#3C0F0F;

    A1["User Requirements"]:::vfill
    A2["System Requirements"]:::vfill
    A3["High Level Design"]:::vfill
    A4["Detailed Design"]:::vfill
    B1["Acceptance Testing"]:::wfill
    B2["System Testing"]:::wfill
    B3["Integration Testing"]:::wfill
    B4["Unit Testing"]:::wfill

    A1 --- B1
    A2 --- B2
    A3 --- B3
    A4 --- B4
```

> **Reading guide:** Left arm flows *down* during design; right arm flows *up* during test execution. **System Testing (B2)** validates that the *System Requirements (A2)* are met.

## 4.2 System Testing Block — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef inFill fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#F29900,color:#5C2E00;
    classDef testFill fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#1E8E3E,color:#0D5226;
    classDef outFill fill:#F3E8FD,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#2C0B43;

    P1["Patient Interface Electrodes Sensors Actuators"]:::inFill
    P2["Analog Front End Amplifier Filter"]:::inFill
    P3["Embedded MCU Firmware Algorithms"]:::inFill
    P4["Power Management Battery Charger"]:::inFill
    P5["User Interface Display Buttons Alarm"]:::inFill
    P6["Connectivity Bluetooth WiFi USB"]:::inFill

    T1["Functional Test"]:::testFill
    T2["Performance Test"]:::testFill
    T3["Safety Test IEC 60601-1"]:::testFill
    T4["EMC Test IEC 60601-1-2"]:::testFill
    T5["Usability Test IEC 62366"]:::testFill
    T6["Software Validation IEC 62304"]:::testFill

    R1["Design History File DHF"]:::outFill
    R2["Risk Management File ISO 14971"]:::outFill
    R3["Test Summary Report"]:::outFill

    P1 --> T1
    P1 --> T3
    P2 --> T2
    P3 --> T6
    P4 --> T3
    P5 --> T5
    P6 --> T4
    P1 --> R1
    P3 --> R1
    P5 --> R1
    T1 --> R3
    T2 --> R3
    T3 --> R3
    T4 --> R3
    T5 --> R3
    T6 --> R3
    P1 --> R2
    P2 --> R2
    P4 --> R2
    P6 --> R2
```

## 4.3 Manufacturing Supply Chain — Product Manufacturing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef gate fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#B06000,color:#3C2A00;
    classDef prod fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#0B2545;
    classDef qc fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#3C0F0F;
    classDef dist fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#1E8E3E,color:#0D5226;

    S0["Stage 0 Design Transfer DHF Checkpoint"]:::gate
    S1["Stage 1 Supplier Qualification ASL Audit"]:::gate
    S2["Stage 2 Incoming QC Raw Material Inspection"]:::qc
    S3["Stage 3 Production Assembly Cleanroom Class 7"]:::prod
    S4["Stage 4 In Process QC AOI and SPC"]:::qc
    S5["Stage 5 Finished Device QC Functional Test"]:::qc
    S6["Stage 6 Sterilization EtO or Gamma UDI Label"]:::prod
    S7["Stage 7 Distribution and Post Market Surveillance"]:::dist

    S0 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7
    S7 -.feedback loop.-> S0
```

> **Reading guide:** The **feedback loop** from Stage 7 → Stage 0 represents *Post-Market Surveillance* feeding field complaints back into design improvement — a **mandatory** element of ISO 13485 Clause 8.2.1.

## 4.4 Testing Pyramid for Biomedical Software

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef lvl fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#0B2545;

    L1["Acceptance and Clinical Evaluation Slow Few"]:::lvl
    L2["System Testing Mid Volume"]:::lvl
    L3["Integration Testing"]:::lvl
    L4["Unit Testing Fast Many"]:::lvl

    L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1
```

> **Engineering wisdom:** Cheap bugs live at the *bottom*, expensive bugs at the *top*. KTU specifically tests if students know *where* to apply *which* test.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Differentiate between Verification and Validation in the context of biomedical device development. Which category does System Testing fall under?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| Question answered | "Are we building the product *right*?" | "Are we building the *right* product?" |
| Performed on | Design outputs, specifications | Finished device, final user environment |
| Timing | Throughout development | At end of each development phase |
| Output evidence | Test reports, inspection records | Clinical evaluation, user acceptance |

**System Testing** falls under **Validation** (specifically, it is the highest form of *internal* validation before clinical evaluation). *(1 mark for the differentiation table, 1 mark for category, 1 mark for justification.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**List any four specialized tests that must be performed on a Class IIb implantable biomedical device (e.g., pacemaker) prior to market release. State the corresponding ISO/IEC standard for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Biocompatibility testing** — ISO 10993-1 *(0.75 mark)*
2. **Electrical safety testing** — IEC 60601-1 *(0.75 mark)*
3. **Software lifecycle validation** — IEC 62304 *(0.75 mark)*
4. **Usability / Human Factors engineering** — IEC 62366-1 *(0.75 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Provided*

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Test Selection & Error Budgeting*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat V-Model diagram, explain the various levels of testing performed during the design and development of a biomedical device. Highlight where **System Testing** is positioned and what it specifically validates. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A digital thermometer system has the following independent error sources in its measurement chain:

| Stage | Source | $\sigma$ ($^\circ$C) |
|---|---|---|
| Thermistor sensor | Self-heating | 0.10 |
| Bridge circuit | Resistor tolerance | 0.05 |
| Instrumentation amplifier | Thermal noise | 0.04 |
| 16-bit ADC | Quantization | 0.02 |
| Firmware polynomial fit | Rounding | 0.03 |

**Compute the total system-level error and determine whether the device meets the ISO 80601-2-56 limit of $\pm 0.20\,^\circ$C (95% confidence, i.e., $1.96\sigma$ total).** *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution A(a) — 7 marks

| V-Model Element | Explanation | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Left arm — User Requirements | Defines clinical need, intended use, user profile | 1 |
| Left arm — System Requirements | Translates user needs into measurable specs | 0.5 |
| Left arm — High-Level Design | Architecture, sub-system interfaces | 0.5 |
| Left arm — Detailed Design | Circuit schematics, code modules, materials | 0.5 |
| Right arm — Unit Testing | Tests individual modules (one op-amp, one C file) | 1 |
| Right arm — Integration Testing | Tests sub-system interfaces (sensor + ADC + MCU) | 1 |
| **Right arm — System Testing** | **Tests complete integrated device; black-box validation against System Requirements** | 2 |
| Right arm — Acceptance Testing | User-site testing by clinicians | 0.5 |

> System Testing **specifically validates**: functional correctness, performance under defined conditions, electrical safety, EMC, usability, and compliance with applicable IEC/ISO standards — *before* clinical evaluation.

#### Model Solution A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify all 5 independent error sources** $(n = 5)$: *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 2 — Apply RSS (root-sum-square) formula for independent errors** *(1 mark for formula, 1.5 marks for substitution)*:

$$
\sigma_{total} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^{2} + \sigma_2^{2} + \sigma_3^{2} + \sigma_4^{2} + \sigma_5^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Square each term** *(1 mark)*:

$$
\sigma_{total} = \sqrt{(0.10)^{2} + (0.05)^{2} + (0.04)^{2} + (0.02)^{2} + (0.03)^{2}} \,\, ^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
\sigma_{total} = \sqrt{0.0100 + 0.0025 + 0.0016 + 0.0004 + 0.0009} \,\, ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the squares** *(0.5 mark)*:

$$
\sigma_{total} = \sqrt{0.0154} \,\, ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root** *(0.5 mark)*:

$$
\sigma_{total} \approx 0.1241 \,\, ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute 95% confidence bound** *(1 mark)*:

$$
\varepsilon_{95\%} = 1.96 \times \sigma_{total} = 1.96 \times 0.1241 \approx 0.243 \,\, ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 7 — Compare with ISO 80601-2-56 limit (0.20 °C)** *(1 mark)*:

Since $0.243\,^\circ\text{C} > 0.20\,^\circ\text{C}$, the device **FAILS** the standard. **Redesign recommendation:** reduce the largest contributor (thermistor self-heating, $\sigma_1 = 0.10$°C) by lowering excitation current or by adding a switch-on pulse scheme.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Manufacturing Supply Chain & Yield Analysis*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **8 stages of the manufacturing supply chain** for a Class II biomedical device (e.g., a digital blood-pressure monitor). For each stage, list one **key regulatory or quality deliverable**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An automated assembly line for ECG electrodes produces 5,000 units/day. The line has the following first-pass yield (FPY) at each of four sequential stations:

| Station | FPY |
|---|---|
| Foil lamination | 0.96 |
| Hydrogel dispensing | 0.93 |
| Snap attachment | 0.98 |
| Final QC | 0.97 |

**Calculate:**
*(i)* The **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** of the line. *(2 marks)*
*(ii)* The **expected daily number of defective units** (i.e., require rework). *(2 marks)*
*(iii)* If rework costs ₹40 per unit and a CAPA investigation costs ₹50,000 per lot, **comment** on the financial and regulatory implications. *(3 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution B(a) — 7 marks

| # | Stage | Key Deliverable (regulatory/quality) | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Design Transfer | Design Transfer Plan + DHF (Design History File) per 21 CFR 820.30 | 1 |
| 2 | Supplier Qualification | Approved Supplier List (ASL) with ISO 13485 audit certificates | 0.75 |
| 3 | Incoming Quality Control | Certificate of Analysis (CoA) + Incoming Inspection Report | 0.75 |
| 4 | Production / Assembly | Manufacturing Execution in ISO 14644 cleanroom; work instructions | 1 |
| 5 | In-Process QC | SPC charts, p-FMEAs, AOI records | 0.75 |
| 6 | Finished Device QC | Functional Test Report + visual inspection record | 0.75 |
| 7 | Sterilization & Labeling | Sterility validation per ISO 11137/11135; UDI per 21 CFR 830 | 1 |
| 8 | Distribution & PMS | Distribution records, complaint handling per ISO 13485 Cl. 8.2.1 | 1 |

> **Key insight:** Every stage must have a **documented evidence trail** — this is the *audit-ready* nature of medical-device manufacturing.

#### Model Solution B(b) — 7 marks

**(i) RTY Calculation** *(2 marks)*:

$$
RTY = FPY_1 \times FPY_2 \times FPY_3 \times FPY_4
$$

$$
RTY = 0.96 \times 0.93 \times 0.98 \times 0.97
$$

$$
RTY = (0.96 \times 0.93) \times (0.98 \times 0.97)
$$

$$
RTY = 0.8928 \times 0.9506 \approx 0.8487 \text{ or } 84.87\%
$$

**(ii) Defective units per day** *(2 marks)*:

$$
N_{defect} = N_{total} \times (1 - RTY) = 5000 \times (1 - 0.8487)
$$

$$
N_{defect} = 5000 \times 0.1513 \approx 756.5 \text{ units/day}
$$

**Rounded: 757 defective units per day** (assume partial unit rounded up; in practice, all 5,000 go through the line and the percentage is the metric).

**(iii) Financial & Regulatory Implications** *(3 marks)*:

- **Daily rework cost:** $757 \times \text{₹}40 = \text{₹}30{,}280$ per day.
- **Annual rework cost:** $30{,}280 \times 365 \approx \text{₹}1{,}10{,}52{,}200$ (≈ **₹1.1 Crore/year**) *(1 mark)*.
- **Regulatory concern:** With an RTY of only 84.87%, a CAPA is **mandatorily triggered** per ISO 13485 Clause 8.5.2 whenever defect trends exceed a defined threshold. Each CAPA investigation costs ₹50,000; if 4 CAPAs/year are raised, that adds ₹2,00,000. *(1 mark)*.
- **Process improvement priority:** The **Hydrogel dispensing station (FPY = 0.93)** is the **bottleneck** — improving it from 93% → 96% would lift RTY to 0.96 × 0.96 × 0.98 × 0.97 = 0.8759, saving ~₹14,000/day. *(1 mark)*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing verification vs. validation** — examiners will mark **0** for the wrong category in Q1. Memorize: *Verification = right product right; Validation = right product.*
> 2. **Forgetting units** in error-budget calculations — 0.5 mark deducted per missing unit.
> 3. **Squaring each term incorrectly** — common error: $0.10^{2}$ written as $0.10$ instead of $0.0100$. KTU strictly checks arithmetic.
> 4. **Not stating the regulatory deliverable** in supply-chain questions — 1 mark is reserved *specifically* for the deliverable (e.g., DHF, UDI, CoA). Writing only the stage name without the document loses that mark.
> 5. **In RTY problems**, students often write **additive** yields (e.g., $0.96 + 0.93$); this is **wrong**. RTY is strictly **multiplicative** for sequential stations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- ✅ **System Testing** = black-box test of *fully integrated* device against **System Requirements**, positioned at the **right arm of V-Model, second from top**.
- ✅ **V-Model left arm = Verification**, **right arm = Validation** — never confuse them.
- ✅ **Defect Density** $DD = N_d / KLOC$ — software quality metric.
- ✅ **MTBF** $MTBF = T_{total} / N_f$ — reliability benchmark; life-supporting devices target **≥ 100,000 hours**.
- ✅ **Cumulative system error** uses **RSS (root-sum-square)** formula — not linear sum.
- ✅ **SAL of 10⁻⁶** is the universal sterility target for terminal sterilization of invasive devices.
- ✅ **RTY (Rolled Throughput Yield)** is **multiplicative** across sequential stations.
- ✅ **8 regulated stages** of medical device manufacturing: Design Transfer → Supplier Qualification → IQC → Production → IPQC → Finished QC → Sterilization/Labeling → Distribution/PMS.
- ✅ **Mandatory standards for KTU viva:** **ISO 13485 (QMS)**, **ISO 14971 (Risk)**, **IEC 60601-1 (Electrical Safety)**, **IEC 62304 (Software)**, **IEC 62366 (Usability)**, **ISO 10993 (Biocompatibility)**, **21 CFR Part 820 (US FDA QSR)**.
- ✅ **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** is a Six-Sigma metric used in Class III device manufacturing.
- ✅ **Post-Market Surveillance** has a **mandatory feedback loop** back to design — ISO 13485 Clause 8.2.1.
- ✅ **UDI (Unique Device Identification)** is mandatory labeling per **21 CFR 830** and **EU MDR 2017/745**.
- ✅ For **Part B 14-mark questions**, structure answer as: **diagram/table (2 marks) + theory (5 marks) + numerical (5 marks) + comment (2 marks)**.
- ✅ Always end a numerical answer with a **decision sentence** (PASS/FAIL, justified).

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *Document, Validate, Verify, Monitor, Improve — in that order.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IQ/OQ/PQ
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IQ/OQ/PQ

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are IQ, OQ, and PQ?

In the regulated landscape of **Biomedical Device Design and Manufacturing**, **IQ/OQ/PQ** refers to the three sequential and mandatory stages of **Equipment Qualification** — a core pillar of process validation under **FDA 21 CFR Part 820**, **ISO 13485:2016**, and **GAMP 5 (Good Automated Manufacturing Practice)** guidelines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** IQ/OQ/PQ is the *cornerstone* of regulatory compliance for Class II and Class III biomedical devices. Without documented qualification, a device **cannot** be released for clinical or commercial use.

### Formal Definitions (As per KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

| Stage | Full Form | Definition |
|-------|-----------|------------|
| **IQ** | Installation Qualification | Documented verification that the equipment is **installed correctly**, in the correct environment, and according to the manufacturer's pre-approved specifications. |
| **OQ** | Operational Qualification | Documented verification that the equipment **operates correctly** across its entire predefined operating ranges and control limits under *challenge conditions*. |
| **PQ** | Performance Qualification | Documented verification that the equipment **consistently performs** to specification under *real-world production conditions* with actual or simulated product/process loads. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "New Car" Intuition

Imagine you have just purchased a brand-new car from a factory:

- **IQ (Installation Qualification)** is like the **delivery inspection**: the salesperson checks that the car arrived without dents, the engine number matches the invoice, all 4 wheels are mounted, and the car is parked in a proper garage (correct environment). *Did we get what we ordered, and is it set up correctly?*

- **OQ (Operational Qualification)** is like the **test drive on a controlled track**: the engineer pushes the car to its *maximum* speed, brakes harshly, drives on rough terrain, and tests the AC in extreme heat. *Does the car work correctly across its full operational range under stress?*

- **PQ (Performance Qualification)** is like the **daily commute for 30 days**: you drive the car in actual city traffic with your family in real weather to see if it reliably gets you to work every day. *Does it consistently work in real-world conditions with actual load?*

---

## 1.3 Key Regulatory Constants & Standards

The following **mandatory standards** govern IQ/OQ/PQ execution for biomedical devices:

- **FDA 21 CFR Part 820.70** — *Process Validation* (Manufacturing Process)
- **FDA 21 CFR Part 820.75** — *Process Validation Software*
- **ISO 13485:2016, Clause 7.5.6** — *Validation of Processes for Production and Service Provision*
- **GAMP 5** — *ISPE Baseline Pharmaceutical Engineering Guide, 2nd Edition (2022)*
- **ICH Q9** — *Quality Risk Management*
- **ASTM E2503** — *Standard Practice for Qualification of Imaging Equipment*

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical/Statistical Constants Used in PQ:**
> - Confidence Level: **95%** (typical minimum for biomedical PQ)
> - Process Capability Index: **Cpk ≥ 1.33** (industry standard)
> - Sample Size for PQ: typically **n ≥ 30** runs for statistical validity
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The V-Model of Equipment Qualification

IQ/OQ/PQ follows a strict **V-Model hierarchy**, meaning each stage produces evidence that feeds upward into the next. Skipping a stage invalidates the entire chain in the eyes of regulatory auditors (US FDA, Notified Bodies, CDSCO).

```
User Requirements (URS)
        |
        v
Functional Specification (FS)
        |
        v
Design Specification (DS)
        |
        v
    IQ --> OQ --> PQ --> Process Validation
```

---

## 2.2 Stage-by-Stage Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Installation Qualification (IQ) — The "Hardware Audit"

**Purpose:** Confirm that the *as-installed* equipment matches the *as-designed* specification.

**Key Verification Points (Bullet Logic):**

1. **Equipment Identification Verification**
   - Serial number, model number, firmware version match purchase order.
   - Manufacturer's Certificate of Conformance (CoC) present.

2. **Environmental Verification**
   - Room temperature: **18°C to 25°C** (typical for medical device labs).
   - Relative humidity: **30% to 65% RH**.
   - Cleanroom classification (ISO Class 7 or 8 for most assembly).

3. **Utility Verification**
   - Electrical supply: **230V AC ± 10%, 50 Hz** (India standard) or **120V AC, 60 Hz** (US).
   - Compressed air pressure, vacuum levels, deionized water quality.

4. **Documentation Verification**
   - User manuals, wiring diagrams, calibration certificates, piping & instrumentation diagrams (P&IDs).

5. **Software Installation (if applicable)**
   - Verification of version, license keys, antivirus status, network configuration.

---

### 2.2.2 Operational Qualification (OQ) — The "Functional Stress Test"

**Purpose:** Demonstrate that the equipment operates correctly across its *entire operating range*, including worst-case conditions.

**Key Verification Points:**

1. **Operational Range Challenge**
   - Test at **lower control limit (LCL)**, **setpoint**, and **upper control limit (UCL)**.
   - Example: For an autoclave set at **121°C**: test at 118°C, 121°C, 124°C.

2. **Alarm & Interlock Testing**
   - Trigger over-temperature, over-pressure, door-open alarms — verify safe shutdown.

3. **Calibration Verification**
   - Confirm sensors read within specified tolerance (e.g., ±0.5°C for incubator).

4. **Boundary/Edge-Case Testing**
   - Maximum load, minimum load, empty cycle, power failure recovery.

5. **Software Functional Testing (IQ-OQ boundary)**
   - User access levels, audit trail, electronic signature compliance per **21 CFR Part 11**.

---

### 2.2.3 Performance Qualification (PQ) — The "Real-World Proof"

**Purpose:** Prove the equipment *reliably and reproducibly* produces acceptable output under actual production conditions.

**Key Verification Points:**

1. **Process Capability Study**
   - Run the equipment with actual product/materials for a defined number of batches (typically **3 consecutive successful batches** minimum).

2. **Statistical Analysis**
   - Calculate **Cpk (Process Capability Index)** to prove the process is in control.
   - Acceptance criterion: **Cpk ≥ 1.33** (KTU board exam standard value).

3. **Repeatability & Reproducibility (R&R)**
   - Gauge R&R < 30% of process tolerance.

4. **Long-term Stability**
   - Monitor trend over 30–90 days.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula / Metric | Symbol / Equation | Boundary Condition | Unit / Typical Value | Engineering Utility |
|------------------|-------------------|--------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Process Capability Index** | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ | Dimensionless | Measures how well output fits within spec limits during PQ |
| **Process Capability (no shift)** | $C_{p} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $C_{p} \geq 1.33$ | Dimensionless | Assumes process is perfectly centered |
| **Mean** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | — | Same as variable | Center of PQ data |
| **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | — | Same as variable | Variability measure |
| **95% Confidence Interval** | $\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | n ≥ 30 | Same as variable | PQ acceptance interval |
| **Measurement Uncertainty (IQ)** | $U = k \cdot u_{c}$ | k = 2 (95% confidence) | Same as variable | Calibration verification |
| **Recovery Rate (PQ)** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Measured Value}}{\text{True Value}} \times 100\%$ | 95% – 105% | Percent | PQ accuracy test |
| **Acceptance Value (AV)** | $AV = 2 \cdot \vert \bar{x} - \text{Mean Target}\vert + k \cdot s$ | AV ≤ max allowed | — | USP <905> content uniformity |
| **OQ Tolerance Band** | $\text{Tol} = \pm \dfrac{FS}{2}$ | FS = Full Scale | Same as variable | Sensor OQ pass criteria |
| **PQ Sample Size (Normal)** | $n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$ | $\alpha = 0.05$ | Count | Required PQ runs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala University Examiner Tip:** Always cite the **standard** along with the formula (e.g., $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ as per **ISO 22514-2**).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

| Stage | Biomedical Application Example |
|-------|-------------------------------|
| **IQ** | Installing an MRI 1.5T scanner — verifying magnetic shielding, cryogen (helium) levels, RF shielding, structural floor load (≥ 5 tonnes/m²) |
| **OQ** | Stress-testing the MRI gradient coils at maximum slew rate (200 mT/m/ms) |
| **PQ** | Imaging 30 standardized phantoms (e.g., ACR MRI Accreditation Phantom) and measuring SNR uniformity |

**Other examples:** Sterilizer validation (IQ-OQ-PQ on autoclaves), infusion pump accuracy testing, defibrillator energy delivery verification, ventilator tidal volume accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Calculations

## 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Execution Procedure

Below is the **exhaustive procedural matrix** that an IQ/OQ/PQ engineer must follow. Every step is documented in the **Validation Master Plan (VMP)**.

### 3.1.1 IQ Execution — Full Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Raise a *Validation Protocol* document (IQ Protocol, Doc ID: IQ-VAL-001) and obtain approvals from QA Head, Engineering Head, and Regulatory Affairs.

**Step 2:** Verify the **purchase order (PO)** matches the *delivered equipment*:

$$
\text{Match}_{\text{criteria}} = \begin{cases} \text{PASS} & \text{if } \left( \text{Model}_{PO} = \text{Model}_{Delivered} \right) \land \left( \text{SN}_{PO} = \text{SN}_{Delivered} \right) \\ \text{FAIL} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3:** Verify **installation environment** parameters:

$$
T_{env} \in [T_{min}, T_{max}] = [18^{\circ}\text{C}, \, 25^{\circ}\text{C}]
$$

$$
RH_{env} \in [RH_{min}, RH_{max}] = [30\%, \, 65\%]
$$

**Step 4:** Verify **utilities**:

$$
V_{supply} = 230 \text{ V AC} \pm 10\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad 207 \text{ V} \leq V_{supply} \leq 253 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 5:** Document the **"as-built" schematic** and compare with the *as-designed* P&ID.

**Step 6:** Sign-off the **IQ Report**. *No OQ can begin without a signed IQ report.*

---

### 3.1.2 OQ Execution — Full Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Reference IQ Report and confirm all IQ deviations are closed.

**Step 2:** Define the **operating range** to be tested. For a sterilizer set at $T_{set} = 121^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$
T_{test} \in \{T_{LCL}, T_{set}, T_{UCL}\} = \{119^{\circ}\text{C}, \, 121^{\circ}\text{C}, \, 123^{\circ}\text{C}\}
$$

**Step 3:** For each test point, run **3 cycles** (triplicate) to check repeatability.

**Step 4:** Compute the **observed standard deviation** at each test point using:

$$
s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}
$$

**Step 5:** Verify the **deviation** from setpoint is within tolerance:

$$
\vert T_{observed} - T_{setpoint} \vert \leq \text{Tol}_{OQ} = \pm 1^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 6:** Trigger each **alarm condition** and verify safe shutdown response time.

**Step 7:** Sign-off the **OQ Report**.

---

### 3.1.3 PQ Execution — Full Step-by-Step (Including Cpk Derivation)

**Step 1:** Confirm OQ closure.

**Step 2:** Run **n = 30 consecutive production batches** using the actual product.

**Step 3:** Record the **Critical Quality Attribute (CQA)** for each batch. For example, the *tidal volume* of a ventilator set at $V_{T,set} = 500 \text{ mL}$.

**Step 4:** Calculate the sample mean:

$$
\bar{V}_{T} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} V_{T,i}
$$

Assume measured values: $\{ 498, 502, 499, 501, 503, 497, 500, 502, 499, 501,$ $503, 498, 500, 502, 499, 501, 500, 502, 498, 501,$ $499, 503, 500, 502, 501, 499, 500, 502, 501, 499 \}$ mL

$$
\sum V_{T,i} = 15000 \text{ mL}
$$

$$
\bar{V}_{T} = \dfrac{15000}{30} = 500.0 \text{ mL}
$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the sample standard deviation:

$$
\sum(V_{T,i} - \bar{V}_{T})^2 = 70 \text{ mL}^2
$$

$$
s = \sqrt{\dfrac{70}{29}} = \sqrt{2.4138} = 1.554 \text{ mL}
$$

**Step 6:** Compute **Cpk** with specification limits $USL = 520$ mL and $LSL = 480$ mL:

$$
C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3s} = \dfrac{520 - 500}{3 \times 1.554} = \dfrac{20}{4.662} = 4.29
$$

$$
C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3s} = \dfrac{500 - 480}{3 \times 1.554} = \dfrac{20}{4.662} = 4.29
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(4.29, \, 4.29) = 4.29
$$

**Step 7:** Since $C_{pk} = 4.29 \geq 1.33$, **the equipment PASSES PQ**. ✓

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Determining PQ Sample Size

**Problem:** A biomedical device manufacturer wants to estimate the mean accuracy of a glucometer within $E = \pm 2 \text{ mg/dL}$ at 95% confidence. Historical $\sigma = 5 \text{ mg/dL}$. How many samples are needed for PQ?

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters: $z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$ (for 95% confidence), $\sigma = 5 \text{ mg/dL}$, $E = 2 \text{ mg/dL}$.

**Step 2:** Apply the sample size formula:

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{1.96 \times 5}{2}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{9.8}{2}\right)^2 = (4.9)^2
$$

**Step 4:** Compute:

$$
n = 24.01
$$

**Step 5:** Round **up** (always round up for sample size):

$$
n = 25 \text{ samples}
$$

**Conclusion:** At least **25 glucometer readings** are required for a statistically valid PQ study.

---

### 3.3 Summary Matrix: IQ vs OQ vs PQ

| Parameter | IQ | OQ | PQ |
|-----------|----|----|----|
| **Question answered** | Is it installed correctly? | Does it work as intended? | Does it work in real conditions? |
| **Stage in lifecycle** | Pre-use | Pre-use | Pre-commercial / routine |
| **Material used** | No product | No product / simulated | Actual product |
| **Sample size** | 1 (single) | 3 (triplicate) | ≥ 30 (statistical) |
| **Statistical test** | None | Basic (range check) | Cpk, ANOVA, R&R |
| **Done by** | Engineering + Vendor | Engineering + QA | QA + Production |
| **Frequency** | Once (per install) | Once + after major repair | Once + revalidation every 1–2 years |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — IQ/OQ/PQ Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Equipment Arrives] --> B[User Requirement Specification URS]
    B --> C[Functional and Design Specification FS DS]
    C --> D[Risk Assessment FMEA]
    D --> E[Validation Master Plan VMP Drafted]
    E --> F[IQ Protocol Approved]
    F --> G[Equipment Physical Installation]
    G --> H{IQ Checks Pass?}
    H -- No --> I[Document Deviation and Reinstall]
    I --> G
    H -- Yes --> J[Sign Off IQ Report]
    J --> K[OQ Protocol Approved]
    K --> L[Operational Challenge Tests LCL Setpoint UCL]
    L --> M{OQ Checks Pass?}
    M -- No --> N[Calibrate or Repair]
    N --> L
    M -- Yes --> O[Sign Off OQ Report]
    O --> P[PQ Protocol Approved]
    P --> Q[Run 30 Production Batches with Actual Product]
    Q --> R[Calculate Cpk and Process Capability]
    R --> S{Cpk greater than 1.33?}
    S -- No --> T[Process Improvement CAPA]
    T --> Q
    S -- Yes --> U[Sign Off PQ Report]
    U --> V[Release Equipment for Routine Production]
    V --> W[Revalidation Every 1 to 2 Years]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Documentation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Level1[Level 1 Strategic]
        A1[Validation Master Plan VMP]
    end
    subgraph Level2[Level 2 Protocols]
        B1[IQ Protocol]
        B2[OQ Protocol]
        B3[PQ Protocol]
    end
    subgraph Level3[Level 3 Test Scripts]
        C1[IQ Test Scripts]
        C2[OQ Test Scripts]
        C3[PQ Test Scripts]
    end
    subgraph Level4[Level 4 Reports]
        D1[IQ Report]
        D2[OQ Report]
        D3[PQ Report]
    end
    subgraph Level5[Level 5 Release]
        E1[Certificate of Release for Use]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Inter-Departmental Coordination

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant QA as Quality Assurance
    participant EN as Engineering
    participant RA as Regulatory Affairs
    participant PD as Production
    participant VN as Vendor
    QA->>EN: Draft VMP
    EN->>RA: Review VMP
    RA-->>QA: Approve VMP
    QA->>EN: Issue IQ Protocol
    EN->>VN: Schedule Installation
    VN->>EN: Install Equipment
    EN->>QA: IQ Test Results
    QA->>RA: IQ Sign-off
    QA->>EN: Issue OQ Protocol
    EN->>EN: Execute OQ Tests
    EN->>QA: OQ Test Results
    QA->>PD: Hand over for PQ
    PD->>PD: Run Production Batches
    PD->>QA: PQ Data
    QA->>QA: Calculate Cpk
    QA->>RA: Final PQ Sign-off
    RA->>PD: Release Equipment
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Equipment Lifecycle Status

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Received
    Received --> UnderIQ: Installation Begins
    UnderIQ --> IQFailed: Deviations Found
    IQFailed --> UnderIQ: Corrective Action
    UnderIQ --> IQPassed: All Checks Pass
    IQPassed --> UnderOQ: OQ Protocol Issued
    UnderOQ --> OQFailed: Out of Tolerance
    OQFailed --> UnderOQ: Calibration
    UnderOQ --> OQPassed: All Tests Pass
    OQPassed --> UnderPQ: PQ Protocol Issued
    UnderPQ --> PQFailed: Cpk Less Than 1.33
    PQFailed --> UnderPQ: CAPA Process
    UnderPQ --> PQPassed: Cpk Greater or Equal 1.33
    PQPassed --> Released: Certificate Issued
    Released --> Revalidation: 1 to 2 Years Elapsed
    Revalidation --> UnderPQ: Re-qualification
    Released --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Installation Qualification (IQ). List any four key elements verified during IQ.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Installation Qualification (IQ) is the documented verification that the equipment is installed correctly, in the correct environment, and according to the manufacturer's pre-approved design specifications.

**Four Key Elements (2 Marks — ½ Mark each):**
1. Verification of equipment identification (serial number, model, firmware).
2. Verification of installation environment (temperature, humidity, cleanliness).
3. Verification of utility connections (power, water, gas, vacuum).
4. Verification of documentation (manuals, calibration certificates, P&IDs).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Operational Qualification (OQ) and Performance Qualification (PQ) in 3 points.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark per point):**

| Parameter | OQ | PQ |
|-----------|----|----|
| **Material used** | Uses no product or simulated material | Uses actual product or process load |
| **Sample size** | Typically triplicate (3 runs) per test point | Statistically significant (≥ 30 runs) |
| **Statistical rigor** | Range and tolerance check only | Cpk, ANOVA, R&R analysis required |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED — Answer ANY ONE of the following]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the complete IQ/OQ/PQ qualification process for a **sterilizing autoclave** used in a biomedical device manufacturing facility. Discuss the regulatory basis and key acceptance criteria for each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A syringe pump is qualified during PQ. The set flow rate is 50 mL/h with specification limits of $\pm 5\%$. Thirty consecutive measurements yield:

- Mean flow rate: $\bar{x} = 50.4 \text{ mL/h}$
- Standard deviation: $s = 0.85 \text{ mL/h}$

**Calculate the Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) and determine whether the pump passes PQ.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Regulatory Basis (1 Mark):**
- FDA 21 CFR Part 820.70 & 820.75
- ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.5.6
- GAMP 5 Guidelines

**IQ Stage (2 Marks):**
- Verify autoclave model (e.g., Getinge GSS67H) and serial number match purchase order.
- Verify installation in cleanroom environment: $T = 20\text{–}25^{\circ}\text{C}$, $RH < 65\%$.
- Verify utility connections: 3-phase 415V supply, deionized water inlet, steam supply at 3.5 bar.
- Verify presence of calibration certificates for pressure and temperature sensors.

**OQ Stage (2 Marks):**
- Perform empty chamber heat distribution studies at $121^{\circ}\text{C}$ for 15 min (triplicate).
- Verify temperature uniformity $\pm 1^{\circ}\text{C}$ across all chamber probes (typically 12 thermocouples).
- Test Bowie-Dick air removal test — must show uniform color change.
- Challenge alarms: door-open interlock, over-pressure (>2.5 bar cutoff).

**PQ Stage (2 Marks):**
- Run three consecutive production loads with actual wrapped surgical instruments.
- Place biological indicators (*Geobacillus stearothermophilus* spores) in the most challenging load location.
- Confirm **log reduction ≥ 6** (SAL of $10^{-6}$).
- Document PQ data and obtain QA sign-off.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Identify specification limits.

$$
USL = 50 \times 1.05 = 52.5 \text{ mL/h}
$$

$$
LSL = 50 \times 0.95 = 47.5 \text{ mL/h}
$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute upper $C_{pk}$.

$$
C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3s} = \dfrac{52.5 - 50.4}{3 \times 0.85} = \dfrac{2.1}{2.55} = 0.824
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Compute lower $C_{pk}$.

$$
C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3s} = \dfrac{50.4 - 47.5}{3 \times 0.85} = \dfrac{2.9}{2.55} = 1.137
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Determine the controlling $C_{pk}$.

$$
C_{pk} = \min(0.824, \, 1.137) = 0.824
$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Compare with acceptance criterion.

$$
C_{pk} = 0.824 < 1.33 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{PQ FAILS}
$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Conclusion.
The syringe pump **fails PQ** because the process is **not centered** (mean is shifted upward at 50.4 mL/h). The manufacturer must perform a CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action) — typically recalibrate the pump and shift the setpoint slightly down.

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Recommendation.

The mean should be re-centered closer to 50.0 mL/h. Ideally:

$$
\bar{x}_{target} = \dfrac{USL + LSL}{2} = 50.0 \text{ mL/h}
$$

After re-centering and reducing $s$ to 0.6, $C_{pk}$ would become 1.39, satisfying the criterion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **complete IQ/OQ/PQ documentation hierarchy** from the Validation Master Plan (VMP) to the Certificate of Release. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A biomedical temperature-controlled incubator is qualified during OQ. The setpoint is $37^{\circ}\text{C}$ with tolerance $\pm 0.5^{\circ}\text{C}$. Twelve measurements are taken across the chamber at the setpoint:

$$
\{36.8, 36.9, 37.0, 37.1, 37.2, 36.9, 37.0, 37.1, 36.8, 37.0, 37.1, 37.2\} \text{ °C}
$$

**Calculate mean, standard deviation, and determine whether the incubator passes OQ.** **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Documentation Hierarchy Flow (5 Marks):**

| Document Level | Document Name | Purpose |
|----------------|---------------|---------|
| Level 1 | **Validation Master Plan (VMP)** | Overall validation strategy, scope, responsibilities |
| Level 2 | **IQ / OQ / PQ Protocols** | Test plans and acceptance criteria |
| Level 3 | **Test Scripts / Data Forms** | Step-by-step execution sheets |
| Level 4 | **IQ / OQ / PQ Reports** | Results, deviations, conclusions |
| Level 5 | **Certificate of Release** | Final authorization for production use |

**Approval Chain (2 Marks):**
- Author: Engineer (Validation / Manufacturing).
- Reviewer: QA Manager.
- Approver: Head of Quality / Plant Head.
- The Certificate of Release is **only** issued after all three reports are signed-off.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Calculate mean.

$$
\bar{x} = \dfrac{36.8+36.9+37.0+37.1+37.2+36.9+37.0+37.1+36.8+37.0+37.1+37.2}{12}
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \dfrac{443.1}{12} = 36.925^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Calculate deviations and squared deviations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_i - \bar{x}) &= \{-0.125, -0.025, 0.075, 0.175, 0.275, -0.025, 0.075, 0.175, -0.125, 0.075, 0.175, 0.275\} \\
(x_i - \bar{x})^2 &= \{0.0156, 0.0006, 0.0056, 0.0306, 0.0756, 0.0006, 0.0056, 0.0306, 0.0156, 0.0056, 0.0306, 0.0756\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Sum of squared deviations:

$$
\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 0.2922
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Standard deviation:

$$
s = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.2922}{11}} = \sqrt{0.02656} = 0.163^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Determine if mean is within tolerance.

$$
\vert \bar{x} - T_{set} \vert = \vert 36.925 - 37.0 \vert = 0.075^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

$$
0.075 \leq 0.5 \quad \text{(tolerance satisfied)}
$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Determine if the **range** is within tolerance.

$$
\text{Range} = x_{max} - x_{min} = 37.2 - 36.8 = 0.4^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

Since the entire range $0.4^{\circ}\text{C} \leq 0.5^{\circ}\text{C}$ tolerance, the incubator **passes OQ**. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Common Mistake #1 — Mixing Up Stages:** Students often confuse IQ with OQ. *Remember:* IQ verifies **installation**; OQ verifies **operation**; PQ verifies **performance with product**. (Deduct 2 marks)
>
> 2. **Common Mistake #2 — Wrong Cpk Formula:** Many students write $C_{pk} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ which is actually $C_{p}$ (assumes centered process). Use $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ for uncentered processes. (Deduct 1–2 marks)
>
> 3. **Common Mistake #3 — Forgetting Regulatory References:** Always cite the standard (ISO 13485, FDA 21 CFR, GAMP 5). Examiners award **0.5–1 extra mark** for citing regulations.
>
> 4. **Common Mistake #4 — Using n instead of n−1 in denominator:** For sample standard deviation, use $n-1$ (Bessel's correction). Using $n$ is a frequent error. (Deduct 1 mark)
>
> 5. **Common Mistake #5 — Not Rounding Up Sample Size:** When computing $n$ for PQ, always **round up** to the next integer — never down.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **IQ** answers: *"Is the equipment installed correctly?"* — **Hardware & Documentation check.**
- ✅ **OQ** answers: *"Does the equipment operate as intended?"* — **Functional & Challenge testing.**
- ✅ **PQ** answers: *"Does it consistently work in real production?"* — **Statistical proof with actual product.**
- ✅ IQ → OQ → PQ is a **sequential, non-skippable** order — each stage signs off before the next begins.
- ✅ **Regulatory backbone:** FDA 21 CFR Part 820, ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.5.6, GAMP 5.
- ✅ **Cpk ≥ 1.33** is the gold-standard PQ acceptance criterion for biomedical devices.
- ✅ **Sample size for PQ** typically **n ≥ 30**; computed via $n = (z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma / E)^2$.
- ✅ **Use $n-1$** in sample standard deviation (Bessel's correction).
- ✅ **Revalidation** must occur every **1–2 years** or after any major change.
- ✅ **Document Hierarchy:** VMP → Protocol → Test Scripts → Report → Certificate of Release.
- ✅ **Common pitfall:** Confusing $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$ — the latter accounts for **process centering**.
- ✅ **Document, document, document:** "If it wasn't documented, it wasn't done." — Core FDA mantra.
- ✅ **GAMP 5 software categories:** Category 1 (Infrastructure SW), 3 (Non-configured), 4 (Configured), 5 (Custom).
- ✅ **Risk-based approach:** Per **ICH Q9**, IQ/OQ/PQ depth depends on device risk classification (Class I, II, III).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Transfer Process to Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Transfer Process to Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Transfer** is the formal, documented process by which a medical device design is translated from the research and development (R&D) / engineering phase into production-scale manufacturing, ensuring that the **Device Master Record (DMR)** and the **Design History File (DHF)** are correctly handed off so that the device can be reproducibly manufactured to meet the original design intent, user needs, and regulatory requirements (FDA 21 CFR 820.30(h) and ISO 13485:2016, Clause 7.5.8).

In KTU 2024 parlance, this is the engineering bridge between **Module 2 (Design Verification & Validation)** and **Module 4 (Regulatory Submissions & Post-Market)**. It is *not* simply "making the device" — it is a controlled, traceable, and validated transition.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Recipe Handover"**
> Imagine a master chef (the design engineer) who perfects a signature dish in the test kitchen. They have the exact recipe, plating instructions, oven temperature, ingredient suppliers, and presentation standards. Now, the restaurant chain wants 500 branches worldwide to serve this dish **identically** to the original. Design transfer is the process of writing the **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)**, training the cooks, validating the ovens, locking down suppliers, and ensuring that Branch #487 in Tokyo serves the *same* dish as the master kitchen in Paris. If the recipe is the design, design transfer is the **industrialization** of the recipe.
> 
> In a biomedical context, if the design is a **pacemaker**, design transfer ensures that the *10,000th unit* produced in contract manufacturing performs identically to the *5th prototype* built in R&D.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Formal Meaning | Biomedical Example |
|------|----------------|-------------------|
| **DHF (Design History File)** | Complete documented evidence of the design development process | All sketches, FMEA, test reports for a syringe |
| **DMR (Device Master Record)** | Reference document containing all information needed to produce the device | Bill of materials, drawings, work instructions |
| **DFM** | **D**esign for **M**anufacturing — designing parts to be easy to produce | Choosing injection-moldable plastics over machined titanium |
| **DFA** | **D**esign for **A**ssembly — minimizing the number of parts and assembly steps | Reducing a 30-part housing to 8 snap-fit parts |
| **IQ / OQ / PQ** | **I**nstallation, **O**perational, **P**erformance **Q**ualification | Validating a cleanroom, autoclave, and sterile barrier |
| **Process Validation** | Documented evidence that a process consistently produces a result meeting specifications | Verifying a welding process on catheter tips |
| **Cpk** | Process Capability Index — measures how well a process meets specifications | Ensuring catheter diameter stays within ±0.05 mm |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard manufacturing metrics used in design transfer:
> - **Process Capability (Cpk) target**: ≥ **1.33** for medical devices (often ≥ 1.67 for critical dimensions)
> - **Design Transfer Review success rate**: **100%** of design outputs must be transferred (no missing items)
> - **Pilot batch size (typical)**: **30–100 units** for verification of manufacturing consistency
> - **FDA Design Control reference**: **21 CFR 820.30** (subclause h governs transfer)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Design Transfer

Design transfer is governed by **five interlocking pillars** under ISO 13485:2016 and FDA Quality System Regulation (QSR):

### Pillar 1: Design for Manufacturing & Assembly (DFM/DFA)
- **Why?** Many excellent designs fail in manufacturing because they are too complex, too expensive, or too fragile to be produced at scale.
- **How?** The design team and manufacturing team collaborate *during* (not after) the design phase to:
  1. Reduce the **part count** (Boothroyd DFA method: minimize assembly time)
  2. Standardize components (use off-the-shelf sensors, connectors)
  3. Select **biocompatible, sterilizable** materials (e.g., medical-grade 316L stainless steel, USP Class VI polymers)
  4. Specify tolerances that are *as loose as functionally acceptable*

### Pillar 2: Device Master Record (DMR) Creation
- **Why?** The DMR is the "manufacturing bible" — without it, production cannot be reproduced.
- **How?** The DMR contains:
  - Device specifications
  - Production process specifications (molding temperatures, weld parameters)
  - Quality assurance procedures and acceptance criteria
  - Packaging and labeling specifications

### Pillar 3: Process Validation (IQ → OQ → PQ)
- **Why?** You cannot release a medical device from an *unvalidated* process.
- **How?**
  - **IQ (Installation Qualification)**: Is the equipment installed correctly per manufacturer specs?
  - **OQ (Operational Qualification)**: Does the equipment operate as intended across its specified ranges?
  - **PQ (Performance Qualification)**: Does the process consistently produce acceptable product under real conditions?

### Pillar 4: Tooling, Equipment, and Supplier Qualification
- **Why?** Custom tooling (injection molds, die sets) and supplier materials must be qualified before production.
- **How?**
  - First Article Inspection (FAI)
  - Supplier audits (per ISO 13485 Clause 7.4)
  - Calibration certificates for all measurement equipment

### Pillar 5: Quality System Integration
- **Why?** Design transfer outputs must be absorbed into the **Quality Management System (QMS)** for routine production.
- **How?**
  - Document control handover
  - CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action) system readiness
  - Training records for production operators

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **critical for numerical problems** in KTU ESE for this module.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application in Biomedical Design Transfer |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------------------------------------|
| 1 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | USL: Upper Spec Limit, LSL: Lower Spec Limit, $\sigma$: Std. Dev. | Measures process *spread* relative to tolerance |
| 2 | $C_{pk} = \min\left[\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$ | $\mu$: process mean | Measures process *centering* — **Cpk ≥ 1.33 is KTU benchmark** |
| 3 | $\text{DFA Index} = \dfrac{N_m}{N_a}$ | $N_m$: min. parts, $N_a$: actual parts | Goal: **DFA Index → 1.0** (perfect design) |
| 4 | $\text{Yield} = 1 - e^{-D_o \cdot t}$ | $D_o$: defect density, $t$: time | Used in semiconductor/IC biomedical chips |
| 5 | $\sigma_{stack} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \dots + \sigma_n^2}$ | RSS of tolerances | Tolerance stack-up for assembled devices |
| 6 | $Z = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Standard normal variate | Used in Six Sigma DMAIC for transfer validation |
| 7 | $C_{pk,\text{required}} = Z_{\text{table}} / 3$ | $Z$: defect rate target | If defect rate ≤ 3.4 ppm, Cpk = **1.5** (Six Sigma) |
| 8 | $\text{Cost}_\text{DFM} = C_m + C_a + C_l$ | Material, Assembly, Logistics | Total landed cost after DFM optimization |

> **Units Note**: All capability indices are **dimensionless**; $\sigma$ is in the same unit as the specification (e.g., mm for catheter diameter).

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Engineering Utility

- **Why DFM/DFA first?** Because *retrofitting* a design for manufacturing is 10×–100× more expensive than designing it correctly the first time. In a Class III implant (e.g., heart valve), a single design iteration cycle can cost **$500,000+** and 6+ months.
- **Why IQ/OQ/PQ?** Regulatory bodies (FDA, Notified Bodies in EU MDR) will **not approve** a device manufactured from an unvalidated process. PQ is the most critical — it represents real-world production.
- **Why Cpk ≥ 1.33?** Empirical standard: a Cpk of 1.33 means the process spread fits within ±4σ of the specification, yielding approximately **63 ppm defects** — acceptable for most non-implant devices. For **implantable** devices, Cpk ≥ 1.67 is industry norm.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Process Capability Index (Cpk) — KTU Board Favorite

The Cpk is the most important statistical metric used during design transfer. Let's derive it from first principles.

### Step 1: Define the Process Distribution
Assume a manufacturing process (e.g., injection molding of a syringe barrel) produces a critical dimension (e.g., inner diameter) that follows a **normal (Gaussian) distribution**:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$$

where $\mu$ is the process mean and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation.

**Reasoning**: The Central Limit Theorem states that most repeatable manufacturing processes converge to a normal distribution when many small, independent sources of variation are summed.

### Step 2: Define the Specification Limits
The customer (design) requires the dimension to fall between $LSL$ and $USL$. For our syringe example: $LSL = 4.95$ mm, $USL = 5.05$ mm, target = **5.00 mm**.

### Step 3: Calculate the Process Capability (Cp)
Cp measures the *potential* capability (assumes the process is perfectly centered):

$$\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{\text{Tolerance Width}}{\text{Process Spread}} \\
    &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\
    &= \frac{5.05 - 4.95}{6 \times 0.012} \\
    &= \frac{0.10}{0.072} \\
    &\approx 1.389
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic**: The factor of $6\sigma$ (not $\sigma$) is used because a normal distribution covers **±3σ** on each side of the mean, totaling **6σ** of total spread (covers 99.73% of parts).

### Step 4: Account for Off-Center Process (Cpk)
Cp alone is misleading. A process can have a high Cp but be shifted off-center. We define:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} \\
C_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \\
C_{pk} &= \min\left[C_{pk,\text{upper}},\ C_{pk,\text{lower}}\right]
\end{aligned}$$

For our syringe, if $\mu = 5.012$ mm and $\sigma = 0.012$ mm:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \frac{5.05 - 5.012}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.038}{0.036} \approx 1.056 \\
C_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \frac{5.012 - 4.95}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.062}{0.036} \approx 1.722 \\
C_{pk} &= \min(1.056,\ 1.722) = 1.056
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation**: Even though Cp = 1.389 looks good, Cpk = 1.056 is **borderline** (below 1.33). The process is shifted **upward** toward the USL, and the production engineer must re-center the process before transferring to manufacturing.

## 3.2 Derivation: Design for Assembly (DFA) Index

The DFA index quantifies assembly efficiency:

$$\text{DFA Index} = \frac{N_{m,\text{min}}}{N_{a,\text{actual}}}$$

For a pulse oximeter housing:
- Current design: 14 parts, 28 assembly steps
- Theoretical minimum: 6 parts (1 PCB, 1 housing, 1 lens, 1 battery, 1 strap, 1 sensor cable)
- $N_m = 6$, $N_a = 14$

$$\text{DFA Index} = \frac{6}{14} = 0.43 \quad (\approx 43\%\ \text{efficient})$$

**Goal**: Push the index toward **1.0**. A re-design eliminating 5 unnecessary fasteners and merging the lens with the housing gives $N_a = 8$, DFA Index = 0.75.

## 3.3 Derivation: Tolerance Stack-Up (Root Sum of Squares)

When multiple dimensions add up in an assembly, total variation is **not** additive — it follows the **RSS method**:

$$\sigma_{\text{stack}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2}$$

For a 3-component catheter assembly with $\sigma_1 = 0.01$ mm, $\sigma_2 = 0.015$ mm, $\sigma_3 = 0.008$ mm:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{stack}} &= \sqrt{(0.01)^2 + (0.015)^2 + (0.008)^2} \\
                     &= \sqrt{0.0001 + 0.000225 + 0.000064} \\
                     &= \sqrt{0.000389} \\
                     &\approx 0.0197\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the standard deviation of the **total assembled length**, used to verify that the final assembly meets design tolerance.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Design Transfer Checklist (KTU Exam Table)

| Stage | Input Document | Output Document | Responsible Party | KTU CO Mapping |
|-------|----------------|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1. Transfer Planning | Approved Design Output | **Design Transfer Plan (DTP)** | Design + Mfg. Leads | CO3 |
| 2. DFM/DFA Review | CAD Files, Drawings | DFM/DFA Report | Industrial Eng. | CO3 |
| 3. DMR Finalization | Drawings, BOM, Specs | **Device Master Record** | Document Control | CO3 |
| 4. Tooling Fab. | Approved Drawings | First Article Samples | Tooling Vendor | CO4 |
| 5. IQ/OQ/PQ | Protocol Documents | Validation Reports | Quality Eng. | CO4 |
| 6. Pilot Run | DMR + Validated Process | Pilot Batch + Test Data | Production Lead | CO4 |
| 7. Design Transfer Review | All Above | **Transfer Approval Record** | Cross-functional Team | CO5 |
| 8. Routine Mfg. | Approved DMR | Production Devices | Production Operators | CO5 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Complete Design Transfer Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Design Outputs Approved] --> A2[Form Design Transfer Team]
    A2 --> A3[Develop Design Transfer Plan DTP]
    A3 --> A4[Conduct DFM and DFA Review]
    A4 --> A5[Identify Critical to Quality CTQ Parameters]
    A5 --> A6[Select Manufacturing Processes]
    A6 --> A7[Develop Process Flow Diagrams]
    A7 --> A8[Create or Update Device Master Record DMR]
    A8 --> A9[Fabricate or Procure Tooling]
    A9 --> A10[Perform Installation Qualification IQ]
    A10 --> A11[Perform Operational Qualification OQ]
    A11 --> A12[Perform Performance Qualification PQ]
    A12 --> A13[Calculate Cpk Values]
    A13 --> A14{Cpk greater than 1.33?}
    A14 -->|No| A15[Process Re centering Required]
    A15 --> A6
    A14 -->|Yes| A16[Conduct Pilot Production Run]
    A16 --> A17[First Article Inspection FAI]
    A17 --> A18[Design Transfer Review Meeting]
    A18 --> A19{All Outputs Transferred?}
    A19 -->|No| A20[Open CAPA and Return to Source]
    A20 --> A4
    A19 -->|Yes| A21[Release to Routine Manufacturing]
    A21 --> A22[Update Quality Management System]
    A22 --> A23[Train Production Operators]
    A23 --> A24[Regulatory Submission Support]
    A24 --> A25[Post Market Surveillance Setup]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: DHF vs DMR Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DHF[Design History File DHF]
        D1[Design Inputs]
        D2[Design Outputs]
        D3[Design Reviews]
        D4[Design Verification]
        D5[Design Validation]
        D6[Design Transfer Records]
    end
    subgraph DMR[Device Master Record DMR]
        M1[Device Specifications]
        M2[Production Process Specs]
        M3[Quality Assurance Procedures]
        M4[Packaging and Labeling]
        M5[Installation and Servicing]
    end
    subgraph QMS[Quality Management System QMS]
        Q1[Document Control]
        Q2[CAPA System]
        Q3[Training Records]
        Q4[Calibration Records]
    end
    DHF -->|Feeds| DMR
    DMR -->|Feeds| QMS
    QMS -->|Supports| DHF
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Process Validation Sequence (IQ/OQ/PQ)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V1[User Requirements Specification] --> V2[Design of Process]
    V2 --> V3[IQ Protocol Document]
    V3 --> V4[Install Equipment]
    V4 --> V5[Document IQ Results]
    V5 --> V6[OQ Protocol Document]
    V6 --> V7[Challenge Process at Edge Cases]
    V7 --> V8[Document OQ Results]
    V8 --> V9[PQ Protocol Document]
    V9 --> V10[Run Process with Real Materials]
    V10 --> V11[Document PQ Results]
    V11 --> V12{All Acceptance Criteria Met?}
    V12 -->|Yes| V13[Release Process for Production]
    V12 -->|No| V14[Redesign and Restart from V1]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Set, Module 3]**

### Q1. Define Design Transfer. List any four documents that must be included in the Device Master Record (DMR). `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Design Transfer is the stage in the design control process where the device design is translated from the R&D phase into production manufacturing, ensuring the device can be consistently produced to meet design specifications. *[1 mark]*

Four DMR documents: *[2 marks, 0.5 each]*
1. Device specifications and drawings
2. Bill of materials (BOM)
3. Production process specifications (e.g., molding parameters)
4. Quality assurance procedures and acceptance criteria

*(Acceptable: packaging/labeling specs, installation/servicing procedures, sterilization specs)*

---

### Q2. What is the difference between IQ, OQ, and PQ in process validation? `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
- **IQ (Installation Qualification):** Verifies that the equipment is installed correctly and matches the design specifications and manufacturer recommendations. *[1 mark]*
- **OQ (Operational Qualification):** Verifies that the equipment operates as intended across its specified operating ranges, including worst-case conditions. *[1 mark]*
- **PQ (Performance Qualification):** Verifies that the process consistently produces acceptable product under real, routine production conditions using actual materials. *[1 mark]*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Adapted, Module 3]**

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) — Design for Manufacturing Focus

**(a)** Explain the Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFM/DFA) methodology. Discuss its importance in the design transfer of a Class III implantable device with suitable examples. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** A catheter manufacturer requires the inner diameter of a guide catheter to be $2.00 \pm 0.05$ mm. A pilot run of 25 samples yields a mean $\mu = 2.012$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.018$ mm. Calculate Cp and Cpk. Is the process acceptable for medical device transfer? Justify with reference to industry standards. `[7 Marks]`

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Definition and Methodology** *[3 marks]*:
DFM (Design for Manufacturing) and DFA (Design for Assembly) are concurrent engineering methodologies that optimize the product design for ease of production, assembly, and cost-effectiveness. **DFM** focuses on individual part manufacturability (material selection, tolerances, process choice), while **DFA** focuses on reducing the number of parts and simplifying assembly steps (Boothroyd-Dewhurst method).

**Importance for Class III Implantable Device** *[2 marks]*:
- **Patient safety**: Defects in implants (e.g., pacemaker lead, hip implant) cannot be easily corrected post-market.
- **Regulatory compliance**: FDA and EU MDR demand validated, robust manufacturing.
- **Cost of recalls**: A Class III recall can cost **$50M–$500M** and damage the company's reputation.
- **Biocompatibility preservation**: DFM ensures sterilization (e.g., autoclaving) does not degrade materials.

**Examples** *[2 marks]*:
- *DFM Example*: Choosing titanium alloy (Ti-6Al-4V ELI) for a spinal screw over 316L stainless steel for better osseointegration and machinability.
- *DFA Example*: Designing an insulin pump housing as a single snap-fit assembly (3 parts) instead of a 12-part screwed assembly, reducing assembly time by 70% and contamination risk.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given Data**:
- Specification: $2.00 \pm 0.05$ mm → $USL = 2.05$ mm, $LSL = 1.95$ mm
- $\mu = 2.012$ mm, $\sigma = 0.018$ mm

**Step 1: Calculate Cp** *[2 marks]*
$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{2.05 - 1.95}{6 \times 0.018} = \frac{0.10}{0.108} = 0.926$$

**Step 2: Calculate Cpk (Upper)** *[1.5 marks]*
$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{2.05 - 2.012}{3 \times 0.018} = \frac{0.038}{0.054} = 0.704$$

**Step 3: Calculate Cpk (Lower)** *[1.5 marks]*
$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{2.012 - 1.95}{3 \times 0.018} = \frac{0.062}{0.054} = 1.148$$

**Step 4: Determine Cpk** *[1 mark]*
$$C_{pk} = \min(0.704,\ 1.148) = 0.704$$

**Step 5: Conclusion** *[1 mark]*
- **Cp < 1** ⇒ The process is **incapable** (spread is too wide for the tolerance).
- **Cpk = 0.704 < 1.33** ⇒ **NOT acceptable** for medical device design transfer.
- The process is also **off-center upward** (mean is closer to USL than to LSL).
- **Recommendation**: The process must be improved — re-center the mean toward 2.00 mm, reduce $\sigma$ through better tooling/maintenance, or relax the specification to ±0.08 mm (with design approval).

---

> **Question A (Alternative Choice) — Regulatory & DHF/DMR Focus** is provided in 5.3 below as Question B.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — DHF/DMR & Regulatory Focus

**(a)** Distinguish between the Design History File (DHF) and the Device Master Record (DMR). Why are both essential for FDA 21 CFR 820 compliance during design transfer? `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** Explain the IQ → OQ → PQ sequence for validating an injection molding process used to manufacture a syringe barrel. State at least three critical process parameters and two in-process quality checks for each stage. `[7 Marks]`

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Definitions** *[2 marks]*:
- **DHF (Design History File)**: A compilation of all records that describe the design history of a finished device — it provides the *evidence* that the design was developed per design controls (21 CFR 820.30).
- **DMR (Device Master Record)**: A compilation of all records that contain the *procedures and specifications* for producing the finished device (21 CFR 820.181).

**Key Differences** *[3 marks]*:

| Aspect | DHF | DMR |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Purpose | Proves the design was developed correctly | Provides instructions to manufacture the device |
| Contents | Design inputs, outputs, reviews, V&V, transfer records | Device specs, process specs, QA procedures, packaging/labeling |
| Lifecycle | Frozen after product discontinuation | Continuously updated with engineering changes |
| Audience | Auditors, regulators | Production operators, suppliers |

**Why both are essential for design transfer** *[2 marks]*:
- The **DHF** demonstrates *why* the design choices were made and *proves* the device is safe/effective (Verification & Validation).
- The **DMR** is the *operational output* of design transfer — it is what the manufacturer uses *daily* in production.
- FDA 21 CFR 820.30(h) explicitly requires that design transfer ensures the *DMR* is correct, and 820.30(j) requires the *DHF* to be maintained.
- Without the DHF, you cannot justify the design; without the DMR, you cannot reproduce the device — **both are legally required** for FDA and EU MDR submissions.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**IQ (Installation Qualification)** *[2 marks]*:
Verifies that the injection molding machine and ancillary equipment are installed correctly.
- *Critical Parameters*: Machine model/serial number matches spec; utilities (power, compressed air, chilled water) are within spec; safety interlocks functional; mold installation torques verified; thermocouples calibrated.
- *In-process Checks*: Visual inspection of machine, electrical continuity test, calibration certificate verification.

**OQ (Operational Qualification)** *[2.5 marks]*:
Verifies that the machine operates as intended at the edges of its specified ranges.
- *Critical Parameters*: Barrel temperature zones (e.g., Zone 1: 200°C, Zone 2: 210°C, Zone 3: 220°C), injection pressure (800–1200 bar), injection speed, mold cooling time, cycle time.
- *In-process Checks*: Temperature uniformity survey across platen, pressure profile recording, shot weight repeatability (3σ ≤ 0.5% of nominal), short-shot/leak test on molded parts.

**PQ (Performance Qualification)** *[2.5 marks]*:
Verifies that the process, run under *routine production conditions* with *actual production mold and material* (medical-grade polypropylene), consistently produces acceptable syringe barrels.
- *Critical Parameters*: Hold the IQ/OQ-confirmed parameters constant; run 3 consecutive batches of 100+ units each.
- *In-process Checks*: 
  1. Dimensional inspection (inner/outer diameter, wall thickness) — Cpk ≥ 1.33 required.
  2. Visual inspection (no flash, sink marks, weld lines).
  3. Functional test (Luer slip fit per ISO 80369).
  4. Biocompatibility sample retention per ISO 10993.
  5. Sterilization compatibility sample (EtO or gamma).

**Conclusion**: The IQ → OQ → PQ sequence ensures that the manufacturing process is *qualified* (not just the equipment), and the design can be successfully *transferred* to routine production.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Design Transfer Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing DHF with DMR** — Students frequently treat these as synonyms. The DHF is *evidence of design development*; the DMR is *instructions for production*. Examiners specifically test this distinction. (Penalty: up to 2 marks)
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Cpk Calculations** — A common error is omitting mm in the final answer or forgetting that $\sigma$ must be in the same unit as USL/LSL. Always show unit cancellation. (Penalty: 0.5–1 mark)
> 
> 3. **Confusing Cp and Cpk** — Cp ignores centering; Cpk does not. Many students write $Cpk = (USL-LSL)/(6\sigma)$, which is *wrong* — that formula is for **Cp**. The Cpk formula **must** include $\mu$.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the "Off-Center" Insight** — When calculating Cpk, students often calculate only one side. Always compute *both* upper and lower and take the **minimum**.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring Regulatory References** — KTU expects explicit mention of **FDA 21 CFR 820.30(h)** and **ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.5.8** for full marks on regulatory questions.
> 
> 6. **Vague DFM/DFA Examples** — Saying "DFM reduces cost" is not enough. Cite a *specific* biomedical example (e.g., merging two molded parts into one, replacing metal with polymer for disposability).
> 
> 7. **Missing the "Transfer Review Meeting"** — Many answers omit that the **design transfer review meeting** is the formal *gate* where all five pillars are verified and the transfer is signed off. This is a board-favorite point.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Design Transfer Process to Manufacturing**

- ✅ **Definition**: Design transfer = translating R&D design to production via DMR, validated processes, and qualified tooling.
- ✅ **Legal basis**: **FDA 21 CFR 820.30(h)** and **ISO 13485:2016 Clause 7.5.8**.
- ✅ **Two key documents**:
  - **DHF** = *what was designed and why* (evidence)
  - **DMR** = *how to build it* (instructions)
- ✅ **Five Pillars**: DFM/DFA, DMR creation, Process Validation (IQ/OQ/PQ), Tooling/Supplier Qualification, QMS Integration.
- ✅ **IQ/OQ/PQ sequence**:
  - IQ: Is the equipment installed right?
  - OQ: Does it work across its range?
  - PQ: Does the *process* consistently produce acceptable product?
- ✅ **Cpk benchmarks**:
  - Cpk ≥ **1.33** → General medical device (≈ 63 ppm defects)
  - Cpk ≥ **1.67** → Implantable / Class III device (≈ 0.6 ppm defects)
- ✅ **Cp formula**: $C_p = (USL - LSL) / 6\sigma$ (ignores centering)
- ✅ **Cpk formula**: $C_{pk} = \min[(USL-\mu)/3\sigma,\ (\mu-LSL)/3\sigma]$
- ✅ **DFA Index**: $N_{m,\min} / N_{a,\text{actual}}$ → target = **1.0**
- ✅ **Tolerance stack-up**: $\sigma_{\text{stack}} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$ (RSS method, *not* simple addition).
- ✅ **Design Transfer Review** = formal cross-functional sign-off meeting (mandatory).
- ✅ **DFM/DFA happens DURING design, NOT after** — retroactive DFM is 10–100× costlier.
- ✅ **Biomedical-specific concerns**: biocompatibility (ISO 10993), sterility (ISO 11135/11137/17665), packaging integrity (ISO 11607), and regulatory traceability.
- ✅ **DFM/DFA examples to memorize**: (i) single-part snap-fit housing, (ii) standard off-the-shelf connectors, (iii) injection-moldable polymers over machined metals, (iv) USP Class VI / ISO 10993-compliant materials.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Labelling - IFU
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Labelling - IFU

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Labelling** in the context of biomedical devices refers to all written, printed, graphic, or electronic matter that is affixed to, accompanying, or displayed upon a medical device, its packaging, or its container. According to **21 CFR Part 801 (US FDA)** and **EU MDR Article 2(71)**, "labelling" is broadly interpreted to include not only the physical label on the device but also the outer packaging, package inserts, and any accompanying promotional or instructional materials.

The **Instructions for Use (IFU)** is a specific, structured document that provides the end-user (clinician, patient, or lay caregiver) with the necessary information for the safe and effective use of a medical device. It is a regulatory deliverable mandated by **ISO 20417:2021**, **IEC 60601-1 (for electromedical devices)**, **ISO 15223-1:2021** (symbols), and **EU MDR 2017/745**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
