A **thermometer** is the "bicycle" (Class I/A), a **blood pressure monitor** is the "motorcycle" (Class II/B), a **ventilator** is the "car" (Class IIb/C), and a **pacemaker** is the "airplane" (Class III/D). The more risk of harm to the patient, the tougher the regulatory hoops the device must jump through before reaching the market.

## 1.3 Why the Distinction Between a "Device" and a "Drug" Matters

A medical device is fundamentally **non-chemical** in its primary mode of action. This is the decisive point. A pill that lowers blood pressure is a **drug** (chemical action). A **pacemaker** that regulates heart rhythm is a **device** (electrical/physical action on the body). This distinction determines which regulatory pathway is followed, which submissions are required, and what post-market surveillance applies.

> [!WARNING]
> **Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)** — A critical modern twist. Under both EU MDR (2017/745) and FDA guidance, **standalone software** (e.g., an AI algorithm that detects arrhythmias from ECG) is now explicitly recognized as a medical device, even though it is intangible. CDSCO in India also covers SaMD under its 2017 Rules.

## 1.4 Why Three Frameworks?

| Region | Authority | Governing Document |
|---|---|---|
| **United States** | Food and Drug Administration (FDA) | FD&C Act, 21 CFR Parts 800–898 |
| **European Union** | European Commission + Notified Bodies | Regulation (EU) 2017/745 (MDR) |
| **India** | Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO) | Medical Devices Rules, 2017 |

Each framework has slightly different risk philosophies, but all converge on the principle of **risk-based classification** — the higher the risk, the stricter the conformity assessment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic of Risk-Based Classification

All three frameworks use the same fundamental risk drivers. Before the specific rules, the classification depends on:

- **Duration of use** (transient, short-term, long-term)
- **Invasiveness** (non-invasive, body orifice, surgically invasive, implantable)
- **Active vs. non-active** (whether the device depends on a source of energy other than the human body)
- **Intended purpose** (diagnostic, therapeutic, monitoring, surgical)
- **Contact with central nervous system / cardiovascular system**
- **Criticality of the body function being affected**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight:** A device is classified based on its **intended purpose** (what the manufacturer claims it does) and the **inherent risk** of that purpose, not based on its technological complexity. A sophisticated AI software may be Class I, while a simple bone screw may be Class III — risk, not sophistication, drives the class.

## 2.2 FDA Classification System (U.S.)

The FDA classification system is product-code based and is codified in the **Product Classification Database**. Devices are classified into three regulatory classes:

| FDA Class | Risk Level | Examples | Regulatory Pathway |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | Low | Elastic bandages, stethoscopes, surgical gloves, tongue depressors | General Controls (e.g., establishment registration, labeling) — most are **exempt** from 510(k) |
| **Class II** | Moderate | Infusion pumps, blood pressure monitors, surgical needles, X-ray machines, pregnancy test kits | General Controls **+ Special Controls** (e.g., performance standards, special labeling) — almost always require a **510(k) Premarket Notification** |
| **Class III** | High | Pacemakers, heart valves, implantable defibrillators, breast implants, deep brain stimulators | General Controls **+ Premarket Approval (PMA)** — full clinical and laboratory review |

### 2.2.1 FDA Premarket Pathways
- **510(k) (Premarket Notification):** Submit to demonstrate "substantial equivalence" to a legally marketed predicate device. Used for most Class II.
- **PMA (Premarket Approval):** Rigorous scientific and regulatory review to determine safety and effectiveness. Used for most Class III.
- **De Novo Classification:** For novel low-to-moderate risk devices with no predicate.
- **HDE (Humanitarian Device Exemption):** For rare disease devices.

## 2.3 EU MDR 2017/745 Classification (European Union)

The EU Medical Device Regulation has **four main risk classes** governed by **22 classification rules** in Annex VIII:

| EU Class | Risk Level | Example Devices | Conformity Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | Low | Stethoscopes, examination gloves, wheelchairs, non-invasive dressings | Self-certification by manufacturer (CE marking) |
| **Class IIa** | Low–Medium | Hearing aids, contact lenses, surgical gloves, infusion pumps (external), diagnostic ultrasound | Notified Body involvement |
| **Class IIb** | Medium–High | Ventilators, dialysis machines, surgical lasers, blood bags, implantable screws | Notified Body involvement (more rigorous) |
| **Class III** | High | Pacemakers, heart valves, drug-eluting stents, breast implants, total joint replacements | Notified Body + Design Dossier examination |

### 2.3.1 The 22 Rules in Brief
The EU MDR rules are grouped into four conceptual families:
- **Rules 1–4:** Non-invasive devices
- **Rules 5–8:** Invasive devices and surgical instruments
- **Rules 9–13:** Active devices and devices emitting radiation
- **Rules 14–22:** Special rules (contraceptives, contact lenses, devices containing tissues, etc.)

## 2.4 CDSCO Classification (India)

India adopted its **Medical Devices Rules, 2017** to bring the regulatory structure in line with the Global Harmonization Task Force (GHTF) framework. CDSCO classifies devices into four classes based on the **intended use, risk, duration of contact, and invasiveness**:

| CDSCO Class | Risk Level | Examples | Licensing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** (Low) | Low | Surgical dressings, alcohol swabs, thermometers, stethoscopes, reusable surgical instruments | State Licensing Authority (SLA) |
| **Class B** (Low–Moderate) | Low–Moderate | Infusion sets, blood pressure monitors, glucometers, contact lens cleaners, oxygen masks | SLA + CDSCO for high-risk subcategories |
| **Class C** (Moderate–High) | Moderate–High | Ventilators, surgical lasers, orthopedic implants, defibrillators, dialysis equipment | CDSCO (Central Licensing Authority, CLA) |
| **Class D** (High) | High | Pacemakers, heart valves, stents, implantable drug-delivery systems, intraocular lenses | CDSCO (CLA) + extensive clinical evaluation |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Risk Classification Crosswalk

> [!TIP]
> **Master Comparison Table — Memorize for KTU Exams**

| Risk Feature | FDA | EU MDR | CDSCO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of classes | **3** (I, II, III) | **4** (I, IIa, IIb, III) | **4** (A, B, C, D) |
| Number of classification rules | Product-code based | **22 rules** (Annex VIII) | GHTF-based (rules analogous to EU) |
| Lowest class | Class I | Class I | Class A |
| Highest class | Class III | Class III | Class D |
| Active device rules | Yes | Yes (Rules 9–13) | Yes |
| Implantable considered high? | Often Class III | Class IIb/III | Class C/D |
| Software (SaMD) classified? | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Premarket pathway | 510(k) / PMA / De Novo | CE marking by Notified Body | MD-9/MD-14/MD-15 licenses |
| Risk drivers | Intended use, energy source, contact duration | All of the above + invasiveness + body-system criticality | All of the above (GHTF-aligned) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on mathematical notation:** Any expression involving absolute value (e.g., $\vert \text{risk} \vert$) or set-membership (e.g., $x \in \text{Class II}$) is written with the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\in`, **never** with the literal pipe character `\vert`, to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of This System

- **Designers/Engineers:** Determines the design controls, verification & validation rigor, and documentation required.
- **Manufacturers:** Determines the registration dossier, clinical data needs, and time-to-market.
- **Hospitals/Procurement:** Determines the regulatory clearances to verify before purchase.
- **KTU Students:** This is the *lingua franca* of medical device design — every subsequent design decision (risk management per ISO 14971, usability per IEC 62366, software lifecycle per IEC 62304) is calibrated to the device's risk class.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Classification Logic (Analytical Derivation)

## 3.1 The Universal Classification Decision Algorithm

Although each framework has its own rules, the **decision logic** is similar. Below is the universal flow used by FDA, EU, and CDSCO. We treat this as an "algorithm" that the regulatory body (or designer) walks through:

### Step 1: Identify the Device
Determine what the device is, what it contacts (skin, mucosa, blood, bone, CNS, etc.), and its **intended purpose** as written in the manufacturer's labeling and IFU (Instructions For Use).

### Step 2: Determine Invasiveness
Ask: Does the device penetrate inside the body?

$$
\text{Invasiveness} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Non-invasive} & \rightarrow \text{Default Class I (FDA) / I (EU) / A (CDSCO)} \\
\text{Body orifice} & \rightarrow \text{Class I or II} \\
\text{Surgically invasive (transient/short-term)} & \rightarrow \text{Class II / IIa / B} \\
\text{Surgically invasive (long-term) or implantable} & \rightarrow \text{Class IIb, III / C, D}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3: Determine Active vs. Non-Active

$$
\text{Active Status} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Yes} \rightarrow \text{Apply Rules 9–13 (EU); often Class II or higher (FDA/CDSCO)} \\
\text{No} \rightarrow \text{Apply Rules 1–4 (EU); default Class I/II}
\end{cases}
$$

> An *active device* is one whose operation depends on a source of energy other than the human body or gravity. Examples: infusion pump (active), stethoscope (non-active).

### Step 4: Determine Duration of Contact

$$
\text{Duration} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Transient} \ (\leq 60 \ \text{min}) & \rightarrow \text{Lower class} \\
\text{Short-term} \ (60 \ \text{min} \ \text{to} \ 30 \ \text{days}) & \rightarrow \text{Mid class} \\
\text{Long-term} \ (> 30 \ \text{days}) & \rightarrow \text{Higher class}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 5: Determine Body System Criticality
Contact with the **central nervous system, central circulatory system, or heart** typically escalates the class to **Class III / Class D**.

### Step 6: Check Special Rules
- **EU:** Check Rules 14–22 (e.g., Rule 22 — active therapeutic devices intended to administer or exchange energy to/from the body in a potentially hazardous way → Class IIb or III).
- **FDA:** Use Product Classification Database lookup.
- **CDSCO:** Use the four-class schedule in Schedule I of MDR-2017.

### Step 7: Assign Final Class
Take the **highest** class indicated by any of the applicable rules. This is a conservative principle.

$$
\text{Final Class} = \max \big\{ \text{Class}_{\text{invasiveness}},\ \text{Class}_{\text{active}},\ \text{Class}_{\text{duration}},\ \text{Class}_{\text{system}},\ \text{Class}_{\text{special}} \big\}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — Classification of a Specific Device

**Device: An external infusion pump used in a hospital to deliver saline and drugs intravenously to patients.**

**Step 1 — Identify:** External infusion pump, electrically powered, delivers fluid into the venous system, in contact with blood.

**Step 2 — Invasiveness:** Although the pump itself is external, it connects to an IV catheter that is **invasive** (Class II or higher).

**Step 3 — Active:** Yes, it is electrically powered. Apply EU Rule 9 or 12 (active device intended to administer substances to the body). FDA: Class II (product code FRN).

**Step 4 — Duration:** Used for hours to days — short-term.

**Step 5 — Body system:** Indirect contact with central circulation.

**Step 6 — Special:** Not applicable.

**Step 7 — Final Class:**
- **FDA:** **Class II** (510(k) required, e.g., product code FRN).
- **EU MDR:** **Class IIb** (Rule 9 — active therapeutic device administering substances; Rule 12 also relevant).
- **CDSCO:** **Class C** (active therapeutic device, moderate-high risk).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 510(k) submission in the U.S. (Class II) and a CE marking under EU MDR (Class IIb) require very different clinical evidence. The same device sits in different risk buckets, and the designer must satisfy the **stricter** of the two for global market access.

## 3.3 Mapping Example — Bone Screw

| Step | Reasoning | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Identify | Bone screw, surgical, implantable, metallic | – |
| 2. Invasiveness | Surgically invasive, implantable (long-term) | EU: at least IIa, IIb |
| 3. Active | Non-active | – |
| 4. Duration | Long-term (>30 days) | Pushes to IIb or III |
| 5. Body system | In contact with bone, possibly CNS or load-bearing spine | Possible III |
| 6. Special | EU Rule 8 (implantable + long-term, intended to monitor/affect bone) → Class IIb/III | – |
| 7. Final | **FDA Class II (with some Class III)**, **EU Class IIb/III**, **CDSCO Class C/D** | – |

A spinal screw used to stabilize the spine under mechanical load is **Class III (FDA), Class IIb/III (EU), Class D (CDSCO)** because failure can cause paralysis.

## 3.4 The Mathematical Risk-Benefit Expression

Although regulatory frameworks do not use a strict equation, the underlying principle can be expressed as:

$$
\text{Regulatory Class} = f \big( R_{\text{patient}},\ I_{\text{invasiveness}},\ D_{\text{duration}},\ A_{\text{active}},\ S_{\text{system}},\ T_{\text{technology}} \big)
$$

where each variable is a categorical factor, and $f$ is a rule-based mapping. For a given device, the classification is the rule-output of the **most restrictive** applicable rule:

$$
\text{Class}_{\text{final}} = \max_{i \in \text{applicable rules}} \big( \text{Class}_i \big)
$$

This is the **conservative principle** that all three frameworks follow.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal Risk-Classification Flow (FDA / EU / CDSCO)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Device Identified] --> stepB{Is the device invasive?}
    stepB -- No --> stepC{Is the device active?}
    stepB -- Yes --> stepD{Is it surgically invasive or implantable?}
    stepC -- No --> stepE[Non-active non-invasive]
    stepC -- Yes --> stepF{Does it administer energy or substances?}
    stepD -- Transient short-term --> stepG[Surgically invasive transient]
    stepD -- Long-term implantable --> stepH[Long-term implantable]
    stepE --> stepI{Class determination: FDA I, EU I, CDSCO A}
    stepF -- Energy --> stepJ[Apply special rules 9-13 EU]
    stepF -- Substances --> stepK[Administering substances]
    stepG --> stepL{Class determination: FDA II, EU IIa, CDSCO B}
    stepH --> stepM{Contact with CNS or cardiovascular?}
    stepJ --> stepN[Class II or IIb]
    stepK --> stepO[Class IIa or IIb]
    stepL --> stepP[Class II FDA, IIa EU, B CDSCO]
    stepM -- Yes --> stepQ[Highest class: FDA III, EU III, CDSCO D]
    stepM -- No --> stepR[Class IIb EU, C CDSCO, II FDA]
    stepN --> stepS[Apply manufacturer labeling and risk management]
    stepO --> stepS
    stepP --> stepS
    stepQ --> stepT[Full PMA / CE Notified Body / CDSCO Class D]
    stepR --> stepU[510k or CE Class IIb / CDSCO Class C]
    stepI --> stepV[General Controls only]
    stepS --> stepW[End: Final class assigned]
    stepT --> stepW
    stepU --> stepW
    stepV --> stepW
```

## 4.2 Side-by-Side Comparison: FDA vs. EU vs. CDSCO

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FDA_F[FDA Framework]
        fdaA[Class I - Low] --> fdaB[Class II - Moderate]
        fdaB --> fdaC[Class III - High]
        fdaA --> fdaPath1[510k exempt]
        fdaB --> fdaPath2[510k Premarket Notification]
        fdaC --> fdaPath3[PMA Premarket Approval]
    end

    subgraph EU_F[EU MDR 2017/745]
        euA[Class I] --> euB[Class IIa]
        euB --> euC[Class IIb]
        euC --> euD[Class III]
        euA --> euP1[Self CE mark]
        euB --> euP2[Notified Body]
        euC --> euP3[Notified Body]
        euD --> euP4[Notified Body plus Design Dossier]
    end

    subgraph CDSCO_F[CDSCO India]
        cdA[Class A] --> cdB[Class B]
        cdB --> cdC[Class C]
        cdC --> cdD[Class D]
        cdA --> cdP1[State License]
        cdB --> cdP2[SLA plus CDSCO]
        cdC --> cdP3[CDSCO Central License]
        cdD --> cdP4[CDSCO plus extensive clinical]
    end

    fdaA -.- euA
    euA -.- cdA
    fdaB -.- euB
    euB -.- cdB
    fdaB -.- euC
    fdaC -.- euD
    euD -.- cdD
```

## 4.3 Device Classification Decision Tree (EU MDR 22 Rules — Simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a0[Device under Classification] --> a1{Non-invasive?}
    a1 -- Yes --> a2{Contact with skin or mucosa only?}
    a1 -- No --> a3{Invasive}
    a2 -- Skin only --> a4[Class I]
    a2 -- Mucosa or wound --> a5{Transient or short-term}
    a5 -- Transient --> a6[Class I]
    a5 -- Short-term --> a7[Class IIa]
    a3 --> a8{Surgically invasive transient}
    a3 --> a9{Short-term surgically invasive}
    a3 --> a10{Long-term implantable}
    a8 --> a11[Class I if reusable, IIa otherwise]
    a9 --> a12{Contact with CNS or heart?}
    a12 -- Yes --> a13[Class III]
    a12 -- No --> a14[Class IIa]
    a10 --> a15{Implantable long-term}
    a15 --> a16{Direct contact with heart CNS or blood?}
    a16 -- Yes --> a17[Class III]
    a16 -- No --> a18[Class IIb]
    a4 --> aend[Final Class]
    a6 --> aend
    a7 --> aend
    a11 --> aend
    a13 --> aend
    a14 --> aend
    a17 --> aend
    a18 --> aend
```

## 4.4 Medical Device Definition — Boundary Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    scope1[Medical Device Definition Scope] --> defA[Instruments and Apparatus]
    scope1 --> defB[Implants and Accessories]
    scope1 --> defC[Software SaMD]
    scope1 --> defD[In Vitro Diagnostics]
    scope1 --> defE[Reagents and Materials]

    defA --> useA1[Diagnosis]
    defA --> useA2[Treatment]
    defA --> useA3[Prevention]
    defA --> useA4[Monitoring]

    defB --> useB1[Long-term implants like stents and valves]
    defC --> useC1[AI ECG analysis, radiotherapy planning software]
    defD --> useD1[Blood glucose strips, pregnancy tests]
    defE --> useE1[Contact lens solutions, disinfectants]

    scope1 --> boundary1{Not a Device if:}
    boundary1 --> bA[Primary mode is pharmacological chemical action]
    boundary1 --> bB[It is a food or cosmetic]
    boundary1 --> bC[It is human tissue or blood product]
    boundary1 --> bD[It is general laboratory equipment not for medical purpose]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **"Define a medical device as per the U.S. FDA and explain why software is also considered a medical device under the FD&C Act."** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]`

**Model Answer:**

As per Section 201(h) of the U.S. Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic (FD&C) Act, a "medical device" is defined as an instrument, apparatus, implement, machine, contrivance, implant, in vitro reagent, or other similar or related article, including a component part or accessory, that is:
- recognized in the official National Formulary or the United States Pharmacopoeia (USP),
- intended for use in the diagnosis, cure, mitigation, treatment, or prevention of disease, in man or other animals,
- intended to affect the structure or any function of the body, **AND**
- does not achieve its primary intended purposes through chemical action within or on the body, and is not dependent upon being metabolized for the achievement of its primary intended purposes.

Software is considered a medical device under the FD&C Act (and FDA's separate guidance on Software as a Medical Device, SaMD) because it is an "accessory" or "instrument" that performs a medical purpose — such as diagnosing a condition (e.g., ECG arrhythmia detection) or aiding in treatment planning. As long as the software is "intended for use" in a medical purpose by the manufacturer, it falls under the same legal definition, even though it is intangible.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **"List the risk classes used by the EU MDR 2017/745 and CDSCO. Give one example device for each of the four CDSCO classes."** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 model]`

**Model Answer:**

**EU MDR 2017/745 Risk Classes (4 classes):**
1. Class I (low risk)
2. Class IIa (low–medium risk)
3. Class IIb (medium–high risk)
4. Class III (high risk)

**CDSCO Risk Classes (4 classes under Medical Devices Rules, 2017):**
1. **Class A** (low) — example: surgical dressings, alcohol swabs, reusable surgical instruments
2. **Class B** (low–moderate) — example: blood pressure monitors, glucometers, infusion sets
3. **Class C** (moderate–high) — example: ventilators, orthopedic implants, surgical lasers
4. **Class D** (high) — example: pacemakers, heart valves, drug-eluting stents

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
> **"Compare and contrast the classification systems of FDA, EU MDR, and CDSCO. In your answer, clearly state the number of classes in each, the underlying risk drivers, the conformity assessment pathway, and illustrate with two examples of how the same device (e.g., an infusion pump and a pacemaker) is classified differently across the three frameworks."** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**Model Answer (7 + 7 structure):**

**Part (a) — Comparison of classification systems (7 marks):**

| Feature | FDA (US) | EU MDR 2017/745 | CDSCO (India) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of classes** | 3 (I, II, III) | 4 (I, IIa, IIb, III) | 4 (A, B, C, D) |
| **Risk driver logic** | Product-code based + intended use | 22 rules in Annex VIII | GHTF-aligned; 4 risk classes based on intended use, duration, invasiveness |
| **Invasiveness considered?** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Active device considered?** | Yes | Yes (Rules 9–13) | Yes |
| **Software as device?** | Yes (SaMD guidance) | Yes (Rule 11, Annex VIII) | Yes (MDR-2017) |
| **Conformity assessment** | 510(k) / PMA / De Novo | CE marking by manufacturer (Class I) or Notified Body (IIa, IIb, III) | MD-9 license (SLA) for A, B; MD-14/MD-15 license (CDSCO) for C, D |
| **Duration of contact factor** | Yes (especially in 510(k) Special Controls) | Yes (transient, short-term, long-term) | Yes |
| **Body-system criticality** | Yes | Yes (especially Rules 6, 7, 8) | Yes |

The three systems share the **same philosophical foundation** — risk-based classification — but differ in granularity. The FDA uses 3 classes, while the EU and CDSCO use 4. All three use the conservative principle: the **most restrictive applicable rule** determines the final class.

**Part (b) — Worked examples across the three frameworks (7 marks):**

**Example 1: External Infusion Pump**

- **FDA:** Class II (product code FRN), requires **510(k) Premarket Notification** under 21 CFR 880.5725.
- **EU MDR:** Class IIb (Rule 9 — active device intended to administer substances to the body; and Rule 12 — active devices intended to control the performance of active therapeutic devices). Requires **Notified Body** assessment.
- **CDSCO:** Class C (active therapeutic device, moderate–high risk). Requires **MD-14/MD-15 license from CDSCO** and clinical evaluation.

**Example 2: Implantable Pacemaker**

- **FDA:** Class III, requires **Premarket Approval (PMA)** under 21 CFR 870.3600.
- **EU MDR:** Class III (Rule 8 — implantable long-term device in contact with the heart, intended to monitor/control a vital physiological process; and Rule 22). Requires **Notified Body Design Dossier examination**.
- **CDSCO:** Class D (high risk, implantable life-sustaining). Requires **MD-14/MD-15 license from CDSCO** plus extensive clinical investigation under Schedule III of MDR-2017.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall / Common Mistake:** Students often incorrectly state that "FDA Class II = EU Class II." This is **wrong**. FDA has only one "Class II," while EU has two — **IIa and IIb** — with **very different conformity routes**. A device may be FDA Class II but EU Class IIa, IIb, or even III, depending on the rule applied. Always check the specific rule, not just the class label.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)
> **"Explain the EU MDR 2017/745 classification system in detail. Discuss the four risk classes, the underlying decision logic based on the 22 classification rules, and the conformity assessment route for each class. Using the example of a surgical bone screw and a contact lens, show how the rules drive different classifications."** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]`

**Model Answer (7 + 7 structure):**

**Part (a) — EU MDR 2017/745 Classification System (7 marks):**

The EU Medical Device Regulation 2017/745 (MDR) classifies medical devices into four risk-based categories, governed by **22 rules** in **Annex VIII** of the regulation. The classification is based on the **intended purpose** of the device, its **invasiveness**, **duration of contact**, **active nature**, and **body-system criticality**.

**The four classes are:**
1. **Class I (Low risk):** Devices with low risk, e.g., non-invasive devices like stethoscopes, wheelchairs, and elastic bandages. The manufacturer may self-certify (CE mark) without a Notified Body for most Class I devices (except sterile or measuring function devices — Is, Im, Ir).
2. **Class IIa (Low–Medium risk):** Devices with low-to-moderate risk, e.g., hearing aids, contact lens cleaners, infusion pumps, and short-term surgically invasive devices. Requires Notified Body involvement for assessment of technical documentation.
3. **Class IIb (Medium–High risk):** Devices with moderate-to-high risk, e.g., ventilators, dialysis machines, surgical lasers, and long-term surgically invasive devices in contact with bone or tissue. Requires Notified Body involvement plus audit of the quality management system and review of the technical file.
4. **Class III (High risk):** Devices with the highest risk, e.g., pacemakers, heart valves, drug-eluting stents, and implantable long-term devices in contact with the central nervous system or heart. Requires Notified Body Design Dossier examination, clinical investigation review, and routine surveillance audits.

**Underlying decision logic (the 22 rules grouped):**
- **Rules 1–4:** Non-invasive devices (default Class I; escalate based on contact with blood, central circulation, etc.).
- **Rules 5–8:** Invasive devices and surgical instruments (escalate based on duration and contact with vital systems).
- **Rules 9–13:** Active devices and devices emitting radiation (escalate based on whether they administer energy, substances, or are diagnostic/therapeutic).
- **Rules 14–22:** Special rules (e.g., contraceptives, contact lenses, devices incorporating tissues, devices for sterilization, etc.).

The **conservative principle** applies: if multiple rules could apply, the **highest** resulting class is the final classification.

**Part (b) — Worked Examples (7 marks):**

**Example 1: Surgical Bone Screw (e.g., spinal fixation screw)**
- **Rule application:** Rule 8 (Implantable devices and long-term surgically invasive devices).
- **Reasoning:** The bone screw is surgically invasive, implantable, and intended for long-term use (>30 days) to provide mechanical stability to the spine. It is in contact with bone, and for spinal applications, can be in contact with the central nervous system (spinal cord).
- **Class:** **Class IIb or III.** If the screw is used in spinal surgery and could affect the CNS, it escalates to **Class III**. If used in peripheral bone (e.g., a fracture plate in the femur), it is **Class IIb**.
- **Conformity route:** Notified Body assessment of technical documentation, audit of QMS (ISO 13485), and review of clinical evaluation (MEDDEV 2.7/1 rev 4 or MDR Annex XIV).

**Example 2: Contact Lens (soft, corrective)**
- **Rule application:** Rule 5 (Devices invasive in body orifices) and **Rule 17** (Special rule — devices specifically intended for direct contact with the eye).
- **Reasoning:** Contact lenses are placed in contact with the eye. Per Rule 17, all contact lenses are automatically classified as **Class IIa or IIb** depending on whether they are intended for short-term or long-term wear.
- **Class:** **Class IIb** for long-term wear lenses (most corrective soft lenses are daily wear) or **Class IIa** for daily disposable lenses. Under the new MDR, contact lenses are generally **Class IIb**.
- **Conformity route:** Notified Body assessment of the technical file, biocompatibility evaluation per ISO 10993, and clinical evaluation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to confuse **Rule 8** (implantable + long-term invasive) with **Rule 5** (invasive via body orifice). The distinction matters: a contact lens is **NOT implantable** (it is not surgically placed), so it falls under **Rule 5** and the special **Rule 17**, not Rule 8. Always start from the most specific rule first.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know Items for KTU PEEBT752 Module 4**

- ✅ **A medical device is defined by its intended purpose**, not by its technology. A device whose primary mode of action is **chemical/pharmacological** is a **drug**, not a device.
- ✅ **FDA (US) — 3 classes:** I, II, III. Premarket pathways: 510(k) for most Class II, PMA for most Class III.
- ✅ **EU MDR 2017/745 — 4 classes:** I, IIa, IIb, III. Classification based on **22 rules in Annex VIII**.
- ✅ **CDSCO India — 4 classes:** A, B, C, D. Aligned with GHTF principles.
- ✅ **Risk drivers** (universal): intended use, invasiveness, duration, active vs. non-active, body-system criticality.
- ✅ **Conservative principle:** the highest applicable rule determines the final class.
- ✅ **Software (SaMD) is a medical device** in all three frameworks.
- ✅ **Class I/A devices** may be self-certified (or 510(k)-exempt) — minimal regulatory burden.
- ✅ **Class III/D devices** (pacemakers, heart valves) require the most rigorous review — PMA, Notified Body Design Dossier, or CDSCO MD-14/MD-15 with extensive clinical data.
- ✅ **Infusion pump:** FDA II, EU IIb, CDSCO C.
- ✅ **Pacemaker:** FDA III, EU III, CDSCO D.
- ✅ **Bone screw (spinal):** EU IIb/III; CDSCO C/D; FDA II/III depending on location.
- ✅ **Contact lens:** EU IIb (Rule 17 special rule); not a Rule 8 implant.
- ✅ **Memorize the crosswalk table** — examiners love asking "FDA Class II = which EU class?" (Answer: it depends — could be IIa or IIb).
- ✅ Always cite the **specific rule number** (EU) or **product code** (FDA) when justifying a classification.
- ✅ **Memorize the three statutory citations:** FD&C Act §201(h), EU MDR Art. 2(1), and CDSCO MDR-2017 Rule 2(1)(iv).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medical Device Safety, Global Framework for Regulations, Regulatory Tools, Medical Device Standards: Development Process, Conformity Assessment, Quality System Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Medical Device Safety, Global Framework for Regulations, Regulatory Tools, Medical Device Standards: Development Process, Conformity Assessment, Quality System Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Medical Device Safety

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Medical Device Safety* is the totality of procedures, design principles, and risk control measures implemented throughout the device life cycle to ensure that the device performs as intended without causing unacceptable risk to the patient, user, or environment. It is governed primarily by **IEC 60601-1** (electrical safety), **ISO 14971** (risk management), and **ISO 10993** (biocompatibility).

### The Three Pillars of Medical Device Safety

1. **Electrical Safety** — protection against shock, burns, fire, and electromagnetic interference (governed by **IEC 60601-1**).
2. **Mechanical Safety** — protection against moving parts, sharp edges, instability, vibration, and mechanical failure (governed by **IEC 60601-1** clauses 9, 15, 21).
3. **Biological / Chemical Safety** — biocompatibility, sterilization residues, and toxicology (governed by **ISO 10993** series).

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Safety vs. Essential Performance (IEC 60601-1)**
> - **Essential Safety:** Absence of unacceptable risk directly caused by physical hazards (electric shock, fire, mechanical).
> - **Essential Performance:** Performance necessary to maintain freedom from unacceptable risk (e.g., an ECG must accurately reproduce cardiac waveforms; failure = unacceptable risk).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital-Grade Car" Intuition

Think of a medical device like a **hospital-grade car**:
- A normal car (consumer product) only needs to drive — it follows automotive safety standards.
- A hospital-grade car (medical device) must **drive, monitor the driver's vitals, alert the hospital on accident, sterilize itself between drivers, and survive daily shock + electromagnetic pulses from MRI machines**.
- That is exactly why medical devices have an *order of magnitude more regulation* than ordinary consumer electronics.

### Key Risk Concepts (ISO 14971 Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Hazard** | Potential source of harm |
| **Harm** | Physical injury or damage to health |
| **Severity** | Measure of possible consequence of harm (negligible → catastrophic) |
| **Probability** | Likelihood of harm occurring |
| **Risk** | Combination of severity and probability of harm |
| **Risk Control** | Measures taken to reduce risk to acceptable level |
| **Residual Risk** | Risk remaining after risk control measures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ALARP Principle (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** — Risk must be reduced until the cost of further reduction is grossly disproportionate to the safety benefit gained.

---

## 1.2 Global Framework for Regulations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The *Global Framework for Medical Device Regulations* refers to the harmonized set of laws, agencies, and guidelines established by major economies (USA, EU, Japan, Canada, Australia, India) to ensure that medical devices placed in their markets are safe, effective, and of high quality.

### Major Regulatory Authorities

| Region | Authority | Governing Regulation |
|--------|-----------|----------------------|
| **United States** | **FDA** (Food and Drug Administration), Center for Devices and Radiological Health (CDRH) | **FD&C Act**, **21 CFR Part 820 (QSR)** |
| **European Union** | National Competent Authorities + **Notified Bodies** | **EU MDR 2017/745**, **EU IVDR 2017/746** |
| **Japan** | **PMDA** (Pharmaceuticals and Medical Devices Agency) | PMD Act |
| **Canada** | **Health Canada** | Medical Device Regulations SOR/98-282 |
| **Australia** | **TGA** (Therapeutic Goods Administration) | TGA Act |
| **India** | **CDSCO** (Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation) | MDR 2017 (India) |
| **Global** | **IMDRF** (International Medical Device Regulators Forum) | Harmonization guidance |

### Global Harmonization Bodies

> [!NOTE]
> **GHTF / IMDRF** — The Global Harmonization Task Force (now **IMDRF**) was founded in 1992 to standardize medical device regulation across countries. Its successor, the **International Medical Device Regulators Forum**, publishes guidance documents adopted (in part) by FDA, EU, Japan, and others.

---

## 1.3 Regulatory Tools

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Regulatory Tools* are the formal pathways, classifications, and documents used by authorities to bring a medical device from concept to market and to monitor it post-market.

### Risk-Based Classification Systems

Most regulators use a **four-class (or three-class) risk-based system**:

| Class | FDA (US) | EU MDR | Risk Level | Examples |
|-------|----------|--------|------------|----------|
| **Class I** | General Controls only | Class I | Low | Bandages, stethoscopes, tongue depressors |
| **Class II** | General + Special Controls + **510(k)** | Class IIa | Moderate | Infusion pumps, surgical gloves, BP monitors |
| **Class III** | General + Special + **PMA** | Class III | High | Pacemakers, heart valves, implantable defibrillators |

> [!NOTE]
> **EU MDR also has Class Is (sterile), Class Im (measuring), and Class Ir (reusable surgical instruments)** — sub-classifications under Class I.

### Core Regulatory Tools

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| **510(k) Premarket Notification** | Demonstrates substantial equivalence to a legally marketed device |
| **PMA (Premarket Approval)** | FDA's most stringent review for Class III devices |
| **De Novo Classification** | Novel low-to-moderate risk device with no predicate |
| **CE Marking** | EU conformity mark (mandatory for EU market access) |
| **Technical Documentation / Design Dossier** | Demonstrates conformity to Essential Requirements |
| **Clinical Evaluation Report (CER)** | Demonstrates clinical safety and performance |
| **Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)** | Monitors real-world safety after launch |

---

## 1.4 Medical Device Standards — Development Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Medical Device Standards* are documents established by consensus that provide rules, guidelines, or characteristics for activities or their results, aimed at the promotion of optimum order. The principal standards body is **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)**, supplemented by **IEC** (electrical), **ASTM** (materials), and **AAMI** (US medical devices).

### Hierarchy of Standards

$$\text{Consensus Standards} = \underbrace{\text{ISO 13485}}_{\text{QMS}} + \underbrace{\text{ISO 14971}}_{\text{Risk}} + \underbrace{\text{ISO 10993}}_{\text{Biocompatibility}} + \underbrace{\text{IEC 60601}}_{\text{Electrical Safety}}$$

### Design and Development Process (per ISO 13485 §7.3)

The design and development process follows a structured **Stage-Gate** model:

1. **Design and Development Planning** — define stages, reviews, responsibilities.
2. **Design and Development Inputs** — user needs, regulatory requirements, risk outputs.
3. **Design and Development Outputs** — drawings, specifications, software, labeling.
4. **Design and Development Review** — systematic evaluation at each stage.
5. **Design and Development Verification** — "Did we make the product right?" (does it meet specifications?).
6. **Design and Development Validation** — "Did we make the right product?" (does it meet user needs and intended use?).
7. **Design and Development Transfer** — transfer from design to manufacturing.
8. **Control of Design and Development Changes** — review, verify, validate, approve changes.
9. **Design and Development Files (DHF — Design History File)** — complete record of all design activities.

---

## 1.5 Conformity Assessment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Conformity Assessment* is the systematic examination of evidence generated under a quality management system and used to demonstrate that a medical device meets specified regulatory requirements (Essential Requirements under EU MDR; General + Special Controls under FDA).

### EU MDR Conformity Assessment Routes

$$\text{Route} = f(\text{Device Class})$$

| Class | Conformity Assessment Route | Notified Body Involvement |
|-------|-----------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Class I** | Self-declaration by manufacturer | None |
| **Class IIa** | Annex IX (QMS) or Annex XI (Production QA) | Yes |
| **Class IIb** | Annex IX (QMS) + Annex X (Type Examination) | Yes |
| **Class III** | Annex IX + Annex X + EU Technical Documentation Assessment | Yes (extensive) |

### FDA Conformity Assessment Equivalents

| FDA Pathway | Equivalent EU Concept |
|-------------|----------------------|
| **510(k)** | Conformity Assessment for Class II |
| **PMA** | Conformity Assessment for Class III |
| **Establishment Registration** | Manufacturer registration |
| **Device Listing** | UDI registration (EUDAMED) |

---

## 1.6 Quality System Requirements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Quality System Requirements* are the documented policies, procedures, and practices that a manufacturer must establish and maintain to ensure that medical devices consistently meet design specifications, safety requirements, and intended use. The cornerstone standard is **ISO 13485:2016**, supplemented by **21 CFR Part 820** in the US.

### Core Subsystems of a Quality Management System (QMS)

1. **Management Responsibility** — quality policy, planning, communication.
2. **Resource Management** — human resources, infrastructure, work environment.
3. **Product Realization** — planning, customer-related processes, design and development, purchasing, production, service.
4. **Measurement, Analysis, Improvement** — monitoring, CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action).

> [!NOTE]
> **DHF vs. DMR vs. DHR** (US FDA definitions):
> - **DHF (Design History File)** — record of *all design activities* for a finished device.
> - **DMR (Device Master Record)** — set of *all instructions, drawings, and specifications* needed to produce the device.
> - **DHR (Device History Record)** — *actual production records* for a specific manufactured batch.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Risk Management per ISO 14971 — Process Model

The **ISO 14971 Risk Management Process** is a continuous lifecycle model:

$$\text{Risk Management Process} = \text{Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Evaluation} \rightarrow \text{Control} \rightarrow \text{Monitoring}$$

### Step-by-Step Logic (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Risk Management Process Initiation** — define scope, responsibility, criteria for risk acceptability.
2. **Risk Analysis**
   - Identify hazards (HAZID).
   - Estimate severity and probability for each hazardous situation.
   - For each combination → estimate risk.
3. **Risk Evaluation** — compare estimated risk against acceptability criteria; decide if risk reduction is required.
4. **Risk Control** — apply control measures in this priority order:
   - **Inherent Safety by Design** (e.g., limit voltage to ≤ 24 V).
   - **Protective Measures in the Medical Device Itself** (e.g., insulation, alarms).
   - **Information for Safety** (warnings, training, labeling).
5. **Residual Risk Evaluation** — verify risk is acceptable; perform **benefit-risk analysis** if not.
6. **Risk Management Review** — confirm overall residual risk is acceptable.
7. **Production and Post-Production Activities** — monitor field data, complaints, recalls.

### Risk Acceptability Matrix

| Probability ↓ \ Severity → | Negligible (1) | Minor (2) | Serious (3) | Critical (4) | Catastrophic (5) |
|---------------------------|----------------|-----------|-------------|--------------|------------------|
| **Frequent (5)** | Medium | High | High | Unacceptable | Unacceptable |
| **Probable (4)** | Low | Medium | High | High | Unacceptable |
| **Occasional (3)** | Low | Medium | Medium | High | High |
| **Remote (2)** | Negligible | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Improbable (1)** | Negligible | Negligible | Low | Medium | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** Unacceptable risks must be reduced; ALARP must be demonstrated for medium risks; low/acceptable risks may be documented and accepted.

### Risk Reduction Equation

$$R_{\text{final}} = R_{\text{initial}} - \Delta R_{\text{design}} - \Delta R_{\text{protective}} - \Delta R_{\text{informational}}$$

where each $\Delta R$ is the risk reduction contributed by each control measure in the priority order.

---

## 2.2 Electrical Safety — IEC 60601-1 Key Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Limit | Test Method |
|-----------|--------|-------|-------------|
| Earth Leakage Current | $I_{\text{earth}}$ | $\leq 500\,\mu\text{A}$ (Class I, NC) | IEC 60601-1 §8.7 |
| Enclosure Leakage Current | $I_{\text{enc}}$ | $\leq 100\,\mu\text{A}$ (Class I, NC) | IEC 60601-1 §8.7 |
| Patient Leakage Current | $I_{\text{pat}}$ | $\leq 100\,\mu\text{A}$ (Type B) | IEC 60601-1 §8.7 |
| Patient Auxiliary Current | $I_{\text{aux}}$ | $\leq 100\,\mu\text{A}$ | IEC 60601-1 §8.7 |
| Dielectric Strength | $V_{\text{test}}$ | $1500\,\text{V}_{\text{RMS}}$ for 1 min | IEC 60601-1 §8.8 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Means of Protection (MOP):** IEC 60601-1 distinguishes **MOOP** (Means of Operator Protection) and **MOPP** (Means of Patient Protection). Patient is the weaker link — hence stricter MOPP requirements.

---

## 2.3 Design Control Flow — Inputs to Outputs

$$\underbrace{\text{Inputs}}_{\text{User Needs, Reg. Req.}} \xrightarrow{\text{Design Process}} \underbrace{\text{Outputs}}_{\text{Specs, Drawings, Software, Labels}}$$

### Verification vs. Validation (CRITICAL — KTU Exam Favorite)

| Parameter | Verification | Validation |
|-----------|--------------|------------|
| **Question** | Did we make the product right? | Did we make the right product? |
| **Compares** | Outputs ↔ Inputs | Outputs ↔ User Needs / Intended Use |
| **When** | During design (after each output) | At end of design (with finished product) |
| **Typical activity** | Bench testing, code review | Clinical/simulated use testing |
| **Standard ref.** | ISO 13485 §7.3.6 | ISO 13485 §7.3.7 |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Formula | Use |
|---|-------------------|-----|
| 1 | $\text{Risk} = f(\text{Severity}, \text{Probability})$ | ISO 14971 quantitative basis |
| 2 | $\text{Risk Acceptability} = \text{ALARP}$ | UK/EU decision principle |
| 3 | $\text{Class} \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{Control} \uparrow$ | Classification logic |
| 4 | $V_{\text{test}} = 1500\,\text{V}_{\text{RMS}}$ | Dielectric strength (Class I) |
| 5 | $I_{\text{leak}} \leq 100\,\mu\text{A}$ (Patient) | IEC 60601-1 leak limit |
| 6 | $t_{\text{dwell}} \geq 15\,\text{s}$ | Dielectric test duration |
| 7 | $R_{\text{residual}} \leq R_{\text{acceptable}}$ | ISO 14971 acceptance criterion |
| 8 | $\text{Class IIa (EU)} \leftrightarrow \text{510(k) (US)}$ | Regulatory equivalence |
| 9 | $\text{DHF} \subseteq \text{DMR} \subseteq \text{QMS Records}$ | Documentation hierarchy |
| 10 | $\text{Conformity} = \text{QMS} \otimes \text{Technical Documentation}$ | Conformity assessment |

---

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

- A **biomedical engineer designing a defibrillator** must demonstrate (via risk management file) that residual risk is acceptable after all controls — not just "build it well."
- For **Indian market access (CDSCO)**, ISO 13485 certification + clinical investigation data is mandatory.
- For **US market**, 510(k) submission requires demonstration of *substantial equivalence* to a predicate — engineering testing must match the predicate's performance characteristics.
- **IEC 60601-1** testing (CB Scheme) is a prerequisite for global acceptance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Patient Leakage Current Through Defibrillation-Proof Protection

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation is the **classical IEC 60601-1 patient leakage calculation**, frequently asked in KTU as a "Part B numerical."

### Scenario
A patient is connected to a Type BF (Body Floating) device. During a single-fault condition (e.g., broken protective earth), the patient leakage current flows from the applied part through the patient to ground. Determine the maximum permissible patient leakage current during Normal Condition (NC) and Single Fault Condition (SFC).

### Derivation — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant standard table.**
Per **IEC 60601-1, Table 3** (Patient Leakage Current):

$$I_{\text{pat, NC}} \leq 100\,\mu\text{A} \quad (\text{Type B})$$

$$I_{\text{pat, SFC}} \leq 500\,\mu\text{A} \quad (\text{Type B})$$

**Step 2 — Identify Type BF requirements.**
For Type BF (F = isolated / floating patient circuit, B = body — but with isolation):

$$I_{\text{pat, NC}} \leq 100\,\mu\text{A}$$

$$I_{\text{pat, SFC}} \leq 500\,\mu\text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Identify Type CF (Cardiac Floating) requirements (most stringent):**

$$I_{\text{pat, NC}} \leq 10\,\mu\text{A}$$

$$I_{\text{pat, SFC}} \leq 50\,\mu\text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate resistive limit for measurement circuit.**
The measuring device (MD) per IEC 60601-1 §8.7.4 has a fixed impedance:

$$R_{\text{MD}} = 1000\,\Omega \pm 10\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute maximum permissible applied voltage for test.**
Under SFC for Type BF, voltage across $R_{\text{MD}}$:

$$V_{\text{max, SFC}} = I_{\text{pat, SFC}} \times R_{\text{MD}}$$

$$V_{\text{max, SFC}} = (500 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1000$$

$$V_{\text{max, SFC}} = 0.5\,\text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Apply test to power supply rail.**
If the device operates from a $230\,\text{V}_{\text{RMS}}$ mains with a basic insulation resistance $R_{\text{ins}} = 50\,\text{M}\Omega$, fault current is:

$$I_{\text{fault}} = \frac{V_{\text{mains}}}{R_{\text{ins}}} = \frac{230}{50 \times 10^6}$$

$$I_{\text{fault}} = 4.6\,\mu\text{A}$$

Since $I_{\text{fault}} = 4.6\,\mu\text{A} < I_{\text{pat, NC}} = 100\,\mu\text{A}$, the device **passes** patient leakage test.

### Conclusion of Derivation

$$\boxed{I_{\text{fault}} = \frac{V_{\text{mains}}}{R_{\text{ins}}} \leq I_{\text{limit, NC}} \text{ (for compliance)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Risk Score for Hazard Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **standard KTU 14-mark calculation** for risk management.

### Scenario
A blood pressure monitor has the following hazard: "Cuff pressure overshoot causing tissue damage." The hazard severity is **Serious (3)** and probability of occurrence is **Occasional (3)**.

**Step 1 — Compute base risk score.**

$$R_{\text{base}} = S \times P$$

$$R_{\text{base}} = 3 \times 3 = 9$$

**Step 2 — Apply Risk Control #1: Inherent Safety by Design** (limit maximum cuff pressure to 300 mmHg).

New severity: **Minor (2)** → New probability: **Remote (2)**.

$$R_{\text{after C1}} = 2 \times 2 = 4$$

Risk reduction:

$$\Delta R_1 = R_{\text{base}} - R_{\text{after C1}} = 9 - 4 = 5$$

**Step 3 — Apply Risk Control #2: Protective Measure** (add pressure-relief valve).

New probability: **Improbable (1)** → Severity unchanged at Minor (2).

$$R_{\text{after C2}} = 2 \times 1 = 2$$

Risk reduction:

$$\Delta R_2 = R_{\text{after C1}} - R_{\text{after C2}} = 4 - 2 = 2$$

**Step 4 — Apply Risk Control #3: Information for Safety** (label maximum inflation time).

Probability unchanged. Severity unchanged.

$$R_{\text{after C3}} = 2 \times 1 = 2$$

$$\Delta R_3 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Verify Residual Risk.**

$$R_{\text{residual}} = 2 \quad (\text{Low / Acceptable})$$

Since $R_{\text{residual}} \leq R_{\text{acceptable}}$, risk control is successful.

**Step 6 — Total Risk Reduction.**

$$\Delta R_{\text{total}} = \Delta R_1 + \Delta R_2 + \Delta R_3 = 5 + 2 + 0 = 7$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{base}} - \Delta R_{\text{total}} = 9 - 7 = 2 \leq R_{\text{acceptable}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Regulatory Systems

| Feature | US (FDA) | EU (MDR 2017/745) | India (CDSCO) |
|---------|----------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Risk Classes** | I, II, III | I, IIa, IIb, III | A, B, C, D |
| **Pathway for Class II** | 510(k) | Annex IX/X | CDSCO License |
| **Body Mark Required** | None (FDA cleared/approved) | **CE Mark** | **ISI / CDSCO License** |
| **QMS Standard** | 21 CFR 820 (→ transitioning to ISO 13485) | ISO 13485 (mandatory) | ISO 13485 / ICMED |
| **Clinical Data Required** | Class III only (PMA) | All classes (Clinical Evaluation Report) | Risk-based |
| **Notified/Notified Body** | N/A (FDA) | Notified Body | CDSCO / Notified Body (new rules) |
| **Post-Market Surveillance** | MDR (Medical Device Reporting) | PMS + PSUR | PMS reporting |
| **UDI Required** | Yes | Yes (EUDAMED) | Yes (under MDR 2017) |
| **Person Responsible** | US Agent | PRRC (Person Responsible for Regulatory Compliance) | Indian Agent |

---

## 3.4 Cost–Benefit Justification for Risk Control (Quantitative ALARP)

The **ALARP test** (UK HSE methodology):

$$\text{For Risk Reduction: } \frac{\text{Risk Reduction}}{\text{Cost of Implementation}} \geq \text{Disproportion Factor}$$

Equivalently:

$$\text{Cost of Implemented Measure} \ll \text{Monetized Value of Risk Reduction}$$

**Numerical Example:**
A control measure reduces patient harm probability from $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ (factor of 100). Severity cost = ₹5,000,000 per harm event.

Expected loss without control:

$$E[L_{\text{before}}] = 10^{-3} \times 5{,}000{,}000 = \text{₹}5000$$

Expected loss with control:

$$E[L_{\text{after}}] = 10^{-5} \times 5{,}000{,}000 = \text{₹}50$$

Benefit:

$$B = E[L_{\text{before}}] - E[L_{\text{after}}] = \text{₹}4950$$

If implementation cost $C = \text{₹}500$, then $B \gg C$, so the measure is justified.

$$\boxed{\text{Justified if } \frac{B}{C} \geq 10 \text{ (typical gross-disproportion factor)}}$$

---

## 3.5 Design Control Workflow — Decision Logic

```
Inputs available?
   ├── NO → Iterate back to planning
   └── YES
       ↓
Outputs generated?
   ├── NO → Iterate to design process
   └── YES
       ↓
Verification passed?
   ├── NO → Iterate to design process
   └── YES
       ↓
Validation passed?
   ├── NO → Iterate to design inputs (user need)
   └── YES
       ↓
Design Transfer approved
   ├── NO → Iterate to design transfer
   └── YES
       ↓
DHF complete → Released to Production
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Medical Device Safety — Three-Pillar Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Pillar1[Electrical Safety - IEC 60601]
        nodeA[Shock Protection] --> nodeB[Leakage Current Limitation]
        nodeB --> nodeC[Dielectric Strength]
        nodeC --> nodeD[EMC Compliance]
    end

    subgraph Pillar2[Mechanical Safety - IEC 60601]
        nodeE[Sharp Edge Control] --> nodeF[Mechanical Stability]
        nodeF --> nodeG[Moving Parts Guarding]
        nodeG --> nodeH[Vibration and Shock]
    end

    subgraph Pillar3[Biological Safety - ISO 10993]
        nodeI[Biocompatibility Testing] --> nodeJ[Sterilization Validation]
        nodeJ --> nodeK[Toxicology Assessment]
        nodeK --> nodeL[Residue Limits]
    end

    Pillar1 --> nodeM[Medical Device Safety Profile]
    Pillar2 --> nodeM
    Pillar3 --> nodeM

    nodeM --> nodeN[Risk Management File ISO 14971]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Regulatory Submission]
```

## 4.2 Risk Management Process (ISO 14971) — Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Initiate Risk Management Process] --> nodeB[Define Scope and Criteria]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Identify Hazards HAZID]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Estimate Severity and Probability]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Compute Risk Score]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Risk Acceptable?}
    F -- No --> nodeG[Apply Risk Control Priority]
    G --> nodeH1[Inherent Safety by Design]
    nodeH1 --> nodeH2[Protective Measures in Device]
    nodeH2 --> nodeH3[Information for Safety Labels Training]
    nodeH3 --> nodeI[Compute Residual Risk]
    I --> nodeF
    F -- Yes --> nodeJ[Benefit Risk Analysis if Marginal]
    J --> nodeK[Risk Management Review]
    K --> nodeL[Production and Post Production Activities]
    L --> nodeM[Update Risk Management Report]
    M --> nodeC
```

## 4.3 Global Regulatory Framework — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USA[USA FDA]
        us1[Establishment Registration]
        us2[Device Classification]
        us3[510k for Class II] --> us4a[Predicate Comparison]
        us4a --> us5a[Substantial Equivalence]
        us3b[PMA for Class III] --> us4b[Clinical Data Review]
        us4b --> us5b[Approval]
    end

    subgraph EU[European Union MDR 2017 745]
        eu1[Device Classification]
        eu2[Class I Self Declaration]
        eu3[Class IIa IIb Notified Body Audit]
        eu4[Class III Type Examination plus QMS Audit]
        eu3 --> eu5[CE Mark Affixed]
        eu4 --> eu5
        eu2 --> eu5
    end

    subgraph IND[India CDSCO]
        in1[Device Classification A B C D]
        in2[Class A Self Registration]
        in3[Class B C D CDSCO License]
        in3 --> in4[License Granted]
    end

    USA --> final[Market Access]
    EU --> final
    IND --> final
```

## 4.4 Design and Development V-Model (ISO 13485 §7.3)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[User Needs and Intended Use] --> nodeB[Design Inputs Requirements]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Design Outputs Specifications]
    C --> nodeD[Design Verification]
    D -- Fail --> B
    D -- Pass --> nodeE[Design Validation]
    E -- Fail --> B
    E -- Pass --> nodeF[Design Transfer to Manufacturing]
    F --> nodeG[Production and Post Production]
    G --> nodeH[Post Market Surveillance]
    H --> nodeI[Feedback to Design Inputs]
    I --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The V-Model** is the gold-standard for medical device development: each "down" arrow on the left (specification) corresponds to a matching "up" arrow on the right (verification/validation). This ensures traceability from user needs → production.

## 4.5 Quality Management System — Process-Based Architecture (ISO 13485)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MR[Management Responsibility]
        mr1[Quality Policy] --> mr2[Quality Objectives]
        mr2 --> mr3[Management Review]
    end

    subgraph RM[Resource Management]
        rm1[Human Resources] --> rm2[Infrastructure]
        rm2 --> rm3[Work Environment]
    end

    subgraph PR[Product Realization]
        pr1[Customer Related Processes] --> pr2[Design and Development]
        pr2 --> pr3[Purchasing]
        pr3 --> pr4[Production and Service Provision]
        pr4 --> pr5[Control of Monitoring Equipment]
    end

    subgraph MA[Measurement Analysis Improvement]
        ma1[Monitoring and Measurement] --> ma2[Control of Nonconforming Product]
        ma2 --> ma3[Analysis of Data]
        ma3 --> ma4[CAPA Corrective and Preventive Action]
    end

    MR --> RM
    RM --> PR
    PR --> MA
    MA -.feedback loop.-> MR
```

## 4.6 Conformity Assessment — Decision Tree for EU MDR

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Device Classification] --> nodeB{Class?}
    B -- Class I --> nodeC[Self Declaration - Annex II plus VIII]
    B -- Class IIa --> nodeD[Annex IX QMS] --> nodeF[CE Mark]
    B -- Class IIb --> nodeE[Annex IX plus Annex X Type Exam] --> nodeF
    B -- Class III --> nodeG[Annex IX plus Annex X plus EU Tech Doc Assessment] --> nodeF
    F --> nodeH[Post Market Surveillance Plan]
    H --> nodeI[Periodic Safety Update Report]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Medical Device Safety. Mention the role of IEC 60601-1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Medical Device Safety is the totality of procedures, design principles, and risk control measures implemented throughout the device life cycle to ensure that the device performs as intended without causing unacceptable risk to the patient, user, or environment. IEC 60601-1 is the international standard for the basic safety and essential performance of medical electrical equipment. It specifies requirements for electrical shock protection, mechanical hazards, excessive temperatures, fire prevention, and accurate measurement. Compliance with IEC 60601-1 is a prerequisite for global market access (CB Scheme) and forms the foundation of safety testing for any medical electrical device. (3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: What is the difference between Design Verification and Design Validation? **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|--------|--------------|------------|
| Definition | Confirmation that design outputs meet design inputs | Confirmation that the device meets user needs and intended use |
| Question | "Did we make the product right?" | "Did we make the right product?" |
| Timing | During design, after each output | At end of design, on finished product |

Both activities are mandatory under ISO 13485 §7.3.6 and §7.3.7. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** Choose **ONE** of the following. Both options are equally weighted.

---

### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Explain the ISO 14971 Risk Management Process with a neat block diagram. Discuss the three priority orders of risk control. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** ISO 14971 is the international standard for the application of risk management to medical devices. It defines a systematic process for identifying hazards, estimating and evaluating risks, controlling risks, and monitoring effectiveness of controls throughout the product life cycle.

**Process Steps (4 marks):**

1. **Risk Management Process Initiation** — Define scope, assign responsibilities, set risk acceptability criteria.
2. **Risk Analysis** — Identify hazards (HAZID), estimate severity and probability of harm, compute initial risk.
3. **Risk Evaluation** — Compare computed risk with acceptability criteria; decide if reduction is needed.
4. **Risk Control** — Apply control measures in priority order (see below).
5. **Evaluation of Residual Risk** — Verify that residual risk is acceptable.
6. **Risk Management Review** — Confirm overall residual risk is acceptable before market release.
7. **Production and Post-Production Activities** — Collect field data, complaints, recalls; feed back into risk analysis.

**Three Priority Orders of Risk Control (2 marks):**

1. **Inherent Safety by Design** — *First choice.* Eliminate hazard by changing the device itself. Example: limit maximum output voltage of a defibrillator to a safe value.
2. **Protective Measures in the Medical Device Itself** — *Second choice.* Add guards, insulation, alarms. Example: temperature sensor in a warming blanket that cuts power at 42 °C.
3. **Information for Safety** — *Last choice.* Warnings, labels, training manuals. Example: "Do not use near MRI."

> Block diagram as in **SECTION 4.2** above should be redrawn by the student.

---

#### Part (b) — A patient monitoring device has a hazard with Severity = 4 (Critical) and Probability = 3 (Occasional). Compute the base risk, then apply two risk controls (Design and Protective) and find the residual risk. Comment on ALARP. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Base Risk:**

$$R_{\text{base}} = S \times P = 4 \times 3 = 12 \quad (\text{Unacceptable})$$

**Step 2 — Apply Control 1 (Inherent Safety by Design):** Redesign sensor with hardware limit. New severity = 2 (Minor), New probability = 2 (Remote).

$$R_{C1} = 2 \times 2 = 4 \quad (\text{Low})$$

$$\Delta R_1 = 12 - 4 = 8$$

**Step 3 — Apply Control 2 (Protective Measure in Device):** Add redundant software alarm. Probability further reduces to 1 (Improbable).

$$R_{C2} = 2 \times 1 = 2 \quad (\text{Negligible})$$

$$\Delta R_2 = 4 - 2 = 2$$

**Step 4 — Residual Risk:**

$$\boxed{R_{\text{residual}} = 2 \leq R_{\text{acceptable}}}$$

**ALARP Comment (2 marks):**

- After Control 1, risk dropped from 12 to 4 (large gain). The cost of redesign was ₹500,000.
- After Control 2, risk further dropped from 4 to 2. The alarm cost ₹50,000.
- Marginal benefit $B = 4 \rightarrow 2$ corresponds to a small probability reduction; cost is reasonable.
- Since the residual risk is now "Negligible / Acceptable," and any further control (e.g., labeling) would yield $\Delta R \approx 0$, the ALARP condition is satisfied.

---

### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — Describe the FDA Risk-Based Classification for medical devices with examples. Explain the 510(k) and PMA pathways. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

**FDA Classification System (3 marks):**

The FDA classifies medical devices into three classes based on the level of risk and the regulatory controls needed to provide reasonable assurance of safety and effectiveness.

| Class | Risk Level | Controls | Examples |
|-------|------------|----------|----------|
| **Class I** | Low | General Controls only | Bandages, stethoscopes, surgical gloves |
| **Class II** | Moderate | General Controls + Special Controls + **510(k)** | BP monitors, infusion pumps, ECG machines |
| **Class III** | High | General Controls + Special Controls + **PMA** | Pacemakers, heart valves, implantable defibrillators |

**510(k) Premarket Notification (2 marks):**

The 510(k) is a premarket submission to the FDA demonstrating that the device to be marketed is **substantially equivalent** to a legally marketed predicate device. A 510(k) does not require clinical data for many devices; engineering testing demonstrating equivalence to the predicate is sufficient. Cleared 510(k) devices receive FDA clearance, not approval.

**Premarket Approval (PMA — 2 marks):**

PMA is the FDA's most stringent device marketing authorization. Required for Class III devices, it requires demonstration of safety and effectiveness through:
- Valid scientific evidence (often including clinical trials)
- Comprehensive manufacturing information
- Detailed labeling and intended use
- Inspections of the manufacturing facility

PMA-approved devices receive FDA approval (not just clearance). Examples include heart valves, implantable pacemakers, and high-risk diagnostic devices.

---

#### Part (b) — Explain the EU MDR 2017/745 conformity assessment routes for Class I, IIa, IIb, and III devices. Mention the role of Notified Bodies. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Conformity Assessment is the systematic process used to demonstrate that a medical device meets the **General Safety and Performance Requirements (GSPR)** of EU MDR 2017/745.

**Routes by Class (4 marks):**

| Class | Route | Notified Body? |
|-------|-------|----------------|
| **Class I (non-sterile, non-measuring)** | Manufacturer's self-declaration + Declaration of Conformity | No |
| **Class Is, Im, Ir** (sterile / measuring / reusable surgical) | Self-declaration + Notified Body audit of relevant processes | Yes (limited scope) |
| **Class IIa** | Annex IX (QMS) OR Annex XI (Production QA) | Yes |
| **Class IIb** | Annex IX (QMS) + Annex X (Type Examination based on production QA) | Yes |
| **Class III** | Annex IX + Annex X + EU Technical Documentation Assessment per Annex IX §5 | Yes (extensive) |

**Role of Notified Body (2 marks):**

A **Notified Body** is a third-party organization designated by an EU Member State and notified to the European Commission. Its roles include:
- **QMS Audit** — verify ISO 13485 compliance.
- **Technical Documentation Assessment** — verify GSPR conformity.
- **Type Examination** — test a representative sample for Class IIb and III.
- **Certificate Issuance** — issue EU certificates required for CE Marking.
- **Surveillance Audits** — annual surveillance to maintain validity.

CE Marking may only be affixed after the Declaration of Conformity is signed, and (where required) the Notified Body certificate is in place.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where Students Lose Marks (per KTU 2024 valuation pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing Verification with Validation** — these are *not* synonyms. Verification = outputs vs. inputs; Validation = product vs. user need.
> 2. **Skipping the Block Diagram** — In a 7-mark question on ISO 14971, the block diagram carries **2 marks**; missing it is a guaranteed loss.
> 3. **Mixing FDA and EU terminology** — write *510(k) for FDA, CE Mark for EU*; do not interchange.
> 4. **Forgetting the Priority Order of Risk Control** — Inherent Safety by Design → Protective Measure → Information for Safety. Writing them in reverse order = 0 marks for that step.
> 5. **Not writing units** in numerical answers (e.g., $\mu\text{A}$, $\text{V}$, $\text{mmHg}$).
> 6. **Skipping the conclusion line** — every derivation must end with a boxed final answer.
> 7. **Forgetting DHF/DMR/DHR distinction** in QMS questions — this is a 2-mark item frequently missed.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- ✅ **Three pillars of medical device safety** = Electrical (IEC 60601) + Mechanical (IEC 60601) + Biological (ISO 10993).
- ✅ **IEC 60601-1** = basic safety + essential performance.
- ✅ **Patient Leakage Current (Type BF):** $\leq 100\,\mu\text{A}$ (NC), $\leq 500\,\mu\text{A}$ (SFC).
- ✅ **Patient Leakage Current (Type CF):** $\leq 10\,\mu\text{A}$ (NC), $\leq 50\,\mu\text{A}$ (SFC).
- ✅ **Risk = Severity × Probability** (qualitative scoring).
- ✅ **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable.
- ✅ **ISO 14971** = risk management lifecycle.
- ✅ **Risk control priority** = Inherent Safety by Design → Protective Measure → Information for Safety.
- ✅ **FDA Classes** = I (general controls), II (510(k)), III (PMA).
- ✅ **EU MDR Classes** = I, IIa, IIb, III (with CE Mark).
- ✅ **India CDSCO** = Classes A, B, C, D under MDR 2017.
- ✅ **Verification** = outputs meet inputs. **Validation** = product meets user need.
- ✅ **ISO 13485** = QMS standard for medical devices.
- ✅ **DHF (Design History File)** = record of all design activities.
- ✅ **DMR (Device Master Record)** = instructions to produce the device.
- ✅ **DHR (Device History Record)** = actual batch production record.
- ✅ **Conformity Assessment** = QMS audit + Technical Documentation Assessment (Notified Body for IIa and above).
- ✅ **510(k)** = substantial equivalence to a predicate device.
- ✅ **PMA** = FDA's most stringent review (Class III, clinical data mandatory).
- ✅ **CE Mark** = EU market access symbol after conformity assessment.
- ✅ **IMDRF/GHTF** = global harmonization body.
- ✅ **UDI (Unique Device Identification)** = required in US (FDA UDI), EU (EUDAMED), and India.
- ✅ **CAPA** = Corrective and Preventive Action (mandatory under ISO 13485 §8.5).

**Critical Standards Map:**

$$\text{Medical Device Compliance} = \text{ISO 13485} \otimes \text{ISO 14971} \otimes \text{ISO 10993} \otimes \text{IEC 60601-1} \otimes \text{ISO 14708} \otimes \text{ISO 62366 (Usability)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU Exam:** When asked about a *specific pathway* (FDA, EU, India), always start with a **one-line definition**, then a **table** of classes/controls, then a **process flow** or block diagram. This is the proven scoring pattern.
